selected_rules.activation_condition: Clinically N-0 neck
selected_rules.execute: Do not use PET as a better alternative to conventional imaging for nodal staging
selected_rules.source_statement: In the clinically N-0 neck, PET does not appear to be better than conventional imaging, because of an unacceptably high false-negative rate.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Clinically accessible site of suspected recurrence or surveillance after treatment
selected_rules.execute: Do not rely on PET for assessment because accessible sites did not benefit from PET imaging
selected_rules.source_statement: With respect to recurrence and tumour surveillance after treatment, the evidence suggests that sites of disease that are clinically accessible for assessment did not benefit from PET imaging.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has head and neck squamous cell carcinoma and conventional imaging is equivocal, or treatment may be significantly modified
selected_rules.execute: Use PET for M staging and bilateral nodal staging
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is recommended in the M and bilateral nodal staging of all patients with head and neck squamous cell carcinoma where conventional imaging is equivocal, or where treatment may be significantly modified.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Primary site is unknown after conventional imaging
selected_rules.execute: Use PET after conventional imaging and before or in addition to diagnostic panendoscopy
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is recommended in all patients after conventional imaging and in addition to, or prior to, diagnostic panendoscopy where the primary site is unknown.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has cervical lymph node metastases and the primary site is unknown after conventional imaging or clinical examination
selected_rules.execute: Use PET/CT prior to panendoscopy to help identify the primary tumor
selected_rules.source_statement: One primary study indicated that patients with cervical metastasis and an unknown primary site after undergoing conventional imaging or clinical examination benefit from PET/CT prior to panendoscopy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has nasopharyngeal carcinoma and conventional imaging is equivocal
selected_rules.execute: Use PET for staging
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is recommended for staging and assessment of recurrence of patients with nasopharyngeal carcinoma if conventional imaging is equivocal.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has nasopharyngeal carcinoma and conventional imaging is equivocal for recurrence assessment
selected_rules.execute: Use PET for assessment of recurrence
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is recommended for staging and assessment of recurrence of patients with nasopharyngeal carcinoma if conventional imaging is equivocal.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Need to detect distant metastatic disease in head and neck cancer
selected_rules.execute: Use PET because it is better than conventional imaging for distant metastasis detection
selected_rules.source_statement: There is strong evidence that PET imaging is valuable in detecting distant metastatic disease and is better than conventional imaging.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 59-year-old man presents with a firm, painless left level II cervical lymph node that has enlarged over 6 weeks. He has no dysphagia, odynophagia, otalgia, weight loss, or visible mucosal lesion on careful head and neck examination. Flexible nasopharyngolaryngoscopy is unrevealing, and contrast-enhanced CT of the neck does not identify a clear primary tumor. The neck mass is easily palpable and accessible for physical examination and biopsy. The clinician is considering PET/CT to look for nodal disease and to help localize a primary site before panendoscopy.
recommended_actions: Use PET/CT prior to panendoscopy to help identify the primary tumor in a patient with cervical lymph node metastases and an unknown primary after conventional imaging or clinical examination.
Do not rely on PET for assessment of the accessible neck mass because clinically accessible sites did not benefit from PET imaging.
source_case.rationale: This vignette triggers the recommendation to use PET/CT before panendoscopy because the patient has cervical nodal metastasis with an unknown primary after conventional imaging and exam. It also triggers the caution against relying on PET for assessment of a clinically accessible site, since the palpable neck mass is directly examinable.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 59-year-old man presents with a firm, painless left level II cervical lymph node that has enlarged over 6 weeks. He has no dysphagia, odynophagia, otalgia, weight loss, or visible mucosal lesion on careful head and neck examination. Flexible nasopharyngolaryngoscopy is unrevealing, and contrast-enhanced CT of the neck does not identify a clear primary tumor. The neck mass is palpable but somewhat deep and lies adjacent to the carotid sheath, so the clinician is considering PET/CT before panendoscopy to help localize a primary site. However, fine-needle aspiration of the node has already shown metastatic squamous cell carcinoma with p16 positivity, and the patient’s primary workup is being expedited for definitive chemoradiation planning.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like an unknown primary with cervical nodal disease after unrevealing exam and CT, but the nodal disease has already been pathologically characterized and the workflow has shifted to treatment planning rather than primary-site discovery before endoscopy.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Order PET/CT prior to panendoscopy to localize the occult primary tumor
Proceed with PET/CT as the next diagnostic step in an unknown primary neck mass
Use PET/CT to guide the initial search before tissue confirmation and treatment planning
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the cited rule because the primary-site workup has effectively moved beyond the scenario of an unknown cervical metastasis pending endoscopic evaluation; the node is already biopsied and confirmed as metastatic SCC. A safer interpretation is to continue standard head and neck oncologic staging and treatment planning, rather than assuming PET/CT is indicated solely to identify the primary before panendoscopy.
challenging_examples.rationale: Rule R005 applies to cervical lymph node metastases with unknown primary after conventional imaging or clinical examination. Here, the subtle change is that the node has already been proven metastatic and the case is no longer a straightforward pre-panendoscopy unknown-primary workup; the recommendation is therefore not directly triggered.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 59-year-old man presents with a firm, painless left level II cervical lymph node that has enlarged over 6 weeks. He has no dysphagia, odynophagia, otalgia, weight loss, or visible mucosal lesion on careful head and neck examination. Flexible nasopharyngolaryngoscopy is unrevealing, and contrast-enhanced CT of the neck does not identify a clear primary tumor. The neck mass is readily palpable and accessible for examination, and the clinician is considering PET/CT before panendoscopy to help localize a primary site. The only additional finding is a mildly enlarged right upper cervical node on ultrasound that was not seen on the initial exam, while the index left node remains the dominant accessible lesion.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The overall presentation still resembles an accessible neck mass with unknown primary after unrevealing exam and CT, but there is a second node on ultrasound that complicates the idea that the lesion being assessed is simply an easily accessible site for surveillance or direct evaluation.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Use PET/CT to assess the palpable neck mass because it is part of the unknown-primary workup
Rely on PET/CT to evaluate both cervical nodes before panendoscopy
Order PET/CT as the best next test for a clinically accessible neck lesion
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not concordant with the rule cautioning against PET for clinically accessible sites. The palpable node itself can be directly examined and biopsied, and the added ultrasound finding does not make PET the preferred assessment for that accessible lesion. A safer interpretation is to prioritize direct tissue evaluation and standard head and neck staging steps rather than using PET to assess the palpable mass.
challenging_examples.rationale: R002 concerns recurrence or surveillance after treatment and warns against relying on PET for clinically accessible sites. The vignette is made deceptive by keeping the mass palpable and the workup similar, but the presence of an accessible lesion means PET should not be treated as the primary assessment tool for that site.

selected_rules.activation_condition: breast cancer is HER2-positive
selected_rules.execute: Do not use multigene profiling assays because evidence is insufficient.
selected_rules.source_statement: There is currently insufficient evidence to use multigene profiling assays among patients with either HER2-positive ... breast cancers.

selected_rules.activation_condition: breast cancer is triple negative
selected_rules.execute: Do not use multigene profiling assays because evidence is insufficient.
selected_rules.source_statement: There is currently insufficient evidence to use multigene profiling assays among patients with either HER2-positive or triple negative breast cancers.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering multiple multigene profiling assays on the same tumour specimen for one treatment decision
selected_rules.execute: Do not order multiple different multigene profiling assays on the same tumour specimen for a single decision.
selected_rules.source_statement: Requesting multiple tests with different multigene profiling assays on an individual tumour specimen to guide a single treatment decision is discouraged.

selected_rules.activation_condition: node-positive ER-positive HER2-negative breast cancer with higher risk features and considering withholding adjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Do not rely on Prosigna, EndoPredict/EPclin, or Breast Cancer Index to justify withholding adjuvant chemotherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: The Prosigna, EndoPredict/EPclin, and Breast Cancer Index assays can identify low-risk node-positive patients whose prognostic outcomes are favourable; however, these assays have not demonstrated predictive evidence to support withholding adjuvant chemotherapy among higher risk, node-positive, ER-positive, HER2-negative breast cancer patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering MammaPrint, Oncotype DX, Prosigna, or EndoPredict to extend adjuvant endocrine therapy
selected_rules.execute: Do not use these assays to guide extension of adjuvant endocrine therapy because evidence is insufficient.
selected_rules.source_statement: MammaPrint, Oncotype DX, Prosigna, and EndoPredict currently have insufficient evidence to guide extension of adjuvant endocrine therapy;

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has lymph node-negative disease or 1-3 positive lymph nodes and is being considered for adjuvant chemotherapy, with supporting clinical/pathological/patient factors
selected_rules.execute: Use a multigene profiling assay to support the adjuvant chemotherapy decision.
selected_rules.source_statement: Multigene profiling assays are recommended for use in patients with lymph node-negative or lymph node-positive (1-3 lymph nodes) disease who are under consideration for adjuvant chemotherapy if the use is supported by other clinical, pathological, or patient-related factors.

selected_rules.activation_condition: one multigene profiling assay is being ordered for a patient
selected_rules.execute: Request only one assay per patient for a specific treatment decision.
selected_rules.source_statement: One multigene profiling assay should be requested per patient to guide a specific treatment decision.

selected_rules.activation_condition: early-stage ER-positive, HER2-negative breast cancer and systemic therapy decision is needed
selected_rules.execute: Consider using a multigene profiling assay to help guide systemic therapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients with early-stage estrogen receptor (ER)-positive/human epidermal growth factor 2 (HER2)-negative breast cancer, clinicians should consider using multigene profiling assays ... to help guide the use of systemic therapy.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old postmenopausal woman is seen after lumpectomy for a 1.8 cm invasive ductal carcinoma. Pathology shows ER-positive, HER2-negative disease with 2 of 12 axillary lymph nodes involved, no distant metastases, and intermediate grade. She is otherwise healthy and is anxious to avoid chemotherapy if it would not add meaningful benefit. Her oncologist is deciding on adjuvant systemic therapy and is considering whether a genomic assay could help refine the chemotherapy discussion. The team plans to use the result for this single treatment decision and does not intend to send multiple different assays on the same specimen.
recommended_actions: Use a multigene profiling assay to support the adjuvant chemotherapy decision, since she has node-positive disease with 1-3 positive nodes and relevant clinical/pathological factors.
Request only one multigene profiling assay for this specific treatment decision.
Consider using a multigene profiling assay to help guide systemic therapy in this early-stage ER-positive, HER2-negative breast cancer case.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches early-stage ER-positive, HER2-negative breast cancer with 1-3 positive nodes and a real adjuvant chemotherapy decision, which supports use of a multigene profiling assay. The rules also specify that only one assay should be ordered for a single treatment decision. The case does not trigger any rule against assay use, because it is not HER2-positive, triple-negative, or a scenario of withholding chemotherapy based on a low-risk result.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old postmenopausal woman returns after lumpectomy for a 1.8 cm invasive ductal carcinoma. Pathology shows ER-positive, HER2-negative disease with 2 of 12 axillary lymph nodes involved, no distant metastases, and intermediate grade. She is otherwise healthy and is anxious to avoid chemotherapy if it would not add meaningful benefit. Her oncologist is deciding on adjuvant systemic therapy and is considering whether a genomic assay could help refine the chemotherapy discussion. The team is planning to send both Oncotype DX and MammaPrint on the same tumor block to see whether the two results point in the same direction before making a recommendation.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The tumor biology, nodal burden, and adjuvant chemotherapy decision all remain the same, but the case now involves ordering more than one multigene assay on the same specimen for a single treatment decision.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Order both Oncotype DX and MammaPrint to increase confidence in the adjuvant chemotherapy decision.
Proceed with parallel genomic profiling on the same specimen before finalizing systemic therapy recommendations.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant because multiple multigene profiling assays should not be ordered on the same tumor specimen to guide one treatment decision. The safer interpretation is to select a single assay if genomic testing is being used at all.
challenging_examples.rationale: The original scenario supported use of one multigene assay in node-positive ER-positive, HER2-negative disease. The mismatch is the addition of two assays for one decision, which is specifically discouraged.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old postmenopausal woman is seen after lumpectomy for a 1.8 cm invasive ductal carcinoma. Pathology shows ER-positive, HER2-negative disease with 2 of 12 axillary lymph nodes involved, no distant metastases, and intermediate grade. She is otherwise healthy and wants to avoid chemotherapy if possible. Her oncologist is deciding on adjuvant systemic therapy and wonders whether a genomic assay could help. However, the surgical pathology report notes a single small focus of metastatic carcinoma in one sentinel node only, and all other sampled nodes are negative, with no extranodal extension. The team is considering whether the result could justify omitting chemotherapy.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like node-positive ER-positive, HER2-negative breast cancer with an adjuvant chemotherapy question, but the nodal burden is lower-risk and limited to a single small sentinel-node focus rather than the higher-risk node-positive setting targeted by the rule.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Use a low-risk multigene assay result to omit adjuvant chemotherapy.
Order a prognostic assay specifically to justify withholding chemotherapy in this node-positive setting.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for using these assays to withhold chemotherapy in higher-risk node-positive disease. The safer interpretation is that the patient has limited nodal involvement, and any genomic test would need to be used cautiously without relying on it to rule out chemotherapy benefit in a higher-risk node-positive context.
challenging_examples.rationale: The recommendation against withholding chemotherapy is tied to higher-risk node-positive ER-positive, HER2-negative disease. Here, the nodal finding is subtly less concerning than the source case, undermining direct application of that rule.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Newly diagnosed biopsy-confirmed prostate cancer; intermediate- or high-risk; being considered for radical therapy; knowledge of extraprostatic extension would alter management
selected_rules.execute: Consider multiparametric MRI (mpMRI) for pre-treatment local staging to assess extraprostatic extension (EPE).
selected_rules.source_statement: Multiparametric MRI (mpMRI) use for pre-treatment local staging of prostate cancer is a reasonable option for assessment of extraprostatic extension (EPE) in intermediate-and highrisk patients being considered for radical therapy if knowledge of EPE will alter management.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man presents after a transrectal biopsy confirmed acinar adenocarcinoma of the prostate, Gleason 4+3=7, with PSA 14.8 ng/mL and cancer in 6 of 12 cores. He has no bone pain or urinary retention, and his exam is otherwise unremarkable. His urologist is discussing radical prostatectomy versus radiation-based treatment, and the team notes that knowing whether there is extraprostatic extension would affect the surgical plan and whether nerve-sparing could be attempted. He is otherwise fit and wants definitive treatment.
recommended_actions: Consider multiparametric MRI (mpMRI) for pre-treatment local staging to assess extraprostatic extension (EPE).
source_case.rationale: This is a newly diagnosed, biopsy-confirmed intermediate-risk prostate cancer case being considered for radical therapy, and knowledge of possible extraprostatic extension would change management. That matches the rule’s activation condition, supporting pre-treatment mpMRI for local staging.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man is referred after transrectal biopsy confirmed acinar adenocarcinoma of the prostate, Gleason 4+3=7, with PSA 14.8 ng/mL and cancer in 6 of 12 cores. He has no bone pain or urinary retention, and his exam is otherwise unremarkable. His urologist is discussing radical prostatectomy versus radiation-based treatment, and the team initially considered local staging because extraprostatic extension could affect nerve-sparing. However, he underwent a high-quality prostate mpMRI last week at an outside center, which showed a confined lesion without suspicious extraprostatic extension or seminal vesicle invasion. He is otherwise fit and wants definitive treatment.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The patient still has newly diagnosed intermediate-risk prostate cancer and is being considered for radical therapy, but the needed local staging information has already been obtained recently and would not be expected to change the plan.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Order another pre-treatment multiparametric prostate MRI for local staging
Recommend mpMRI to assess extraprostatic extension before deciding on surgery
Delay definitive treatment until repeat MRI is completed
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the rule because the key question has already been answered by a recent high-quality mpMRI showing no extraprostatic extension. The safer interpretation is to proceed with definitive treatment planning using the existing imaging rather than repeating local staging solely to look for EPE.
challenging_examples.rationale: The recommendation applies when knowledge of EPE would alter management and imaging is still needed. Here, the same staging information was already obtained and does not create a new indication for additional mpMRI.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man presents after a transrectal biopsy confirmed acinar adenocarcinoma of the prostate, Gleason 4+3=7, with PSA 14.8 ng/mL and cancer in 6 of 12 cores. He has no bone pain or urinary retention, and his exam is otherwise unremarkable. His urologist is discussing radical prostatectomy versus radiation-based treatment, and the team notes that knowing whether there is extraprostatic extension would affect the surgical plan and whether nerve-sparing could be attempted. On review, however, he also has newly diagnosed metastatic disease on staging CT with enlarged retroperitoneal lymph nodes and multiple sclerotic bone lesions. He is otherwise fit and wants treatment.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Most of the presentation matches a newly diagnosed intermediate-risk prostate cancer being considered for radical therapy, but the staging scan has already shown distant metastatic spread, shifting the clinical context away from localized pre-treatment staging for surgery planning.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Order multiparametric MRI to assess extraprostatic extension before planning radical prostatectomy
Use mpMRI for local staging to guide nerve-sparing surgery
Proceed with prostate MRI because the patient is newly diagnosed and intermediate risk
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the rule because the patient is no longer in a localized pre-treatment setting where EPE assessment would inform radical local therapy. The safer interpretation is that management should focus on metastatic prostate cancer treatment planning rather than local staging for prostatectomy decisions.
challenging_examples.rationale: The rule is intended for intermediate- or high-risk patients being considered for radical therapy when EPE knowledge changes management. Documented distant metastases invalidate that localized staging scenario.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Women with cervical cancer for whom radiotherapy is being considered
selected_rules.execute: Offer concurrent cisplatin with the course of radiotherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: Women with cervical cancer for whom treatment with radiotherapy is being considered (described above) should be offered concurrent cisplatin with their course of radiotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Women with cervical cancer receiving cisplatin-based chemotherapy plus radiotherapy compared with radiotherapy alone or hydroxyurea
selected_rules.execute: Use concurrent cisplatin-based chemotherapy with radiotherapy because pooled survival favored this approach.
selected_rules.source_statement: Pooled survival rates detected a statistically significant effect in favour of cisplatin-based chemotherapy plus radiotherapy compared with radiotherapy alone or with hydroxyurea (relative risk of death, 0.74; 95% confidence interval, 0.64 to 0.86).

selected_rules.activation_condition: Women with locally advanced cervical cancer treated with radiotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Offer concurrent cisplatin-based chemotherapy with radiotherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: The pooled relative risk of death among the six trials that enrolled only women with locally advanced cervical cancer was 0.78 (95% confidence interval, 0.67 to 0.90) in favour of cisplatin-based chemotherapy and radiotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Women with high-risk early-stage cervical cancer treated with radiotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Offer concurrent cisplatin-based chemotherapy with radiotherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: The pooled relative risk for the two trials in high-risk early-stage disease also demonstrated a significant benefit for the addition of cisplatin-based chemotherapy to radiotherapy (relative risk, 0.56; 95% confidence interval, 0.41 to 0.77).

selected_rules.activation_condition: Women with bulky clinical stage IB cervical cancer (>4 cm) who are treated with radiotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Offer concurrent cisplatin with radiotherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: These recommendations apply to women with cervical cancer for whom primary treatment with radiotherapy is being considered: -those with bulky clinical stage IB (>4 cm) cervical cancer, who are treated with radiotherapy,

selected_rules.activation_condition: Women with high-risk early-stage cervical cancer after hysterectomy who will be treated with radiotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Offer concurrent cisplatin with radiotherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: These recommendations apply to women with cervical cancer for whom primary treatment with radiotherapy is being considered: -those with high-risk early-stage cervical cancer (node-positive or margin-positive), who will be treated with radiotherapy following hysterectomy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Women with locally advanced cervical cancer for whom primary treatment with radiotherapy is being considered
selected_rules.execute: Offer concurrent cisplatin with radiotherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: These recommendations apply to women with cervical cancer for whom primary treatment with radiotherapy is being considered: -those with locally advanced cervical cancer,

selected_rules.activation_condition: When choosing a cisplatin regimen for concurrent chemoradiation
selected_rules.execute: Consider weekly cisplatin 40 mg/m2 based on available toxicity data.
selected_rules.source_statement: Based on the review of the available toxicity data from the randomized controlled trials, the Disease Site Group felt that cisplatinum should be given weekly (40 mg/m2).

source_case.patient_vignette: A 47-year-old woman presents with postcoital bleeding and pelvic pain. Biopsy confirms squamous cell carcinoma of the cervix. MRI shows a 5.5 cm cervical mass with parametrial extension but no distant metastases, consistent with locally advanced disease. Her oncology team is planning definitive pelvic radiotherapy with brachytherapy. She has adequate renal function and performance status to receive chemotherapy. The patient asks whether anything should be added to radiation to improve outcomes.
recommended_actions: Offer concurrent cisplatin with the course of radiotherapy.
Offer concurrent cisplatin-based chemotherapy with radiotherapy for locally advanced cervical cancer.
Consider weekly cisplatin 40 mg/m2 when selecting a concurrent cisplatin regimen.
source_case.rationale: The vignette describes a woman with cervical cancer in whom radiotherapy is being considered, which triggers the general recommendation to add concurrent cisplatin. Because her disease is locally advanced and primary treatment is radiotherapy, the locally advanced cervical cancer rule also applies. The weekly cisplatin 40 mg/m2 option is included as a conditional regimen consideration based on available toxicity data.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 47-year-old woman presents with postcoital bleeding and pelvic pain. Biopsy confirms squamous cell carcinoma of the cervix. MRI shows a 5.5 cm cervical mass with parametrial extension and a suspicious pelvic sidewall node, but there are also small pulmonary nodules that were not present on prior imaging and are concerning for distant metastases. Her oncology team is discussing treatment options and initially considers pelvic radiotherapy with brachytherapy for symptom control. She has adequate renal function and a good performance status. The patient asks whether anything should be added to radiation to improve outcomes.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still features cervical squamous cell carcinoma, a large locally invasive mass, and radiation planning, but the new lung lesions shift her from nonmetastatic locally advanced disease to metastatic disease.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer concurrent weekly cisplatin with pelvic radiotherapy
Proceed with definitive cisplatin-based chemoradiation
Use weekly cisplatin 40 mg/m2 during brachytherapy-based treatment
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for adding cisplatin to definitive pelvic radiotherapy because the disease is no longer limited to locally advanced nonmetastatic cervical cancer. The safer interpretation is that she needs staging confirmation and a metastatic-disease treatment discussion; pelvic radiation may be used for palliation, but the cited concurrent cisplatin recommendations do not apply.
challenging_examples.rationale: The recommendation applies to women with cervical cancer for whom radiotherapy is being considered in the setting of locally advanced or other nonmetastatic disease. The presence of probable distant metastases invalidates that setting, so the original concurrent cisplatin recommendation is no longer applicable.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 47-year-old woman presents with postcoital bleeding and pelvic pain. Biopsy confirms squamous cell carcinoma of the cervix. MRI shows a 3.8 cm cervical mass confined to the cervix without parametrial extension or nodal disease. Because of her renal function, she is scheduled for pelvic radiotherapy alone with brachytherapy rather than combined chemoradiation. She asks whether anything should be added to radiation to improve outcomes.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The tumor type, symptoms, and use of pelvic radiotherapy remain the same, but the mass is now under the bulky threshold and lacks the locally advanced features that would support concurrent cisplatin.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Add concurrent cisplatin to the radiation plan
Recommend weekly cisplatin during pelvic radiotherapy
Treat her as locally advanced cervical cancer with chemoradiation
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the concurrent cisplatin recommendation because she does not have locally advanced disease or bulky stage IB disease. With a 3.8 cm cervix-confined tumor and no parametrial extension, the cited rules for adding cisplatin to radiotherapy do not clearly apply; management should follow the appropriate early-stage pathway rather than default chemoradiation.
challenging_examples.rationale: The rule set supports concurrent cisplatin when radiotherapy is being used for locally advanced disease or bulky stage IB tumors. Here, the tumor is smaller than 4 cm and confined to the cervix, so the key activation criteria are absent.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with resected stage II colon cancer
selected_rules.execute: Do not use routine adjuvant chemotherapy for all stage II patients.
selected_rules.source_statement: The routine use of adjuvant chemotherapy for all patients with stage II colon cancer is not recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: stage II patient without high-risk features who does not require adjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Do not perform MSI/dMMR testing for this decision.
selected_rules.source_statement: In stage II (in the absence of high-risk features) where a patient does not require adjuvant chemotherapy, MSI/dMMR testing is not recommended as it will not influence that decision.

selected_rules.activation_condition: stage II colon cancer with MSI/dMMR after surgery
selected_rules.execute: Do not give fluoropyrimidine monotherapy as adjuvant treatment.
selected_rules.source_statement: Adjuvant chemotherapy with a fluoropyrimidine monotherapy regimen following surgery in patients who have MSI/dMMR is not recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: high-risk stage II colon cancer treated with adjuvant therapy
selected_rules.execute: Use a fluoropyrimidine.
selected_rules.source_statement: When treated with adjuvant therapy, high-risk stage II patients should receive a fluoropyrimidine.

selected_rules.activation_condition: stage II patient being considered for adjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Perform MSI/dMMR testing.
selected_rules.source_statement: MSI/dMMR testing should be performed for all stage II patients for whom adjuvant chemotherapy is being considered.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with resected stage III colon cancer
selected_rules.execute: Use an oxaliplatin-containing regimen such as FOLFOX, FLOX, or XELOX.
selected_rules.source_statement: These oxaliplatin-containing regimens have demonstrated superior overall survival when compared with 5-FU plus LV and are the recommended regimens.

selected_rules.activation_condition: low-risk stage III colon cancer (T1-3 N1)
selected_rules.execute: Prefer 3 months of CAPOX over FOLFOX.
selected_rules.source_statement: Low-risk stage III (T1-3 N1): 3 months of CAPOX is preferred over FOLFOX.

selected_rules.activation_condition: high-risk stage III colon cancer (T4 +/- N2)
selected_rules.execute: Give 6 months of oxaliplatin-based chemotherapy with CAPOX or FOLFOX.
selected_rules.source_statement: High-risk stage III (T4 +/-N2): 6 months of oxaliplatin-based chemotherapy (CAPOX or FOLFOX).

source_case.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man underwent right hemicolectomy for a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma found on screening colonoscopy. Final pathology shows pT3N0 stage II colon cancer with clear margins, 18 lymph nodes examined, no lymphovascular invasion, no perineural invasion, no bowel obstruction, and no perforation. He has recovered well after surgery and is asking whether he should start chemotherapy to reduce recurrence risk. His oncology team is reviewing the pathology and discussing postoperative management.
recommended_actions: Do not use routine adjuvant chemotherapy for all stage II patients.
Do not perform MSI/dMMR testing for this decision because the patient has stage II disease without high-risk features and the result would not influence the adjuvant chemotherapy decision.
source_case.rationale: This is a resected stage II colon cancer case without high-risk features, so routine adjuvant chemotherapy is not recommended. Because he does not require adjuvant chemotherapy based on the available features, MSI/dMMR testing is not recommended for this decision.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man underwent right hemicolectomy for a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma found on screening colonoscopy. Final pathology shows pT3N0 colon cancer with clear margins, 18 lymph nodes examined, no lymphovascular invasion, no perineural invasion, no bowel obstruction, and no perforation. He recovered well after surgery and is asking whether he should start chemotherapy to reduce recurrence risk. His oncology team is reviewing the pathology and considering postoperative management, but notes that his preoperative CEA was mildly elevated at 7.8 ng/mL and has not yet been repeated.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This case closely matches the original resected stage II colon cancer vignette with the same operation, T stage, node count, and otherwise favorable pathology; the subtle difference is the persistently elevated preoperative CEA, which makes him higher risk than the source case and changes the decision framework.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start routine adjuvant fluoropyrimidine chemotherapy for stage II disease
Order MSI/dMMR testing only to decide whether to give postoperative chemotherapy
Reassure him that no additional risk stratification is needed because the pathology is otherwise favorable
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not the same low-risk stage II scenario because an elevated CEA is a potential high-risk feature and should prompt individualized risk assessment rather than automatic application of the no-adjuvant-therapy recommendation. The safer interpretation is to review whether the CEA remains elevated and consider guideline-based high-risk evaluation before deciding on adjuvant treatment.
challenging_examples.rationale: The source recommendation against routine adjuvant chemotherapy and against MSI/dMMR testing applies to stage II disease without high-risk features. A persistently elevated CEA introduces a potential high-risk element, so the case no longer cleanly fits the non-testing, no-routine-chemo pathway.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man underwent right hemicolectomy for a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma found on screening colonoscopy. Final pathology shows pT3N0 stage II colon cancer with clear margins, 18 lymph nodes examined, no lymphovascular invasion, no perineural invasion, no bowel obstruction, and no perforation. He has recovered well after surgery and is asking whether he should start chemotherapy to reduce recurrence risk. His oncology team is reviewing the pathology and discusses postoperative management, but the pathology report also notes that one of the examined nodes contained an isolated tumor cell cluster classified as pN0(i+).
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The presentation remains almost identical to the source case, including the same surgery and otherwise low-risk features. The subtle nodal finding of isolated tumor cells makes this no longer a straightforward stage II, node-negative case.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Treat him as routine stage II colon cancer and omit further clarification
Decide against any adjuvant discussion because the primary tumor is still T3N0
Order MSI/dMMR testing only to decide whether chemotherapy is needed
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not a clean stage II, node-negative colon cancer scenario because isolated tumor cells in a lymph node may alter staging interpretation and should be clarified before applying stage II adjuvant recommendations. The safer interpretation is to confirm the pathologic staging details and then determine whether the patient truly belongs in the low-risk stage II group.
challenging_examples.rationale: The no-routine-chemotherapy and no-MSI-testing recommendations are for resected stage II colon cancer without high-risk features. A pN0(i+) finding creates a staging ambiguity that prevents direct application of the source recommendation until pathology is reconciled.

selected_rules.activation_condition: No clinical recommendations are present in the provided text
selected_rules.execute: Do not extract any treatment, staging, or diagnostic action from this text
selected_rules.source_statement: Recommendations Section 2: Recommendations and Key Evidence Section 3: Guideline Methods Overview Section 4: Systematic Review Section 5: Internal and External Review

selected_rules.activation_condition: Document contains only administrative and copyright/contact information
selected_rules.execute: No clinical action should be derived from the document content
selected_rules.source_statement: For information about this document, please contact Alex Sun, the lead author, through the PEBC via: Phone: 905-527-4322 ext. 42822 Fax: 905 526-6775 E-mail: ccopgi@mcmaster.ca

selected_rules.activation_condition: Text provides report citation and website/contact details only
selected_rules.execute: Do not apply any guideline-based clinical management rule
selected_rules.source_statement: For information about the PEBC and the most current version of all reports, please visit the CCO website at http://www.cancercare.on.ca or contact the PEBC office at: Phone: 905-527-4322 ext. 42822 Fax: 905 526-6775 E-mail: ccopgi@mcmaster.ca

selected_rules.activation_condition: Text states copyright and reproduction restrictions only
selected_rules.execute: No clinical recommendation can be extracted from this statement
selected_rules.source_statement: Copyright This report is copyrighted by Cancer Care Ontario; the report and the illustrations herein may not be reproduced without the express written permission of Cancer Care Ontario.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man is seen in clinic for follow-up after a recent oncology visit. He asks whether he should start any new treatment based on the document he was given, but the handout contains only report citation information, website details, and contact information for the guideline office. There are no clinical findings, diagnoses, staging details, or treatment recommendations included in the text he brought. He has no new symptoms and is mainly seeking clarification about what the document means for his care.
recommended_actions: Do not apply any guideline-based clinical management rule from this document.
Do not derive any clinical action from the report citation, website, or contact details alone.
source_case.rationale: The provided text contains only report citation and website/contact details, with no clinical recommendations or management guidance. This triggers the no-recommendation rule for citation/contact-only content, so no treatment, staging, or diagnostic action should be extracted.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man returns to clinic after an oncology visit, bringing the same handout he was given. This time, the document includes a short disclaimer that the report is for educational use and a footer with copyright language, but it also contains one brief management note embedded in the text: “patients with unresectable stage III disease should be considered for definitive chemoradiation.” The patient, however, has a newly reviewed PET/CT showing widespread liver and bone metastases from the lung primary, and his disease is now documented as stage IV. He asks whether he should start the treatment mentioned in the handout. He has mild fatigue but no new focal symptoms and is mainly trying to understand what the document means for his care.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The vignette still centers on a guideline handout with citation/copyright-style wording and an oncology follow-up visit, but the patient’s actual stage has changed to metastatic disease, so the embedded stage-specific recommendation no longer fits.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start definitive concurrent chemoradiation based on the handout
Treat the case as unresectable stage III non-small cell lung cancer and proceed with curative-intent thoracic radiation
Use the document’s treatment note to guide immediate oncology management
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the cited recommendation because the patient is no longer stage III; the PET/CT now shows distant metastatic disease. A curative-intent definitive chemoradiation recommendation for unresectable stage III disease should not be applied. The safer interpretation is to reassess for stage IV systemic/palliative management rather than follow the stage-specific handout language.
challenging_examples.rationale: The subtle change from unresectable stage III to stage IV metastatic disease invalidates the original management recommendation. The document may still look guideline-like, but the clinical context no longer matches the eligible population.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man is seen in follow-up after an oncology visit and asks whether he should start the treatment mentioned in the paper he brought. The handout now includes a short clinical section stating that patients with EGFR-mutated advanced lung adenocarcinoma should receive an EGFR-targeted agent first line. On review, his tumor testing showed an EGFR exon 19 deletion on the initial biopsy, but a repeat plasma assay obtained after rapid progression was negative, and the report notes that the circulating tumor fraction was very low. Imaging shows bilateral pulmonary nodules and mediastinal adenopathy, but no accessible lesion has been rebiopsied. He feels well aside from a mild cough and wants to know whether the targeted treatment is appropriate now.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case preserves the same oncology follow-up workflow and a molecularly driven lung cancer treatment question, but the most recent molecular result is non-informative/negative in low-tumor-fraction plasma rather than a clear actionable positive result at decision time.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start the EGFR-targeted therapy immediately
Treat the negative plasma result as confirmation that the mutation is absent and switch to another targeted agent
Base treatment solely on the handout without further tissue confirmation
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant because the current actionable biomarker status is uncertain: the repeat plasma test is negative in the setting of very low circulating tumor fraction, which does not reliably negate the prior EGFR exon 19 deletion. The safer interpretation is that treatment should not be selected from the handout alone; the molecular status needs confirmation or careful reconciliation before committing to an EGFR-targeted regimen.
challenging_examples.rationale: The near-miss hinges on biomarker ambiguity. A superficial read suggests a classic EGFR-positive lung cancer scenario, but the available current test is not a definitive positive and cannot be used to directly apply the recommendation without additional confirmation.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has erectile dysfunction alone
selected_rules.execute: Do not use erectile dysfunction alone as a symptom prompting DRE and PSA testing for metastatic prostate cancer
selected_rules.source_statement: The working group did not think it necessary for a man with erectile dysfunction to undergo a DRE and PSA test and therefore excluded it as a symptom of metastatic prostate cancer

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has unexplained hematuria
selected_rules.execute: Do not treat unexplained hematuria as part of the prostate cancer care algorithm; arrange urologic assessment as appropriate
selected_rules.source_statement: The working group also excluded unexplained hematuria as a symptom of metastatic prostate cancer... hematuria requires urologic assessment but is not part of a prostate cancer care algorithm

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has symptoms of metastatic prostate cancer and is age 40 or older
selected_rules.execute: Perform digital rectal examination (DRE) and prostate-specific antigen (PSA) testing
selected_rules.source_statement: patients with symptoms of metastatic prostate cancer should have a DRE and PSA was endorsed

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has lower urinary tract symptoms (LUTS)
selected_rules.execute: Perform DRE
selected_rules.source_statement: a man with LUTS should have a DRE

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has LUTS and PSA testing is being considered
selected_rules.execute: Exclude urinary infection before PSA testing
selected_rules.source_statement: The working group also endorsed the recommendations to exclude urinary infection before PSA testing

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has LUTS and PSA testing is being considered
selected_rules.execute: Postpone PSA testing for at least one month after treatment
selected_rules.source_statement: The working group also endorsed the recommendations to... postpone PSA testing for at least one month after treatment

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has an abnormal incidental PSA result
selected_rules.execute: Perform DRE
selected_rules.source_statement: if an incidental PSA test was abnormal, then standard practise would be to perform a DRE

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has LUTS
selected_rules.execute: Discuss PSA testing and consider PSA based on individual risk assessment
selected_rules.source_statement: a man with LUTS should have a DRE and a discussion about PSA testing

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man presents with 8 months of lower urinary tract symptoms, including hesitancy, weak stream, and nocturia twice nightly. He denies bone pain, weight loss, fever, dysuria, hematuria, or erectile dysfunction. He has no known urinary infection symptoms and has not had any recent treatment for urinary issues. On review, his main concern is whether he might have prostate disease because of the persistent urinary symptoms. He is otherwise well and takes no regular medications. His exam is unremarkable aside from mild suprapubic fullness; no prior PSA testing has been done.
recommended_actions: Perform a digital rectal examination (DRE).
Discuss PSA testing and consider PSA based on individual risk assessment.
source_case.rationale: The vignette includes lower urinary tract symptoms in a man aged over 40, which triggers the recommendation for DRE. LUTS also supports a discussion about PSA testing with consideration of individual risk. No features of metastatic prostate cancer, abnormal incidental PSA, erectile dysfunction alone, or unexplained hematuria were included, so those rules were not triggered.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man presents with 8 months of lower urinary tract symptoms, including hesitancy, weak stream, and nocturia twice nightly. He denies bone pain, weight loss, fever, dysuria, hematuria, or erectile dysfunction. He is otherwise well and takes no regular medications. On review, his main concern is whether he might have prostate disease because of the persistent urinary symptoms. His primary care clinician notes that a urine dip done last week was positive for leukocytes and nitrites, and he was started on antibiotics yesterday for presumed urinary infection. The patient asks whether PSA testing should still be arranged now, and no prior PSA testing has been done.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The presentation still centers on chronic LUTS in a man over 40, with the same prostate-cancer concern and no obvious metastatic symptoms, but a concurrent urinary infection and immediate post-treatment timing alter the PSA workflow.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Order PSA testing immediately during the current urinary symptoms
Proceed with prostate cancer workup without waiting for the infection to clear
Schedule PSA testing now because the LUTS have been persistent for months
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for immediate PSA testing because a urinary infection has not been excluded and treatment has just started. The safer interpretation is to defer PSA until the infection has resolved and at least one month has passed after treatment before testing, while still performing DRE for LUTS if clinically appropriate.
challenging_examples.rationale: The case preserves the LUTS-driven prostate evaluation context but adds active urinary infection and treatment yesterday, which invalidate immediate PSA testing under the rule requiring infection exclusion and postponement after treatment.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man presents with 8 months of lower urinary tract symptoms, including hesitancy, weak stream, and nocturia twice nightly. He denies bone pain, weight loss, fever, dysuria, hematuria, or erectile dysfunction. He is otherwise well and takes no regular medications. On review, his main concern is whether he might have prostate disease because of the persistent urinary symptoms. He had a PSA drawn at a workplace screening event 2 weeks ago, which returned mildly elevated, and he was told to follow up with his doctor. A repeat urine test today is negative for infection, and he has not been treated for any urinary problem recently.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The man still has classic LUTS and the same prostate-cancer concern, but the key change is that the PSA is already abnormal from outside screening rather than being part of the initial decision to test.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Repeat PSA and begin the full prostate cancer evaluation pathway immediately
Ignore the incidental PSA result and treat the urinary symptoms only
Assume the elevated PSA alone confirms metastatic prostate cancer
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not a case for deciding whether to discuss PSA testing; the PSA has already been obtained and is incidentally abnormal. The guideline-concordant next step in this setting is DRE rather than treating it as a new testing decision, with further assessment based on the abnormal result and overall clinical picture.
challenging_examples.rationale: The vignette looks like routine LUTS-based evaluation, but the presence of an already abnormal incidental PSA changes the applicable rule set and invalidates the original PSA-discussion recommendation.

selected_rules.activation_condition: newly diagnosed low-risk prostate cancer requiring or choosing active treatment and eligible for treatment
selected_rules.execute: Offer low-dose rate brachytherapy alone, external beam radiation therapy alone, and/or radical prostatectomy.
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with low-risk prostate cancer who require or choose active treatment, LDR alone, EBRT alone, or RP should be offered to eligible patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: newly diagnosed intermediate-risk prostate cancer choosing EBRT with or without androgen-deprivation therapy and eligible for brachytherapy
selected_rules.execute: Offer brachytherapy boost using low-dose rate or high-dose rate brachytherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with intermediate-risk prostate cancer choosing EBRT with or without androgen-deprivation therapy (ADT), brachytherapy boost (LDR or high-dose rate should be offered to eligible patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: newly diagnosed high-risk prostate cancer receiving EBRT and androgen-deprivation therapy and eligible for brachytherapy
selected_rules.execute: Offer brachytherapy boost using low-dose rate or high-dose rate brachytherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with high-risk prostate cancer receiving EBRT and ADT, brachytherapy boost (LDR or HDR) should be offered to eligible patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is receiving low-dose rate brachytherapy
selected_rules.execute: Use iodine-125 or palladium-103 as reasonable isotope options.
selected_rules.source_statement: 125 I and 103 Pd are each reasonable isotope options for patients receiving LDR brachytherapy;

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with prostate cancer is being managed under this guideline
selected_rules.execute: Encourage participation in clinical trials testing novel or targeted approaches.
selected_rules.source_statement: Patients should be encouraged to participate in clinical trials to test novel or targeted approaches to this disease.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is being counseled about management options
selected_rules.execute: Counsel about all applicable management options in a balanced, objective manner, preferably from multiple disciplines.
selected_rules.source_statement: Patients should be counseled about all their management options (surgery, EBRT, active surveillance, as applicable) in a balanced, objective manner, preferably from multiple disciplines.

selected_rules.activation_condition: brachytherapy is being performed
selected_rules.execute: Perform brachytherapy at a center following strict quality-assurance standards.
selected_rules.source_statement: Brachytherapy should be performed at a center following strict quality-assurance standards.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has intermediate-risk prostate cancer and is choosing EBRT with or without ADT
selected_rules.execute: Offer brachytherapy boost with LDR or HDR to eligible patients.
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with intermediate-risk prostate cancer choosing EBRT with or without androgen-deprivation therapy (ADT), brachytherapy boost (LDR or high-dose rate should be offered to eligible patients.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 66-year-old man with newly diagnosed prostate adenocarcinoma presents after biopsy and staging show low-risk disease: PSA 5.2 ng/mL, Gleason 3+3=6, and cT1c exam. He has a life expectancy well beyond 10 years and, after discussing his options, says he prefers active treatment rather than surveillance because of significant anxiety about living with untreated cancer. He is otherwise healthy and eligible for local therapy. In clinic, he asks about surgery versus radiation and wants a clear explanation of the available approaches before deciding. The team reviews his case in a multidisciplinary setting and discusses the main treatment choices with him in a balanced way.
recommended_actions: Offer low-dose rate brachytherapy alone, external beam radiation therapy alone, and radical prostatectomy as treatment options for this eligible patient with low-risk prostate cancer.
Counsel the patient about all applicable management options in a balanced, objective manner, preferably from multiple disciplines.
source_case.rationale: This vignette fits newly diagnosed low-risk prostate cancer requiring or choosing active treatment in an eligible patient, triggering the recommendation to offer LDR brachytherapy alone, EBRT alone, and/or radical prostatectomy. It also involves counseling about management options, supporting a balanced multidisciplinary discussion.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 66-year-old man with newly diagnosed prostate adenocarcinoma returns after biopsy and staging show low-risk disease: PSA 5.2 ng/mL, Gleason 3+3=6, and cT1c exam. He has a life expectancy well beyond 10 years and is otherwise healthy, but he reports being completely undecided about treatment and says he has not ruled out monitoring. He asks for an overview of local therapy options because he wants to understand the differences before making a final choice. The team reviews his case in clinic and provides a multidisciplinary discussion of surgery and radiation approaches, while also mentioning surveillance as a reasonable option to consider. He has no symptoms and remains eligible for local treatment if he ultimately chooses it.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like newly diagnosed low-risk prostate cancer with long life expectancy and a clinic discussion of surgery versus radiation, but he has not actually chosen active treatment.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer low-dose rate brachytherapy alone, external beam radiation therapy alone, or radical prostatectomy as the default next step
Proceed directly to definitive local treatment planning without revisiting surveillance
Frame the visit as a treatment-selection appointment rather than an options counseling discussion
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the low-risk active-treatment recommendation because the patient has not chosen active treatment; active surveillance remains a live option. The safer interpretation is balanced counseling on all applicable management choices, including surveillance, rather than recommending definitive therapy options as required for patients who require or choose active treatment.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 requires low-risk prostate cancer patients who require or choose active treatment and are eligible for treatment. Here, the key mismatch is that he has not chosen active treatment, so the specific offer of LDR brachytherapy, EBRT, or radical prostatectomy is not yet indicated.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 66-year-old man with newly diagnosed prostate adenocarcinoma is seen after biopsy and staging show low-risk disease: PSA 5.2 ng/mL, Gleason 3+3=6, and cT1c exam. He has a life expectancy well beyond 10 years and has chosen active treatment because of anxiety about living with untreated cancer. He is otherwise healthy and initially asked about surgery versus radiation. On review, however, he also has severe baseline urinary obstruction with a very high post-void residual and recurrent catheter dependence, and the radiation oncologist notes that he is not a good candidate for brachytherapy-based local therapy at this time. The team still discusses management options in a balanced multidisciplinary visit and reviews that surgical and radiation-based approaches may differ in feasibility given his urinary status.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Most of the vignette matches low-risk prostate cancer with a long life expectancy and a desire for active treatment, but one subtle local factor makes one of the standard options ineligible.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer low-dose rate brachytherapy alone among the standard definitive options
Recommend definitive local treatment without addressing the urinary obstruction first
List brachytherapy as a routine alternative alongside surgery and EBRT
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the blanket low-risk definitive-treatment recommendation because he is not eligible for all of the standard local options, specifically brachytherapy is not appropriate with his severe obstructive urinary dysfunction. The safer interpretation is to tailor counseling to feasible options and address the urinary issue before considering brachytherapy.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 applies when the patient with low-risk disease is eligible for the offered treatments. Severe urinary obstruction/catheter dependence makes him a poor brachytherapy candidate, so the full recommendation does not cleanly apply.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering Doppler technology alone for adnexal mass evaluation
selected_rules.execute: Do not use Doppler technology alone to evaluate the mass.
selected_rules.source_statement: Evaluation of an adnexal mass by Doppler technology alone is not recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering serum CA-125 as a stand-alone test
selected_rules.execute: Do not use CA-125 alone to distinguish benign from malignant adnexal masses.
selected_rules.source_statement: As a stand-alone modality, serum CA-125 is not recommended for distinguishing between benign and malignant adnexal masses.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult woman with a suspicious isolated ovarian mass and ultrasound is technically feasible
selected_rules.execute: Use transvaginal sonography as the first imaging modality.
selected_rules.source_statement: Transvaginal sonography should be the first modality of choice, where technically feasible, in patients with a suspicious, isolated ovarian mass.

selected_rules.activation_condition: need to differentiate malignant from benign ovarian masses
selected_rules.execute: Use sonography, particularly 3D sonography, MRI, or CT imaging for differentiation.
selected_rules.source_statement: Sonography, particularly three-dimensional (3D) sonography, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), and computerized tomography (CT) imaging are each recommended for differentiating malignant from benign ovarian masses.

selected_rules.activation_condition: ultrasound may be unreliable
selected_rules.execute: Use MRI to help clarify malignant potential.
selected_rules.source_statement: MRI is the most appropriate test to help clarify the malignant potential in patients where ultrasound may be unreliable.

selected_rules.activation_condition: extra-ovarian disease is suspected or needs to be ruled out
selected_rules.execute: Use CT imaging to assess for extra-ovarian disease.
selected_rules.source_statement: CT is most useful in cases where extra ovarian disease is suspected or needs to be ruled out.

selected_rules.activation_condition: using Doppler technology in adnexal mass evaluation
selected_rules.execute: Combine Doppler technology with morphological assessment.
selected_rules.source_statement: Doppler technology should be combined with a morphological assessment.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering morphological scoring systems for adnexal mass evaluation
selected_rules.execute: Use a morphological scoring system to differentiate benign from malignant masses.
selected_rules.source_statement: Morphological scoring systems can be used to differentiate benign from malignant adnexal masses.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 57-year-old postmenopausal woman presents with 3 weeks of intermittent left lower abdominal bloating and early satiety. Pelvic exam suggests a fullness in the left adnexa, and a recent office ultrasound was limited by overlying bowel gas, so the mass is not well characterized. She has no known prior ovarian disease. The clinician is considering the next imaging step to clarify whether this isolated ovarian mass is benign or malignant. Because the initial ultrasound is technically unreliable, additional imaging is needed to better define malignant potential. There is no clear evidence yet of extra-ovarian spread, but the adnexal lesion remains suspicious and incompletely assessed.
recommended_actions: Use MRI to help clarify the malignant potential because ultrasound may be unreliable.
Use sonography, particularly 3D sonography, MRI, or CT imaging to help differentiate malignant from benign ovarian masses.
source_case.rationale: The vignette describes a suspicious isolated ovarian/adnexal mass with technically unreliable ultrasound, which triggers MRI as the most appropriate clarifying test. It also involves differentiating malignant from benign ovarian masses, supporting use of sonography, MRI, or CT for characterization.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 57-year-old postmenopausal woman presents with 3 weeks of intermittent left lower abdominal bloating and early satiety. Pelvic exam suggests fullness in the left adnexa, and transvaginal ultrasound shows a 4.5 cm mostly cystic left ovarian mass with a few thin septations. The sonographer notes that the lesion is well visualized, with no technical limitation from bowel gas. Color Doppler is being reviewed and shows minimal peripheral flow, while the clinician is considering whether the vascular pattern alone could help determine malignant potential. There is no ascites, no obvious peritoneal nodularity, and no evidence yet of extra-ovarian spread.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Same postmenopausal symptomatic adnexal mass workup, but the ultrasound is technically adequate and the question has shifted toward relying on Doppler findings.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Order Doppler-only assessment to decide if the mass is benign or malignant
Use the vascular pattern alone to triage the adnexal lesion
Base the next step on Doppler flow rather than the full morphologic description
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for Doppler alone because the mass is already well characterized on ultrasound and Doppler should not be used in isolation to distinguish benign from malignant adnexal masses. The safer interpretation is to integrate morphologic findings with Doppler, and if further characterization is needed, use a complete imaging approach rather than Doppler alone.
challenging_examples.rationale: The case preserves a suspicious isolated ovarian mass, but the subtle change is that Doppler is being considered as a stand-alone discriminator despite adequate morphologic imaging. That invalidates the rule against Doppler-only evaluation.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 57-year-old postmenopausal woman presents with 3 weeks of intermittent left lower abdominal bloating and early satiety. Pelvic exam suggests a fullness in the left adnexa, and a recent office ultrasound was limited by bowel gas, so the lesion is not well characterized. MRI is being considered to clarify malignant potential. However, she has a permanent pacemaker that is not MRI-conditional, and the local radiology service will not perform MRI under any protocol. There is no acute instability, and CT abdomen-pelvis is available if cross-sectional imaging is needed to look for spread beyond the ovary.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The clinical picture still looks like an incompletely assessed suspicious adnexal mass with unreliable ultrasound, but MRI is not actually feasible because of a non-MRI-compatible pacemaker.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with MRI to better characterize the ovarian mass
Schedule MRI as the preferred next imaging step
Delay evaluation until MRI can be arranged
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for MRI because ultrasound may be unreliable, but MRI cannot be safely obtained in this patient. The safer interpretation is to use an alternative cross-sectional or expert ultrasound-based strategy, such as CT when extra-ovarian disease is a concern, rather than assuming MRI remains available.
challenging_examples.rationale: The case closely mirrors the source, but the key mismatch is MRI contraindication due to a nonconditional pacemaker. That invalidates the recommendation to use MRI as the next step.

selected_rules.activation_condition: individual uses indoor tanning devices
selected_rules.execute: avoid use of indoor tanning devices to reduce melanoma risk
selected_rules.source_statement: Use of indoor tanning devices should be avoided to reduce risk of melanoma.

selected_rules.activation_condition: individual is considering or using indoor tanning devices
selected_rules.execute: avoid use of indoor tanning devices, especially at younger age
selected_rules.source_statement: All individuals should avoid use of indoor tanning devices, especially those at a younger age.

selected_rules.activation_condition: individual is exposed to artificial UVR from indoor tanning devices
selected_rules.execute: recognize that there is no safe lower limit of exposure
selected_rules.source_statement: There is no safe lower limit of exposure to artificial UVR from indoor tanning devices.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 19-year-old woman presents for a routine visit and mentions that she has been using a tanning bed at a salon twice weekly for the past 3 months to “get a base tan” before spring break. She says several friends started tanning in high school, and she has been considering increasing her sessions because she likes the color it gives her skin. She has no current skin complaints and no personal history of skin cancer, but she is unaware of any specific risks associated with indoor tanning. She asks whether occasional tanning bed use is safe if she does not burn.
recommended_actions: Avoid use of indoor tanning devices to reduce melanoma risk.
Avoid use of indoor tanning devices, especially at a younger age.
Recognize that there is no safe lower limit of exposure to artificial UVR from indoor tanning devices.
source_case.rationale: The vignette describes a young individual who is currently using and considering increased use of indoor tanning devices, which triggers the rules advising avoidance. Because she is exposed to artificial UVR from indoor tanning devices, the no-safe-lower-limit statement also applies. The recommendations are limited to avoidance and counseling about risk, as specified by the triggered rules.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 19-year-old woman comes for a routine health visit and mentions that she used a tanning bed at a salon twice weekly for the past 3 months to “get a base tan” before spring break. She says several friends started tanning in high school, and she has been thinking about increasing her sessions because she likes the color it gives her skin. She has no current skin complaints and no personal history of skin cancer, but she is aware that tanning beds increase skin cancer risk. She asks whether occasional tanning bed use is safe if she does not burn.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The setting, age, frequency of tanning bed use, and counseling context closely mirror the source case, but she is no longer unaware of the risks.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Tell her to avoid indoor tanning devices because of melanoma risk and young age
Provide counseling that there is no safe level of tanning bed exposure
Reinforce that occasional tanning is harmless if she does not burn
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This remains a tanning-bed exposure case, so the risk discussion is still appropriate, but it is not a clean match to the avoidance rules keyed to a person considering or using indoor tanning devices who is unaware of the risks. The subtle change is that she already understands the cancer risk, so the vignette is less about initiating avoidance counseling from ignorance and more about reinforcing informed risk reduction.
challenging_examples.rationale: The source recommendation targets avoidance counseling for indoor tanning, especially in younger people, and the no-safe-lower-limit statement applies to artificial UV exposure. Here, the main mismatch is that the patient is already aware of the risk, which alters the exact guideline trigger being tested.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old man presents for a pre-travel visit and asks about using a tanning bed once or twice before a beach vacation because he has very fair skin and wants to avoid looking pale in photos. He has no current skin complaints and no personal history of skin cancer. He used an indoor tanning device briefly in college but has not used one in years. He says he mainly wants to know whether a very short course of tanning is “basically safe” if he limits it to one session.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This is still a straightforward indoor tanning counseling scenario, but the exposure is framed as a hypothetical one-time use rather than an established ongoing exposure.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: State that there is no safe lower limit of artificial UV exposure from tanning devices
Advise him to avoid indoor tanning devices to reduce melanoma risk
Recommend a brief tanning session if it is limited to once or twice
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The vignette does not establish current exposure to artificial UVR from indoor tanning devices, so the no-safe-lower-limit exposure statement is not directly triggered. The safer interpretation is anticipatory counseling against use rather than applying the exposure-based recommendation as if he is already tanning.
challenging_examples.rationale: R003 requires actual exposure to artificial UVR from indoor tanning devices. This patient is only asking about a possible future session, so the precise exposure condition is not met even though avoidance counseling would still be reasonable.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has cytogenetically normal AML
selected_rules.execute: Include DNMT3A mutation testing as a biomarker test
selected_rules.source_statement: DNMT3A mutation testing should be included as a biomarker test in cytogenetically normal AML patients.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man presents with 3 weeks of progressive fatigue, easy bruising, and intermittent low-grade fevers. CBC shows hemoglobin 8.7 g/dL, platelets 42,000/µL, and WBC 18,400/µL with circulating blasts. Bone marrow biopsy confirms acute myeloid leukemia. Cytogenetic analysis shows a normal karyotype, and there is no prior history of myelodysplastic syndrome or chemotherapy exposure. He is being evaluated at initial diagnosis for prognostic and treatment-planning biomarkers.
recommended_actions: Include DNMT3A mutation testing as a biomarker test in this patient with cytogenetically normal AML.
source_case.rationale: The vignette describes newly diagnosed AML with cytogenetically normal disease, which directly activates the rule recommending DNMT3A mutation testing as part of biomarker evaluation.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man presents with 3 weeks of progressive fatigue, easy bruising, and intermittent low-grade fevers. CBC shows hemoglobin 8.7 g/dL, platelets 42,000/µL, and WBC 18,400/µL with circulating blasts. Bone marrow biopsy confirms acute myeloid leukemia. Cytogenetic analysis reveals trisomy 8 in 6 of 20 metaphases, and there is no prior history of myelodysplastic syndrome or chemotherapy exposure. He is being evaluated at initial diagnosis for prognostic and treatment-planning biomarkers.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The presentation is identical to a newly diagnosed AML workup, but the karyotype is not normal.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Order DNMT3A mutation testing as part of the initial AML biomarker panel
Document the case as cytogenetically normal AML and proceed with the usual normal-karyotype biomarker workflow
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is newly diagnosed AML, but it is not cytogenetically normal because trisomy 8 is present. The cited recommendation for DNMT3A testing does not apply on the basis of normal-karyotype AML, so the safer interpretation is to follow biomarker testing guided by the actual cytogenetic risk category rather than assuming this specific indication.
challenging_examples.rationale: The rule is triggered only by cytogenetically normal AML. Any clonal cytogenetic abnormality, including trisomy 8, invalidates that condition.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man presents with 3 weeks of progressive fatigue, easy bruising, and intermittent low-grade fevers. CBC shows hemoglobin 8.7 g/dL, platelets 42,000/µL, and WBC 18,400/µL with circulating blasts. Bone marrow biopsy confirms acute myeloid leukemia. Cytogenetic analysis shows a normal karyotype, but rapid molecular testing has already identified an NPM1 mutation on the diagnostic sample. He is being evaluated at initial diagnosis for prognostic and treatment-planning biomarkers.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like de novo AML with normal cytogenetics at diagnosis, but the relevant biomarker decision has already been resolved by prior molecular testing.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Add DNMT3A mutation testing to the initial biomarker workup
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: Although the AML is cytogenetically normal, the key near-miss is that this vignette already includes completed molecular profiling showing an NPM1 mutation. The original recommendation is framed as a biomarker test to include in the initial evaluation of cytogenetically normal AML, but this case is no longer a clean match for that specific testing decision because the biomarker workup has been partially completed and should be interpreted in the context of the existing results rather than automatically adding the cited test.
