selected_rules.source_statement: To ensure optimal quality of life in these men, individual patient-reported outcomes should be measured.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Prostate cancer survivor after completing curative-intent therapy
selected_rules.execute: Provide ongoing surveillance
selected_rules.source_statement: For prostate cancer survivors who have completed curative-intent therapy, surveillance is required

selected_rules.activation_condition: Any healthcare practitioner provides PSA surveillance
selected_rules.execute: Manage PSA according to the current CCO Prostate Cancer Pathway
selected_rules.source_statement: All healthcare practitioners that provide PSA surveillance should manage PSA as per the current CCO Prostate Cancer Pathway.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Planning survivorship care models for prostate cancer survivors
selected_rules.execute: Include family physicians in survivorship care models
selected_rules.source_statement: Expert opinion supports family physicians being involved in all survivorship care models.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man completed curative-intent external beam radiotherapy for localized prostate cancer 14 months ago and has been followed in a survivorship clinic. He reports worsening fatigue, mild urinary urgency, and intermittent hot flashes that have affected sleep and daily activities. His PSA surveillance has been done through the clinic, but he is unsure which symptoms are expected long-term effects and which should prompt further review. He asks whether a special PET scan would help if his PSA rises again and also asks if he should start a specific “prostate cancer diet” or supplement to reduce recurrence risk. He has no new bone pain, weight loss, or neurologic deficits.
recommended_actions: Provide ongoing surveillance after completion of curative-intent therapy.
Manage PSA surveillance according to the current CCO Prostate Cancer Pathway.
Measure individual patient-reported outcomes to assess quality of life.
Ensure follow-up providers are aware of the quality-of-life domains affected by treatment and the available management options.
Include the family physician in the survivorship care model.
Treat NaF PET and choline PET as experimental if considering them for recurrence/restaging.
Do not recommend any specific diet plan or food supplement for improved cancer outcomes.
source_case.rationale: The vignette describes a prostate cancer survivor after curative-intent therapy, triggering the need for ongoing surveillance, PSA management per the CCO pathway, patient-reported outcome measurement, survivorship follow-up awareness of quality-of-life domains, and inclusion of the family physician. The patient also asks about recurrence imaging with PET and dietary supplements, which triggers the rules stating NaF and choline PET are experimental and that no specific diet or supplement should be recommended for improved cancer outcomes.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man completed curative-intent external beam radiotherapy for localized prostate cancer 14 months ago and has been followed in a survivorship clinic. He reports worsening fatigue, mild urinary urgency, and intermittent hot flashes that have affected sleep and daily activities. His PSA surveillance has been done through the clinic, but he is unsure which symptoms are expected long-term effects and which should prompt further review. He asks whether a special PET scan would help if his PSA rises again and also asks if he should start a specific “prostate cancer diet” or supplement to reduce recurrence risk. He has no new bone pain, weight loss, or neurologic deficits. On review, however, he also notes that he recently changed to annual urology follow-up only and has not had a PSA since his radiation oncologist discharged him 8 months ago.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This remains a routine prostate cancer survivorship visit after curative-intent therapy, with the same PSA and PET-scan questions, but the patient is no longer under regularly scheduled clinical surveillance and has a new symptom-related gap in follow-up.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Order a PSA only if the fatigue worsens further and otherwise continue routine survivorship counseling
Reassure him that his current symptom pattern is expected and defer any additional assessment until the next annual visit
Discuss NaF or choline PET as a possible next test if his PSA increases
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The case is not concordant with the rule for symptom-triggered evaluation outside regular follow-up because he is no longer being seen through regularly scheduled clinical visits. A PSA test is appropriate now, and symptom-directed evaluation should be considered rather than waiting for the next routine appointment.
challenging_examples.rationale: The key mismatch is that he has left structured surveillance and has not had recent PSA monitoring. That shifts the scenario away from routine survivorship follow-up and toward symptom-triggered reassessment.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man completed curative-intent external beam radiotherapy for localized prostate cancer 14 months ago and has been followed in a survivorship clinic. He reports worsening fatigue, mild urinary urgency, and intermittent hot flashes that have affected sleep and daily activities. His PSA surveillance has been done through the clinic, but he is unsure which symptoms are expected long-term effects and which should prompt further review. He asks whether a special PET scan would help if his PSA rises again and also asks if he should start a specific “prostate cancer diet” or supplement to reduce recurrence risk. He has no new bone pain, weight loss, or neurologic deficits. At this visit, his PSA is undetectable and he is also being seen for a confirmed biochemical recurrence workup after a recent rising PSA trend, with imaging already planned using conventional CT and bone scan because the lesion is suspected to be metastatic rather than isolated local recurrence.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The same survivorship setting and PET-scan question are preserved, but the clinical context has shifted to suspected metastatic recurrence with conventional staging already underway, making the specific NaF or choline PET question no longer the relevant guideline trigger.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Recommend NaF PET or choline PET to restage the recurrence
Add PET imaging before completing the planned conventional staging studies
Use the PET result to decide whether recurrence is present
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the NaF/choline PET recommendation because the case is no longer a simple recurrence/restaging question in the survivorship setting; the recurrence is already being worked up with standard imaging and the clinical issue is broader metastatic staging. The safer interpretation is to continue with the planned conventional evaluation rather than defaulting to those PET modalities.
challenging_examples.rationale: The original recommendation applies when considering NaF or choline PET for recurrence/restaging. Here, the patient already has a different staging pathway in motion and the question is no longer a straightforward isolated PET-for-restaging decision.

selected_rules.activation_condition: routine monitoring or surveillance of lymphoma
selected_rules.execute: Do not use FDG PET/CT for routine surveillance.
selected_rules.source_statement: An FDG PET/CT scan is not recommended for the routine monitoring and surveillance of lymphoma.

selected_rules.activation_condition: functional imaging is important and anatomical imaging is equivocal
selected_rules.execute: Perform FDG PET/CT for staging.
selected_rules.source_statement: When functional imaging is considered to be important in situations where anatomical imaging is equivocal, and/or in potentially curable cases, a FDG PET/CT scan is recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: potentially curable case and functional imaging is important
selected_rules.execute: Perform FDG PET/CT for staging.
selected_rules.source_statement: When functional imaging is considered to be important in situations where anatomical imaging is equivocal, and/or in potentially curable cases, a FDG PET/CT scan is recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: functional imaging is important, anatomical imaging is equivocal, and treatment choices may be affected in limited stage indolent lymphoma
selected_rules.execute: Perform FDG PET/CT for staging.
selected_rules.source_statement: When functional imaging is considered to be important in situations where anatomical imaging is equivocal and treatment choices may be affected in limited stage indolent lymphomas, a FDG PET/CT scan is recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: early stage (I or II) Hodgkin lymphoma after 2 or 3 cycles of chemotherapy and chemotherapy is being considered as definitive single-modality therapy
selected_rules.execute: Perform FDG PET/CT to assess early response and inform whether to complete therapy or give more therapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: An FDG PET/CT scan is recommended for the assessment of early response in early stage (I or II) HL following two or three cycles of chemotherapy when chemotherapy is being considered as the definitive single-modality therapy, to inform completion of therapy or if more therapy is warranted.

selected_rules.activation_condition: potentially curable case with suspected recurrence and conventional imaging is equivocal
selected_rules.execute: Perform FDG PET/CT to investigate recurrence of Hodgkin or non-Hodgkin lymphoma.
selected_rules.source_statement: In potentially curable cases, when functional imaging is considered to be important and conventional imaging is equivocal, a FDG PET/CT scan is recommended to investigate recurrence of HL or NHL.

selected_rules.activation_condition: residual mass after chemotherapy in Hodgkin or non-Hodgkin lymphoma and further potentially curative therapy is being considered and biopsy cannot be safely or readily performed
selected_rules.execute: Perform FDG PET/CT to evaluate the residual mass.
selected_rules.source_statement: An FDG PET/CT scan is recommended for the evaluation of residual mass(es) following chemotherapy in a patient with HL or NHL when further, potentially curative, therapy (such as radiation or stem cell transplantation) is being considered and when biopsy cannot be safely or readily performed.

selected_rules.activation_condition: FDG PET is abnormal during initial diagnosis of lymphoma
selected_rules.execute: Use clinical follow-up or biopsy to confirm findings because false positives can occur.
selected_rules.source_statement: FDG PET may disclose higher rates of systemic disease; however, due to false-positive results, FDG PET scans should be subject to clinical follow-up or biopsy.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman with previously treated diffuse large B-cell lymphoma completed six cycles of R-CHOP eight months ago and achieved a complete metabolic response. She now returns for a routine follow-up visit without B symptoms, palpable adenopathy, or abnormal laboratory findings. Her oncologist notes that she feels well and asks whether she should have an FDG PET/CT scan to “make sure nothing is coming back.” There is no new clinical concern, and prior post-treatment imaging was reassuring. The main question is whether to continue scheduled surveillance imaging or use PET/CT as a routine monitoring test.
recommended_actions: Do not use FDG PET/CT for routine surveillance of lymphoma.
source_case.rationale: This vignette describes routine monitoring/surveillance after lymphoma treatment without new symptoms or equivocal findings, which matches the rule against routine FDG PET/CT surveillance.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman with previously treated diffuse large B-cell lymphoma completed six cycles of R-CHOP eight months ago and achieved a complete metabolic response. She now returns for a routine follow-up visit without B symptoms, palpable adenopathy, or abnormal laboratory findings. Her oncologist notes that she feels well and asks whether she should have an FDG PET/CT scan to “make sure nothing is coming back.” There is no new clinical concern, and prior post-treatment imaging was reassuring. The current question is whether to continue scheduled surveillance imaging or use PET/CT as a routine monitoring test, although she also mentions mild knee pain after gardening that her primary doctor has already attributed to osteoarthritis.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Very similar to a routine post-treatment lymphoma follow-up visit, but the only new symptom is clearly unrelated musculoskeletal pain with an alternative explanation.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Order an FDG PET/CT as routine surveillance because of the lymphoma history.
Schedule serial PET/CT scans at follow-up visits to monitor for relapse.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is still a routine surveillance scenario for lymphoma remission, and the mild knee pain does not create a lymphoma-specific indication for PET/CT. The safer interpretation is to avoid routine FDG PET/CT surveillance and assess the musculoskeletal complaint separately if clinically needed.
challenging_examples.rationale: The vignette preserves the routine follow-up context but adds a non-oncologic symptom with an alternate explanation, making the PET/CT request tempting yet still not guideline-concordant for surveillance.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man with stage III diffuse large B-cell lymphoma completed six cycles of R-CHOP nine months ago and entered complete remission. He comes to clinic for surveillance, reports no fevers, night sweats, weight loss, or palpable nodes, and his CBC and LDH remain normal. He asks whether another FDG PET/CT should be obtained because he had one at the end of treatment and wants to be certain the lymphoma has not returned. His prior imaging was entirely negative. The visit is otherwise focused on routine follow-up, but the chart notes that he recently had a contrast-enhanced CT chest/abdomen/pelvis at an outside facility for renal stone evaluation, which was also unrevealing.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case strongly resembles standard post-remission surveillance in lymphoma, with the extra imaging detail making it easy to overcall a need for PET/CT.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Repeat FDG PET/CT for reassurance during surveillance.
Add PET/CT to the routine lymphoma follow-up protocol.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: There is no new lymphoma-related symptom, exam abnormality, or equivocal finding that would justify FDG PET/CT. The prior outside CT being negative further reduces any imaging trigger; this remains a surveillance visit where routine PET/CT is not recommended.
challenging_examples.rationale: The subtle addition of a recent negative CT can make the request seem more image-driven, but it does not convert routine follow-up into an indication for FDG PET/CT.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When providing palliative care across the disease course
selected_rules.execute: Use a team-based approach that addresses both physical and non-physical symptoms and supports patients and families.
selected_rules.source_statement: Palliative care focuses on care that addresses both physical and non-physical symptoms; it can be delivered throughout the course of the disease; it involves a team of healthcare providers and supports patients as well as their families.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When selecting palliative care guidelines for review
selected_rules.execute: Include only papers that provide recommendations on palliative care and exclude papers that only describe guideline or consensus methods without recommendations.
selected_rules.source_statement: Papers were included if they provided any recommendations on palliative care. Papers were excluded if they only provided guideline or consensus methods and no recommendations.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When screening palliative care guidelines for this review
selected_rules.execute: Restrict inclusion to guidelines published from 2011 to 2016.
selected_rules.source_statement: Due to the large number of guidelines, the Working Group decided to restrict the review to include only guidelines published in the past five years (2011 to 2016).

selected_rules.activation_condition: When selecting higher-quality guidelines for this review
selected_rules.execute: Include only guidelines with an AGREE Rigour of Development subsection score of 28 or higher.
selected_rules.source_statement: To identify the best-quality guidelines, the AGREE Instrument Rigour of Development scale was used and only guidelines that scored 28 or above were included in this review.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When using the broad palliative care guideline for adults
selected_rules.execute: Apply the guideline covering the palliative care path from first diagnosis of serious illness to death.
selected_rules.source_statement: The "Palliative Care for Adults" guideline by McCusker et al. ... covers the palliative care path from the patient's first diagnosis of a serious illness to death.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When caring for patients in the last days and hours of life
selected_rules.execute: Use the Registered Nurses Association of Ontario guideline for nurse-specific recommendations on assessment, decision support, care and management, education, and organization/policy.
selected_rules.source_statement: This is a Canadian guideline about patient care in the last days and hours of life, and the recommendations in this guideline were specific to nurses. The guideline makes recommendations in the following areas: Assessment for end of life, Decision support at end of life, Care and management at end of life, Education, and Organization and policy recommendations.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When assessing or preventing pressure ulcers in palliative care
selected_rules.execute: Use the Registered Nurses Association of Ontario guideline for nurse-specific recommendations on assessment, planning, interventions, discharge or transfer/care arrangements, and education/organization-policy.
selected_rules.source_statement: The topic of this guideline was the assessment and prevention of pressure ulcers. This guideline does not focus solely on palliative care, but the treatment and assessment of pressure ulcers in palliative care is a part of the guideline. ... The guideline makes recommendations in the following areas: Assessment, Planning, Interventions, Discharge or transfer or the care arrangements, and Education and organization/policy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When managing chronic cough in patients receiving palliative care
selected_rules.execute: Use simple linctus, then a therapeutic trial of sodium cromoglycate, then an opioid or opioid derivative such as dextromethorphan, morphine, or codeine.
selected_rules.source_statement: The recommendations include use of simple linctus, therapeutic trial of sodium cromoglycate, and then prescription of an opioid or opioid derivative (dextromethorphan, morphine, or codeine).

source_case.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with metastatic non-small cell lung cancer was diagnosed 9 months ago and is now on second-line treatment after progression. He reports increasing fatigue, dyspnea on exertion, poor appetite, and intermittent anxiety about his family’s future. His wife says he has become more withdrawn and is struggling to talk about goals of care. He is not in the final days of life, but his symptoms are worsening and he asks for help managing them at home while continuing cancer-directed therapy. The oncology team notes that his physical symptoms are only part of the problem; he also needs emotional support and help for his wife, who is overwhelmed by caregiving responsibilities.
recommended_actions: Use a team-based palliative care approach that addresses both physical and non-physical symptoms.
Provide support for both the patient and his family, including caregiver support and help with coping.
Apply the broad adult palliative care guideline across the disease course, from first diagnosis of serious illness through death.
source_case.rationale: This vignette triggers R001 because palliative care is being provided across the disease course and should address physical symptoms, emotional needs, and family support. It also triggers R005 because the patient has a serious illness and is being managed along the palliative care path from diagnosis onward. No other rules are directly relevant because he is not in the last days of life, and no pressure ulcer, chronic cough-specific, or guideline-selection scenario is described.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with metastatic non-small cell lung cancer was diagnosed 9 months ago and is now on second-line treatment after progression. He reports increasing fatigue, dyspnea on exertion, poor appetite, and intermittent anxiety about his family’s future. His wife says he has become more withdrawn and is struggling to talk about goals of care. He asks for help managing symptoms at home while continuing cancer-directed therapy. On examination, however, he is now bedbound, minimally responsive, and has had no oral intake for 36 hours, with mottling and periods of apnea overnight. The team is discussing comfort measures with the family because he appears to be actively dying.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Still a patient with metastatic lung cancer, worsening symptoms, family distress, and a palliative-care-oriented discussion, but the clinical status has shifted into the last days and hours of life.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Initiate broad team-based palliative care planning focused on long-term symptom support and caregiver coping
Apply the full adult palliative care pathway from diagnosis through death as the main management framework
Use the general palliative care approach rather than end-of-life-specific guidance for nursing assessment and decision support
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is no longer a general palliative care scenario across the disease course; the patient appears to be in the last days and hours of life. The safer interpretation is to follow end-of-life-specific management and nursing guidance rather than the broader chronic palliative care framework.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 and R005 are invalidated because the vignette now describes the actively dying phase, not routine palliative care across the disease course. R006 becomes relevant instead of the broad palliative-care pathway because the care focus has shifted to the last days and hours of life.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with metastatic non-small cell lung cancer was diagnosed 9 months ago and is now on second-line treatment after progression. He reports increasing fatigue, dyspnea on exertion, poor appetite, and intermittent anxiety about his family’s future. His wife says he has become more withdrawn and is struggling to talk about goals of care. He is not in the final days of life, but his symptoms are worsening and he asks for help managing them at home while continuing cancer-directed therapy. The oncology team also asks about his painful sacral skin breakdown, which developed after several weeks of immobility and is now a stage 1 pressure injury with intact skin and nonblanchable erythema only.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The overall palliative context, symptom burden, and caregiver strain are unchanged, but the skin finding is only early erythema rather than an established pressure ulcer requiring the palliative pressure-ulcer pathway.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Use the palliative pressure-ulcer guideline for assessment, planning, interventions, and transfer arrangements
Treat the skin change as a pressure ulcer in palliative care and follow the specialized RN guideline
Fold the wound issue into a broad palliative care plan without distinguishing the limited stage 1 finding
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This case does not clearly meet the pressure-ulcer-in-palliative-care scenario that would justify applying the specialized pressure-ulcer guideline. The skin finding is only an early stage 1 pressure injury, so management should focus on standard preventive skin care and offloading rather than assuming a more advanced palliative wound pathway.
challenging_examples.rationale: R007 is invalidated because the vignette describes a stage 1 pressure injury, not a clear pressure ulcer care scenario that would trigger the palliative pressure-ulcer guideline. R001 and R005 are also not the best fit for the same reason as the original broad palliative course is preserved but the wound-specific recommendation is not applicable.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has stage IIIB cervical cancer
selected_rules.execute: do not apply the bevacizumab recommendation from GOG-0240 eligibility
selected_rules.source_statement: In addition, GOG-0240 did not include patients with stage IIIB cancer (local extension to pelvic sidewall) or IVA cancer (invasion into bladder or rectum).

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has stage IVA cervical cancer
selected_rules.execute: do not apply the bevacizumab recommendation from GOG-0240 eligibility
selected_rules.source_statement: In addition, GOG-0240 did not include patients with stage IIIB cancer (local extension to pelvic sidewall) or IVA cancer (invasion into bladder or rectum).

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has recurrent, metastatic, or persistent cervical cancer and randomized clinical trials are available
selected_rules.execute: offer participation in randomized clinical trials evaluating systemic therapy regimens
selected_rules.source_statement: It is recommended that all patients with recurrent, metastatic or persistent cervical cancer be offered the opportunity to participate in randomized clinical trials, if available, that evaluate the efficacy of and adverse effects of systemic therapy regimens.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has recurrent, metastatic, or persistent cervical cancer and systemic therapy is indicated
selected_rules.execute: use cisplatin with paclitaxel
selected_rules.source_statement: Cisplatin with paclitaxel is recommended for this patient population

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has recurrent, metastatic, or persistent cervical cancer and a cisplatin combination is being used
selected_rules.execute: substitute carboplatin for cisplatin when appropriate
selected_rules.source_statement: The substitution of carboplatin for cisplatin in these combinations is also recommended for this target population because carboplatin is associated with fewer adverse effects and greater ease of administration.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has stage IVB, recurrent, or persistent cervical cancer not amenable to curative surgery or radiotherapy, performance status 0 to 1, adequate renal hepatic and bone marrow function, and no prior chemotherapy for recurrence, nonhealing wounds, active bleeding, or inadequately anticoagulated thromboembolism
selected_rules.execute: use bevacizumab with cisplatin-paclitaxel
selected_rules.source_statement: Bevacizumab in combination with cisplatin-paclitaxel is recommended for a specific subset of the target population, which includes only patients that match the characteristics of the GOG-0240 study population.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has recurrent, metastatic, or persistent cervical cancer and carboplatin-paclitaxel is feasible to administer
selected_rules.execute: use carboplatin-paclitaxel as a treatment option
selected_rules.source_statement: Based on these results, and on the feasibility of administration, carboplatin-paclitaxel is recommended as a treatment option for recurrent, metastatic, or persistent cervical cancer.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has recurrent, metastatic, or persistent cervical cancer and systemic therapy is indicated
selected_rules.execute: consider cisplatin-vinorelbine
selected_rules.source_statement: cisplatin in other combinations, including cisplatin-vinorelbine, cisplatin-gemcitabine, and cisplatin-topotecan may also be considered.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 49-year-old woman with biopsy-proven cervical squamous cell carcinoma previously treated with definitive chemoradiation now has progressive pelvic pain, hydronephrosis, and enlarging para-aortic nodes on imaging. Her disease is deemed recurrent and not amenable to curative surgery or radiotherapy. She has an ECOG performance status of 1, normal renal and hepatic function, and adequate marrow counts. She has not received chemotherapy for recurrence. A randomized trial is available at her center. She has no active bleeding, no nonhealing wounds, and no history of inadequately anticoagulated thromboembolism. Her oncology team is discussing first-line systemic therapy options for recurrent disease.
recommended_actions: Offer participation in a randomized clinical trial evaluating systemic therapy regimens, if the patient is interested and eligible.
Use cisplatin with paclitaxel as systemic therapy.
Carboplatin-paclitaxel is also a feasible treatment option if administration considerations favor carboplatin.
source_case.rationale: The patient has recurrent cervical cancer with systemic therapy indicated, so she meets criteria for cisplatin-paclitaxel and for consideration of carboplatin-paclitaxel as an alternative option. Because randomized clinical trials are available, she should also be offered trial participation. Bevacizumab is not triggered because the vignette does not establish the specific GOG-0240-eligible subset, and no stage IIIB/IVA exclusion is needed here.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 49-year-old woman with biopsy-proven cervical squamous cell carcinoma previously treated with definitive chemoradiation now has progressive pelvic pain and enlarging para-aortic nodes on imaging. Her disease is deemed recurrent and not amenable to curative surgery or radiotherapy. She has an ECOG performance status of 1, normal renal and hepatic function, and adequate marrow counts. She has not received chemotherapy for recurrence. A randomized trial is available at her center. During evaluation, she is also found to have a small area of persistent vaginal cuff bleeding requiring intermittent packing, but no hemodynamic instability. Her oncology team is discussing first-line systemic therapy options for recurrent disease.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like recurrent cervical cancer with good performance status, intact organ function, no prior recurrence chemotherapy, and trial availability; the only important change is the presence of active bleeding.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer bevacizumab with cisplatin-paclitaxel
Proceed with the GOG-0240-style bevacizumab regimen because she otherwise matches the usual eligibility profile
Start cisplatin-paclitaxel and add bevacizumab for maximal response
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for bevacizumab because active bleeding is present, which excludes her from the specific subset used for that recommendation. She remains a candidate for systemic therapy and trial discussion, but bevacizumab should be avoided until the bleeding issue is resolved and reassessed.
challenging_examples.rationale: The recommendation for bevacizumab applies only to a narrowly defined GOG-0240-like population without active bleeding. A small but ongoing bleeding source invalidates that eligibility even though the rest of the vignette is highly similar.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 56-year-old woman with recurrent cervical squamous cell carcinoma after prior definitive chemoradiation presents with worsening pelvic discomfort and new para-aortic adenopathy. Her disease is not amenable to curative surgery or radiotherapy. She is ambulatory with ECOG performance status 1 and has normal renal, hepatic, and marrow function. She has not received chemotherapy for recurrence, and a randomized clinical trial is available at the treating center. At this visit, staging workup also shows invasion of the posterior bladder wall with a confirmed vesicovaginal fistula. Her oncology team is discussing first-line systemic therapy options for recurrent disease.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The patient otherwise fits a typical recurrent cervical cancer systemic-therapy scenario with good functional status and trial access; the subtle change is bladder invasion, which makes the stage IVA label easy to miss.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer bevacizumab with cisplatin-paclitaxel
Treat her as eligible for the standard bevacizumab-containing first-line regimen
Begin cisplatin-paclitaxel and add bevacizumab because she has recurrent disease and adequate organ function
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This case is not concordant with bevacizumab eligibility because stage IVA disease with bladder invasion was excluded from the relevant evidence base. She may still be eligible for systemic therapy and trial enrollment, but bevacizumab should not be applied under this rule set.
challenging_examples.rationale: The presence of bladder invasion changes the stage to IVA, which is an explicit exclusion for the bevacizumab recommendation. The rest of the clinical picture is intentionally similar to a standard recurrent-disease case to make the mismatch easy to overlook.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has melanoma and sentinel lymph node micrometastatic disease is being diagnosed
selected_rules.execute: Do not use PET for diagnosis
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is not recommended for the diagnosis of sentinel lymph node micrometastatic disease or for staging of I, IIa, or IIb melanoma.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has melanoma stage I, IIa, or IIb
selected_rules.execute: Do not use PET for staging
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is not recommended for the diagnosis of sentinel lymph node micrometastatic disease or for staging of I, IIa, or IIb melanoma.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has suspected brain metastases from melanoma
selected_rules.execute: Do not routinely use PET to diagnose brain metastases
selected_rules.source_statement: The routine use of PET or PET/CT is not recommended for the diagnosis of brain metastases.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has primary uveal malignant melanoma
selected_rules.execute: Do not routinely use PET to detect primary uveal malignant melanoma
selected_rules.source_statement: The routine use of PET is not recommended for the detection of primary uveal malignant melanoma.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has melanoma and is high-risk with potentially resectable disease
selected_rules.execute: Use PET for staging
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is recommended for staging of high-risk patients with potentially resectable disease.

selected_rules.activation_condition: At recurrence, an isolated metastasis is identified
selected_rules.execute: Use PET
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is recommended for isolated metastases at time of recurrence or when contemplating metastectomy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: At recurrence, metastectomy is being contemplated
selected_rules.execute: Use PET before metastectomy
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is recommended for isolated metastases at time of recurrence or when contemplating metastectomy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Assessing treatment response in malignant melanoma
selected_rules.execute: No recommendation for or against PET
selected_rules.source_statement: A recommendation cannot be made for or against the use of PET for the assessment of treatment response in malignant melanoma due to insufficient evidence.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with cutaneous melanoma of the left upper back underwent wide local excision and sentinel lymph node biopsy. Pathology now shows a single sentinel node with a 0.4 mm micrometastatic deposit. He has no neurologic symptoms, no known distant disease, and his primary tumor was otherwise consistent with stage II disease. His oncology team is discussing whether PET imaging should be ordered to further characterize the nodal finding before deciding on additional management.
recommended_actions: Do not use PET for diagnosis of the sentinel lymph node micrometastatic disease.
Do not use PET for staging of this melanoma presentation.
source_case.rationale: This vignette triggers the rule for melanoma with sentinel lymph node micrometastatic disease being diagnosed. The guideline states PET is not recommended for diagnosing sentinel lymph node micrometastatic disease or for staging stage I, IIa, or IIb melanoma. No other rule is clearly triggered because the case does not describe high-risk potentially resectable disease, recurrence, isolated metastasis, or treatment-response assessment.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with cutaneous melanoma of the left upper back underwent wide local excision and sentinel lymph node biopsy. Pathology shows a single sentinel node with a 0.4 mm micrometastatic deposit, and the primary lesion has an ulcerated 3.8 mm Breslow depth with multiple suspicious in-transit nodules on exam. He has no neurologic symptoms and no known visceral disease. His oncology team is discussing whether PET imaging should be ordered to better define the extent of disease before deciding on management.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still centers on melanoma after sentinel node biopsy and a PET question, but the primary tumor is now clearly higher risk with additional in-transit disease rather than an isolated stage II presentation.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Order PET to characterize the nodal finding and complete staging
Use PET routinely to stage this melanoma before treatment planning
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is no longer the same low-stage sentinel-node micrometastatic scenario. The presence of ulceration, deeper Breslow depth, and suspicious in-transit disease shifts the presentation away from stage I/IIa/IIb staging where PET is not recommended for the original question. The safer interpretation is that management should be driven by the now higher-risk, clinically more extensive disease picture rather than the sentinel node deposit alone.
challenging_examples.rationale: The source recommendation against PET applies to diagnosis of sentinel node micrometastatic disease and staging of stage I, IIa, or IIb melanoma. This vignette adds higher-risk features and in-transit disease, making the original rule mismatch the most important subtle change.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with cutaneous melanoma of the left upper back underwent wide local excision and sentinel lymph node biopsy. Pathology shows a single sentinel node with a 0.4 mm micrometastatic deposit. Three months later, he returns with a painful enlarging subcutaneous nodule near the prior scar and a separate 1.2 cm left axillary node on exam. He has no neurologic symptoms and no known distant disease. The team is discussing whether PET imaging should be obtained now that recurrence is suspected and a limited metastasis may be present before considering resection.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The melanoma history, sentinel node micrometastasis, and PET discussion remain the same, but the question is now at recurrence with a clinically apparent new lesion rather than initial diagnosis/staging.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Skip further workup and avoid PET because the earlier sentinel node deposit was only micrometastatic
Use PET only for diagnosis of the original nodal finding
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not the original upfront staging scenario. The new enlarging scar-adjacent nodule and axillary adenopathy suggest recurrence or possibly isolated metastatic disease, which changes the imaging question. The safer interpretation is to evaluate recurrence in the appropriate restaging framework rather than applying the rule that PET should not be used for diagnosing sentinel node micrometastatic disease.
challenging_examples.rationale: The invalidated rule is tied to initial diagnosis of sentinel node micrometastatic disease. Here, the clinically important change is recurrence with a suspected limited metastasis, so the original non-PET recommendation no longer fits.

selected_rules.activation_condition: localized Ewing's sarcoma of bone after neoadjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Do not use surgery plus radiation therapy as the initial local treatment option.
selected_rules.source_statement: Either surgery alone or RT alone is a reasonable treatment option; the combination of surgery plus RT is not recommended as an initial treatment option.

selected_rules.activation_condition: localized Ewing's sarcoma of bone and surgery is planned
selected_rules.execute: Take both pre-chemotherapy and post-chemotherapy MRI scans into consideration for surgical planning.
selected_rules.source_statement: Both pre-chemotherapy and post-chemotherapy MRI scans should be taken into consideration for surgical planning.

selected_rules.activation_condition: localized Ewing's sarcoma of bone after neoadjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Consider surgery alone as a local treatment option.
selected_rules.source_statement: Either surgery alone or RT alone is a reasonable treatment option; the combination of surgery plus RT is not recommended as an initial treatment option.

selected_rules.activation_condition: localized Ewing's sarcoma of bone after neoadjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Consider radiation therapy alone as a local treatment option.
selected_rules.source_statement: Either surgery alone or RT alone is a reasonable treatment option; the combination of surgery plus RT is not recommended as an initial treatment option.

selected_rules.activation_condition: localized Ewing's sarcoma of bone after neoadjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Decide local treatment with a multidisciplinary tumour board together with the patient, considering age, tumour location, tumour size, response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy, comorbidities, expected benefit versus surgical/RT harms, and patient preference.
selected_rules.source_statement: The local treatment for an individual patient should be decided by a multidisciplinary tumour board together with the patient after consideration of the following: 1) patient characteristics (e.g., age, tumour location, tumour size, response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy, and existing comorbidities), 2) the potential benefit compared with the potential complications from surgery and/or toxicities associated with RT, and 3) patient preference.

selected_rules.activation_condition: complete tumour resection is impossible
selected_rules.execute: Consider radiation therapy alone as the optimal local treatment.
selected_rules.source_statement: If complete tumour resection is impossible, RT alone may be the optimal choice.

selected_rules.activation_condition: postoperative residual tumour or positive margins
selected_rules.execute: Consider postoperative radiation therapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: RT may be a treatment option postoperatively in patients who have residual tumours or positive margins.

selected_rules.activation_condition: localized Ewing's sarcoma of bone, surgery is planned, and there is good chemotherapy response in certain anatomic locations
selected_rules.execute: Use the post-chemotherapy MRI to plan surgical resection margins when appropriate.
selected_rules.source_statement: In certain anatomic locations with good chemotherapy response, the post-chemotherapy MRI may be the appropriate imaging modality to plan surgical resection margins.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 14-year-old boy with localized Ewing’s sarcoma of the distal femur completed neoadjuvant chemotherapy and has had a marked radiographic and symptomatic response. Repeat MRI shows substantial shrinkage of the intramedullary and soft-tissue components, while the pre-chemotherapy MRI demonstrated more extensive marrow involvement. The orthopedic oncologist is planning limb-sparing surgery, but the lesion’s location near the metaphysis raises concern about achieving clear margins without excessive functional loss. The case is reviewed at a multidisciplinary tumor board with the patient and family, who prefer an approach that preserves function if oncologically safe. The team notes that complete resection appears feasible, though the final extent of surgery will depend on careful comparison of the pre- and post-chemotherapy MRI studies.
recommended_actions: Take both the pre-chemotherapy and post-chemotherapy MRI scans into consideration for surgical planning.
Use the post-chemotherapy MRI to help plan surgical resection margins when appropriate.

Decide local treatment at a multidisciplinary tumour board together with the patient, weighing age, tumour location, tumour size, response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy, comorbidities, expected benefit versus surgical/RT harms, and patient preference.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches localized Ewing’s sarcoma of bone with surgery planned after neoadjuvant chemotherapy, triggering preoperative staging and planning rules. The good chemotherapy response and an anatomic location where margin planning is important support using the post-chemotherapy MRI for resection margins. The multidisciplinary decision-making rule is also triggered because local treatment should be individualized with the patient and team.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 14-year-old boy with localized Ewing’s sarcoma of the distal femur completed neoadjuvant chemotherapy and has had a marked symptomatic response, but the repeat MRI shows only minimal shrinkage of the intramedullary and soft-tissue components. The pre-chemotherapy MRI had demonstrated more extensive marrow involvement, and the orthopedic oncologist is still considering limb-sparing surgery because the lesion is near the metaphysis and resection would be technically challenging. The case is reviewed at a multidisciplinary tumor board with the patient and family, who prefer the least disabling option if oncologically safe. The team notes that complete resection appears feasible, but because the post-treatment imaging response is modest, they plan to rely primarily on the original MRI for defining the surgical field and avoid overcalling the smaller residual abnormality on the post-chemotherapy scan.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Localized Ewing’s sarcoma of bone after neoadjuvant chemotherapy with surgery still planned, but the post-chemotherapy response is not clearly good enough for the margin-planning recommendation to apply.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Use the post-chemotherapy MRI to define resection margins
Plan limb-sparing surgery based mainly on the smaller residual lesion seen after chemotherapy
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for using the post-chemotherapy MRI to plan margins because the response is only modest rather than clearly good in a setting where that imaging strategy is specifically suggested. The safer interpretation is to integrate both pre- and post-treatment imaging, with greater caution about relying on the post-chemotherapy scan alone.
challenging_examples.rationale: The case preserves localized Ewing’s sarcoma, planned surgery, and tumor-board workflow, but weakens the key condition for R008 by changing the chemotherapy response from marked/good to minimal/modest. That makes post-chemotherapy MRI margin planning less clearly appropriate.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 15-year-old girl with Ewing’s sarcoma of the proximal tibia completed neoadjuvant chemotherapy and has been discussed repeatedly at tumor board. Imaging suggests the primary bone lesion is localized, but a small indeterminate pulmonary nodule was noted on chest CT at diagnosis and has remained stable on interval scans. The orthopedic surgeon believes limb-sparing resection may still be feasible, though the team is also considering definitive radiation if the lung finding proves metastatic. The family strongly prefers to preserve function if cancer control is not compromised. Because the pulmonary lesion leaves some uncertainty about whether disease is truly localized, the team decides to postpone a definitive local plan until after short-interval reassessment rather than proceeding with the usual individualized local-treatment discussion for a clearly localized case.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The patient still has Ewing’s sarcoma after neoadjuvant chemotherapy, is being reviewed by a multidisciplinary team, and local therapy is being considered; the subtle change is the indeterminate lung nodule making the case not clearly localized.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with a routine multidisciplinary decision for localized Ewing’s sarcoma
Choose the local treatment solely based on age, tumor location, and family preference
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the localized-disease local-treatment recommendation because the indeterminate pulmonary nodule makes the status of truly localized disease uncertain. The safer interpretation is to clarify staging first before applying the localized Ewing’s sarcoma local-treatment framework.
challenging_examples.rationale: The vignette closely mirrors a localized post-chemotherapy Ewing’s sarcoma case but introduces a stable indeterminate lung nodule, which undermines the assumption of definitively localized disease needed for the specific multidisciplinary local-treatment recommendation.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer treated with palliative intent
selected_rules.execute: Consider either planned sequential chemotherapy or upfront combination chemotherapy as acceptable standards of care
selected_rules.source_statement: Planned sequential chemotherapy and upfront combination chemotherapy are both acceptable standards of care.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Choose treatment case by case based on patient and tumour characteristics, toxicity of each strategy, and patient preference
selected_rules.source_statement: Therefore, choice of treatment should be made on a caseby-case basis based on considerations that include patient and tumour characteristics, toxicity of each strategy and patient preference.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Use sequential chemotherapy as a strategy to provide access to all effective cytotoxic drugs
selected_rules.source_statement: Based on the results of this systematic review, patients should have access to all effective cytotoxic drugs using a sequential strategy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with rapidly progressing, very symptomatic, or bulky life-threatening visceral disease
selected_rules.execute: Consider upfront combination chemotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Combination chemotherapy may be more appropriate for patients with rapidly progressing, very symptomatic or bulky life-threatening visceral disease given their higher overall response rates.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Consider that combination chemotherapy has a small survival advantage over sequential chemotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: A meta-analysis of five trials (1-5) demonstrates a survival advantage for combination chemotherapy (HR, 0.92; 95%CI, 0.86-0.99, p=0.02).

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Consider that the survival advantage of combination chemotherapy is likely very small and may not be clinically significant
selected_rules.source_statement: Median survival advantage in most trials is 3 to 6 weeks (range <1 week to 12 weeks). Therefore, any survival advantage that exists is likely to be very small and not clinically significant.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Consider that upfront combination chemotherapy causes more neutropenia
selected_rules.source_statement: Hematological toxicities include significantly more neutropenia (1,4) ... with upfront combination chemotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Consider that upfront combination chemotherapy causes more febrile neutropenia
selected_rules.source_statement: Hematological toxicities include significantly more neutropenia (1,4), febrile neutropenia (1) ... with upfront combination chemotherapy.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man with ECOG 1 performance status has newly diagnosed unresectable metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma with multiple bilobar liver metastases and small pulmonary nodules. He has no bowel obstruction or severe cancer-related symptoms, but he is anxious to start treatment quickly. His creatinine and liver tests are acceptable, and he has no major comorbidities other than well-controlled hypertension. After discussing goals of care, he states he wants treatment aimed at prolonging life while preserving quality of life. The oncology team reviews that both sequential chemotherapy and upfront combination chemotherapy are reasonable options in this palliative setting, and notes that the survival difference between approaches is likely small. They also discuss that combination therapy may increase the risk of neutropenia and febrile neutropenia.
recommended_actions: Consider either planned sequential chemotherapy or upfront combination chemotherapy as acceptable standards of care.
Consider that combination chemotherapy has a small survival advantage over sequential chemotherapy.
Consider that upfront combination chemotherapy causes more neutropenia.
Consider that upfront combination chemotherapy causes more febrile neutropenia.
source_case.rationale: This vignette describes an adult with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer treated with palliative intent, triggering R001. The case also supports the general metastatic setting for R005, R007, and R008. No rule about rapidly progressing, very symptomatic, or bulky life-threatening disease was used, since those features were not present.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man with ECOG 1 performance status has newly diagnosed metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma with multiple bilobar liver metastases and small pulmonary nodules. He has no bowel obstruction or severe cancer-related symptoms, but he is anxious to start treatment quickly. His creatinine and liver tests are acceptable, and he has no major comorbidities other than well-controlled hypertension. After discussing goals of care, he states he wants treatment aimed at prolonging life while preserving quality of life. The oncology team reviews that both sequential chemotherapy and upfront combination chemotherapy are reasonable options in this palliative setting, and notes that the survival difference between approaches is likely small. They also discuss that combination therapy may increase the risk of neutropenia and febrile neutropenia. However, the liver metastases are considered potentially resectable after response, and the tumor board is evaluating him for curative-intent conversion therapy.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case preserves the same age, performance status, metastatic colorectal cancer, and discussion of sequential versus combination chemotherapy, but the treatment intent is no longer purely palliative because the disease may be convertible to resection.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer either sequential chemotherapy or upfront combination chemotherapy as acceptable standards of care
Discuss a small survival advantage with upfront combination chemotherapy
Counsel that upfront combination chemotherapy carries higher rates of neutropenia and febrile neutropenia
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the cited recommendation because the disease is being considered for conversion to resection, so the vignette is no longer straightforward unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer treated with palliative intent. A curative-intent multimodality strategy should be considered instead of applying the palliative sequencing recommendation.
challenging_examples.rationale: The original rule applies to unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer with palliative intent. Here, the metastases are being evaluated as potentially resectable after response, which changes the treatment framework and invalidates the palliative recommendation.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man with ECOG 1 performance status has newly diagnosed unresectable metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma with multiple bilobar liver metastases and small pulmonary nodules. He has no bowel obstruction or severe cancer-related symptoms, but he is anxious to start treatment quickly. His creatinine and liver tests are acceptable, and he has no major comorbidities other than well-controlled hypertension. After discussing goals of care, he states he wants treatment aimed at prolonging life while preserving quality of life. The oncology team reviews that both sequential chemotherapy and upfront combination chemotherapy are reasonable options in this palliative setting, and notes that the survival difference between approaches is likely small. They also discuss that combination therapy may increase the risk of neutropenia and febrile neutropenia. He has already received adjuvant oxaliplatin-based chemotherapy for stage III colon cancer six months ago and developed persistent grade 2 peripheral neuropathy.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The metastatic palliative setting is unchanged on first read, but prior recent oxaliplatin exposure with ongoing neuropathy makes the specific chemotherapy choice materially different from the source scenario.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with upfront combination chemotherapy as an acceptable standard option
Frame sequential chemotherapy and combination chemotherapy as equivalent choices
Emphasize only the modest survival difference and proceed without adjusting for prior neurotoxicity
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the original recommendation because the patient has clinically important residual neuropathy after recent oxaliplatin exposure, which changes the risk-benefit balance and makes the source comparison of sequential versus upfront combination therapy less directly applicable. The safer interpretation is to individualize regimen selection around prior toxicity, especially neurotoxicity, rather than defaulting to the generic palliative recommendation.
challenging_examples.rationale: The recommendation is built for unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer in general. The added detail of persistent neuropathy after recent oxaliplatin makes standard combination therapy a poor fit and prevents straightforward application of the source guidance.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with MDS
selected_rules.execute: Do not use autologous stem cell transplantation outside a clinical trial.
selected_rules.source_statement: Autologous stem cell transplantation is not recommended for patients with MDS.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with MDS
selected_rules.execute: Do not recommend autologous SCT outside of a clinical trial.
selected_rules.source_statement: One systematic review comprising a total of 22 studies did not detect any benefit associated with autologous SCT (ASCT), and does not recommend it outside of a clinical trial (1).

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with AML in CR1
selected_rules.execute: Do not use autologous SCT as the preferred post-remission strategy.
selected_rules.source_statement: ASCT is not recommended for patients with AML in first complete remission.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with AML in CR1
selected_rules.execute: Do not use autologous SCT when long-term survival is the primary outcome of interest.
selected_rules.source_statement: While associated with more favourable treatment-related mortality (TRM) rates, if longterm survival is the primary outcome of interest, then there is no evidence to support the use of ASCT in first complete remission.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with AML in second or subsequent remission
selected_rules.execute: Do not use autologous SCT as supported therapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: There is insufficient evidence to support the use of ASCT for patients with AML in second or subsequent remission.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with AML in second or subsequent remission and long-term survival is the primary outcome
selected_rules.execute: Do not use autologous SCT for long-term survival benefit.
selected_rules.source_statement: If long-term survival is the primary outcome of interest, then there is no evidence to support the use of ASCT in second or subsequent remission.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with AML in CR1 and intermediate-risk or poor-risk disease
selected_rules.execute: Use allogeneic SCT rather than autologous SCT or chemotherapy when transplant is being considered.
selected_rules.source_statement: Significant benefits in favour of alloSCT for both intermediate and poor risk patients (p<0.01) were detected, but no difference was detected with good risk patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with AML who achieves a second or subsequent remission
selected_rules.execute: Recommend allogeneic transplantation.
selected_rules.source_statement: Allogeneic transplantation is the recommended option for patients with AML who achieve a second or subsequent remission.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with intermediate-risk acute myeloid leukemia was treated with induction chemotherapy and is now in first complete remission. His performance status is good, and he asks whether an autologous stem cell transplant would be the best way to reduce relapse risk. The team is considering post-remission options, including transplant versus chemotherapy. He has no matched sibling donor identified yet, and he is particularly focused on maximizing long-term disease control.
recommended_actions: Do not use autologous stem cell transplantation as the preferred post-remission strategy.
Do not use autologous stem cell transplantation when long-term survival is the primary outcome of interest.
If transplantation is being considered for this intermediate-risk AML in first complete remission, use allogeneic SCT rather than autologous SCT or chemotherapy.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches an adult patient with AML in first complete remission and intermediate-risk disease, triggering the rules against autologous SCT in CR1 and the recommendation favoring allogeneic SCT over autologous SCT or chemotherapy when transplant is being considered. The patient’s concern about long-term disease control also aligns with the rule stating there is no evidence to support autologous SCT when long-term survival is the primary outcome.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with intermediate-risk acute myeloid leukemia completed induction chemotherapy and is now in first complete remission by blood counts and marrow blast clearance. His performance status remains good, and he asks whether an autologous stem cell transplant would be the best way to reduce relapse risk. The team is weighing post-remission options, but he has a documented cardiomyopathy with a left ventricular ejection fraction of 38%, and the transplant service is concerned about tolerance of intensive conditioning. He has no matched sibling donor identified yet and remains focused on maximizing disease control.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still involves intermediate-risk AML in first complete remission with a discussion of transplant versus chemotherapy, but comorbid cardiac dysfunction makes the transplant decision less straightforward.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with autologous stem cell transplantation as the preferred consolidation strategy.
Recommend allogeneic transplantation over chemotherapy despite the cardiac comorbidity.
Counsel that autologous transplant is the best option for long-term disease control.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the original recommendation because the patient’s significant cardiomyopathy makes standard transplant pathways less applicable in the same way. The safer interpretation is that transplant candidacy must be individualized; the original allogeneic-vs-autologous recommendation in intermediate-risk CR1 does not cleanly apply until fitness for conditioning is clarified.
challenging_examples.rationale: The vignette preserves AML in CR1 and intermediate-risk disease, but the reduced ejection fraction introduces a major competing clinical constraint. That makes a direct application of the transplant-preference rule inappropriate without reassessing eligibility and regimen intensity.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with acute myeloid leukemia is in first complete remission after induction chemotherapy and asks whether an autologous stem cell transplant would best reduce relapse risk. His performance status is good, and he is eager to maximize long-term disease control. Cytogenetics from diagnosis, however, show favorable-risk core-binding factor AML with inv(16), and his marrow after induction is MRD-negative. The team is considering post-remission options, including transplant versus chemotherapy, and no matched sibling donor has been identified.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The workflow is nearly identical to the source case: AML in first remission, good performance status, and a discussion of transplant versus chemotherapy with concern about relapse reduction.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Recommend allogeneic stem cell transplantation over chemotherapy because he is in first remission.
Advise autologous stem cell transplantation to maximize long-term disease control.
Escalate directly to transplant planning without reconsidering risk category.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not concordant with the original rule because the patient is not intermediate-risk; he has favorable-risk AML. The evidence favoring allogeneic transplant over autologous transplant or chemotherapy in CR1 does not apply here, so post-remission therapy should be guided by favorable-risk AML management rather than automatically favoring transplant.
challenging_examples.rationale: The subtle change in cytogenetic risk status invalidates the intermediate-risk trigger. Although the rest of the vignette closely matches the source, favorable-risk disease removes the basis for the specific transplant recommendation.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with thymic epithelial tumours in Ontario
selected_rules.execute: do not use peptide receptor radionuclide therapy as an approved treatment
selected_rules.source_statement: Also, peptide receptor radionuclide therapy has not been approved for patients with thymic epithelial tumours in Ontario.

selected_rules.activation_condition: all adult patients with thymic epithelial tumours
selected_rules.execute: use the TNM staging system
selected_rules.source_statement: The TNM staging system should be used for all patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: all patients with thymic epithelial tumours undergoing surgery
selected_rules.execute: aim to achieve complete resection
selected_rules.source_statement: The aim of surgery in all cases is to achieve a complete resection.

selected_rules.activation_condition: all patients with thymic epithelial tumours
selected_rules.execute: discuss the patient at a multidisciplinary cancer conference, including higher-volume centres when possible
selected_rules.source_statement: Discussion of all patients at multidisciplinary cancer conference (MCC) is strongly recommended, not just at local MCC but also with higher-volume centres.

selected_rules.activation_condition: all patients with thymic epithelial tumours
selected_rules.execute: consider presentation at the International Thymic Malignancies Interest Group tumour board
selected_rules.source_statement: Presentation at the International Thymic Malignancies Interest Group tumour board should be considered.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man in Ontario is referred after a CT chest done for persistent cough shows an anterior mediastinal mass. Biopsy confirms a thymic epithelial tumour. He is otherwise well, with no myasthenic symptoms and normal baseline bloodwork. The case is being reviewed before any treatment decision is made. The oncology team plans formal staging and wants input from thoracic surgery, medical oncology, and radiation oncology, with consideration of consultation from a higher-volume centre because the tumour appears potentially resectable. The patient asks whether a specialized radionuclide therapy could be used instead of surgery or standard systemic treatment.
recommended_actions: Use the TNM staging system for this patient.
Discuss the case at a multidisciplinary cancer conference, and include a higher-volume centre when possible.
Do not use peptide receptor radionuclide therapy as an approved treatment for this thymic epithelial tumour in Ontario.
source_case.rationale: This vignette triggers the general thymic epithelial tumour rules for all adult patients and the Ontario-specific restriction. The patient has a confirmed thymic epithelial tumour in Ontario, so TNM staging applies, multidisciplinary review is strongly recommended, and peptide receptor radionuclide therapy is not an approved treatment.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man in Ontario is referred after a CT chest done for persistent cough shows an anterior mediastinal mass. Biopsy confirms a thymic epithelial tumour, but the pathology report describes the lesion as a small pulmonary carcinoid arising from the anterior mediastinum rather than a primary thymic tumour. He is otherwise well, with no myasthenic symptoms and normal baseline bloodwork. The case is being reviewed before any treatment decision is made. The oncology team is debating staging and whether to involve thoracic surgery, medical oncology, and radiation oncology, with consideration of consultation from a higher-volume centre because the lesion appears potentially resectable. The patient asks whether a specialized radionuclide therapy could be used instead of surgery or standard systemic treatment.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The presentation still centers on an Ontario patient with an anterior mediastinal mass and pre-treatment review, but the diagnosis is not a thymic epithelial tumour.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Use the TNM staging system for this tumour
Present the case at a multidisciplinary thymic cancer conference with a higher-volume centre
Discuss peptide receptor radionuclide therapy as a treatment option for the mediastinal tumour
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the thymic epithelial tumour recommendations because the biopsy no longer supports a thymic epithelial tumour diagnosis. A pulmonary carcinoid in the mediastinum should be managed according to its own disease-specific pathway rather than the thymic tumour rules.
challenging_examples.rationale: The rules apply to patients with thymic epithelial tumours. Reclassifying the lesion as a pulmonary carcinoid removes the target diagnosis, so the thymic-specific staging, MCC, and Ontario PRRT restriction do not apply.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man in Ontario is referred after a CT chest done for persistent cough shows an anterior mediastinal mass. Biopsy confirms a thymic epithelial tumour, and the multidisciplinary team believes it is likely resectable. He is otherwise well, with no myasthenic symptoms and normal baseline bloodwork. Because he already received neoadjuvant chemotherapy at an outside hospital, the team is now considering whether to proceed directly to surgery or continue systemic therapy. The patient asks whether a specialized radionuclide therapy could be used instead of standard treatment. The case is reviewed by thoracic surgery, medical oncology, and radiation oncology, and the team plans formal staging before deciding next steps.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The overall scenario still involves an Ontario patient with a thymic epithelial tumour and preoperative review, but he has already started systemic therapy and surgery is no longer the immediate management question.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Plan surgery with the expectation of complete resection as the next step
Use peptide receptor radionuclide therapy as an approved alternative treatment
Treat this as a straightforward upfront resectable thymic tumour pathway
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not a direct match for the surgery recommendation because the patient is no longer at the initial surgery decision point after receiving neoadjuvant chemotherapy. The safer interpretation is that he needs reassessment of response, resectability, and multidisciplinary planning before any operative intent is chosen; PRRT still remains unapproved in Ontario for thymic epithelial tumours.
challenging_examples.rationale: Rule R003 is about patients undergoing surgery and aiming for complete resection in that setting. Here, prior neoadjuvant therapy changes the workflow and makes the vignette a post-treatment reassessment rather than a straightforward surgical case. Ontario PRRT remains inappropriate, but the surgery recommendation is no longer directly applicable in the same way.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with symptomatic endobronchial non-small cell lung cancer and previously untreated disease
selected_rules.execute: Use HDREB alone for palliation of respiratory symptoms
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with previously untreated, symptomatic, endobronchial non-small cell lung cancer: External beam radiation therapy (EBRT) alone is more effective for palliation of respiratory symptoms than HDREB alone.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with symptomatic endobronchial non-small cell lung cancer and previously untreated disease
selected_rules.execute: Use routine HDREB plus EBRT to improve symptom relief over EBRT alone
selected_rules.source_statement: The evidence does not provide conclusive results to suggest that routine use of HDREB and EBRT would provide improved symptom relief over EBRT alone.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with complete collapse of the lung due to endobronchial obstruction
selected_rules.execute: Use a surgical core out procedure before EBRT or EBRT with HDREB
selected_rules.source_statement: December 2022: the recommendation "For patients with complete collapse of the lung due to endobronchial obstruction, a surgical core out procedure may be needed before EBRT or EBRT with HDREB" is no longer endorsed because the updated evidence does not show a benefit of this procedure.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient previously treated by EBRT who is symptomatic with endobronchial obstruction due to recurrent disease
selected_rules.execute: Use HDREB if technically feasible
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients previously treated by EBRT who are symptomatic with endobronchial obstruction due to recurrent disease, HDREB is recommended, providing that endobronchial brachytherapy is technically feasible.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with symptomatic endobronchial non-small cell lung cancer and previously untreated disease where selected combined treatment is being considered
selected_rules.execute: Consider HDREB plus EBRT in selected patients
selected_rules.source_statement: June 2018: It is the opinion of the Lung Cancer Disease Site Group that HDREB and EBRT might be suitable in selected patients.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with no prior thoracic radiation presents with 3 weeks of worsening cough, intermittent hemoptysis, and dyspnea. CT chest shows a centrally located right upper lobe non-small cell lung cancer causing partial endobronchial obstruction of the segmental bronchus with post-obstructive atelectasis, but not complete lung collapse. Bronchoscopy confirms a friable endobronchial lesion narrowing the airway. He is being evaluated for palliative treatment to relieve respiratory symptoms. The oncology team is considering whether to add endobronchial brachytherapy to external beam radiation, but there is no prior EBRT history and no clear reason to prefer a combined approach over radiation alone.
recommended_actions: Use external beam radiation therapy alone for palliation of respiratory symptoms rather than HDREB alone.
Do not routinely use HDREB plus EBRT to improve symptom relief over EBRT alone.
Consider HDREB plus EBRT only in selected patients, recognizing this is an opinion-based option rather than a routine recommendation.
source_case.rationale: The patient has previously untreated, symptomatic endobronchial non-small cell lung cancer, which triggers the rules addressing initial palliation. EBRT alone is favored over HDREB alone, and routine combined HDREB plus EBRT is not supported by conclusive evidence. A combined approach may be considered only selectively.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with no prior thoracic radiation presents with 3 weeks of worsening cough, intermittent hemoptysis, and dyspnea. CT chest shows a centrally located right upper lobe non-small cell lung cancer causing near-complete narrowing of the segmental bronchus with post-obstructive atelectasis, but the distal lung remains aerated and there is no complete collapse. Bronchoscopy confirms a friable endobronchial lesion narrowing the airway. He is being evaluated for palliative treatment to relieve respiratory symptoms. The oncology team asks whether endobronchial brachytherapy should be added to external beam radiation because the lesion is accessible bronchoscopically and symptoms are bothersome.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Symptomatic, previously untreated, centrally located endobronchial NSCLC with airway obstruction and palliative intent closely mirrors the original case, but the lung is not completely collapsed.
