likely_but_incorrect_actions: Recommend a surgical core out procedure before EBRT or EBRT with HDREB to open the airway.
Proceed with bronchoscopy-guided debulking before starting palliative radiation.
Treat the near-obstruction as complete atelectasis and plan airway recanalization first.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not the complete lung collapse scenario. The safer interpretation is palliative radiation planning for symptomatic endobronchial obstruction without assuming a core-out procedure is needed.
challenging_examples.rationale: Rule R003 only applies when there is complete collapse of the lung due to endobronchial obstruction. Here the imaging explicitly shows post-obstructive atelectasis but not complete collapse, so that recommendation is not triggered.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with no prior thoracic radiation presents with 3 weeks of worsening cough, intermittent hemoptysis, and dyspnea. CT chest shows a centrally located right upper lobe non-small cell lung cancer causing partial endobronchial obstruction of the segmental bronchus with post-obstructive atelectasis. Bronchoscopy confirms a friable endobronchial lesion narrowing the airway, but the bronchoscopist notes that the lesion cannot be safely accessed for brachytherapy because the distal airway is too tortuous and the catheter cannot be positioned. He is being evaluated for palliative treatment to relieve respiratory symptoms, and the team is considering whether to use HDREB after external beam radiation.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still involves symptomatic endobronchial NSCLC and palliative airway symptoms, but prior EBRT is absent and HDREB is not technically feasible.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Plan HDREB because the obstruction is symptomatic and endobronchial.
Add brachytherapy after EBRT for additional symptom relief.
Refer for endobronchial brachytherapy once the airway is partially opened.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This does not meet the rule for HDREB after prior EBRT in recurrent symptomatic obstruction. The safer interpretation is that HDREB is not indicated here, particularly because it is technically infeasible and there is no prior EBRT history.
challenging_examples.rationale: Rule R004 requires prior EBRT and recurrent symptomatic endobronchial obstruction with technical feasibility. This patient has neither prior EBRT nor a technically feasible brachytherapy setup, so the recommendation cannot be applied.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with completely resected cutaneous melanoma at high risk of recurrence
selected_rules.execute: do not use ipilimumab as adjuvant therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Ipilimumab is not recommended as adjuvant therapy for patients with completely resected cutaneous melanoma with high risk of recurrence.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with completely resected cutaneous melanoma outside a clinical trial
selected_rules.execute: do not use interferon alpha for adjuvant treatment
selected_rules.source_statement: Use of interferon alpha (IFN-α) for adjuvant treatment of cutaneous melanoma outside of a clinical trial is no longer recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with completely resected cutaneous melanoma
selected_rules.execute: do not use chemotherapy regimens, vaccines, levamisole, bevacizumab, Bacillus Calmette-Guerin, or isolated limb perfusion as adjuvant treatment except in a clinical trial
selected_rules.source_statement: Chemotherapy regimens, vaccines, levamisole, bevacizumab, Bacillus Calmette-Guerin, and isolated limb perfusion are not recommended for adjuvant treatment of cutaneous melanoma except as part of a clinical trial.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with completely resected cutaneous melanoma at high risk of recurrence and no BRAF V600E or V600K mutation
selected_rules.execute: use nivolumab or pembrolizumab as adjuvant therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Nivolumab or pembrolizumab is recommended as adjuvant therapy for patients with completely resected cutaneous melanoma without BRAF V600E or V600K mutations with high risk of recurrence (stage IIIA to IIID, IV).

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with completely resected cutaneous melanoma at high risk of recurrence and BRAF V600E or V600K mutation
selected_rules.execute: use nivolumab, pembrolizumab, or dabrafenib plus trametinib as adjuvant therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Nivolumab, pembrolizumab, or dabrafenib plus trametinib is recommended as adjuvant therapy for patients with completely resected cutaneous melanoma with BRAF V600E or V600K mutations and high risk of recurrence (stage IIIA to IIID, IV).

selected_rules.activation_condition: high-risk melanoma patient being considered for adjuvant treatment
selected_rules.execute: perform molecular testing to characterize mutations to guide treatment decisions
selected_rules.source_statement: Molecular testing of high-risk melanoma patients to characterize mutations should be conducted to help guide appropriate treatment decisions.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with clinically positive lymph nodes before adjuvant therapy or adjuvant radiotherapy
selected_rules.execute: perform dissection of lymph nodes in the involved area before adjuvant therapy or adjuvant radiotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: These trials and recommendations regarding axillary resection do not apply to patients with clinically positive lymph nodes (by palpation or radiologic investigation), and the standard of care is dissection of lymph nodes in that area (axillary, groin, or head and neck) prior to adjuvant therapy or adjuvant radiotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has mucosal melanoma with high risk of recurrence
selected_rules.execute: use nivolumab or pembrolizumab, or targeted therapy if an actionable mutation is identified, as adjuvant therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Immune checkpoint inhibitors (nivolumab or pembrolizumab) or targeted therapy (in patients with identified mutations) are recommended for adjuvant therapy of mucosal melanoma with high risk of recurrence.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man underwent wide local excision and sentinel node biopsy for a 2.1-mm ulcerated cutaneous melanoma on the upper back. Final pathology shows complete resection with two involved regional lymph nodes, placing him at high risk of recurrence (stage III). He is recovering well and asks about “older immune shots” he read about online. He has no known BRAF V600E or V600K mutation status yet, and no clinical evidence of persistent nodal disease on exam or imaging. He is being seen to discuss adjuvant treatment options after surgery.
recommended_actions: Do not use ipilimumab as adjuvant therapy.
Do not use interferon alpha for adjuvant treatment outside a clinical trial.
Do not use chemotherapy regimens, vaccines, levamisole, bevacizumab, Bacillus Calmette-Guérin, or isolated limb perfusion as adjuvant treatment except in a clinical trial.
Perform molecular testing to characterize mutations to help guide adjuvant treatment decisions.
source_case.rationale: The patient has completely resected cutaneous melanoma with high-risk features, which triggers the non-recommendations against ipilimumab, interferon alpha, and other older adjuvant approaches. Because he is a high-risk melanoma patient being considered for adjuvant therapy, molecular testing is also recommended to determine mutation status and guide treatment selection.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man is seen after wide local excision and sentinel node biopsy for a 2.1-mm ulcerated cutaneous melanoma on the upper back. Final pathology shows complete resection with two positive regional lymph nodes, and he is recovering well. His oncologist is discussing adjuvant options after surgery, and the patient asks about “older immune shots” he found online. He has not yet had BRAF V600E or V600K testing, and there is no evidence of persistent nodal disease on exam. The notes also mention that he enrolled in a blinded postoperative study at the time of surgery and is still within that protocol’s treatment window.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like a completely resected stage III melanoma patient discussing postoperative adjuvant therapy, but the key difference is that he is currently enrolled in a clinical trial.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start interferon alpha as adjuvant treatment now that the tumor has been resected
Offer an adjuvant chemotherapy or vaccine regimen for recurrence prevention
Order molecular testing only after deciding on adjuvant therapy rather than now
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not a straightforward outside-trial adjuvant decision. Because he is already enrolled in a blinded postoperative study, the recommendation against interferon alpha and other older adjuvant approaches outside a clinical trial does not directly apply in the same way. Molecular testing is still reasonable for high-risk melanoma being considered for adjuvant management, but the trial context should guide what is and is not appropriate.
challenging_examples.rationale: The source recommendation targets adjuvant treatment outside a clinical trial. Adding active enrollment in a postoperative trial invalidates the direct application of the non-recommendation for interferon alpha and other older adjuvant regimens. The patient remains high-risk and molecular testing is still relevant, but the trial setting changes the guideline context.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man underwent wide local excision and sentinel node biopsy for a 2.1-mm ulcerated cutaneous melanoma on the upper back. Final pathology shows complete resection with two involved regional lymph nodes, and he is referred to oncology to discuss postoperative treatment. He is clinically well, and there is no palpable adenopathy or radiographic evidence of persistent nodal disease. BRAF V600E/V600K status is not yet known. On review of the operative report, the sentinel node biopsy was performed from the axilla, but the surgeon documented a suspicious residual 1.4-cm axillary node on preoperative ultrasound that was not removed during the initial procedure.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Most of the vignette still matches a resected stage III melanoma patient being evaluated for adjuvant therapy, but there is a subtle preoperative imaging finding of clinically positive nodal disease.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed directly to adjuvant immunotherapy without further nodal surgery
Skip additional axillary surgery because the sentinel node biopsy already showed nodal involvement
Treat him like a routine completely resected stage III patient and move straight to adjuvant therapy
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not the usual post-sentinel-node adjuvant setting because there was a clinically suspicious axillary node on ultrasound before adjuvant therapy. That finding makes the case one of clinically positive lymph nodes, where the involved nodal basin should be dissected before adjuvant therapy or radiotherapy rather than proceeding as if it were simply completely resected nodal disease.
challenging_examples.rationale: The rule for pre-adjuvant lymph node dissection applies when nodes are clinically positive by palpation or imaging. A documented suspicious residual axillary node on ultrasound means the patient no longer fits the clean completely resected, clinically node-negative workflow assumed by the adjuvant recommendation.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has undergone initial complete resection of GIST
selected_rules.execute: Do not use imatinib as adjuvant therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: At present, there is insufficient evidence to support the use of imatinib as an adjuvant therapy in patients who have undergone initial complete resection of disease.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering neoadjuvant treatment with imatinib
selected_rules.execute: Do not use neoadjuvant imatinib
selected_rules.source_statement: At present, the use of neoadjuvant imatinib is not recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with unresectable or metastatic GIST expressing KIT (CD117+)
selected_rules.execute: Use imatinib as therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients with KIT-expressing (CD117+) unresectable or metastatic GIST, treatment with imatinib is a recommended therapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: starting imatinib for KIT-expressing (CD117+) unresectable or metastatic GIST
selected_rules.execute: Prescribe imatinib 400 mg daily as the initial dose
selected_rules.source_statement: Until additional data become available, the initial dose of imatinib should be prescribed at a dose of 400 mg daily.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with stable disease on imatinib
selected_rules.execute: Continue imatinib until disease progression
selected_rules.source_statement: Patients with stable disease should be treated until disease progression.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient develops serious toxicity on imatinib
selected_rules.execute: Discontinue imatinib
selected_rules.source_statement: Treatment should be discontinued if serious toxicity develops.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient achieves complete clinical response and radiologic remission with imatinib
selected_rules.execute: Continue imatinib indefinitely until further data define optimal duration
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients who achieve a complete clinical response and radiologic remission with imatinib, treatment should be continued indefinitely until further data is available regarding the optimum duration of therapy in such patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with unresectable or metastatic GIST expressing KIT (CD117+)
selected_rules.execute: Apply these recommendations to this population
selected_rules.source_statement: These recommendations apply to adult patients with unresectable or metastatic GIST expressing KIT (CD117+).

source_case.patient_vignette: A 61-year-old man with biopsy-proven gastric gastrointestinal stromal tumor underwent complete surgical resection with negative margins after presenting with melena and iron-deficiency anemia. Final pathology confirmed a low-to-intermediate risk GIST, and postoperative recovery has been uncomplicated. At his oncology follow-up, he asks whether he should start imatinib to reduce the chance of recurrence. He has no evidence of residual or metastatic disease on postoperative imaging, and the tumor was not being treated as unresectable or metastatic disease. He feels well and is eager to do anything that might lower recurrence risk, but he has not started any systemic therapy yet.
recommended_actions: Do not use imatinib as adjuvant therapy after initial complete resection of GIST.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches the rule for patients who have undergone initial complete resection of GIST. The guideline states there is insufficient evidence to support adjuvant imatinib in this setting, so imatinib should not be used postoperatively.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 61-year-old man with biopsy-proven gastric gastrointestinal stromal tumor underwent complete surgical resection with negative margins after presenting with melena and iron-deficiency anemia. Final pathology was reviewed at tumor board and described the lesion as low-risk, with no mitotic briskness and no evidence of rupture. Postoperative imaging shows no residual or metastatic disease. At follow-up, he asks whether he should start imatinib to reduce the chance of recurrence because he wants to do everything possible after surgery. He has not started any systemic therapy yet, and his recovery has been uncomplicated.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still features a completely resected gastric GIST with postoperative imaging negative for disease, but the pathology now places it in a low-risk category rather than the original low-to-intermediate risk scenario.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start adjuvant imatinib to lower recurrence risk after resection.
Prescribe imatinib now since the patient is postoperative and anxious about recurrence.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for adjuvant imatinib because the tumor has already been completely resected and the case does not establish a setting where adjuvant imatinib is recommended; the safer interpretation is observation after curative resection rather than routine postoperative imatinib.
challenging_examples.rationale: The recommendation against adjuvant imatinib applies after initial complete resection of GIST. This vignette remains in the postoperative setting, but the near-miss is that the pathology is low-risk, making adjuvant therapy even less justifiable; the correct approach is surveillance rather than imatinib.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old woman with a known gastric GIST is seen after staging workup for enlarging abdominal pain and early satiety. Imaging shows a 9.5 cm mass abutting the lesser curvature with several peritoneal implants, and biopsy confirms KIT-positive disease. Her oncologist is considering treatment before any operation because the primary tumor is technically difficult to remove without major morbidity. She asks whether imatinib should be started now to shrink the tumor and make surgery easier. She has good performance status and no prior systemic therapy.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case preserves the gastric GIST context and the question of whether imatinib should be started before surgery, but the disease is now metastatic with peritoneal implants, shifting the scenario away from a pure neoadjuvant setting.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Begin neoadjuvant imatinib to downstage the primary tumor before surgery.
Use imatinib preoperatively to make the mass more resectable.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not a straightforward neoadjuvant-imatinib scenario because the patient has metastatic disease. The safer interpretation is systemic therapy for unresectable/metastatic KIT-positive GIST, with any later surgery considered only in a multidisciplinary context rather than as neoadjuvant treatment for a localized tumor.
challenging_examples.rationale: The near-miss resembles a preoperative imatinib question, but the presence of peritoneal metastases means the case is no longer an initial localized neoadjuvant situation. The original recommendation not to use neoadjuvant imatinib is not the key issue here; instead, treatment should be framed as therapy for metastatic disease.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for allogeneic SCT
selected_rules.execute: Do not use allogeneic stem cell transplantation for AL amyloidosis
selected_rules.source_statement: There is no evidence supporting the use of allogeneic SCT for patients with AL.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for treatment including bone marrow or stem cell transplantation
selected_rules.execute: Consider high-dose chemotherapy with autologous stem cell transplantation as an option for selected patients, preferably in an investigative setting
selected_rules.source_statement: High-dose chemotherapy (CT) and autologous SCT is an option for selected patients with primary systemic amyloidosis, preferably within an investigative setting.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for autologous SCT
selected_rules.execute: Use autologous SCT only in selected patients after weighing performance status, comorbidities, patient preferences, and treatment goals
selected_rules.source_statement: In consideration of the lack of curative treatment options and the limitations of the evidence reviewed, the Expert Panel believes that offering ASCT may be a reasonable option for some patients, depending on performance status, co-morbidities, patient preferences, and ultimate treatment goals.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for SCT
selected_rules.execute: Base the transplant decision on disease-related characteristics, comorbidities, and patient preferences
selected_rules.source_statement: The patient selection process and the ultimate decision to perform an SCT should take into account not only disease-related characteristics, but also comorbidities and patient preferences.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for SCT
selected_rules.execute: Consider careful patient selection based on degree of light chain amyloidosis involvement and organ function to reduce transplant-related mortality
selected_rules.source_statement: Careful patient selection based on degree of light chain amyloidosis involvement and organ function is an emerging concept in amyloidosis that should be considered to reduce transplant-related mortality.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for SCT
selected_rules.execute: Recognize that transplantation in amyloidosis is an evolving area and that first relapse transplantation may be considered as an emerging area pending new evidence
selected_rules.source_statement: Transplantation in amyloidosis is an evolving area. New emerging areas include consideration of transplantation in first relapse and the impact of novel proteasome inhibitors on outcomes.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for SCT
selected_rules.execute: No recommendation can be made on how newer agents affect the need for SCT
selected_rules.source_statement: Newer agents are being investigated in the treatment of AL amyloidosis. At this time it is not known how they may impact the need for SCT.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for SCT
selected_rules.execute: No recommendation can be made on the impact of novel proteasome inhibitors on transplantation outcomes
selected_rules.source_statement: Transplantation in amyloidosis is an evolving area. New emerging areas include consideration of transplantation in first relapse and the impact of novel proteasome inhibitors on outcomes.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with primary systemic AL amyloidosis presents after 5 months of bortezomib-based therapy with partial hematologic response. He has persistent nephrotic-range proteinuria and mild peripheral edema, but no symptomatic heart failure. Echocardiography shows preserved ejection fraction with mild concentric thickening, and his creatinine is stable at 1.1 mg/dL. He remains active, works full-time, and wants the most aggressive treatment that offers the best chance of long-term disease control. His hematologist discusses stem cell transplantation as a next step. Because he has AL amyloidosis and is being evaluated for SCT, the team reviews his organ involvement, overall functional status, comorbidities, and treatment goals before deciding whether he is an appropriate transplant candidate.
recommended_actions: Use autologous stem cell transplantation only in selected patients after weighing performance status, comorbidities, patient preferences, and treatment goals.
Base the transplant decision on disease-related characteristics, comorbidities, and patient preferences.
Consider careful patient selection based on the degree of light chain amyloidosis involvement and organ function to reduce transplant-related mortality.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches an adult with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for SCT, which triggers the rules supporting selective use of autologous SCT and emphasizing individualized assessment. He is clinically stable and functionally well, so the vignette appropriately highlights evaluation of performance status, comorbidities, disease burden, organ function, and patient preferences before proceeding.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with primary systemic AL amyloidosis returns after 5 months of bortezomib-based therapy with a partial hematologic response. His nephrotic-range proteinuria persists and he has mild ankle edema, but he reports no dyspnea or chest pain. Echocardiography shows preserved ejection fraction with only mild concentric thickening, and creatinine remains 1.1 mg/dL. He is active, still working full time, and is eager for the most aggressive therapy available. The hematology team discusses transplantation, but his evaluation also notes that he has severe, longstanding pulmonary hypertension with a resting right ventricular systolic pressure in the high 70s and exertional desaturation despite preserved baseline functional status. The team reviews organ involvement, comorbidities, and his goals before deciding whether to proceed.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still centers on primary systemic AL amyloidosis after bortezomib-based therapy with a partial response and a transplant discussion, but an important comorbidity makes transplant candidacy much less straightforward.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with autologous stem cell transplantation now that he has had a partial response and remains highly motivated.
Refer for SCT based mainly on his preserved ejection fraction and stable creatinine.
Plan transplant without further concern because he is working full time and appears functionally well.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for routine SCT selection because the severe pulmonary hypertension and exertional hypoxemia materially worsen transplant risk. The safer interpretation is that he needs reassessment for transplant eligibility, with comorbidity burden likely outweighing the apparent functional reserve.
challenging_examples.rationale: The original recommendation applies to selected AL amyloidosis patients considered for autologous SCT after weighing performance status, comorbidities, preferences, and organ involvement. Here, a major cardiopulmonary comorbidity substantially changes the risk profile and undermines straightforward transplant candidacy.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 64-year-old woman with primary systemic AL amyloidosis has completed 4 months of bortezomib-based therapy and achieved a partial hematologic response. She still has nephrotic-range proteinuria and mild lower-extremity edema, but no overt heart failure symptoms. Echocardiography shows preserved ejection fraction with mild concentric thickening, and her creatinine is 0.9 mg/dL. She remains independent in daily activities and wants the most aggressive treatment available. Her hematologist asks the transplant team to evaluate her for stem cell transplantation. During staging, however, she is found to have marked hepatic involvement with cholestatic jaundice, an alkaline phosphatase of 980 U/L, and progressive ascites over the past month. The team reviews treatment goals and whether transplantation is appropriate in light of these findings.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This vignette closely mirrors a stable AL amyloidosis patient being considered for SCT after partial response, but the added hepatic disease is a subtle high-risk feature that changes the transplant decision.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer autologous stem cell transplantation because her kidneys and heart appear relatively preserved.
Move forward with SCT based on her good day-to-day independence and desire for aggressive treatment.
Treat the liver findings as secondary and proceed with transplant planning.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The case is not guideline-concordant for proceeding directly to SCT because the degree of organ involvement is now severe, particularly the significant hepatic dysfunction and ascites. The safer interpretation is that she requires careful transplant risk reassessment, and current organ burden may make her a poor candidate.
challenging_examples.rationale: Although the patient resembles a typical SCT-evaluation case in AL amyloidosis, the rules emphasize careful selection based on organ function and disease involvement. Severe liver involvement meaningfully increases transplant-related mortality risk and makes uncomplicated SCT selection inappropriate.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with early (T1) glottic cancer and recurrence after prior radiation therapy
selected_rules.execute: Do not use re-irradiation
selected_rules.source_statement: However, re-irradiation is not an option in cases of recurrence.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with previously untreated early (T1) glottic cancer
selected_rules.execute: Offer endolaryngeal surgery, with or without laser, or radiation therapy as equally effective treatment options
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with early (T1) glottic cancer, recommended treatment options include the equally effective endolaryngeal surgery, with or without laser, or radiation therapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with previously untreated early (T1) glottic cancer
selected_rules.execute: Choose between surgery and radiation based on patient and clinician preferences and general medical condition
selected_rules.source_statement: The choice between treatment modalities should be based on patient and clinician preferences and general medical condition.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with early (T1) glottic cancer and anterior commissure involvement
selected_rules.execute: Consider radiation therapy over surgery
selected_rules.source_statement: Anterior commissure involvement may be a factor that favours a recommendation of radiotherapy over surgery due to a common opinion that voice outcomes are particularly affected.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with early (T1) glottic cancer and tumor localized to the midportion of the vocal fold with uncompromised endoscopic accessibility
selected_rules.execute: Consider endolaryngeal surgery as an ideal candidate treatment
selected_rules.source_statement: Tumours localized to the midportion of the vocal fold, and where endoscopic accessibility is uncompromised, may be considered ideal candidates for surgery.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with early (T1) glottic cancer who cannot tolerate a general anaesthetic
selected_rules.execute: Prefer radiation therapy over surgery
selected_rules.source_statement: Other important practical considerations include the ability for patients to tolerate a general anaesthetic, which is required for surgery.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with early (T1) glottic cancer who cannot cooperate with daily treatment for four to six weeks
selected_rules.execute: Avoid radiation therapy and consider surgery if otherwise appropriate
selected_rules.source_statement: In contrast, radiotherapy requires patient cooperation for daily treatment for four to six weeks.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with early (T1) glottic cancer and local recurrence after surgery
selected_rules.execute: Consider partial laryngeal surgery, including revision endoscopic surgery
selected_rules.source_statement: Partial laryngeal surgery, including revision endoscopic surgery, is possible for local recurrence following surgery.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 59-year-old man presents with persistent hoarseness 9 months after completing definitive radiation therapy for a T1 glottic squamous cell carcinoma of the left true vocal fold. Repeat laryngoscopy and biopsy confirm a small local recurrence confined to the glottis, still staged as early T1 disease. He remains otherwise well and asks whether another course of radiation could treat the recurrence. He is anxious to preserve voice but understands that the lesion is limited and potentially amenable to local salvage treatment.
recommended_actions: Do not use re-irradiation for this recurrence.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches an adult with early T1 glottic cancer and recurrence after prior radiation therapy, which triggers the rule that re-irradiation is not an option in cases of recurrence.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 59-year-old man returns with persistent hoarseness 8 months after completing definitive radiation therapy for a T1 glottic squamous cell carcinoma of the left true vocal fold. Repeat laryngoscopy shows an irregular lesion, and biopsy confirms recurrent disease. However, imaging now demonstrates subtle spread across the anterior commissure with extension into the supraglottis, and the tumor is restaged as T2 rather than confined to early T1 disease. He asks whether another course of radiation could still be used to preserve his voice. He remains otherwise well and is hoping for a local treatment option.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still centers on post-radiation hoarseness with biopsy-proven local recurrence in the glottis, but the recurrence is no longer limited to early T1 disease because of supraglottic/anterior commissure extension and T2 restaging.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Recommend repeat radiation as salvage treatment for the recurrence
Discuss re-irradiation to avoid laryngeal surgery
Proceed with another definitive radiation course because the recurrence still appears localized
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the no-re-irradiation rule because the lesion has progressed beyond the specific early T1 glottic recurrence scenario. The safer interpretation is that this is a restaged recurrent laryngeal cancer requiring multidisciplinary salvage planning rather than automatic application of the early-stage re-irradiation prohibition.
challenging_examples.rationale: Rule R001 applies specifically to adult patients with early T1 glottic cancer recurring after prior radiation. Here, the recurrence is no longer early T1 because it has been restaged as T2 with additional extension, so the exact rule trigger is not met.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 61-year-old woman presents with hoarseness 10 months after finishing definitive radiation therapy for a T1 glottic squamous cell carcinoma. Surveillance laryngoscopy and biopsy confirm a small recurrent tumor on the right true vocal fold. She asks whether re-irradiation can be used, since she tolerated the first course reasonably well. The lesion remains limited to the glottis on imaging, but she is now enrolled in a trial at a different center where the team is considering focal endoscopic salvage because the recurrent site is technically accessible and the rest of the larynx is uninvolved.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The scenario preserves the classic setup of biopsy-proven recurrence after prior radiation for an early glottic cancer, but the recurrence is now being managed in a salvage trial where the question is no longer simply whether to re-irradiate; the recurrence itself is being approached as technically resectable local disease.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer another course of radiation to the recurrent glottic lesion
Schedule re-irradiation because the recurrence is still small and localized
Treat the recurrence with repeat definitive radiotherapy instead of salvage surgery
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for re-irradiation because the case is framed around salvage local management after prior radiation, and the appropriate next step is not to assume repeat radiation is an option. The safer interpretation is to favor non-radiation salvage evaluation, typically endoscopic or surgical review, rather than re-irradiation.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 prohibits re-irradiation for recurrence after prior radiation in early T1 glottic cancer. Although the patient has recurrent disease after radiation, the vignette shifts the management context toward salvage endoscopic treatment rather than a straightforward indication for re-irradiation, making the original recommendation inapplicable.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is receiving conventional fractionation radiotherapy for unresectable stage III NSCLC
selected_rules.execute: do not escalate dose beyond 66 Gy
selected_rules.source_statement: Dose escalation beyond 66 Gy with conventional fractionation is not recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is receiving radiotherapy for unresectable stage III NSCLC
selected_rules.execute: do not use hyperfractionated radiotherapy regimens that do not accelerate the treatment course
selected_rules.source_statement: Hyperformated radiotherapy regimens that do not result in acceleration of the treatment course, even though the total nominal radiotherapy dose may be modestly increased, are not recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with unresectable stage III NSCLC is being considered for induction chemotherapy before concurrent chemoradiotherapy
selected_rules.execute: do not routinely give induction chemotherapy before concurrent chemoradiotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Routine use of induction chemotherapy prior to concurrent chemoradiotherapy is not recommended;

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is being considered for durvalumab after chemoradiation
selected_rules.execute: do not exclude durvalumab solely because of EGFR or PD-L1 biomarker status
selected_rules.source_statement: At this time, it is inappropriate to exclude patients from therapy based solely on a biomarker such as epidermal growth factor receptor or programmed death-ligand 1 status.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has unresectable, lymph node-positive stage III NSCLC (N2 or N3) and curative-intent treatment is planned
selected_rules.execute: use concurrent chemoradiation
selected_rules.source_statement: Concurrent chemoradiation should be used for curative-intent treatment of patients with unresectable, lymph node-positive (N2 or N3) stage III NSCLC.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is receiving concurrent chemoradiation for unresectable stage III NSCLC
selected_rules.execute: use standard radiation dose fractionation of 60 to 66 Gy in 2 Gy once-daily fractions over six weeks
selected_rules.source_statement: A standard dose fractionation of 60 to 66 Gy given in fractions of 2 Gy once per day over six weeks is recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has stage III NSCLC and has not progressed after chemoradiation and has no contraindications to an immune checkpoint inhibitor
selected_rules.execute: use durvalumab
selected_rules.source_statement: Durvalumab should be used in patients with stage III NSCLC who have not progressed postchemoradiation and have no contraindications to an immune checkpoint inhibitor.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is having chemoradiotherapy and surgery for stage IIIA/B-N2 NSCLC
selected_rules.execute: schedule surgery three to five weeks after chemoradiotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with stage IIIA/B-N2 NSCLC who are having chemoradiotherapy and surgery, ensure that their surgery is scheduled for three to five weeks after the chemoradiotherapy.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 64-year-old man with COPD and a 45-pack-year smoking history is diagnosed with unresectable stage IIIA NSCLC with biopsy-proven N2 mediastinal lymph node involvement. He is being treated with curative intent and has started concurrent chemoradiation. The radiation oncology plan specifies 60 Gy in 30 once-daily fractions over six weeks with weekly on-treatment review. A medical oncologist asks whether the dose should be escalated to improve local control, but the team is otherwise proceeding with standard concurrent therapy. The patient has not yet completed chemoradiation and has no current contraindications to future immunotherapy.
recommended_actions: Use concurrent chemoradiation for curative-intent treatment of this unresectable, lymph node-positive stage III NSCLC.
Use standard radiation dose fractionation of 60 to 66 Gy in 2 Gy once-daily fractions over six weeks.
Do not escalate the radiation dose beyond 66 Gy with conventional fractionation.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches unresectable, node-positive stage III NSCLC planned for curative treatment, which triggers concurrent chemoradiation. Because the patient is receiving concurrent chemoradiation, standard fractionation of 60 to 66 Gy once daily is recommended. The question of dose escalation is also addressed by the rules: conventional fractionation should not be escalated beyond 66 Gy.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 64-year-old man with COPD and a 45-pack-year smoking history is diagnosed with unresectable stage IIIA NSCLC with biopsy-proven N2 mediastinal lymph node involvement. He is being treated with curative intent and has started concurrent chemoradiation. The radiation oncology plan specifies 66 Gy in 33 once-daily fractions over six and a half weeks, and the team is discussing whether to increase the total dose slightly for local control. He has not yet completed chemoradiation and remains eligible for future immunotherapy if his disease does not progress. No surgery is planned, and there are no current contraindications to checkpoint inhibitor therapy.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still has unresectable, node-positive stage IIIA NSCLC on curative-intent concurrent chemoradiation, but the planned dose is at the upper end of the standard range rather than above it.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with the current concurrent chemoradiation plan and consider modest dose escalation for improved local control.
Increase the radiation dose beyond 66 Gy because the patient is fit enough for definitive treatment.
Reassure the team that a slightly higher conventional dose is appropriate in unresectable stage III disease.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not a case for dose escalation beyond 66 Gy with conventional fractionation. The planned 66 Gy in 33 fractions is already within the recommended standard range for concurrent chemoradiation in unresectable stage III NSCLC. The safer interpretation is to keep conventional dosing within 60 to 66 Gy and avoid escalation.
challenging_examples.rationale: The vignette closely matches unresectable stage IIIA, N2-positive NSCLC treated with curative-intent concurrent chemoradiation, but the only actionable difference is that the dose is already 66 Gy. The guideline forbids escalation beyond 66 Gy with conventional fractionation, so recommending a higher dose would be discordant.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 71-year-old woman with a long smoking history is diagnosed with unresectable stage III NSCLC involving multiple mediastinal nodes. She is planned for curative-intent concurrent chemoradiation and is receiving 60 Gy in 30 once-daily fractions. After completing treatment, restaging CT shows a near-complete response but a new small pleural-based nodule suspicious for metastatic disease. Her oncology team is reviewing next steps and asks whether she should start durvalumab, noting that her tumor had low PD-L1 expression on initial testing. She has mild chronic dyspnea from emphysema but no absolute contraindication to immunotherapy.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The patient still has stage III NSCLC treated with definitive chemoradiation and the team is considering consolidation durvalumab, but post-treatment imaging now suggests progression outside the original stage III setting.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start durvalumab consolidation now that chemoradiation is complete.
Do not let the low PD-L1 result prevent durvalumab use.
Treat this as standard post-chemoradiation maintenance with an immune checkpoint inhibitor.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for durvalumab after chemoradiation because the patient has a new pleural-based nodule suspicious for progression/metastatic spread on restaging. Durvalumab is recommended for stage III NSCLC patients who have not progressed after chemoradiation and have no contraindications. The safer interpretation is to confirm the new lesion and manage based on updated disease stage rather than proceed automatically with consolidation durvalumab.
challenging_examples.rationale: The case is intentionally close to the post-chemoradiation durvalumab scenario, including unresectable stage III disease and no biomarker-based exclusion. The subtle change is radiographic concern for progression, which breaks the eligibility condition that the patient has not progressed after chemoradiation.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering adding gemcitabine or capecitabine to an anthracycline-taxane regimen for adjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: do not add gemcitabine or capecitabine to the anthracycline-taxane regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: The addition of gemcitabine or capecitabine to an anthracycline-taxane regimen is not recommended for adjuvant chemotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is older than 65 years and capecitabine is being considered instead of adjuvant AC or CMF
selected_rules.execute: do not use capecitabine as the adjuvant chemotherapy option in lieu of AC or CMF
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients older than 65 years, capecitabine is not recommended as an adjuvant chemotherapy option in lieu of adjuvant AC or CMF (oral cyclophosphamide).

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has ER-positive cancer and is premenopausal and considering ovarian ablation or suppression plus tamoxifen
selected_rules.execute: do not treat ovarian ablation or suppression plus tamoxifen as standard of care
selected_rules.source_statement: In premenopausal patients with ER+ tumours (treated with or without chemotherapy) the addition of ovarian ablation or suppression to tamoxifen is not the standard of care.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has ER-positive cancer and is premenopausal and considering ovarian ablation or suppression plus 5 years of an aromatase inhibitor
selected_rules.execute: do not use ovarian ablation or suppression plus 5 years of an aromatase inhibitor as standard of care
selected_rules.source_statement: In premenopausal patients with ER+ tumours, treated with or without chemotherapy, ovarian ablation or suppression plus five years of an AI is not the standard of care.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient does not have HER2-positive breast cancer
selected_rules.execute: do not offer adjuvant trastuzumab
selected_rules.source_statement: Only patients with HER2+ breast cancer (IHC 3+, ISH ratio ≥2, or 6+ HER2 gene copies per cell nucleus) should be offered adjuvant trastuzumab.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is receiving trastuzumab and has any of the following: LVEF under 55%, documented congestive heart failure, high-risk uncontrolled arrhythmias, angina requiring medication, clinically significant valvular disease, transmural infarction on ECG, or poorly controlled hypertension
selected_rules.execute: do not offer trastuzumab
selected_rules.source_statement: trastuzumab should not be offered to patients with any of the following: a left ventricular ejection fraction LVEF of <55%; a history of documented congestive heart failure; high-risk uncontrolled arrhythmias; angina pectoris requiring medication; clinically significant valvular disease; evidence of transmural infarction on electrocardiograph (ECG); poorly controlled hypertension.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering Ki-67 for routine risk stratification in early-stage breast cancer
selected_rules.execute: do not use Ki-67 as a standard parameter outside clinical trials
selected_rules.source_statement: It is premature to recommend its use as a standard parameter for patient risk stratification, although it may be evaluated in clinical trials.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering intrinsic breast cancer subtypes for clinical decision making outside a trial
selected_rules.execute: do not use intrinsic subtypes outside a clinical trial
selected_rules.source_statement: At this point, the use of these subtypes in clinical decision making outside of a clinical trial is not recommended.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman with stage II, node-positive breast cancer has completed surgery and is being seen to finalize adjuvant therapy. Her tumor is ER-positive, PR-positive, and HER2-negative by IHC 1+. She is postmenopausal and has no cardiac history. The oncology team is discussing several options, including adding capecitabine to an anthracycline-taxane regimen and offering adjuvant trastuzumab because of her high-risk features. A colleague also suggests using Ki-67 and intrinsic molecular subtype results to refine recurrence risk and guide treatment intensity. The patient asks for the most evidence-based plan and wants to avoid unnecessary treatment.
recommended_actions: Do not add gemcitabine or capecitabine to the anthracycline-taxane regimen for adjuvant chemotherapy.
Do not offer adjuvant trastuzumab because the cancer is not HER2-positive.
Do not use Ki-67 as a standard parameter for routine risk stratification outside clinical trials.
Do not use intrinsic breast cancer subtypes for clinical decision making outside a clinical trial.
source_case.rationale: The vignette is designed to trigger rules about adjuvant chemotherapy intensification, HER2-directed therapy, and biomarker use. Because the tumor is HER2-negative, trastuzumab is not recommended. The proposed addition of capecitabine to an anthracycline-taxane regimen is also not recommended. Ki-67 and intrinsic subtype testing are mentioned only as decision aids, but both are not recommended for routine clinical decision making outside trials.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman with stage II, node-positive breast cancer has completed surgery and is returning to finalize adjuvant therapy. Her tumor is ER-positive, PR-positive, and HER2-negative by IHC 2+ with a negative reflex ISH assay. She is postmenopausal and has no cardiac history. The oncology team is discussing whether to intensify treatment by adding capecitabine to an anthracycline-taxane regimen, and one physician briefly wonders whether trastuzumab should be included because of the patient’s high-risk features. Another colleague mentions Ki-67 and intrinsic molecular subtype testing to help refine recurrence risk before treatment is finalized. The patient wants the most evidence-based plan and hopes to avoid unnecessary therapy.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Nearly identical early-stage, node-positive ER/PR-positive breast cancer after surgery, with the same adjuvant-treatment discussion and biomarker language; the key difference is that HER2 testing is not positive because the reflex ISH is negative.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Add capecitabine to the anthracycline-taxane adjuvant regimen
Start adjuvant trastuzumab based on the high-risk clinical features
Use Ki-67 to guide recurrence-risk stratification
Use intrinsic molecular subtype results to choose adjuvant treatment intensity
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This remains a HER2-negative early breast cancer case, so trastuzumab is not guideline-concordant. The proposed chemotherapy intensification with capecitabine is also not recommended, and Ki-67 or intrinsic subtype testing should not be used routinely to direct treatment outside a trial.
challenging_examples.rationale: The vignette preserves the original workflow and risk profile but changes HER2 from clearly negative by IHC 1+ to IHC 2+ with negative ISH, which still does not meet criteria for HER2-positive disease. That subtle change makes trastuzumab inappropriate while keeping the rest of the case tempting.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman with stage II, node-positive breast cancer is seen after surgery to finalize adjuvant therapy. Her tumor is ER-positive, PR-positive, and HER2-negative by IHC 1+. She is premenopausal, with regular menses documented three months ago, and has no cardiac history. The oncology team is discussing several options, including adding capecitabine to an anthracycline-taxane regimen and offering adjuvant trastuzumab because of her high-risk features. A colleague also suggests using Ki-67 and intrinsic molecular subtype results to refine recurrence risk and guide treatment intensity. The patient asks for the most evidence-based plan and wants to avoid unnecessary treatment.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case mirrors the source almost exactly, including the same stage, receptor profile, and adjuvant-planning discussion; the subtle mismatch is that she is now premenopausal rather than postmenopausal.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Add capecitabine to the adjuvant anthracycline-taxane regimen
Offer adjuvant trastuzumab despite the HER2-negative result
Order Ki-67 to decide how intensively to treat
Use intrinsic subtype testing to tailor adjuvant therapy
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is still not a setting for adjuvant trastuzumab because the tumor is HER2-negative. Capecitabine is not recommended as an adjuvant add-on here, and Ki-67 or intrinsic subtyping should not be used routinely for clinical decision making outside trials.
challenging_examples.rationale: The case is intentionally deceptive because the treatment discussion is unchanged, but the menopause status no longer matches the original source. That change does not create any indication for the proposed therapies, so the original recommendation remains inappropriate.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with head and neck cancer
selected_rules.execute: coordinate care among an experienced core team responsible for assessment, treatment, planning, management, survivorship, and rehabilitation
selected_rules.source_statement: The care of patients with head and neck cancer should be coordinated among members of an experienced Core Team, comprised of a group of physicians and allied healthcare providers who will be responsible for the assessment, treatment, planning, management, survivorship, and rehabilitation of the patient.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with head and neck cancer
selected_rules.execute: ensure the primary care provider is responsible for ongoing overall health and post-treatment supportive care
selected_rules.source_statement: The Primary Care Provider will be responsible for the ongoing overall health of the patient and will offer supportive care after treatment.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with head and neck cancer
selected_rules.execute: involve an extended team as needed to facilitate treatment, planning, management, survivorship, and rehabilitation
selected_rules.source_statement: The Extended Team will be called upon by the core team to facilitate treatment, planning, management, survivorship, and rehabilitation of the patient.

selected_rules.activation_condition: members of the care team
selected_rules.execute: ensure team members have training or experience managing patients with head and neck cancers
selected_rules.source_statement: Members of the Teams must have training or experience managing patients with head and neck cancers.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with head and neck cancer
selected_rules.execute: recognize the primary care physician as not involved in day-to-day treatment but important for post-treatment supportive care and ongoing overall health
selected_rules.source_statement: The primary care physician is not involved in the day to day treatment of the head and neck cancer patient but plays an important role in post-treatment supportive care and is responsible for the ongoing overall health of the patient.

selected_rules.activation_condition: surgical management of patients with head and neck tumours
selected_rules.execute: use reconstruction expertise from a fellowship-trained microvascular surgeon with specific training in head and neck reconstruction
selected_rules.source_statement: Reconstruction expertise is required for the surgical management of patients with head and neck tumours and necessitates a fellowship-trained microvascular surgeon with specific training in head and neck reconstruction.

selected_rules.activation_condition: reporting head and neck malignancies
selected_rules.execute: use CAP-CCO standards for pathology reporting
selected_rules.source_statement: Pathologists are advised to use the CAP-CCO standards for reporting head and neck malignancies (5).

selected_rules.activation_condition: reporting nodal dissections
selected_rules.execute: include levels and structures in the specimen, number of involved and uninvolved nodes, level of nodes, and presence and location of extracapsular spread
selected_rules.source_statement: The reporting of nodal dissections should include a description of the levels and structures included in the specimen, including number of involved and uninvolved nodes, level of these nodes, and the presence and location of extracapsular spread of tumour.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man is diagnosed with stage III squamous cell carcinoma of the oropharynx after biopsy of a persistent throat lesion and CT imaging showing a primary tumor with suspicious cervical nodes. He is referred to a multidisciplinary head and neck cancer clinic where the medical oncologist, radiation oncologist, head and neck surgeon, speech-language pathologist, dietitian, and social worker review his case together. The team discusses definitive treatment planning, swallowing rehabilitation, nutritional support, and survivorship needs. His family physician is informed of the diagnosis and remains responsible for his general medical care and supportive follow-up after cancer treatment. Because he may need surgery depending on response to therapy, the surgeon notes that reconstruction expertise would be required if an operation becomes necessary.
recommended_actions: Coordinate care among an experienced core team responsible for assessment, treatment, planning, management, survivorship, and rehabilitation.
Ensure the primary care provider is responsible for ongoing overall health and post-treatment supportive care.
Recognize that the primary care physician is not involved in day-to-day treatment but remains important for post-treatment supportive care and ongoing overall health.
source_case.rationale: This vignette describes a patient with head and neck cancer, which triggers the general care-coordination recommendations. The multidisciplinary clinic and planned follow-up align with the need for an experienced core team and ongoing primary care involvement. No pathology-reporting or surgical-management-specific rule was selected as a triggered rule.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man is diagnosed with stage III squamous cell carcinoma of the oropharynx after biopsy of a persistent throat lesion and CT imaging showing a primary tumor with suspicious cervical nodes. He is referred to a multidisciplinary head and neck cancer clinic where the medical oncologist, radiation oncologist, head and neck surgeon, speech-language pathologist, dietitian, and social worker review his case together. The team discusses definitive treatment planning, swallowing rehabilitation, nutritional support, and survivorship needs. His family physician is informed of the diagnosis, but the patient has recently moved and has not yet established ongoing primary care locally. Because he may need surgery depending on response to therapy, the surgeon notes that reconstruction expertise would be required if an operation becomes necessary.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still presents a classic head and neck cancer multidisciplinary evaluation with treatment planning and supportive services, but the usual ongoing primary care relationship is missing.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Document the family physician as responsible for ongoing overall health and post-treatment supportive care
Assume primary care follow-up is already in place and does not need to be arranged
Proceed with the multidisciplinary plan without addressing the absence of a local primary care provider
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the primary care support recommendation because the patient does not currently have an established primary care provider to assume ongoing overall health and post-treatment supportive care. The safer interpretation is to arrange primary care follow-up before or during treatment planning rather than simply assuming it exists.
challenging_examples.rationale: The vignette preserves the head and neck cancer care-coordination context, but the lack of an established local primary care physician invalidates the recommendation that the PCP is responsible for ongoing supportive care and overall health.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man is diagnosed with stage III squamous cell carcinoma of the oropharynx after biopsy of a persistent throat lesion and CT imaging showing a primary tumor with suspicious cervical nodes. He is referred to a multidisciplinary head and neck cancer clinic where the medical oncologist, radiation oncologist, and speech-language pathologist review his case together. The team discusses definitive chemoradiation planning, swallowing rehabilitation, nutritional support, and survivorship needs. His family physician is informed of the diagnosis and will continue supportive follow-up after treatment. The head and neck surgeon notes that the tumor appears unresectable on imaging and that surgery is not planned unless symptoms change, so reconstruction expertise is not being considered at this time.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The setting still looks like standard head and neck multidisciplinary management, but the surgical component that would require specialized reconstruction expertise is no longer part of the plan.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Arrange a fellowship-trained microvascular reconstruction surgeon for the operative plan
Treat this as a case requiring reconstruction expertise for head and neck tumor surgery
Schedule preoperative reconstruction consultation despite the current nonsurgical intent
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the reconstruction recommendation because no surgical management is planned; the tumor is described as unresectable and the team is pursuing definitive nonoperative treatment. The safer interpretation is that reconstruction expertise is only relevant if surgery later becomes indicated.
challenging_examples.rationale: The case remains a head and neck cancer multidisciplinary discussion, but the key surgical-management condition is removed by making the tumor unresectable with no current plan for operation, so the reconstruction rule does not apply.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering radiotherapy alone given preoperatively or postoperatively
selected_rules.execute: Do not use radiotherapy alone as it showed no survival benefit compared with surgery alone
selected_rules.source_statement: Randomized trials demonstrated no survival benefit for radiotherapy given alone, either preoperatively or postoperatively, compared with surgery alone.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering postoperative chemotherapy alone
selected_rules.execute: Do not use postoperative chemotherapy alone as it showed no survival benefit compared with surgery alone
selected_rules.source_statement: Randomized trials demonstrated no survival benefit for postoperative chemotherapy given alone compared with surgery alone.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient has resectable, operable, potentially curable thoracic esophageal cancer and surgery is appropriate
selected_rules.execute: Use preoperative platinum-based chemotherapy plus radiotherapy as the preferred treatment modality
selected_rules.source_statement: Preoperative cisplatin-based chemotherapy plus radiotherapy is recommended as the preferred modality for the management of surgically resectable patients with esophageal cancer.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient has resectable, operable, potentially curable thoracic esophageal cancer and surgery is appropriate
selected_rules.execute: Consider preoperative platinum-based chemotherapy alone as an alternative treatment option
selected_rules.source_statement: Preoperative cisplatin-based chemotherapy alone is an alternative choice for the management of surgically resectable patients with esophageal cancer.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering preoperative therapy for resectable esophageal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Prefer preoperative chemoradiotherapy over surgery alone when choosing preoperative treatment
selected_rules.source_statement: Based on the majority of the evidence available at this time, the GI DSG believes that preoperative chemoradiotherapy for resectable carcinoma of the esophagus is the preferred approach.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering preoperative therapy for resectable esophageal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Recognize that either preoperative chemotherapy or preoperative chemoradiotherapy may provide survival benefit compared with surgery alone
selected_rules.source_statement: The GI DSG acknowledges there is evidence indicating survival benefits with either preoperative chemotherapy or chemoradiotherapy compared with surgery alone.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Before administering preoperative therapy
selected_rules.execute: Discuss potential adverse effects with the patient because the survival advantage may be minimal
selected_rules.source_statement: Clinicians should recognize that the survival advantage of preoperative therapy may be minimal and a discussion with patients regarding potential adverse effects is required.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Deciding whether to give preoperative therapy
selected_rules.execute: Base the decision on patient preferences, comorbidities, and suitability for trimodality therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Decisions to administer preoperative therapy should be based on patient preferences, comorbidities, and suitability for trimodality therapy.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 63-year-old man with progressive dysphagia to solids and a 7-kg weight loss is diagnosed with a mid-thoracic esophageal squamous cell carcinoma on endoscopy and biopsy. Staging CT and PET show no distant metastases, and endoscopic ultrasound suggests a locally advanced but resectable tumor with preserved performance status. Thoracic surgery feels he is an appropriate candidate for esophagectomy after neoadjuvant treatment. He has well-controlled hypertension and no major cardiopulmonary disease. In clinic, he asks whether he can proceed directly to surgery or if treatment before surgery would improve his chances. The oncology team is considering preoperative therapy options and discussing expected toxicities and the fact that any survival benefit may be modest. The patient says he values the best chance of cure but wants to understand how treatment might affect swallowing, fatigue, and recovery before making a decision.
recommended_actions: Use preoperative platinum-based chemotherapy plus radiotherapy as the preferred treatment modality.
Discuss potential adverse effects with the patient because the survival advantage may be minimal.
Base the decision on patient preferences, comorbidities, and suitability for trimodality therapy.
source_case.rationale: This vignette describes an adult with resectable, operable, potentially curable thoracic esophageal cancer for whom surgery is appropriate, triggering the recommendation for preoperative platinum-based chemoradiotherapy. Because preoperative therapy is being considered, the team should discuss adverse effects and recognize that the survival advantage may be minimal. The choice should also be individualized based on preferences, comorbidities, and fitness for trimodality therapy.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 63-year-old man with progressive dysphagia to solids and a 7-kg weight loss is diagnosed with a mid-thoracic esophageal squamous cell carcinoma on endoscopy and biopsy. Staging CT and PET show no distant metastases, and endoscopic ultrasound suggests a locally advanced but potentially curable tumor. He has been evaluated by thoracic surgery, but the surgeon notes that the tumor is abutting the aorta and would be technically difficult to resect with negative margins, so upfront surgery is not being offered. His performance status remains good, and he has well-controlled hypertension without major cardiopulmonary disease. In clinic, he asks whether he should proceed directly to surgery or if treatment before surgery would improve his chances. The oncology team is reviewing preoperative options and discussing toxicities and the possibility that any survival advantage may be modest. The patient says he wants the best chance of cure and wants to understand how treatment might affect swallowing, fatigue, and recovery before deciding.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like a typical nonmetastatic thoracic esophageal cancer referral for trimodality therapy, but surgical resectability is no longer established because the lesion is judged technically difficult to resect with negative margins.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start preoperative platinum-based chemoradiotherapy and plan for esophagectomy after restaging.
Recommend trimodality treatment as the preferred curative approach without revisiting operability.
Frame the decision around patient preference for cure while assuming surgery remains appropriate.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the preoperative chemoradiotherapy recommendation because the tumor is not clearly resectable/operable in the usual sense; upfront surgery is not being offered due to concern for inability to achieve negative margins. The safer interpretation is that he needs further multidisciplinary reassessment of local resectability and overall treatment intent before applying a trimodality pathway.
challenging_examples.rationale: The original recommendation applies to surgically resectable, operable, potentially curable thoracic esophageal cancer. Here, the surgeon has already indicated that negative-margin resection is technically doubtful, which invalidates the key eligibility condition for the preoperative recommendation.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 63-year-old man with progressive dysphagia to solids and a 7-kg weight loss is diagnosed with a mid-thoracic esophageal squamous cell carcinoma on endoscopy and biopsy. Staging CT and PET show no distant metastases, and endoscopic ultrasound suggests a locally advanced but potentially curable thoracic tumor. Thoracic surgery feels he is an appropriate candidate for esophagectomy after neoadjuvant treatment, and he has preserved performance status with no major cardiopulmonary disease. The main additional issue is that he has chronic kidney disease with an eGFR of 34 mL/min/1.73 m2 and prior cisplatin-associated hearing loss from treatment for head and neck cancer years ago. In clinic, he asks whether he can proceed directly to surgery or if treatment before surgery would improve his chances. The oncology team discusses preoperative options, expected toxicities, and whether the benefit may be modest. The patient values cure but is worried about treatment side effects and recovery.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The vignette preserves the classic resectable thoracic esophageal cancer setting and even mentions surgery as appropriate, but the patient has major cisplatin-limiting comorbidities that make the preferred platinum-based regimen unsuitable.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with preoperative platinum-based chemoradiotherapy because the tumor is resectable and surgery is planned.
Recommend the standard cisplatin-based neoadjuvant approach without modification.
Move ahead with trimodality therapy while minimizing the renal and otologic risks.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This case is not guideline-concordant for the preferred preoperative platinum-based chemoradiotherapy recommendation because the patient has significant chronic kidney disease and prior cisplatin-related hearing loss, making cisplatin-based treatment unsafe or poorly suited. The safer interpretation is that preoperative treatment choice must be individualized, and a non-cisplatin strategy or surgery-based approach should be considered by the multidisciplinary team.
challenging_examples.rationale: Although the cancer is resectable and surgery is appropriate, the recommendation specifically calls for platinum-based therapy, and cisplatin is not a good fit here because of renal impairment and prior ototoxicity. That makes the straightforward application of the original recommendation inappropriate.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has bone pain from bone metastases and is receiving a BMA
selected_rules.execute: do not use the BMA alone as the only treatment for bone pain
selected_rules.source_statement: The analgesic effects of BMAs are modest, and they should not be used alone for bone pain.

selected_rules.activation_condition: monitoring BMA use with biochemical markers
selected_rules.execute: do not use biochemical markers to monitor BMA use routinely
selected_rules.source_statement: this ASCO guideline update does not alter the prior recommendation (No. 7) that the use of the biochemical markers to monitor BMA use is not recommended for routine care.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has breast cancer with evidence of bone metastases
selected_rules.execute: treat with a bone-modifying agent (BMA)
selected_rules.source_statement: patients with breast cancer who have evidence of bone metastases should be treated with BMAs.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with bone metastases is being treated with denosumab
selected_rules.execute: use denosumab 120 mg subcutaneously every 4 weeks
selected_rules.source_statement: Options include denosumab, 120 mg subcutaneously, every 4 weeks.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with bone metastases is being treated with pamidronate
selected_rules.execute: use pamidronate 90 mg intravenously every 3 to 4 weeks
selected_rules.source_statement: Options include ... pamidronate, 90 mg intravenously, every 3 to 4 weeks.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with bone metastases is being treated with zoledronic acid
selected_rules.execute: use zoledronic acid 4 mg intravenously every 12 weeks or every 3 to 4 weeks
selected_rules.source_statement: Options include ... zoledronic acid, 4 mg intravenously every 12 weeks or every 3 to 4 weeks.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with bone metastases is receiving zoledronic acid
selected_rules.execute: administer zoledronic acid over no less than 15 minutes
selected_rules.source_statement: If patients are treated with zoledronic acid, 4 mg intravenously administered over no less than 15 minutes, dosing options are every 12 weeks or every 3 to 4 weeks.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has bone pain from bone metastases
selected_rules.execute: apply standard supportive care and pain management, including analgesia, adjunct therapies, radiotherapy, surgery, systemic anticancer therapy, and referral to supportive care and pain management
selected_rules.source_statement: The Update Committee recommends that the current standard of care for supportive care and pain management-analgesia, adjunct therapies, radiotherapy, surgery, systemic anticancer therapy, and referral to supportive care and pain management-be applied.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman with ER-positive breast cancer presents with worsening deep aching pain in her pelvis and lower back. Imaging shows multiple lytic bone metastases without spinal cord compression. She is already receiving denosumab for skeletal-related event prevention. Despite the agent, her pain has progressed over the past month and is limiting sleep and walking. She has been using acetaminophen with only partial relief. Her oncology team is considering additional pain-directed treatment and referral for symptom control. She asks whether the bone-targeted therapy alone should be enough for the pain.
recommended_actions: Do not use the bone-modifying agent alone as the only treatment for her bone pain.
Apply standard supportive care and pain management, including analgesia, adjunct therapies, radiotherapy, surgery, systemic anticancer therapy, and referral to supportive care and pain management.
source_case.rationale: She has bone pain from bone metastases and is receiving a bone-modifying agent, which triggers the recommendation against using the BMA alone for pain control. Her pain from bone metastases also triggers the recommendation to use standard supportive care and pain management measures.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 61-year-old woman with ER-positive breast cancer and stable bone metastases presents for routine follow-up. She reports intermittent dull aching in the mid-back that is worst after gardening but improves with rest and occasional acetaminophen. Imaging again shows several sclerotic vertebral and pelvic metastases without fracture, spinal cord compression, or new lesions. She has been receiving denosumab for skeletal-related event prevention every 4 weeks and is tolerating it well. Her oncologist notes that the pain is mild and asks whether continuing the bone-targeted agent should be sufficient while they observe the symptoms. The patient is otherwise ambulatory, sleeping adequately, and has not needed opioids or radiation.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Breast cancer with bone metastases, denosumab use, and bone pain are all present, but the pain is mild/intermittent and not the progressive cancer-related pain described in the source case.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Continue denosumab as the only treatment for her bone pain and defer additional symptom-directed therapy.
Treat the current bone-targeted therapy as adequate analgesia without adding standard supportive pain measures.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not the same guideline situation because her pain is mild, intermittent, and functionally limited only minimally; the vignette does not establish the progressive, clinically significant bone pain that drove the supportive-care recommendation. Denosumab remains appropriate for metastatic bone disease, but the case does not justify assuming the bone-modifying agent alone is being used as the only pain treatment for active cancer-related bone pain.
