challenging_examples.rationale: The near-match is the presence of metastatic breast cancer on denosumab, but the pain severity and trajectory are different enough that the recommendation against BMA-only pain control is not clearly triggered.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman with ER-positive breast cancer presents with worsening deep aching pain in her pelvis and lower back. She has known bone metastases on prior imaging, and her oncologist recently started denosumab for skeletal-related event prevention. A repeat MRI now shows an epidural soft-tissue component at L4 with mild thecal sac effacement, but no frank spinal cord compression and no neurologic deficits. Her pain has worsened over the past month and is limiting sleep and walking despite acetaminophen. She asks whether the bone-targeted therapy alone should be enough for pain. The team is discussing urgent next steps and symptom control.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case keeps the same cancer, bone metastases, denosumab use, and significant pain, but adds epidural extension with the possibility of urgent local treatment planning rather than uncomplicated bone pain alone.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Assume denosumab alone is the main pain treatment and simply continue it without further workup.
Treat this as routine metastatic bone pain and delay urgent evaluation for the epidural lesion.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: Although she has metastatic breast cancer and bone pain while on denosumab, the presence of an epidural soft-tissue component changes the clinical context. This is no longer straightforward uncomplicated bone pain from osseous metastases, so the guideline statement about not using a bone-modifying agent alone for bone pain is not the key issue here; she needs urgent assessment for local control and neurologic risk rather than relying on the BMA for analgesia.
challenging_examples.rationale: The added epidural disease makes the vignette a near-miss for the bone-pain recommendation because the pain source is not limited to uncomplicated bone metastases.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering changing management based on HPV test results outside a clinical trial
selected_rules.execute: Do not alter management decisions based on HPV testing except in the context of a clinical trial
selected_rules.source_statement: Altering management decisions based on results from HPV testing is not recommended beyond the context of a clinical trial at this time.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Using HPV PCR testing alone
selected_rules.execute: Avoid qualitative HPV PCR assay detection alone
selected_rules.source_statement: Qualitative HPV PCR assay detection alone should be avoided.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient has oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma
selected_rules.execute: Routinely test the tumour for HPV status
selected_rules.source_statement: The tumours of all adult patients presenting with oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinomas should be routinely tested for HPV status.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient has metastatic squamous cell carcinoma to neck nodes from an unknown head and neck primary
selected_rules.execute: Routinely test the neck nodal tissue for HPV status
selected_rules.source_statement: It is recommended that the neck nodal tissue of patients with metastatic squamous cell carcinoma to neck nodes from an unknown head and neck primary be routinely tested for HPV status.

selected_rules.activation_condition: HPV testing is being performed for oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma
selected_rules.execute: Use p16 immunohistochemical staining as the initial test to determine HPV status
selected_rules.source_statement: It is recommended that HPV status in oropharyngeal SCC be initially determined using immunohistochemical (IHC) staining for p16.

selected_rules.activation_condition: p16 staining shows cytoplasmic and nuclear staining, is moderate to strong and diffuse, and is present in at least 70% of tumour cells
selected_rules.execute: Consider the p16 result positive
selected_rules.source_statement: IHC staining for p16 can be considered positive when the following three criteria are met: cytoplasmic and nuclear staining; staining is moderate to strong and diffuse; staining is present in at least 70% of tumour cells.

selected_rules.activation_condition: HPV testing is being done in the pathology laboratory
selected_rules.execute: Perform analysis on sections from paraffin blocks or unstained slides cut at 4 microns
selected_rules.source_statement: Analysis should be performed on sections from paraffin blocks or unstained slides cut at 4 microns.

selected_rules.activation_condition: HPV-status testing is being performed
selected_rules.execute: Carry out testing in licensed and accredited laboratories
selected_rules.source_statement: All testing should be carried out in licensed and accredited laboratories.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man presents with a 3-month history of progressive sore throat, dysphagia to solids, and a painless left neck mass. Biopsy of the tonsillar lesion shows squamous cell carcinoma, and the pathology team plans HPV-status testing to help characterize the tumor. The specimen is being processed in a licensed, accredited laboratory using paraffin-embedded tissue sections cut at 4 microns. Initial p16 immunohistochemical staining shows diffuse, moderate-to-strong cytoplasmic and nuclear staining in about 80% of tumour cells. The oncologist asks whether the HPV result should change the treatment plan immediately.
recommended_actions: Routinely test the tumour for HPV status because this is an adult with oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma.
Use p16 immunohistochemical staining as the initial test to determine HPV status.
Consider the p16 result positive because the staining is cytoplasmic and nuclear, moderate to strong and diffuse, and present in at least 70% of tumour cells.
Perform the testing on paraffin-block sections or unstained slides cut at 4 microns in a licensed and accredited laboratory.
Do not alter management decisions based on HPV testing outside the context of a clinical trial.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches adult oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma, so routine HPV testing is indicated. Because HPV testing is being performed for oropharyngeal SCC, p16 IHC is the recommended initial test. The described staining pattern meets the positivity criteria. The laboratory and specimen handling also match the testing recommendations. Although the HPV result is available, management should not be changed on the basis of HPV testing outside a clinical trial.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 61-year-old man presents with 4 months of progressive sore throat, mild dysphagia to solids, and a painless left neck mass. Biopsy of a right tonsillar lesion shows squamous cell carcinoma. Because the tumor is in the oropharynx, the team orders HPV-status testing to help characterize it. The specimen is sent to a licensed, accredited laboratory, and the pathologist performs p16 immunohistochemical staining on paraffin-embedded tissue sections cut at 4 microns. The stain shows diffuse cytoplasmic and nuclear positivity, moderate-to-strong in roughly 75% of tumor cells. The oncologist notes the result and asks whether the current treatment plan should be adjusted now that the tumor appears HPV-associated. Staging workup otherwise suggests a small primary lesion with one ipsilateral node.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This preserves adult oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma, routine HPV testing, appropriate specimen handling, and a p16 pattern that meets positivity criteria.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Reclassify the tumor as HPV-associated and de-escalate therapy based on the p16 result
Alter the definitive treatment plan immediately because the HPV test is positive
Use the HPV result to change management outside a research protocol
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The case is not guideline-concordant for changing management based on HPV testing. Even with a positive p16 result, HPV status should not be used to alter treatment decisions outside a clinical trial. The safer interpretation is to document HPV status for characterization, but continue standard management based on established staging and clinical factors.
challenging_examples.rationale: The vignette intentionally matches the diagnostic setting for HPV testing in oropharyngeal SCC, but the management question is the mismatch. The rule against altering management based on HPV results remains in force.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 54-year-old woman presents with a 2-month history of throat discomfort, odynophagia, and a left-sided neck node. Endoscopy finds a left tonsillar mass, and biopsy confirms squamous cell carcinoma. HPV-status testing is requested because this is an adult oropharyngeal primary. The tissue is processed in a licensed, accredited laboratory using paraffin block sections cut at 4 microns. p16 immunohistochemistry shows moderate, diffuse cytoplasmic and nuclear staining in about 68% of tumor cells, with focal areas of stronger staining but not the same extent throughout the specimen. The pathology report is pending final sign-out. The oncologist is considering whether the tumor should be treated as HPV-associated based on the preliminary stain result.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like routine HPV testing for adult oropharyngeal SCC, with the same laboratory setup and a near-threshold p16 pattern that may seem positive on first read.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Call the p16 stain positive and proceed with HPV-associated classification
Treat the tumor as HPV-driven based on the preliminary immunostain
Accept the near-diffuse p16 result as sufficient for HPV positivity
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant p16 positivity because the stain is present in only 68% of tumor cells, below the at-least-70% threshold. The safer interpretation is that the result is equivocal or negative by the stated criteria, and additional pathology review or confirmatory testing may be needed rather than labeling it HPV-positive.
challenging_examples.rationale: The case closely mirrors the source, but the percentage of tumor cells stained falls just short of the positivity cutoff. That subtle quantitative difference invalidates the recommendation to consider p16 positive.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering PET-CT during follow-up
selected_rules.execute: Do not use PET-CT for routine follow-up surveillance.
selected_rules.source_statement: Because their role has not been evaluated in a definitive manner, the following investigations are not advocated: o Positron emission tomography (PET) with computed tomography (PET-CT),

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering other imaging tests in an asymptomatic patient
selected_rules.execute: Do not use other imaging tests routinely in asymptomatic patients.
selected_rules.source_statement: Because their role has not been evaluated in a definitive manner, the following investigations are not advocated: o Other imaging or biomarker tests in asymptomatic patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering biomarker tests in an asymptomatic patient
selected_rules.execute: Do not use biomarker tests routinely in asymptomatic patients.
selected_rules.source_statement: Because their role has not been evaluated in a definitive manner, the following investigations are not advocated: o Other imaging or biomarker tests in asymptomatic patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient was treated with radiotherapy and is in follow-up beyond five years
selected_rules.execute: Do not perform cytological follow-up.
selected_rules.source_statement: Cytological follow-up is not recommended for patients who have been treated with radiotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is clinically disease-free and asymptomatic after potentially curative primary treatment for cervical cancer
selected_rules.execute: Use this follow-up guideline for surveillance after primary treatment; do not apply it to women treated for cervical precancer.
selected_rules.source_statement: This practice guideline applies to women who are clinically disease free and asymptomatic after receiving potentially curative primary treatment for cervical cancer. This guideline does not apply to the follow-up of women who have been treated for cervical precancer.

selected_rules.activation_condition: providing follow-up care after primary treatment for cervical cancer
selected_rules.execute: Have follow-up care conducted and coordinated by a physician experienced in surveillance of patients with cancer.
selected_rules.source_statement: Follow-up care after primary treatment should be conducted and coordinated by a physician experienced in the surveillance of patients with cancer.

selected_rules.activation_condition: during follow-up care after primary treatment
selected_rules.execute: Maintain continuity of care and discuss symptoms of recurrence with the patient to help detect recurrence early.
selected_rules.source_statement: Continuity of care and dialogue between the healthcare professional and patient about symptoms of recurrence may enhance and facilitate early cancer recurrence detection because the majority of women who develop a recurrence have symptoms and signs that occur outside scheduled follow-up visits.

selected_rules.activation_condition: follow-up within the first 5 years after treatment
selected_rules.execute: Schedule visits every 3 to 4 months during years 1 to 2, then every 6 to 12 months during years 3 to 5.
selected_rules.source_statement: A reasonable follow-up strategy involves visits at the following intervals: o every three to four months within the first two years, o every six to 12 months from years 3 to 5.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 52-year-old woman returns for surveillance 18 months after completing chemoradiation for stage IIB cervical cancer. She is clinically disease-free, reports no vaginal bleeding, pelvic pain, weight loss, or bowel/bladder changes, and her exam is unremarkable. Her oncologist is discussing whether to order a PET-CT because she feels anxious about recurrence, but the patient has no symptoms suggestive of relapse. The team reviews her follow-up plan and notes that she is still within the first two years after treatment. She asks how often she should be seen and what warning signs should prompt an earlier visit.
recommended_actions: Do not use PET-CT for routine follow-up surveillance.
Use this follow-up guideline for surveillance after primary treatment; do not apply it to women treated for cervical precancer.
Have follow-up care conducted and coordinated by a physician experienced in surveillance of patients with cancer.
Maintain continuity of care and discuss symptoms of recurrence with the patient to help detect recurrence early.
Schedule visits every 3 to 4 months during years 1 to 2, then every 6 to 12 months during years 3 to 5.
source_case.rationale: She is an asymptomatic, clinically disease-free patient after potentially curative primary treatment for cervical cancer, so the surveillance guideline applies. Because she is considering PET-CT during routine follow-up, that test is not recommended. Follow-up should be coordinated by a clinician experienced in cancer surveillance, with ongoing communication about recurrence symptoms. Since she is within the first two years after treatment, visits every 3 to 4 months are appropriate.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 52-year-old woman returns for surveillance 18 months after completing chemoradiation for stage IIB cervical cancer. She remains clinically disease-free and denies vaginal bleeding, pelvic pain, weight loss, or bowel/bladder changes. On exam, there is no evidence of recurrence. Her oncologist is considering a PET-CT because the patient is anxious about relapse, but the team notes that she also had a recent CT abdomen/pelvis ordered last month by her primary care clinician for unrelated flank pain, which was unrevealing. She asks how often she should be seen over the next few years and what symptoms should prompt an earlier visit.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Same disease, same post-treatment surveillance setting, same anxiety-driven imaging question, and still within the first two years after curative therapy; the only change is a recent unrelated CT that can distract from the guideline issue.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Order a PET-CT now to reassure the patient about recurrence.
Use the prior CT result to justify additional routine imaging surveillance.
Set up imaging-based surveillance visits rather than symptom-directed follow-up.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This remains an asymptomatic surveillance visit after curative treatment for cervical cancer, so routine PET-CT is not guideline-concordant. The recent unrelated CT does not create an indication for PET-CT. Safer interpretation: continue standard clinical follow-up with symptom review and pelvic examination at the recommended intervals.
challenging_examples.rationale: The patient fits the surveillance population, but the guideline discourages routine PET-CT in asymptomatic follow-up. A recent negative CT does not convert this into an imaging-indicated scenario.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 38-year-old woman is seen 14 months after completing definitive chemoradiation for microinvasive cervical adenocarcinoma that had been treated surgically as cervical precancer. She feels well and reports no bleeding, pelvic pain, or constitutional symptoms. Her exam is normal, and her oncologist is reviewing her follow-up plan because she is worried about recurrence. She says she has been told to return every few months and asks whether a PET-CT should be added now that she is nearing the second year after treatment.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case closely mirrors post-treatment cervical cancer surveillance, including the same symptom-free follow-up timing and PET-CT discussion, but the original diagnosis was managed as precancer/microinvasive disease rather than invasive cervical cancer.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Apply the cervical cancer surveillance schedule unchanged.
Arrange ongoing oncology surveillance visits every 3 to 4 months and discuss recurrence symptoms.
Order PET-CT because she is still in the early follow-up window after treatment.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not a straightforward cervical cancer surveillance case under the cited guideline because the patient was treated for cervical precancer/microinvasive disease rather than clearly for invasive cervical cancer. The cervical cancer follow-up schedule should not be applied automatically; follow-up should be based on the specific pathology and treatment history.
challenging_examples.rationale: The guideline applies to women clinically disease-free after potentially curative primary treatment for cervical cancer and explicitly does not apply to women treated for cervical precancer. That distinction makes the standard cervical cancer surveillance recommendation inapplicable here.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Biopsy-naive patient at elevated risk of clinically significant prostate cancer and a candidate for curative management with suspected clinically localized prostate cancer
selected_rules.execute: Perform multiparametric MRI prior to biopsy
selected_rules.source_statement: MPMRI is recommended prior to biopsy in patients who are candidates for curative management with suspected clinically localized prostate cancer.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Biopsy-naive patient has a positive multiparametric MRI
selected_rules.execute: Perform multiparametric MRI-targeted biopsy together with transrectal ultrasound systematic biopsy
selected_rules.source_statement: If the MPMRI is positive, MPMRI-TB and TRUS-SB should be performed together to maximize detection of CSPCa.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient had a prior negative transrectal ultrasound systematic biopsy and is at high risk of clinically significant prostate cancer with curative management being considered
selected_rules.execute: Perform multiparametric MRI
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients who had a prior negative TRUS-SB and demonstrate a high risk of having CSPCa in whom curative management is being considered: MPMRI should be performed,

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient had a prior negative transrectal ultrasound systematic biopsy and multiparametric MRI is positive
selected_rules.execute: Perform targeted biopsy
selected_rules.source_statement: If the MPMRI is positive, targeted biopsy should be performed.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Biopsy-naive patient has a negative multiparametric MRI
selected_rules.execute: Consider forgoing biopsy after discussing risks and benefits with the patient and arranging ongoing follow-up
selected_rules.source_statement: If the MPMRI is negative, consider forgoing any biopsy after discussion of the risks and benefits with the patient as part of shared decision making and ongoing followup.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Biopsy-naive patient has a negative multiparametric MRI
selected_rules.execute: Inform the patient that a negative multiparametric MRI may miss clinically significant prostate cancer
selected_rules.source_statement: Between 8% and 24% of patients with CSPCa may be missed by a negative MPMRI.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering multiparametric MRI for a biopsy-naive patient
selected_rules.execute: Use multiparametric MRI only if high-quality interpretation is available and operators have experience performing targeted biopsies
selected_rules.source_statement: MPMRI should only be performed if there is availability of high-quality MPMRI interpretation and operators with experience performing targeted biopsies (see Recommendation 3).

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering multiparametric MRI for a biopsy-naive patient
selected_rules.execute: Use multiparametric MRI only when there is intent for curative management if biopsy is positive
selected_rules.source_statement: Due to the limited availability, MPMRI is recommended only for patients where there is intent of curative management should the biopsy be positive for CSPCa.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man presents with a PSA of 11.8 ng/mL and an abnormal digital rectal exam showing mild left-sided induration. He has never had a prostate biopsy. He is otherwise healthy, has a life expectancy well beyond 10 years, and says he would want curative treatment if clinically significant prostate cancer is found. His urologist considers him at elevated risk for clinically significant, clinically localized prostate cancer and is planning diagnostic workup before biopsy.
recommended_actions: Perform multiparametric MRI prior to biopsy.
source_case.rationale: This biopsy-naive patient has elevated risk of clinically significant prostate cancer and is a candidate for curative management with suspected clinically localized disease, which matches the indication for pre-biopsy multiparametric MRI. No additional rule is triggered because MRI results are not yet known.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man presents with a PSA of 11.8 ng/mL and a mildly abnormal digital rectal exam showing left-sided induration. He has never had a prostate biopsy and remains otherwise healthy with an estimated life expectancy well beyond 10 years. He says he would want curative treatment if clinically significant prostate cancer is found. His urologist is considering diagnostic workup before biopsy, but the local center has no experienced reader available for prostate MRI interpretation this month, and targeted biopsy expertise is not currently available. The patient asks whether imaging can still be arranged quickly before tissue sampling.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case preserves the biopsy-naive, elevated-risk, curative-intent initial evaluation, but adds limited MRI interpretation and targeted biopsy expertise, which makes pre-biopsy multiparametric MRI no longer guideline-concordant.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Order multiparametric MRI before biopsy anyway and proceed based on the result
Schedule prostate MRI now because the patient appears to be an excellent candidate for pre-biopsy imaging
Use MRI to guide the initial biopsy despite the lack of local expertise
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for pre-biopsy MRI because the recommendation assumes high-quality MRI interpretation and operators experienced with targeted biopsy. In this setting, the safer interpretation is to avoid relying on MRI locally and proceed with standard diagnostic planning or refer to a center with appropriate expertise if imaging is still desired.
challenging_examples.rationale: The source recommendation applies only when high-quality MPMRI interpretation and experienced targeted biopsy operators are available. That condition is missing here.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man presents with a PSA of 11.8 ng/mL and an abnormal digital rectal exam with mild left-sided induration. He has never had a prostate biopsy. He is otherwise healthy and would want curative treatment if clinically significant prostate cancer is found. His urologist is considering diagnostic workup before biopsy, but the patient states he would only pursue treatment if the cancer were metastatic enough to explain symptoms and says he is not interested in curative therapy for localized disease. The team is debating whether to obtain prostate MRI before the first biopsy.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The clinical risk profile and biopsy-naive status remain the same, but the patient no longer has intent for curative management if cancer is found, which removes the indication for pre-biopsy MRI.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Perform multiparametric MRI prior to biopsy because his PSA and exam are concerning
Proceed with prostate MRI to better localize a likely cancer before sampling
Arrange MRI before biopsy since he is still an elevated-risk patient
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This case is not guideline-concordant for pre-biopsy multiparametric MRI because the recommendation is limited to patients in whom there is intent for curative management if biopsy is positive. Since he would not pursue curative treatment for localized disease, MRI is not clearly indicated on this basis and biopsy planning should follow the stated treatment goals.
challenging_examples.rationale: The source recommendation requires curative intent if biopsy is positive; that criterion is absent here.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult or paediatric patient after allogeneic transplant with steroid-dependent or steroid-refractory acute GVHD
selected_rules.execute: Consider extracorporeal photopheresis (ECP) as therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: ECP is an acceptable therapy for the treatment of steroid-dependent / refractory acute GVHD in adult and paediatric patients

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult or paediatric patient after allogeneic transplant with steroid-dependent or steroid-refractory chronic GVHD
selected_rules.execute: Use extracorporeal photopheresis (ECP) as therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: ECP is an effective therapy for the treatment of steroid-dependent / refractory chronic GVHD in adult and paediatric patients

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has steroid-refractory acute GVHD with primarily refractory skin involvement
selected_rules.execute: Consider extracorporeal photopheresis (ECP) treatment
selected_rules.source_statement: patients with primarily refractory skin GVHD should be considered for ECP treatment

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has steroid-refractory GVHD and meets Ontario eligibility criteria
selected_rules.execute: Note that ECP is a covered therapy in Ontario
selected_rules.source_statement: ECP is currently a covered therapy in Ontario for patients with steroid refractory GVHD who meet certain eligibility criteria

source_case.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old woman is 74 days after matched unrelated donor allogeneic stem cell transplant for acute myeloid leukemia. She developed diffuse erythematous rash, watery diarrhea, and mild transaminitis consistent with acute GVHD. Despite 10 days of high-dose prednisone, her diarrhea improved only slightly and the rash persists, so the transplant team considers her acute GVHD steroid-refractory. She remains hemodynamically stable and has no active infection. Because the GVHD is not responding adequately to steroids, the team is discussing next-line therapy.
recommended_actions: Consider extracorporeal photopheresis (ECP) as therapy for steroid-dependent or steroid-refractory acute GVHD.
source_case.rationale: This vignette describes an adult after allogeneic transplant with steroid-refractory acute GVHD, which directly matches R001. No additional rules were triggered because chronic GVHD is not present, skin-predominant refractory disease is not the primary focus, and Ontario coverage criteria were not specified.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old woman is 74 days after matched unrelated donor allogeneic stem cell transplant for acute myeloid leukemia. She developed diffuse erythematous rash and mild transaminitis consistent with acute GVHD, and the transplant team started high-dose prednisone. After 10 days, the rash is still active, so the team considers the GVHD steroid-refractory. She remains hemodynamically stable and has no active infection, but her watery diarrhea has resolved and repeat endoscopy shows no ongoing lower-GI involvement. The team is discussing next-line therapy for the persistent skin and liver findings.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Same post-transplant timeframe, same steroid-refractory acute GVHD framing, and the same ECP discussion, but the current disease is no longer the broad steroid-refractory acute GVHD picture from the source case.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start extracorporeal photopheresis for steroid-refractory acute GVHD
Refer for ECP as next-line therapy after inadequate prednisone response
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not a clean match to the original recommendation because the current presentation is limited to residual skin and mild liver involvement after GI symptoms have resolved, making the overall scenario less like the broad steroid-refractory acute GVHD case that triggered the rule. A safer interpretation is to reassess organ-specific severity and overall GVHD status before selecting a next-line strategy.
challenging_examples.rationale: The case preserves the transplant context and steroid exposure but removes the active diffuse acute GVHD picture that supported the original rule. The ongoing problem is now narrower and less clearly the intended target population for the recommendation.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old woman is 74 days after matched unrelated donor allogeneic stem cell transplant for acute myeloid leukemia. She developed diffuse erythematous rash, watery diarrhea, and mild transaminitis felt to be acute GVHD. After 10 days of high-dose prednisone, the diarrhea improved only slightly and the rash persists, so the transplant team is considering her steroid-refractory. She is hemodynamically stable and has no active infection. However, the treating team now reports that the syndrome has been classified as overlap GVHD with early chronic features rather than isolated acute GVHD, and they are planning a chronic GVHD-directed regimen.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The transplant timing, steroid-refractory symptoms, and consideration of ECP are all preserved, but the GVHD phenotype has shifted from acute to overlap/early chronic disease.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with extracorporeal photopheresis for steroid-refractory acute GVHD
Treat this as acute GVHD needing ECP escalation
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is no longer guideline-concordant for the acute GVHD rule because the syndrome has been reclassified as overlap with early chronic features rather than isolated acute GVHD. The safer interpretation is to follow the chronic/overlap GVHD pathway rather than apply the acute GVHD recommendation directly.
challenging_examples.rationale: The key mismatch is the disease category. The source recommendation applies to steroid-dependent or steroid-refractory acute GVHD, while this vignette now describes overlap/early chronic GVHD, which changes the applicable guideline context.

selected_rules.activation_condition: tumour pathology creates doubt
selected_rules.execute: Have the pathology reviewed by an expert
selected_rules.source_statement: A tumour pathology causing doubt should be reviewed by an expert.

selected_rules.activation_condition: restaging of a sub-optimally staged patient reveals more advanced disease
selected_rules.execute: Use chemotherapy as the preferred treatment option
selected_rules.source_statement: If the restaging of a sub-optimally staged patient reveals a more advanced disease, chemotherapy is the preferred treatment option.

selected_rules.activation_condition: lymph node sampling was not conducted as part of staging surgery
selected_rules.execute: Favor adjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: A subgroup analysis of one randomized controlled trial demonstrated that if lymph node sampling is not conducted as part of the staging surgery then adjuvant chemotherapy is favoured in terms of overall survival.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has undergone optimal surgical staging including pelvic and para-aortic lymph node sampling and has stage I disease
selected_rules.execute: Consider adjuvant platinum-based chemotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Women who have undergone optimal surgical staging, including pelvic and para-aortic lymph node sampling, and have stage I disease may or may not benefit from adjuvant platinum-based chemotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has not undergone optimal surgical staging
selected_rules.execute: Offer re-operation to optimally define tumour stage and then give adjuvant therapy based on findings
selected_rules.source_statement: Women who have not undergone optimal surgical staging can be offered two options. The first option is that they undergo re-operation to optimally define the tumour stage and then be offered adjuvant therapy based on the findings.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has not undergone optimal surgical staging
selected_rules.execute: Offer platinum-based chemotherapy to decrease recurrence risk and improve survival
selected_rules.source_statement: Women who have not undergone optimal surgical staging can be offered two options. The other option is that they be offered platinum-based chemotherapy to decrease the risk of recurrence and improve survival.

selected_rules.activation_condition: reoperation confirms stage I disease after suboptimal staging
selected_rules.execute: Base the adjuvant chemotherapy decision on discussion with the patient about potential benefits and risks
selected_rules.source_statement: The treatment decision must be based on a discussion with the patient about potential benefits and risks.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering adjuvant pelvic radiation
selected_rules.execute: Do not make a recommendation because evidence is insufficient
selected_rules.source_statement: There is insufficient evidence to make a recommendation on the role of adjuvant pelvic radiation, whole abdominal-pelvic radiotherapy, or intraperitoneal radioactive chromic phosphate.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman underwent surgery for a 4.2 cm epithelial ovarian tumor found incidentally during evaluation for bloating. The operative report notes hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, but no pelvic or para-aortic lymph node sampling was performed, so the staging was suboptimal. Final pathology suggests stage I disease, but the report also states that the tumor morphology is somewhat unusual and the pathologist is not fully confident about the exact histologic subtype. She is otherwise recovering well and asks whether she needs more treatment now or whether additional surgery would help clarify the stage.
recommended_actions: Have the pathology reviewed by an expert.
Offer re-operation to optimally define the tumour stage and then give adjuvant therapy based on findings.
Offer platinum-based chemotherapy to decrease recurrence risk and improve survival.
source_case.rationale: The pathology report explicitly creates doubt about the tumour diagnosis, which triggers expert review. The patient has not undergone optimal surgical staging because lymph node sampling was omitted, which triggers the options of re-operation for definitive staging or platinum-based chemotherapy. These recommendations are directly supported by the selected rules.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman is recovering after surgery for a 4.2 cm epithelial ovarian tumor that was found incidentally during evaluation for bloating. The operative report notes hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, but no pelvic or para-aortic lymph node sampling was performed, so the staging was incomplete. Final pathology is otherwise straightforward and agrees with a stage I epithelial ovarian carcinoma, with no comment suggesting uncertainty about the histologic subtype. She feels well overall and asks whether she needs more treatment now or whether additional surgery would help clarify the stage.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case preserves the same age, tumor size, surgery, and incomplete staging, but removes the pathology uncertainty that would trigger expert review.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Send the specimen for expert pathology review before making any further decisions.
Treat the uncertain histology as a reason to delay staging and adjuvant planning.
Recommend pathology re-review to clarify the diagnosis despite the report being clear.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This case is not guideline-concordant for expert pathology review because there is no longer doubt about the tumor pathology. The more appropriate interpretation is a straightforward incompletely staged stage I epithelial ovarian cancer, so management should focus on staging-related options rather than pathology uncertainty.
challenging_examples.rationale: Rule R001 requires pathology that creates doubt. Here the histology is stated to be clear, so that trigger is absent even though the staging remains suboptimal.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman underwent surgery for a 4.2 cm epithelial ovarian tumor discovered during evaluation for bloating. The operative report notes hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, but no pelvic or para-aortic lymph node sampling was performed. On final pathology, the surgeon and pathologist agree she has stage I disease, but the resection specimen is described as completely adequate and the histologic subtype is confidently established. She has recovered well, and her gynecologic oncologist is discussing next steps, including whether she should return to the operating room or start treatment now.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The overall scenario matches an incompletely staged stage I ovarian cancer case, but the near-miss is that restaging would not reveal more advanced disease because the patient is still considered stage I on current assessment.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed directly with chemotherapy because the missing lymph node sampling makes occult advanced disease likely.
Offer re-operation to restage and then give adjuvant therapy based on the findings.
Assume the suboptimal staging means chemotherapy is the preferred treatment option without further discussion.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not a restaging scenario that reveals more advanced disease, so the specific rule favoring chemotherapy after restaging does not apply. The safer interpretation is that she remains incompletely staged stage I disease, where the relevant choices are re-operation for optimal staging or platinum-based chemotherapy after discussion of risks and benefits.
challenging_examples.rationale: Rule R002 applies only when restaging of a sub-optimally staged patient reveals more advanced disease. In this vignette, no more advanced disease has been identified; the case remains an initial post-op staging dilemma.

selected_rules.activation_condition: primary care or community setting and need to improve breast or cervical cancer screening access
selected_rules.execute: Use NPs in alternate provider roles to improve access to breast and cervical cancer screening.
selected_rules.source_statement: In primary care and community-based settings, NPs working in alternate provider roles can be utilized to improve access to breast and cervical cancer screening.

selected_rules.activation_condition: need for Pap smear screening
selected_rules.execute: Allow NPs to perform Pap smears as alternate providers to physicians.
selected_rules.source_statement: Two studies demonstrated that the quality of Papanicolaou (Pap) smears conducted by NPs was not detectably different and, in some cases, was superior to physicians (23,24).

selected_rules.activation_condition: need for esophagoscopy, flexible sigmoidoscopy, or colonoscopy for cancer screening
selected_rules.execute: Use NPs as alternate providers to physicians for esophagoscopy, flexible sigmoidoscopy, and colonoscopy.
selected_rules.source_statement: As alternate providers to physicians, NPs can provide safe and effective care in performing esophagoscopy, flexible sigmoidoscopy, and colonoscopy for cancer screening.

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman with cervical dysplasia needing colposcopy-guided biopsy to diagnose cervical cancer
selected_rules.execute: Use NPs as alternate providers to physicians for colposcopy-guided biopsies.
selected_rules.source_statement: For women with cervical dysplasia, NPs are an appropriate alternate provider to physicians in performing colposcopy-guided biopsies to diagnose cervical cancer.

selected_rules.activation_condition: newly diagnosed cancer patient receiving surgery or radiation therapy and CNS-led alternate model is being considered
selected_rules.execute: Use CNS-led care as an appropriate alternate model to physician care.
selected_rules.source_statement: Clinical nurse specialist (CNS)-led care is an appropriate alternate model to care provided by physicians, particularly for newly diagnosed patients undergoing surgery or radiation therapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: new diagnosis of cancer and post cancer surgery or receiving chemotherapy or radiation treatment
selected_rules.execute: Consider adding complementary CNS care to usual care to improve psychological and mental well-being and possibly survival.
selected_rules.source_statement: The addition of complementary CNS care to usual care may improve psychological and mental well-being and survival for patients with a new diagnosis of cancer who are post cancer surgery or receiving chemotherapy or radiation treatment.

selected_rules.activation_condition: breast or colorectal cancer follow-up and telephone follow-up is being considered
selected_rules.execute: Consider CNS- or NP-delivered telephone follow-up as a safe and acceptable alternate model to outpatient physician follow-up.
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with breast and colorectal cancer, CNS-or NP-delivered telephone follow-up may provide a safe and acceptable alternate model to outpatient clinic follow-up care provided mostly by physicians.

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman following breast cancer treatment with menopausal symptoms
selected_rules.execute: Consider a complementary NP-led comprehensive assessment and intervention program to reduce menopausal symptoms.
selected_rules.source_statement: The addition of a complementary and comprehensive assessment and intervention program provided by a NP may be effective for reducing menopausal symptoms in women following treatment for breast cancer.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old woman presents to a community health clinic for an overdue cervical cancer screening. She recently moved to the area, does not have an established primary care physician, and says the clinic’s schedule is full for the next two months. She is sexually active, has no prior abnormal Pap results, and is due for routine screening. She asks whether the visit can be completed today because taking time off work is difficult and she is concerned about delaying care. The clinic has an experienced nurse practitioner available in an alternate provider role and routinely serves patients with limited access to screening services.
recommended_actions: Use NPs in alternate provider roles to improve access to breast and cervical cancer screening.
Allow NPs to perform Pap smears as alternate providers to physicians.
source_case.rationale: This vignette describes a primary care/community-based setting with limited access to cervical cancer screening, triggering the access-focused recommendation for NP alternate provider roles. It also specifically involves need for Pap smear screening, which supports NP performance of Pap smears as an alternate provider model.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old woman presents to a community health clinic for an overdue cervical cancer screening. She recently moved to the area, does not have an established primary care physician, and says the clinic’s schedule is full for the next two months. She is sexually active, has no prior abnormal Pap results, and is due for routine screening. She asks whether the visit can be completed today because taking time off work is difficult and she is concerned about delaying care. The clinic has an experienced nurse practitioner available in an alternate provider role and routinely serves patients with limited access to screening services. However, when the chart is reviewed, she is found to be only 4 months postpartum after a routine cervical screening earlier this year, so she is not actually due for repeat Pap testing today.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Community clinic, limited access, and an available NP alternate provider are all present, but the patient is not currently due for Pap screening because she had a recent normal test.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Schedule the nurse practitioner to perform a Pap smear during this visit
Use the NP alternate-provider clinic pathway to complete cervical cancer screening today
Treat this as an access-limited overdue screening visit and proceed with routine Pap testing
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the screening recommendation because the patient is not actually due for cervical cancer screening; she had a recent screening within the interval. The safer interpretation is to verify the prior test date and defer repeat screening until the recommended interval has elapsed.
challenging_examples.rationale: The recommendation applies to improving access to breast or cervical cancer screening and to performing Pap smears when screening is indicated. Here, the key mismatch is that routine Pap screening is not currently indicated, so the alternate-provider screening recommendation should not be activated.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 46-year-old woman presents to a community clinic after a new abnormal cervical screening result from an outside facility. She has no established gynecologist and the next specialist appointment is several weeks away. The clinic has a nurse practitioner with advanced procedural experience, and the patient asks whether the biopsy can be done locally to avoid delay. Review of records shows low-grade cervical dysplasia on cytology, but her colposcopy last month was completely normal and no biopsy target was identified; the referring clinician documented that there is no current indication for biopsy unless a new lesion appears. She is anxious about waiting and wants the abnormal result addressed promptly.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still involves cervical dysplasia, limited specialist access, and a request for biopsy in a clinic with an experienced NP, but there is no visible lesion or current biopsy target on colposcopy.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Arrange a nurse practitioner–performed colposcopy-guided biopsy
Send her for urgent biopsy in the NP-led alternate provider pathway
Proceed with cervical biopsy to resolve the abnormal cytology promptly
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for colposcopy-guided biopsy because there is no current biopsy indication or target lesion. The safer interpretation is that the patient needs appropriate surveillance or specialist follow-up based on the dysplasia finding, not an immediate biopsy procedure by an alternate provider.
challenging_examples.rationale: The rule is limited to women with cervical dysplasia needing colposcopy-guided biopsy to diagnose cervical cancer. In this vignette, colposcopy is normal and there is no lesion to biopsy, so the procedural recommendation does not apply.

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman has DCIS and is a candidate for breast-conserving surgery
selected_rules.execute: offer choice of breast-conserving surgery or total mastectomy
selected_rules.source_statement: Women with DCIS of the breast who are candidates for breast-conserving surgery should be offered the choice of breast-conserving surgery or total mastectomy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: breast-conserving surgery is performed for DCIS
selected_rules.execute: remove all mammographically suspicious calcifications and ensure microscopically clear margins of DCIS
selected_rules.source_statement: When breast-conserving surgery is performed, all mammographically suspicious calcifications should be removed and margins should be microscopically clear of DCIS.

selected_rules.activation_condition: DCIS is large enough that breast-conserving surgery would leave an unacceptable cosmetic result
selected_rules.execute: recommend mastectomy with option of reconstruction
selected_rules.source_statement: Mastectomy, with the option of reconstruction, is recommended for those women who have an area of DCIS large enough that breast-conserving surgery would leave them with an unacceptable cosmetic result.

selected_rules.activation_condition: DCIS treated with breast-conserving surgery and whole-breast irradiation
selected_rules.execute: aim for at least 2 mm margin to reduce local recurrence risk
selected_rules.source_statement: A 2016 joint guideline from the Society of Surgical Oncology (SSO), American Society of Radiation Oncology (ASTRO), and American Society of Clinical Oncology (ASCO) recommends margins of at least 2 mm to reduce the risk of local recurrence.

selected_rules.activation_condition: DCIS treated with breast-conserving surgery and whole-breast irradiation and margin is positive (ink on DCIS)
selected_rules.execute: recognize positive margin as associated with significant risk of ipsilateral breast tumour recurrence
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with DCIS treated with breast-conserving surgery and whole-breast irradiation, a positive margin (ink on DCIS) is associated with a significant risk of ipsilateral breast tumour recurrence (IBTR).

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman has DCIS and has undergone breast-conserving surgery
selected_rules.execute: offer adjuvant breast irradiation
selected_rules.source_statement: Women with DCIS who have undergone breast-conserving surgery should be offered adjuvant breast irradiation.

selected_rules.activation_condition: DCIS treated with breast-conserving surgery
selected_rules.execute: consider adjuvant radiotherapy because it reduces invasive and non-invasive ipsilateral recurrence
selected_rules.source_statement: In each, the risk of invasive and non-invasive ipsilateral recurrence was reduced with adjuvant radiotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman prefers to maximize local control
selected_rules.execute: consider mastectomy with option for reconstruction
selected_rules.source_statement: Mastectomy with the option for reconstruction remains an acceptable choice for women preferring to maximize local control.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman is diagnosed with ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) after a screening mammogram showed clustered microcalcifications in the upper outer quadrant of the left breast. Core biopsy confirms intermediate-grade DCIS. Breast imaging shows a 2.1 cm localized area without multifocal disease, and the breast surgeon feels she is a good candidate for breast-conserving surgery. The patient is anxious about recurrence but strongly prefers to preserve her breast if oncologically appropriate. She asks about treatment options and whether radiation would still be needed after surgery.
recommended_actions: Offer her the choice of breast-conserving surgery or total mastectomy.
After breast-conserving surgery, offer adjuvant breast irradiation.
Consider adjuvant radiotherapy because it reduces invasive and non-invasive ipsilateral recurrence.
source_case.rationale: She has DCIS and is a candidate for breast-conserving surgery, which triggers the recommendation to offer a choice between breast-conserving surgery and total mastectomy. Because she undergoes breast-conserving surgery, she should be offered adjuvant breast irradiation, and adjuvant radiotherapy should be considered since it reduces both invasive and non-invasive ipsilateral recurrence.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman is diagnosed with ductal carcinoma in situ after a screening mammogram showed clustered microcalcifications in the upper outer quadrant of the left breast. Core biopsy confirms intermediate-grade DCIS. Breast imaging shows a 2.1 cm localized area without multifocal disease, and the breast surgeon initially thought she would be a reasonable candidate for breast-conserving surgery. On preoperative MRI, however, there are additional suspicious calcifications extending over a much larger segment of the same breast, and the surgeon is concerned that a lumpectomy would leave a poor cosmetic result. The patient remains anxious about recurrence and asks whether she can still preserve the breast if possible.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still involves intermediate-grade DCIS detected on screening with a similar patient preference for breast preservation, but the extent of disease is now larger and more diffuse on MRI.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer breast-conserving surgery as the preferred option and plan adjuvant radiation afterward.
Reassure her that lumpectomy is still appropriate because the initial lesion was only 2.1 cm.
Proceed with breast-conserving surgery without revisiting mastectomy as an option.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is no longer guideline-concordant for the initial breast-conserving surgery choice because the updated imaging suggests DCIS is extensive enough that breast-conserving surgery would likely leave an unacceptable cosmetic result. The safer interpretation is that mastectomy, with reconstruction as an option, should be discussed instead of presenting breast conservation as the default choice.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 requires DCIS with candidacy for breast-conserving surgery; the more extensive calcifications make that candidacy questionable. R003 applies when the DCIS is large enough that breast-conserving surgery would leave an unacceptable cosmetic result, which is the key mismatch here.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman is diagnosed with ductal carcinoma in situ after a screening mammogram showed clustered microcalcifications in the upper outer quadrant of the left breast. Core biopsy confirms intermediate-grade DCIS. She undergoes breast-conserving surgery, and the operative note states that the visible lesion was removed. Final pathology, however, shows a close anterior margin with 1 mm of DCIS clearance, and several of the original suspicious calcifications remain in the breast on the postoperative mammogram. She asks whether she should now proceed directly to radiation and avoid another operation.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The patient still has DCIS treated with breast-conserving surgery, but the postoperative details include residual suspicious calcifications and a margin that is close rather than clearly adequate.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer adjuvant breast irradiation without addressing the residual calcifications.
State that a 1 mm margin is acceptable and proceed with whole-breast irradiation.
Treat the positive/close margin as routine and omit further surgical review.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for assuming complete breast-conserving management because mammographically suspicious calcifications were not fully removed and the margin is only 1 mm. The safer interpretation is that additional surgical assessment is needed to achieve clear margins and remove remaining suspicious calcifications before relying on radiation as the next step.
challenging_examples.rationale: R002 requires that all mammographically suspicious calcifications be removed and margins be microscopically clear of DCIS when breast-conserving surgery is performed. R004’s 2 mm margin target is not met, and R005 is relevant because close/positive margins with whole-breast irradiation carry higher local recurrence risk.

selected_rules.activation_condition: clinical stage I-III colorectal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Do not use routine PET for diagnosis or staging.
selected_rules.source_statement: The routine use of PET is not recommended for the diagnosis or staging of clinical Stage I-III colorectal cancers.

selected_rules.activation_condition: locally advanced rectal cancer before and after preoperative chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Do not routinely use PET to measure treatment response.
selected_rules.source_statement: The routine use of PET is not recommended for the measurement of treatment response in locally advanced rectal cancer before and after preoperative chemotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: short-term response assessment after chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Do not use PET as a substitute for CT.
selected_rules.source_statement: The study suggested that PET should not be used as a substitute for CT for short-term response and should not be used as a surrogate for long-term clinical endpoints.

selected_rules.activation_condition: long-term clinical endpoint assessment such as time to progression or overall survival
selected_rules.execute: Do not use PET as a surrogate for long-term clinical endpoints.
selected_rules.source_statement: The study suggested that PET should not be used as a substitute for CT for short-term response and should not be used as a surrogate for long-term clinical endpoints.

selected_rules.activation_condition: one-time PET after preoperative therapy to predict complete pathologic response
selected_rules.execute: Do not rely on a single post-therapy PET for predicting complete pathologic response.
selected_rules.source_statement: However, one-time PET after preoperative therapy poorly predicted complete pathologic response after preoperative therapy in Capirci et al (13) and Kalff et al (14) and poorly predicted posttherapy staging in Capirci et al (12).

selected_rules.activation_condition: after chemotherapy when deciding on liver resection
selected_rules.execute: Do not use PET in decision making for liver resection.
selected_rules.source_statement: The study showed that ultrasonography also guides surgical decision during intraoperative assessment and suggested PET after chemotherapy should not be used in decision making for liver resection.

selected_rules.activation_condition: single PET/CT after preoperative therapy to predict complete pathologic response
selected_rules.execute: Do not rely on a single PET/CT after therapy for predicting complete pathologic response.
selected_rules.source_statement: Kristiansen et al ( 17) suggested a single PET/CT after preoperative therapy poorly predicted complete pathologic response.

selected_rules.activation_condition: routine surveillance after curative surgery in patients at high risk for recurrence
selected_rules.execute: Do not use PET routinely for surveillance.
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is not recommended for routine surveillance in patients with colorectal cancer treated with curative surgery at high risk for recurrence.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 64-year-old man with newly diagnosed adenocarcinoma of the sigmoid colon undergoes colonoscopy after several months of intermittent hematochezia and iron-deficiency anemia. Biopsy confirms colorectal cancer, and contrast-enhanced CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis shows a localized cT3N1 lesion without definite distant metastases. His oncology team discusses staging before surgery, and the patient asks whether a PET scan would better define the extent of disease. He has no prior treatment and no symptoms suggesting recurrence or metastatic complications. The team plans standard surgical and oncologic management based on the available anatomic imaging and pathology.
recommended_actions: Do not use routine PET for diagnosis or staging of this clinical stage I-III colorectal cancer.
source_case.rationale: This vignette describes a patient with clinical stage I-III colorectal cancer undergoing initial staging. Rule R001 is triggered because routine PET is not recommended for diagnosis or staging in this setting. No other rule applies because the patient is not being assessed after chemotherapy, after preoperative therapy, or in surveillance after curative surgery.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 64-year-old man with newly diagnosed adenocarcinoma of the sigmoid colon undergoes colonoscopy after several months of intermittent hematochezia and iron-deficiency anemia. Biopsy confirms colorectal cancer, and contrast-enhanced CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis shows a localized cT3N1 lesion without definite distant metastases. His oncology team discusses staging before surgery, and the patient asks whether a PET scan would better define the extent of disease. He has no prior treatment and no symptoms suggesting metastatic complications. A liver lesion seen on CT is characterized by multiphasic MRI as a benign hemangioma, and the team plans standard surgical and oncologic management based on the available anatomic imaging and pathology.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Very similar initial staging presentation with stage I-III colorectal cancer, but the workup already includes definitive anatomic imaging showing no unresolved metastatic concern.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Order a routine PET scan for initial staging to look for occult metastases.
Use PET to better define the extent of disease before surgery.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This remains a newly diagnosed clinical stage I-III colorectal cancer case, but there is no unresolved staging question after CT and MRI clarification. Routine PET is still not guideline-concordant for staging; the safer interpretation is to proceed with standard anatomic staging-based management without PET.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 applies to routine PET for diagnosis or staging in clinical stage I-III colorectal cancer. The vignette preserves that setting but removes the practical justification for PET by resolving the incidental liver finding with MRI, making PET unnecessary and not guideline-concordant.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 61-year-old woman with locally advanced rectal adenocarcinoma receives neoadjuvant chemoradiation followed by preoperative FOLFOX. Restaging CT shows no definite metastatic disease, and MRI demonstrates marked tumor shrinkage with residual fibrotic thickening of the rectal wall. The team is reviewing response before surgery, and a PET/CT has already been performed as part of an investigational protocol at an outside center. The patient asks whether the scan means she can avoid surgery if there is no residual uptake. Her CEA has decreased but remains slightly above normal, and the surgeons are still planning total mesorectal excision.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case closely mirrors a post-preoperative-therapy rectal cancer response assessment, but the PET/CT was obtained in a trial setting rather than being used routinely for management.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Use the PET/CT to determine whether the patient has had an adequate treatment response.
Rely on absence of FDG uptake to decide against surgery.
Treat the PET result as a routine response assessment tool.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: Although this is locally advanced rectal cancer before and after preoperative therapy, the PET/CT was not ordered for routine response assessment and should not drive management decisions. A single PET result is not guideline-concordant for measuring treatment response; standard response evaluation should rely on clinical assessment, MRI, and planned surgical management.
challenging_examples.rationale: R002 prohibits routine PET for measuring treatment response in locally advanced rectal cancer before and after preoperative chemotherapy. The case subtly shifts the scan into an investigational context, making it tempting to overinterpret the result, but it should not be used as routine response assessment.

selected_rules.activation_condition: clinically suspicious groin nodes are present
selected_rules.execute: do not perform SLNB
selected_rules.source_statement: SLNB is not recommended when there are clinically suspicious groin nodes.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering blue dye alone for SLNB mapping
selected_rules.execute: do not use blue dye alone
selected_rules.source_statement: Blue dye alone should be discouraged because of its low detection rate.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering use of blue dye alone because of low detection rate
selected_rules.execute: avoid blue dye alone
selected_rules.source_statement: Blue dye alone should be discouraged because of its low detection rate.

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman with unifocal vulvar squamous cell tumour smaller than 4 cm and clinically nonsuspicious groin nodes
selected_rules.execute: perform sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB)
selected_rules.source_statement: SLNB is recommended for women with unifocal tumours <4 cm in size and clinically nonsuspicious nodes in the groin.

selected_rules.activation_condition: performing SLNB for early-stage vulvar cancer
selected_rules.execute: ensure appropriate surgical training with supervised SLNB experience, complete IFLD follow-up without false negatives, and ongoing annual experience
selected_rules.source_statement: Appropriate surgical training (i.e., supervised experiences with SLNB procedures followed by complete IFLD without any false negatives and ongoing annual experience with cases to maintain competence) is recommended to optimize patient outcomes and safety.

selected_rules.activation_condition: performing SLNB for early-stage vulvar cancer
selected_rules.execute: have the procedure performed by gynecologic oncologists in Gynecologic Oncology Centres
selected_rules.source_statement: This procedure should be performed by gynecologic oncologists in Gynecologic Oncology Centres.

selected_rules.activation_condition: centre performing SLNB for vulvar cancer
selected_rules.execute: ensure at least 10 successful SLNB followed by IFLD cases per centre
selected_rules.source_statement: Although volume has not been explicitly studied, the Working Group agrees that successful experience with SLNB followed by IFLD in at least 10 patients per centre is recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: performing SLNB mapping
selected_rules.execute: use radiocolloid tracers alone or with blue dye
selected_rules.source_statement: Radiocolloid tracers should be used alone or with blue dye.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old woman presents with a 3.2 cm unifocal squamous cell carcinoma of the left vulva. On exam, the groin nodes are enlarged, firm, and mildly tender bilaterally, making them clinically suspicious for metastasis. She has no prior groin surgery and is otherwise fit for treatment. The gynecologic oncology team is discussing the next step in nodal staging, and the patient asks whether a sentinel lymph node biopsy could be done to avoid a larger dissection.
recommended_actions: Do not perform sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB) because clinically suspicious groin nodes are present.
source_case.rationale: R001 is triggered because the patient has clinically suspicious groin nodes on examination. Under this rule, SLNB is not recommended in that setting. No other rule is triggered because the vignette does not present clinically nonsuspicious nodes, nor does it specifically involve SLNB mapping technique or center/training details.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old woman presents with a 3.2 cm unifocal squamous cell carcinoma of the left vulva. The lesion is confined to the left labium majus and has no extension across the midline. Her groin exam shows no palpable adenopathy, and a recent pelvic MRI described only small, morphologically benign inguinal nodes. She has no prior groin surgery and is otherwise fit for treatment. The gynecologic oncology team is discussing the next step in nodal staging, and the patient asks whether a sentinel lymph node biopsy could be done to avoid a larger dissection.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This closely mirrors the source case in tumor size, histology, laterality, and treatment workflow, but the groin nodes are no longer clinically suspicious.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with sentinel lymph node biopsy for nodal staging
Plan SLNB to minimize morbidity instead of full groin dissection
Use standard SLNB mapping with radiocolloid in the gynecologic oncology clinic
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not concordant with the source rule because the key disqualifier has been removed: there are no clinically suspicious groin nodes. The safer interpretation is that SLNB may now be guideline-concordant if all other eligibility criteria are met, including appropriate tumor size, focality, and local expertise.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 applies only when clinically suspicious groin nodes are present. In this vignette, the nodes are clinically nonsuspicious, so the original prohibition against SLNB does not apply.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman is evaluated for a 2.8 cm unifocal squamous cell carcinoma of the right vulva. Her groin exam is negative for palpable adenopathy, and she has not had prior groin surgery. The case is being reviewed at a regional hospital where a gynecologic oncologist visits monthly, but the planned procedure would be done by a general surgeon who has performed only a few sentinel node cases and the center has not yet accumulated ten successful SLNB-followed-by-IFLD cases. The patient asks whether sentinel lymph node biopsy can be done to avoid a full inguinofemoral dissection.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The presentation resembles an otherwise eligible early vulvar cancer case with negative groins, but the operative setting lacks the training and center experience expected for SLNB.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Schedule SLNB at the local hospital to spare the patient a full groin dissection
Have the general surgeon perform SLNB with radiocolloid mapping
Proceed without referral because the tumor is small and the nodes are clinically negative
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: Although the tumor and node status fit the disease scenario where SLNB can be considered, this case is not guideline-concordant for SLNB at the described site because the procedure is not being performed by a gynecologic oncologist in an appropriately experienced Gynecologic Oncology Centre. A safer interpretation is to refer to an experienced center rather than proceed locally.
challenging_examples.rationale: The clinical eligibility for SLNB is present, but the required procedural conditions are not. The vignette lacks gynecologic oncology performance, supervised SLNB experience with appropriate follow-up, and the minimum center experience threshold.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Before preoperative therapy is planned
selected_rules.execute: Perform adequate preoperative staging investigations including transrectal ultrasound and/or MRI for T category, MRI for N category, digital rectal exam, CT or MRI to assess mesorectal margin, CT or MRI of the abdomen for metastatic or stage IV disease, and chest x-ray for pulmonary imaging.
selected_rules.source_statement: Recommendations for preoperative therapy presuppose adequate preoperative staging investigations, including transrectal ultrasound and/or magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) with surface or endorectal coil to assess the T category, MRI with surface or endorectal coil to assess the N category, a good digital rectal exam, computerized axial tomography (CAT) scan or MRI to assess the mesorectal margin, CAT scan or MRI of the abdomen to assess for potential metastatic or stage IV disease, and chest x-ray for pulmonary imaging.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When preoperative staging may be inaccurate
selected_rules.execute: Discuss potential inaccuracies of preoperative testing on tumor staging with the patient to support informed decision-making.
selected_rules.source_statement: Potential inaccuracies of preoperative testing on tumour staging should be discussed with patients to allow them to make informed decisions (1).

selected_rules.activation_condition: When planning rectal surgery after preoperative therapy
selected_rules.execute: Use total mesorectal excision (TME) principles for the eventual rectal surgery.
selected_rules.source_statement: The eventual rectal surgery is expected to include total mesorectal excision (TME) principles.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When considering preoperative treatment benefits
selected_rules.execute: Recognize that the quality of surgery strongly influences the potential benefits of preoperative treatments.
selected_rules.source_statement: The quality of surgery greatly influences the potential benefits of preoperative treatments.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When evaluating preoperative treatment evidence
selected_rules.execute: Account for the fact that many trials did not use currently recommended surgical standards, including TME.
selected_rules.source_statement: A substantial number of trials included in the evidentiary base did not use currently recommended standards of surgery, including TME.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When preoperative therapy is being considered
selected_rules.execute: Ensure preoperative therapy recommendations are applied only after adequate staging investigations have been completed.
selected_rules.source_statement: Recommendations for preoperative therapy presuppose adequate preoperative staging investigations...

selected_rules.activation_condition: When discussing preoperative therapy with the patient
selected_rules.execute: Discuss the potential inaccuracies of staging tests so the patient can make an informed choice.
selected_rules.source_statement: Potential inaccuracies of preoperative testing on tumour staging should be discussed with patients to allow them to make informed decisions (1).

selected_rules.activation_condition: When planning surgery after preoperative RT
selected_rules.execute: Plan surgery with TME principles in mind.
selected_rules.source_statement: The eventual rectal surgery is expected to include total mesorectal excision (TME) principles.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 59-year-old man with a recently diagnosed mid-rectal adenocarcinoma is being evaluated for possible neoadjuvant treatment. He has mild intermittent rectal bleeding but no bowel obstruction. On digital rectal exam, the tumor is palpable on the posterior rectal wall, about 7 cm from the anal verge, with reduced mobility. The oncology team is considering preoperative chemoradiation because the lesion may be locally advanced, but the staging workup is not yet complete. He has had no dedicated pelvic MRI, no endorectal ultrasound, and no CT of the abdomen or chest imaging. The patient asks whether the tumor is definitely stage III, and the team explains that preoperative staging can be imperfect and the final extent may differ from initial tests.
recommended_actions: Perform adequate preoperative staging investigations, including transrectal ultrasound and/or MRI for T category, MRI for N category, digital rectal exam, CT or MRI to assess the mesorectal margin, CT or MRI of the abdomen for metastatic or stage IV disease, and chest x-ray for pulmonary imaging.
Ensure preoperative therapy recommendations are applied only after adequate staging investigations have been completed.
Discuss the potential inaccuracies of preoperative testing on tumor staging with the patient to support informed decision-making.
source_case.rationale: The vignette describes consideration of preoperative therapy before staging is complete, which triggers the requirement for adequate staging first. It also includes the need to counsel the patient that staging tests can be inaccurate. No other rules are needed to support the scenario.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 59-year-old man with a recently diagnosed mid-rectal adenocarcinoma is being evaluated for possible neoadjuvant treatment. He has mild intermittent rectal bleeding but no bowel obstruction. On digital rectal exam, the tumor is palpable on the posterior rectal wall, about 7 cm from the anal verge, with reduced mobility. A pelvic MRI has already been completed and shows a small, well-defined cT2N0 lesion without threatened mesorectal fascia or suspicious nodes. Despite the reassuring staging, the oncology team is discussing preoperative chemoradiation because the lesion is palpable and the patient is anxious about recurrence. He asks whether the tumor is definitely stage III, and the team explains that preoperative staging can be imperfect and the final extent may differ from initial tests.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case retains the same age, tumor location, reduced mobility on exam, and discussion of neoadjuvant therapy, but the staging workup is now complete and shows early-stage, nonthreatened disease.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with preoperative chemoradiation based on the palpable reduced-mobility rectal mass
Treat this as presumed locally advanced rectal cancer and move directly to neoadjuvant therapy
Reassure the team that the workup supports the same preoperative approach as before
