challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the staging-based recommendation because adequate preoperative staging has already been completed and does not support locally advanced disease. The safer interpretation is to reassess the need for neoadjuvant therapy in light of cT2N0 findings rather than defaulting to preoperative chemoradiation.
challenging_examples.rationale: The source recommendation requires adequate staging before applying preoperative therapy. Here, the staging is complete and indicates an early-stage, nonthreatened tumor, so the same preoperative recommendation is no longer triggered.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 59-year-old man with a newly diagnosed mid-rectal adenocarcinoma is seen in multidisciplinary clinic for possible neoadjuvant treatment. He reports intermittent rectal bleeding and occasional urgency, but no obstruction or weight loss. The tumor is palpable on digital rectal exam, about 7 cm from the anal verge, and feels somewhat fixed posteriorly. Pelvic MRI and endorectal ultrasound suggest a cT3 lesion with several borderline mesorectal nodes, but the CT chest/abdomen has not yet been completed because he is scheduled for it next week. The oncologist notes that preoperative chemoradiation may be appropriate and starts discussing timing with radiation oncology. The patient asks whether the staging is already sufficient to decide on treatment.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This case closely mirrors the original rectal cancer staging workflow and still appears to be heading toward neoadjuvant therapy, but metastatic evaluation has not yet been finished.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Finalize preoperative chemoradiation planning before the remaining imaging is done
Treat the available pelvic staging as adequate and proceed with neoadjuvant therapy
Assume the patient is nonmetastatic because the pelvic MRI already shows locally advanced disease
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant because adequate preoperative staging is incomplete: chest and abdominal imaging to assess for metastatic or stage IV disease have not been finished. The safer interpretation is to complete the full staging workup before committing to preoperative therapy.
challenging_examples.rationale: Although the pelvic MRI and ultrasound suggest locally advanced rectal cancer, the required staging investigations are not complete. The missing metastatic evaluation means the recommendation to apply preoperative therapy cannot yet be considered satisfied.

selected_rules.activation_condition: newly diagnosed, chemotherapy-eligible, locally advanced squamous cell or undifferentiated nasopharyngeal cancer (stage III or IV)
selected_rules.execute: Use chemoradiotherapy-based management with consideration of adding neoadjuvant, concurrent, or adjuvant chemotherapy as addressed in the guideline
selected_rules.source_statement: The objectives of the guideline are to make recommendations regarding the addition of neoadjuvant/concurrent/adjuvant chemotherapy to chemoradiotherapy in the management of locally advanced squamous cell or undifferentiated nasopharyngeal cancer (NPC).

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with NPC receiving induction chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Administer induction chemotherapy every 3 weeks for 3 cycles; at minimum give 2 cycles
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with NPC receiving induction chemotherapy, the regimens should be administered every three weeks for a total of three cycles, or at the minimum two cycles.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with NPC receiving induction chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Start chemoradiotherapy within 21 to 28 days from the first day of the last induction chemotherapy cycle
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with NPC receiving induction chemotherapy, chemoradiotherapy should be commenced within 21-28 days from the first day of the last cycle of induction chemotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: all patients with NPC receiving adjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Offer PF every 4 weeks for a total of 3 cycles
selected_rules.source_statement: For all patients with NPC receiving adjuvant chemotherapy, PF ... administered every four weeks for a total of three cycles should be offered.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with locally advanced NPC treated in Ontario context
selected_rules.execute: Apply the endorsed CSCO/ASCO recommendations as modified by Ontario Health endorsement
selected_rules.source_statement: The Chemotherapy and Radiotherapy for Nasopharyngeal Cancer Development Group (GDG) of Ontario Health (Cancer Care Ontario) endorses the Chinese Society of Clinical Oncology (CSCO)/American Society of Clinical Oncology (ASCO) recommendations ... modified by the endorsement process described in this document.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with NPC receiving induction chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Use a 3-week cycle interval for induction chemotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with NPC receiving induction chemotherapy, the regimens should be administered every three weeks for a total of three cycles, or at the minimum two cycles.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with NPC receiving adjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Use a 4-week cycle interval for PF adjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: For all patients with NPC receiving adjuvant chemotherapy, PF ... administered every four weeks for a total of three cycles should be offered.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with NPC receiving induction chemotherapy followed by chemoradiotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Do not delay chemoradiotherapy beyond 28 days after the first day of the last induction cycle
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with NPC receiving induction chemotherapy, chemoradiotherapy should be commenced within 21-28 days from the first day of the last cycle of induction chemotherapy.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 52-year-old man in Ontario presents with a newly diagnosed, biopsy-proven undifferentiated nasopharyngeal carcinoma. Staging MRI and PET-CT show a stage IVB locally advanced primary tumor with bilateral cervical nodal involvement but no distant metastases. He has good performance status, normal renal and hepatic function, and is considered chemotherapy-eligible. The oncology team plans induction chemotherapy followed by definitive chemoradiotherapy. He understands the treatment will be coordinated closely because the timing of the transition to radiation is important after induction therapy.
recommended_actions: Use a chemoradiotherapy-based management approach for this newly diagnosed, chemotherapy-eligible stage IV locally advanced nasopharyngeal cancer.
If induction chemotherapy is given, administer it every 3 weeks for a total of 3 cycles; at minimum, 2 cycles are acceptable.
Start chemoradiotherapy within 21 to 28 days from the first day of the last induction chemotherapy cycle.
source_case.rationale: The vignette matches newly diagnosed, chemotherapy-eligible, locally advanced undifferentiated nasopharyngeal cancer (stage IV), triggering the recommendation for chemoradiotherapy-based management. It also specifies induction chemotherapy followed by chemoradiotherapy, which triggers the induction schedule and timing rules: q3-week induction for 3 cycles minimum 2, and initiation of chemoradiotherapy within 21–28 days from the first day of the last induction cycle.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 52-year-old man in Ontario presents with a newly diagnosed, biopsy-proven undifferentiated nasopharyngeal carcinoma. Staging MRI and PET-CT show a stage II primary tumor with unilateral cervical nodal involvement and no distant metastases. He has good performance status, normal renal and hepatic function, and is considered chemotherapy-eligible. The oncology team discusses definitive treatment, and induction chemotherapy followed by chemoradiotherapy is being considered because of the nodal disease burden. He understands the plan will need close coordination if chemotherapy is used before radiation.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case closely mirrors the source with the same diagnosis, Ontario setting, good performance status, and chemotherapy eligibility, but the stage is lower than the locally advanced stage III/IV threshold.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed directly with a chemoradiotherapy-based locally advanced NPC pathway without rechecking stage
Apply the induction chemotherapy timing rules and schedule radiation after neoadjuvant treatment
Treat this as stage IV disease and use the full locally advanced NPC recommendation set
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the locally advanced NPC recommendation because the tumor is stage II, not stage III or IV. A reader should interpret this as an earlier-stage nasopharyngeal cancer case that does not meet the activation condition for the cited locally advanced chemoradiotherapy-based recommendation.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 requires newly diagnosed, chemotherapy-eligible, locally advanced squamous cell or undifferentiated NPC, specifically stage III or IV. The vignette is intentionally similar but the stage is II, so the rule does not apply.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 52-year-old man in Ontario presents with a newly diagnosed, biopsy-proven undifferentiated nasopharyngeal carcinoma. Staging MRI and PET-CT show a stage IVB locally advanced primary tumor with bilateral cervical nodal involvement and a small hepatic lesion that is indeterminate on MRI but not yet biopsy-confirmed. He has good performance status and normal renal function, so the team is considering systemic therapy followed by definitive chemoradiotherapy if the lesion is not metastatic. The transition to radiation is being discussed, but final staging is still pending review of the liver finding.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Most elements match the source case, including histology, stage label, nodal disease, and treatment planning, but there is a possible distant lesion that makes the extent of disease uncertain.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start the locally advanced NPC chemoradiotherapy pathway immediately
Plan induction chemotherapy followed by definitive chemoradiotherapy without further workup
Apply the stage IV locally advanced recommendation set despite the possible liver lesion
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The case is not clearly guideline-concordant for the locally advanced recommendation because the presence of a suspicious hepatic lesion raises concern for metastatic disease, and the disease is not confidently limited to nonmetastatic stage III/IVB locoregional NPC. The safer interpretation is that complete staging clarification is needed before applying the locally advanced chemoradiotherapy pathway.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 applies to locally advanced NPC without distant metastases. Here, the imaging suggests a possible distant metastasis that has not been resolved, so the activation condition is not securely met.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When the document is used as a guideline reference
selected_rules.execute: No clinical action can be extracted from the provided text.
selected_rules.source_statement: The provided text contains only document front matter, section headings, and copyright/contact information, with no clinical recommendations.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man is seen in clinic for a new guideline review after his primary care physician asked for oncology guidance on a recently diagnosed condition. The document available for reference contains only the front matter, section headings, and copyright/contact information, without any disease-specific management content or treatment recommendations. The patient’s history, exam, and test results are not being used to derive any intervention from this source because no clinical guidance is present in the document.
recommended_actions: No clinical action can be extracted from the provided text.
source_case.rationale: Rule R001 is triggered because the document is being used as a guideline reference, but the source contains only non-clinical front matter and no actionable recommendations. Therefore, no management action can be derived from this text.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man is seen in clinic for a new guideline review after his primary care physician asked for oncology guidance on a recently diagnosed condition. The document available for reference is the full guideline table of contents, preface, and copyright/contact pages, but the disease-specific chapters have not yet been released in the version uploaded to the chart. The patient’s history, exam, and test results are being reviewed, but the available source still contains no management statements, algorithms, or treatment recommendations to apply to his case.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The setup still looks like a routine oncology guideline consult, but the referenced document remains non-clinical front matter and a table of contents without actionable guidance.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Follow the apparent guideline structure and proceed with a standard oncology workup or treatment pathway.
Extract a management recommendation from the document headings and use it to guide care.
Document the guideline review as supporting a specific intervention based on the uploaded reference.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant because the source still contains no clinical recommendations, only front matter and headings. The safer interpretation is that no action can be extracted from the provided text, so management must rely on other valid clinical sources.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 applies when the document is used as a guideline reference, but the available text lacks any disease-specific recommendation content. The presence of headings and preface does not create an actionable guideline statement.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man is referred for oncology guidance after a newly diagnosed condition was identified in primary care. The chart note includes a scanned guideline packet, but only the title page, authorship page, copyright notice, and section dividers are legible; the actual recommendation pages are missing from the upload. The patient’s presentation is clinically relevant, but the referenced material still provides no management text, dosing information, or decision criteria that can be used to support treatment selection.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case preserves the same oncology-guideline consult workflow, but the uploaded reference is incomplete and still lacks any actionable recommendations.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Use the guideline packet to justify a treatment choice based on the visible section titles.
Apply a standard management recommendation after assuming the missing pages match the rest of the document.
Proceed with intervention because a guideline document was referenced in the chart.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This remains discordant with the rule because the accessible source still has no clinical recommendations. A guideline reference is not sufficient unless the actionable content is present; the correct approach is to acknowledge that no recommendation can be extracted from the available text.
challenging_examples.rationale: The source is still limited to non-clinical front matter or section dividers, so there is no extractable recommendation. The apparent presence of a guideline packet is misleading, but it does not satisfy the condition for a usable clinical directive.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has completely resected stage IA NSCLC
selected_rules.execute: do not routinely give adjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: The 2007 joint CCO/ASCO guideline recommended chemotherapy for stage II and IIIA disease but not stage IA.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has completely resected stage IB to IIIA NSCLC with sensitizing EGFR mutation
selected_rules.execute: do not use EGFR-TKI routinely as adjuvant therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: These data were considered insufficient to justify the routine use of EGFR-TKIs in patients with tumors with sensitizing EGFR mutations.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has completely resected stage IB to IIIA NSCLC without EGFR mutation selection
selected_rules.execute: do not use erlotinib routinely as adjuvant therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: No significant differences in DFS or OS were detected in the overall unselected study population.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has completely resected stage IB to IIIA NSCLC without EGFR mutation selection
selected_rules.execute: do not use gefitinib routinely as adjuvant therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: There was no benefit for either of the subgroups with EGFR wild-type tumors or the 15 tumors (4% of the total sample) with EGFR sensitizing mutations.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has completely resected stage I to IIIA NSCLC
selected_rules.execute: do not use MAGE-A3 immunotherapy routinely as adjuvant treatment
selected_rules.source_statement: MAGRIT... found no significant difference in the primary outcome DFS... Because there was no difference between groups, the study was unable to identify a biomarker that would enable selection of patients for this treatment.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has completely resected stage I to IIIA NSCLC
selected_rules.execute: do not use bevacizumab added to chemotherapy routinely as adjuvant therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: a phase III RCT... found that the addition of angiogenesis inhibitor bevacizumab to chemotherapy failed to improve DFS or OS for individuals with surgically resected earlystage NSCLCs compared with chemotherapy alone.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has completely resected stage I or II NSCLC
selected_rules.execute: do not give postoperative radiotherapy (PORT) routinely
selected_rules.source_statement: there has been no new evidence that would alter the recommendation against PORT in patients with stage I or II disease.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has completely resected stage II or IIIA NSCLC
selected_rules.execute: offer adjuvant cisplatin-based chemotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Cisplatin-based adjuvant chemotherapy was recommended for routine use in patients with stage II or IIIA disease extent

source_case.patient_vignette: A 64-year-old woman with a 35-pack-year smoking history underwent right upper lobectomy for a 2.1 cm lung adenocarcinoma. Final pathology showed a completely resected stage IA (pT1bN0) non-small cell lung cancer with clear margins and no nodal involvement on systematic sampling. Postoperative recovery was uncomplicated, and she is now seen in oncology clinic 4 weeks later asking whether she should start chemotherapy to reduce recurrence risk. Her performance status is good, and she has no evidence of residual disease on imaging.
recommended_actions: Do not routinely give adjuvant chemotherapy.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches completely resected stage IA NSCLC, which triggers the recommendation against routine adjuvant chemotherapy. No other provided rule is needed because the tumor is stage IA and there is no EGFR-directed or radiotherapy indication in the rule set for this case.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 64-year-old woman with a 35-pack-year smoking history underwent right upper lobectomy for a 2.1 cm lung adenocarcinoma. Final pathology showed a completely resected pT1bN0 tumor with clear margins and no nodal involvement on systematic sampling, but the pathology report also noted visceral pleural invasion. Postoperative recovery was uncomplicated, and she is now seen in oncology clinic 4 weeks later asking whether she should start chemotherapy to reduce recurrence risk. Her performance status is good, and there is no evidence of residual disease on imaging.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Very similar post-lobectomy follow-up for early lung adenocarcinoma, but the tumor is no longer stage IA because of visceral pleural invasion.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start adjuvant cisplatin-based chemotherapy now
Recommend routine postoperative systemic therapy to reduce recurrence risk
Treat this as standard stage IA resected NSCLC and discuss chemotherapy
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This case is not guideline-concordant for the no-chemotherapy recommendation because visceral pleural invasion upstages the tumor out of stage IA. The safer interpretation is that she likely has at least stage IB disease, so adjuvant treatment should be reconsidered using the appropriate higher-stage pathway rather than automatically withholding chemotherapy.
challenging_examples.rationale: Rule R001 applies only to completely resected stage IA NSCLC. A pathologic upstage from visceral pleural invasion invalidates that condition.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 64-year-old woman with a 35-pack-year smoking history underwent right upper lobectomy for a 2.1 cm lung adenocarcinoma. Final pathology showed a completely resected stage IA (pT1bN0) non-small cell lung cancer with clear margins and no nodal involvement on systematic sampling. Postoperative recovery was uncomplicated, and she is now seen in oncology clinic 4 weeks later asking whether she should start chemotherapy to reduce recurrence risk. Her performance status is good, but a surveillance CT done before the visit shows a new 8 mm right hilar lymph node that was not present on the immediate postoperative scan.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The setting still looks like a routine early-stage postresection oncology follow-up, but the new hilar node means there is concerning evidence beyond a straightforward completely resected stage IA case.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Reassure her and advise no adjuvant chemotherapy because the original tumor was stage IA
Proceed with routine observation only
Treat the visit as standard postoperative stage IA surveillance without further workup
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the stage IA no-chemotherapy recommendation because the new hilar lymph node raises concern for residual or recurrent disease and undermines the assumption of uncomplicated completely resected stage IA NSCLC. The safer interpretation is to evaluate the suspicious node before deciding on any adjuvant strategy.
challenging_examples.rationale: Rule R001 requires completely resected stage IA disease with no evidence of residual disease. A new suspicious postoperative node breaks that premise.

selected_rules.activation_condition: positive sentinel node and comparing CLND with observation
selected_rules.execute: Do not expect a survival benefit from CLND based on MSLT-II.
selected_rules.source_statement: The non-significant difference in MSS and increase in adverse events of the CLND group indicates that CLND may not be optimal for patients and does not offer a survival benefit.

selected_rules.activation_condition: positive sentinel node and comparing CLND with observation
selected_rules.execute: Do not expect benefit from CLND for any subgroup based on MSLT-II.
selected_rules.source_statement: Although the majority of patients had low volume tumor metastases, sub set analysis did not demonstrate a benefit for any groups of patient receiving CLND.

selected_rules.activation_condition: positive sentinel node and comparing CLND with observation
selected_rules.execute: Do not assume CLND improves distant metastasis-free survival, overall survival, or recurrence-free survival based on DeCOG-SLT.
selected_rules.source_statement: The DeCOG-SLT trial found no difference in distant metastasis-free survival, overall survival, or recurrence-free survival when SLN positive patients who received CLND were compared to patients who were observed.

selected_rules.activation_condition: positive sentinel node and considering CLND versus observation
selected_rules.execute: Avoid routine CLND when observation with ultrasonography is feasible because CLND increases adverse events.
selected_rules.source_statement: Adverse events occurred with more frequency among the CLND patients than the observation group with lymphedema being the most common (24.1% of patients vs. 6.3% at last follow-up, p<0.001).

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is managed with nodal observation after positive sentinel node
selected_rules.execute: Monitor the affected nodal basin with ultrasonography and clinical exam every 4 to 6 months for the first 2 years, then every 6 months from years 3 to 5.
selected_rules.source_statement: Monitoring with ultrasonography of the affected nodal basin and clinical exam will be required, at minimum, every 4 to 6 months for the first 2 years and every 6 months from 3-5 years.

selected_rules.activation_condition: ultrasound shows any two suspicious nodal features
selected_rules.execute: Confirm suspected nodal recurrence with biopsy of the basin.
selected_rules.source_statement: Suspicions of nodal recurrence via ultrasound should be confirmed with a biopsy of the basin.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has sentinel node metastasis
selected_rules.execute: Consider nodal observation with ultrasonography rather than completion lymph node dissection.
selected_rules.source_statement: Patients with sentinel node metastasis should be considered for nodal observation with ultrasonography rather than CLND.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has extensive sentinel node metastasis and CLND is the only option for local control
selected_rules.execute: Consider CLND as the better option than nodal observation with ultrasonography.
selected_rules.source_statement: patients with extensive sentinel node metastasis in which CLND would be the only option for local control

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with a history of cutaneous melanoma on the left calf underwent wide local excision and sentinel lymph node biopsy. Pathology shows a single positive sentinel node with a small metastatic deposit, and staging scans show no distant disease. He is otherwise well and asks whether he should proceed with completion lymph node dissection (CLND) or be observed. The surgical team discusses that he is eligible for nodal observation with ultrasound rather than routine CLND, since the sentinel node metastasis is limited and ultrasound follow-up is feasible. He is comfortable with close surveillance and prefers to avoid the added morbidity of more extensive surgery.
recommended_actions: Do not pursue routine CLND when observation with ultrasonography is feasible, because CLND increases adverse events.
Consider nodal observation with ultrasonography rather than completion lymph node dissection for sentinel node metastasis.
Monitor the affected nodal basin with ultrasonography and clinical examination every 4 to 6 months for the first 2 years, then every 6 months from years 3 to 5.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches a patient with positive sentinel node metastasis in whom CLND is being compared with observation. The selected rules support observation over routine CLND because survival benefit has not been shown and adverse events are higher with CLND. Since the patient is managed with nodal observation, scheduled ultrasound and clinical follow-up are recommended.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with a history of cutaneous melanoma on the left calf underwent wide local excision and sentinel lymph node biopsy. Pathology shows a single positive sentinel node, but the metastatic focus measures 9 mm and nearly replaces most of the node. Staging scans show no distant disease. He asks whether he should proceed with completion lymph node dissection (CLND) or be observed. The surgical team notes that the involved basin is deep in the groin and cannot be reliably followed with ultrasound because of body habitus and postoperative scarring. He is otherwise well and is trying to choose the least morbid option, but local control is a concern.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still has a positive sentinel node and no distant metastases, but the nodal tumor burden is extensive and ultrasound surveillance is not feasible.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Recommend nodal observation with serial ultrasound because the sentinel node is only a single node involved
Defer CLND to avoid added morbidity and arrange routine ultrasound follow-up
Frame observation as the preferred approach for all sentinel node-positive melanoma patients
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for routine observation because the sentinel node metastasis is extensive and the nodal basin cannot be adequately surveilled with ultrasound. Safer interpretation: CLND may be reasonable for local control when observation is not feasible.
challenging_examples.rationale: The observation recommendation applies when sentinel-node metastasis is limited and ultrasound follow-up is feasible. Here, both conditions are undermined by bulky nodal disease and poor ultrasound accessibility, which shifts management away from routine nodal observation.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old woman underwent wide local excision of a truncal melanoma and sentinel lymph node biopsy. One sentinel node is positive with a small metastatic deposit, and staging scans show no distant disease. She is clinically well and is discussing whether to avoid completion lymph node dissection (CLND). Her surgeon explains that nodal observation with ultrasound is an option, but the patient will be relocating overseas in 3 weeks and does not expect to have reliable access to a melanoma surveillance program or serial basin ultrasounds for the next year. She prefers to minimize surgery, but follow-up logistics are uncertain.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The pathology and staging resemble a typical sentinel node-positive patient eligible for observation, but dependable serial ultrasound surveillance is not realistically available.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Choose nodal observation with ultrasound follow-up despite the limited access to surveillance
Avoid CLND solely because the sentinel node metastasis is small
Schedule routine surveillance visits at standard intervals without addressing the follow-up gap
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant observation because the required ultrasound-based nodal surveillance is not feasible. Safer interpretation: observation is less appropriate when reliable follow-up cannot be ensured, and the treatment plan should be individualized with attention to local control and access to surveillance.
challenging_examples.rationale: The recommendation for nodal observation depends on feasible ultrasound monitoring of the affected basin. In this case, the patient cannot reliably access serial ultrasounds after relocation, so the usual observation pathway is not a good fit despite the small-volume sentinel node metastasis.

selected_rules.activation_condition: sequential radiotherapy and chemotherapy is being considered
selected_rules.execute: Do not use sequential radiotherapy and chemotherapy as standard practice
selected_rules.source_statement: Sequential radiotherapy and chemotherapy should not be recommended as standard practice.

selected_rules.activation_condition: sequential radiotherapy and chemotherapy is being considered
selected_rules.execute: Avoid sequential radiotherapy and chemotherapy because no survival or local control benefit was shown and toxicity was increased
selected_rules.source_statement: There was no evidence to suggest any survival or local control benefit using sequential RTCT compared with RT alone. Given these results and the increased adverse effects associated with sequential RTCT, this approach should not be recommended in standard practice.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with localized esophageal carcinoma, good performance status, considering non-surgical curative treatment, and combined radiotherapy and chemotherapy can be tolerated
selected_rules.execute: Use concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy rather than radiotherapy alone
selected_rules.source_statement: Concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy is recommended over radiotherapy alone.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with localized esophageal carcinoma considering a non-surgical approach and combined radiotherapy and chemotherapy can be tolerated
selected_rules.execute: Consider concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy a reasonable option compared with radiotherapy alone
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients with favourable performance status and who have a reasonable chance of completing concomitant RTCT, this approach is a reasonable option compared with radiotherapy alone.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has favourable performance status and a reasonable chance of completing concomitant RTCT
selected_rules.execute: Offer concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy as a curative-intent non-surgical approach
selected_rules.source_statement: The current review illustrates a benefit in local control and survival with the use of concomitant RTCT.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with localized (T1-3, small volume N1, M0) esophageal carcinoma and good performance status considering non-surgical treatment
selected_rules.execute: Apply the guideline recommendations to this patient group
selected_rules.source_statement: These recommendations apply to adult patients with localized (T1-3, small volume N1, M0) carcinoma of the esophagus and good performance status who are considering a non-surgical approach and for whom combined radiotherapy and chemotherapy can be tolerated in the judgment of the treating oncologist.

selected_rules.activation_condition: when concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy is being used
selected_rules.execute: Prefer a cisplatin-based chemotherapy regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: a cisplatin-based chemotherapy regimen is a reasonable chemotherapy regimen to use when concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy is used.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is being considered for concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Inform the patient about increased acute toxicity with this approach
selected_rules.source_statement: Patients should be made aware of the increased acute toxicity associated with this approach.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man with dysphagia and a newly diagnosed localized mid-esophageal squamous cell carcinoma is evaluated by oncology. Staging shows T2N1 with a small-volume nodal burden and no distant metastases. He has an ECOG performance status of 1, maintains oral intake with soft foods, and has no major cardiac or renal comorbidities. After discussion of treatment options, he declines surgery and asks about curative non-surgical therapy. The team considers combined radiotherapy and chemotherapy, which he is felt likely to tolerate. Because he is otherwise fit, the oncologist discusses a concomitant approach rather than radiotherapy alone. The patient also asks about side effects and which chemotherapy backbone is usually used with radiation.
recommended_actions: Use concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy rather than radiotherapy alone.
Offer concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy as a curative-intent non-surgical approach.
Prefer a cisplatin-based chemotherapy regimen when concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy is used.
Inform the patient about increased acute toxicity associated with concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches an adult with localized esophageal carcinoma, good performance status, and a reasonable chance of completing concomitant chemoradiotherapy in a non-surgical curative setting. The rules support concomitant RTCT over radiotherapy alone, indicate it is a reasonable curative-intent option, suggest cisplatin-based chemotherapy as a reasonable regimen, and advise counseling about increased acute toxicity.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man with progressive dysphagia is newly diagnosed with squamous cell carcinoma of the mid-esophagus. Imaging shows a T2 lesion with a single small regional node and no distant metastases. He has ECOG performance status 1, is still taking soft foods, and has no major cardiac or renal disease. He strongly prefers to avoid surgery and asks about curative non-operative treatment. The oncology team discusses treatment sequencing and notes he could complete either modality separately if needed. Because he is motivated for aggressive therapy, they consider starting with chemotherapy followed by radiotherapy, and he asks whether that approach is standard. The patient also wants to know about expected toxicities and whether a cisplatin-containing regimen is typically used.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case preserves localized esophageal SCC, good performance status, and a curative non-surgical discussion, but the proposed plan is sequential rather than concomitant chemoradiotherapy.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with sequential radiotherapy and chemotherapy as the curative non-surgical plan.
Recommend starting chemotherapy first and then adding radiation once tolerated.
Explain that a cisplatin-based sequential regimen is standard for this presentation.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant because the rules specifically reject sequential radiotherapy and chemotherapy as standard practice. The safer interpretation is that, if non-surgical curative therapy is pursued, concomitant chemoradiotherapy should be favored instead of a sequential approach.
challenging_examples.rationale: The vignette looks like an eligible localized esophageal cancer case, but the treatment sequencing is the key mismatch. The recommendation is against sequential RTCT, even in fit patients, because it did not show benefit and increased toxicity.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 71-year-old man with mid-esophageal squamous cell carcinoma presents with dysphagia and weight loss. Staging shows T3N1 disease with several enlarged mediastinal nodes, but a PET-CT also reveals a small hypermetabolic adrenal lesion that has not yet been biopsied. He has ECOG performance status 1 and otherwise good organ function. He declines surgery because of personal preference and asks whether definitive chemoradiation could still be used. The team discusses concurrent treatment, but one note mentions that the adrenal finding may represent metastatic disease and would change intent if confirmed. He asks about the usual chemotherapy backbone and whether treatment can still be described as curative.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The presentation still suggests a fit patient with localized esophageal SCC considering non-surgical therapy, but the possible adrenal metastasis makes the disease no longer clearly localized M0.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer curative-intent concomitant chemoradiotherapy without further clarification of the adrenal lesion.
Treat this as localized esophageal cancer and recommend radiation with concurrent cisplatin-based chemotherapy.
Counsel him that definitive non-surgical chemoradiation remains the standard curative approach.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the localized-disease recommendation set because the adrenal lesion raises concern for metastatic disease, so the patient cannot be assumed to meet the T1-3, small-volume N1, M0 group. The safer interpretation is to clarify staging before applying curative non-surgical chemoradiation recommendations.
challenging_examples.rationale: The key subtlety is a suspicious extra-regional adrenal lesion. Until metastatic disease is excluded, the case does not fit the localized esophageal carcinoma population for which concomitant RTCT is recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has undergone radical prostatectomy and has pathologic T3 or margin-positive prostate cancer
selected_rules.execute: Offer adjuvant radiotherapy after prostatectomy
selected_rules.source_statement: In the only RCT for which long-term follow-up data are available, adjuvant RT following radical prostatectomy in patients with pathologic T3 or margin-positive prostate cancer has been shown to improve OS and reduce distant metastases compared to observation.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has undergone radical prostatectomy and has positive surgical margins
selected_rules.execute: Offer adjuvant radiotherapy after prostatectomy
selected_rules.source_statement: The OS benefit extends individually to the subgroups with positive surgical margins, extracapsular extension, and seminal vesicle invasion.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has undergone radical prostatectomy and has extracapsular extension
selected_rules.execute: Offer adjuvant radiotherapy after prostatectomy
selected_rules.source_statement: The OS benefit extends individually to the subgroups with positive surgical margins, extracapsular extension, and seminal vesicle invasion.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has undergone radical prostatectomy and has seminal vesicle invasion
selected_rules.execute: Offer adjuvant radiotherapy after prostatectomy
selected_rules.source_statement: The OS benefit extends individually to the subgroups with positive surgical margins, extracapsular extension, and seminal vesicle invasion.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has undergone radical prostatectomy and has any of positive surgical margins, extracapsular extension, or seminal vesicle invasion
selected_rules.execute: Use adjuvant radiotherapy after prostatectomy
selected_rules.source_statement: On the basis of the overall trial findings as well as these subgroup analyses, it can be concluded that men found at radical prostatectomy to have any of positive surgical margins, extracapsular extension, or seminal vesicle invasion benefit from adjuvant RT.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient is being considered for postoperative radiotherapy after prostatectomy and the comparison is observation with salvage therapy reserved
selected_rules.execute: Prefer adjuvant radiotherapy over observation with salvage therapy reserved
selected_rules.source_statement: At a median follow-up of 12.6 years, adjuvant RT has now been shown to confer a significant benefit in terms of both OS and metastasis-free survival.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient is being considered for postoperative radiotherapy after prostatectomy
selected_rules.execute: Consider adjuvant radiotherapy because it improves overall survival
selected_rules.source_statement: At a median follow-up of 12.6 years, adjuvant RT has now been shown to confer a significant benefit in terms of both OS and metastasis-free survival.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient is being considered for postoperative radiotherapy after prostatectomy
selected_rules.execute: Consider adjuvant radiotherapy because it reduces distant metastases
selected_rules.source_statement: At a median follow-up of 12.6 years, adjuvant RT has now been shown to confer a significant benefit in terms of both OS and metastasis-free survival.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 66-year-old man underwent radical prostatectomy for localized prostate adenocarcinoma. Final pathology shows pT3a disease with extracapsular extension and a focally positive posterior surgical margin. His postoperative PSA is currently undetectable, and he is recovering well with improving urinary continence. At follow-up, he asks whether he should wait for a PSA rise before doing anything further. The oncology team reviews the pathology and discusses postoperative management options, noting that his adverse pathologic features place him at higher risk of recurrence despite the undetectable PSA.
recommended_actions: Offer adjuvant radiotherapy after prostatectomy.
Use adjuvant radiotherapy after prostatectomy.
Consider adjuvant radiotherapy because it improves overall survival.
Consider adjuvant radiotherapy because it reduces distant metastases.
source_case.rationale: This vignette triggers postoperative adjuvant radiotherapy rules because the patient has undergone radical prostatectomy and has pathologic T3 disease with extracapsular extension plus a positive surgical margin. Those findings meet the activation conditions for recommending adjuvant radiotherapy and for favoring adjuvant treatment after prostatectomy. The cited rules also support considering adjuvant radiotherapy due to overall survival and distant metastasis benefits.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 66-year-old man underwent radical prostatectomy for localized prostate adenocarcinoma. Final pathology shows pT2 disease confined to the gland with negative surgical margins and no extracapsular extension or seminal vesicle invasion. His postoperative PSA is currently undetectable, and he is recovering well with improving urinary continence. At follow-up, he asks whether he should wait for a PSA rise before doing anything further. The oncology team reviews the pathology and discusses postoperative management options, noting that his pathology lacks the adverse features typically used to justify immediate postoperative radiation.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The overall setting is unchanged: post-prostatectomy follow-up with an undetectable PSA and a discussion of whether to start radiation now. The only change is that the final pathology is organ-confined with negative margins.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer adjuvant radiotherapy after prostatectomy.
Use adjuvant radiotherapy after prostatectomy.
Recommend immediate postoperative radiation because it improves overall survival.
Advise adjuvant radiotherapy to reduce future distant metastases.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for adjuvant radiotherapy because the patient does not have pathologic T3 disease, a positive margin, extracapsular extension, or seminal vesicle invasion. With organ-confined pT2, margin-negative disease and an undetectable PSA, observation with PSA surveillance is the safer interpretation.
challenging_examples.rationale: The recommendation applies to men with adverse post-prostatectomy pathology such as pT3 and/or margin-positive disease. Here those activation criteria are absent, so the case does not support adjuvant radiation.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 66-year-old man underwent radical prostatectomy for localized prostate adenocarcinoma. Final pathology shows pT3a disease with extracapsular extension and a focally positive posterior surgical margin. His postoperative PSA was initially undetectable, but on the first postoperative follow-up it is now 0.28 ng/mL and has risen on a repeat test 3 weeks later to 0.34 ng/mL. He is recovering well with improving urinary continence. At follow-up, he asks whether he should wait for any further rise before doing anything further. The oncology team reviews the pathology and discusses postoperative management options, noting that the patient now has a detectable postoperative PSA consistent with biochemical recurrence rather than a purely adjuvant setting.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The pathology still looks like a classic high-risk post-prostatectomy scenario, but the key difference is that PSA is no longer undetectable and has already risen on repeat testing.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer adjuvant radiotherapy after prostatectomy.
Prefer adjuvant radiotherapy over observation with salvage therapy reserved.
Consider adjuvant radiotherapy because it improves overall survival.
Consider adjuvant radiotherapy because it reduces distant metastases.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: Although postoperative radiation remains relevant, this is no longer a true adjuvant-therapy scenario because the PSA has become detectable and is rising. The safer interpretation is that he needs evaluation for early salvage radiotherapy rather than being managed under an undetectable-PSA adjuvant framework.
challenging_examples.rationale: The adjuvant recommendations are framed for patients being considered for postoperative radiotherapy after prostatectomy, especially with undetectable PSA and adverse pathology. A rising detectable PSA shifts the clinical situation to biochemical recurrence/early salvage rather than adjuvant treatment.

selected_rules.activation_condition: radical prostatectomy specimen is being handled in the operating room
selected_rules.execute: do not perform frozen section analysis for margin status
selected_rules.source_statement: Frozen section analysis of the radical prostatectomy specimen (RPS) for margin status is not recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: processing the apex of radical prostatectomy specimen
selected_rules.execute: do not use an en face shave technique at the apex
selected_rules.source_statement: An en face (shave) technique is to be discouraged at the apex, as this approach can result in false-positive margin interpretation.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is low-risk and surgery is the preferred option
selected_rules.execute: offer radical prostatectomy after full discussion and consideration of patient preferences
selected_rules.source_statement: RP should be offered to low-risk and intermediate-risk patients for whom surgery is the preferred option after full discussion with patient and taking into account patient preferences.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is intermediate-risk and surgery is the preferred option
selected_rules.execute: offer radical prostatectomy after full discussion and consideration of patient preferences
selected_rules.source_statement: RP should be offered to low-risk and intermediate-risk patients for whom surgery is the preferred option after full discussion with patient and taking into account patient preferences.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is high-risk
selected_rules.execute: offer referral for radiation consultation or review at a Multidisciplinary Cancer Conference (MCC)
selected_rules.source_statement: High-risk patients should be offered a referral for radiation consultation or review at a Multidisciplinary Cancer Conference (MCC).

selected_rules.activation_condition: radical prostatectomy is being performed
selected_rules.execute: aim to eradicate the cancer-containing organ with negative surgical margins
selected_rules.source_statement: The main goals of RP are (a) complete eradication of the cancer-containing organ with negative surgical margins, (b) preservation of urinary function, and (c) preservation of erectile function, where appropriate, but, in some cases, it is not possible to achieve all three.

selected_rules.activation_condition: radical prostatectomy is being performed
selected_rules.execute: balance margin rates with functional outcomes
selected_rules.source_statement: Radical Prostatectomy should aim at achieving a negative margin, while ensuring a balance between margin rates and functional outcomes

selected_rules.activation_condition: radical prostatectomy is being performed
selected_rules.execute: consider sparing the neurovascular bundles as the standard approach except in high-risk patients
selected_rules.source_statement: Sparing of the neurovascular bundles should be considered the "standard approach" except for high-risk patients.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man with biopsy-proven localized prostate adenocarcinoma elected radical prostatectomy after counseling about management options and his preference for surgery. He has intermediate-risk disease, good baseline urinary control, and preserved erectile function. In the operating room, the surgeon is beginning the radical prostatectomy and plans to preserve continence and sexual function when oncologically appropriate. The team discusses the operative goals, including complete removal of the cancer-containing organ and achieving negative margins while balancing functional outcomes. Because the patient is not considered high-risk, nerve-sparing is being considered as part of the standard approach. The specimen is then handed off for routine pathologic processing.
recommended_actions: Aim to eradicate the cancer-containing organ with negative surgical margins.
Balance margin rates with functional outcomes.
Consider sparing the neurovascular bundles as the standard approach, since the patient is not high-risk.
source_case.rationale: This vignette describes a patient undergoing radical prostatectomy, which triggers operative goals related to negative margins and functional preservation. The patient is not high-risk, so neurovascular bundle sparing is appropriately considered. No frozen section or apex-specific processing details are included, and no radiation referral is indicated because the patient is intermediate-risk and already selected surgery.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man with biopsy-proven localized prostate adenocarcinoma elected radical prostatectomy after counseling about management options and his preference for surgery. He has intermediate-risk disease, good baseline urinary control, and preserved erectile function. In the operating room, the surgeon is beginning the radical prostatectomy and plans to preserve continence and sexual function when oncologically appropriate. The specimen is sent for routine pathologic processing, but the team also requests an intraoperative frozen section on the apical soft tissue because the preoperative MRI suggested the lesion comes close to the apex. The operative note emphasizes complete removal of the cancer-containing organ and maintaining negative margins while balancing functional outcomes. Because the patient is not considered high-risk, nerve-sparing is being considered as part of the standard approach.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still centers on an intermediate-risk man undergoing radical prostatectomy with the same operative goals and consideration of nerve-sparing, but adds an intraoperative frozen section request for margin status.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with frozen section analysis of the radical prostatectomy specimen to assess margin status.
Use intraoperative margin assessment to guide additional tissue resection.
Continue the standard nerve-sparing prostatectomy plan without further discussion.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for frozen section use: routine frozen section analysis of the radical prostatectomy specimen for margin status is not recommended. The safer interpretation is that the operation should proceed with standard pathologic processing rather than intraoperative frozen-section margin assessment.
challenging_examples.rationale: The vignette closely matches the surgical context, but the added frozen section request directly conflicts with the recommendation against frozen section analysis for margin status in radical prostatectomy specimens.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man with biopsy-proven localized prostate adenocarcinoma elected radical prostatectomy after counseling about management options and his preference for surgery. He has intermediate-risk disease, good baseline urinary control, and preserved erectile function. In the operating room, the surgeon is beginning the radical prostatectomy and plans to preserve continence and sexual function when oncologically appropriate. During specimen handling, the apex is submitted separately, and the grossing team plans an en face shave of the apical margin because the tissue is thin and difficult to orient. The operative goals remain complete removal of the cancer-containing organ with negative margins while balancing functional outcomes. Because the patient is not considered high-risk, nerve-sparing is being considered as part of the standard approach.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The overall case mirrors a routine radical prostatectomy in an intermediate-risk patient with standard functional-preservation goals, but the specimen workflow now includes an en face shave technique at the apex.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Submit the apex using an en face shave technique to simplify margin assessment.
Interpret the apical margin with the shave specimen as the standard approach.
Proceed with nerve-sparing because the patient is not high-risk.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant because an en face shave technique at the apex is discouraged due to the risk of false-positive margin interpretation. The safer interpretation is to use an apical processing method that avoids this pitfall and supports more accurate margin assessment.
challenging_examples.rationale: The case preserves the same prostatectomy context and operative intent, but the apex-specific specimen processing detail invalidates the recommendation by introducing a discouraged margin-assessment technique.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has porphyria or known allergy to porphyrins
selected_rules.execute: Do not use PDT
selected_rules.source_statement: There are a number of contraindications for the use of PDT in patients with lung cancer. These include porphyria or known allergies to porphyrins.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Tumor erodes into a major blood vessel
selected_rules.execute: Do not use PDT
selected_rules.source_statement: There are a number of contraindications for the use of PDT in patients with lung cancer. These include ... tumours that erode into a major blood vessel.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Existing tracheoesophageal fistula
selected_rules.execute: Do not use PDT
selected_rules.source_statement: There are a number of contraindications for the use of PDT in patients with lung cancer. These include ... existing tracheoesophageal fistulas.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient is pregnant, nursing, or a child
selected_rules.execute: Do not use Photofrin because its use has not been established
selected_rules.source_statement: Its use in pregnant women, nursing women, and children has not been established, and, therefore, Photofrin® is not recommended for use in those cases (1).

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient receives endobronchial PDT
selected_rules.execute: Perform toilet bronchoscopy after treatment and repeat as indicated
selected_rules.source_statement: Toilet bronchoscopies should always be completed following endobronchial PDT to minimize the risk of bronchial obstruction, and the risk of hemoptysis should be considered prior to therapy and monitored post therapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient receives PDT
selected_rules.execute: Monitor closely after the procedure for airway obstruction
selected_rules.source_statement: Since tumour necrosis can result in post-treatment airway obstruction, patients should be closely monitored after undergoing the procedure and toilet bronchoscopies repeated as indicated.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient receives Photofrin-based PDT
selected_rules.execute: Advise avoidance of direct sunlight and brightly focused indoor light for at least the photosensitivity period
selected_rules.source_statement: Patients must avoid exposure of eyes and skin to direct sunlight or brightly focused indoor light.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with primary NSCLC and medically inoperable early-stage disease accessible by bronchoscopy
selected_rules.execute: Consider photodynamic therapy as an option
selected_rules.source_statement: Photodynamic therapy could be considered as an option for the treatment of early stage lung cancer in patients with medically inoperable disease that is accessible by bronchoscopy.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 72-year-old man with severe COPD and poor pulmonary reserve has a newly diagnosed 1.8 cm endobronchial squamous cell carcinoma in the right upper lobe bronchus. He is medically inoperable, and bronchoscopy shows the lesion is accessible endobronchially without evidence of invasion into a major vessel or a tracheoesophageal fistula. He has no history of porphyria or porphyrin allergy. Because the tumor is early stage and bronchoscopically accessible, photodynamic therapy is being considered. The team plans treatment with Photofrin-based PDT and discusses the need for post-procedure airway surveillance. The patient also reports he works outdoors and will need clear instructions about light precautions after therapy.
recommended_actions: Consider photodynamic therapy as an option for this medically inoperable early-stage NSCLC that is accessible by bronchoscopy.
After PDT, closely monitor the patient for airway obstruction due to tumour necrosis.
Perform toilet bronchoscopy after endobronchial PDT and repeat as indicated to reduce the risk of bronchial obstruction.
If Photofrin-based PDT is used, advise avoidance of direct sunlight and brightly focused indoor light for at least the photosensitivity period.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches the rule for medically inoperable early-stage primary NSCLC accessible by bronchoscopy, supporting consideration of PDT. It also triggers the endobronchial PDT follow-up recommendations: toilet bronchoscopy and close monitoring for airway obstruction. Because Photofrin-based PDT is used, photosensitivity precautions are also indicated. No contraindication rules are triggered.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 72-year-old man with severe COPD and poor pulmonary reserve has a newly diagnosed 1.8 cm endobronchial squamous cell carcinoma in the right upper lobe bronchus. He is medically inoperable, and bronchoscopy shows the lesion is visible and near the segmental airway but cannot be reached with the therapeutic bronchoscope because of a sharp angulation. There is no evidence of invasion into a major vessel or a tracheoesophageal fistula, and he has no history of porphyria or porphyrin allergy. The team is reviewing local treatment options for this early lung cancer and briefly considers Photofrin-based photodynamic therapy, with post-procedure airway surveillance discussed if it is pursued. He also works outdoors and asks about light precautions after therapy.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like medically inoperable early-stage endobronchial NSCLC being evaluated for PDT, but the lesion is not actually accessible by bronchoscopy.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with photodynamic therapy because the tumor is early stage and endobronchial.
Schedule Photofrin-based PDT with routine airway monitoring afterward.
Provide standard post-PDT light-avoidance instructions and arrange toilet bronchoscopy.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for considering PDT because the lesion cannot be reached endobronchially. The safer interpretation is that bronchoscopy-based PDT is not an appropriate local option here, so other therapies should be reviewed.
challenging_examples.rationale: The recommendation applies to medically inoperable early-stage primary NSCLC that is accessible by bronchoscopy. Lack of bronchoscopic accessibility invalidates the conditional consideration for PDT.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 72-year-old man with severe COPD and poor pulmonary reserve has a newly diagnosed 1.8 cm endobronchial squamous cell carcinoma in the right upper lobe bronchus. He is medically inoperable, and bronchoscopy confirms the lesion is accessible endobronchially. However, CT angiography shows subtle erosion into the adjacent right upper lobe pulmonary artery branch, without a tracheoesophageal fistula. He has no history of porphyria or porphyrin allergy. The team initially discusses Photofrin-based photodynamic therapy for this early-stage tumor and mentions post-procedure airway surveillance and light precautions if treatment is selected.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The vignette preserves the same early endobronchial, medically inoperable lung cancer setup, but adds major-vessel erosion that makes PDT inappropriate.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer photodynamic therapy for local control of the accessible endobronchial lesion.
Plan endobronchial PDT followed by toilet bronchoscopy and close monitoring.
Use Photofrin-based PDT with routine photosensitivity counseling.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for PDT because the tumor erodes into a major blood vessel, which is a contraindication. A safer interpretation is to avoid PDT and consider alternative management for local control.
challenging_examples.rationale: The rule against PDT is triggered by tumor erosion into a major blood vessel. That contraindication overrides the otherwise similar early-stage, bronchoscopically accessible context.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with stage I testicular seminoma and proposed risk-adapted surveillance based on tumour size or rete testis invasion
selected_rules.execute: Do not use a risk-adapted approach as the basis for management
selected_rules.source_statement: Thus, a risk-adapted approach cannot be recommended at this time.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with stage I testicular seminoma after orchidectomy
selected_rules.execute: Use surveillance as the preferred post-orchidectomy management strategy
selected_rules.source_statement: The DSG recommends surveillance as the preferred option, because adjuvant therapy is associated with important short and long-term toxicities and second malignancy risks with no evidence of improved survival.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with stage I testicular seminoma on primary surveillance
selected_rules.execute: Follow a minimum surveillance program with physical examination every 3 to 4 months, chest X-ray every 6 to 12 months, and CT abdomen/pelvis every 3 to 4 months for the first 3 years, then less often thereafter
selected_rules.source_statement: The minimum surveillance program should be a physical examination every three to four months, chest X-ray every six to twelve months, and computerised tomography (CT) of the abdomen and pelvis every three to four months in the first three years and then less often thereafter.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with stage I testicular seminoma on primary surveillance
selected_rules.execute: Include follow-up investigations of sites at risk of relapse
selected_rules.source_statement: In addition, follow-up should include appropriate investigations of sites at risk of relapse.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with stage I testicular seminoma who prefers immediate treatment or is unsuitable for primary surveillance
selected_rules.execute: Offer adjuvant radiotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients who prefer immediate treatment, or who are unsuitable for primary surveillance, adjuvant RT is the recommended option.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with stage I testicular seminoma receiving adjuvant radiotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Use a radiotherapy dose of at least 20 Gy and no more than 30 Gy
selected_rules.source_statement: When adjuvant RT is the preferred option, a radiation dose of at least 20 Gy and no more than 30 Gy is recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with stage I testicular seminoma treated with adjuvant therapy
selected_rules.execute: Continue post-treatment monitoring for relapse; except after extended-field radiotherapy, follow-up should be as thorough as surveillance
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients treated with adjuvant therapy, post treatment monitoring for disease relapse is still necessary. Except in the specific case of extended-field radiotherapy, the follow-up after adjuvant therapy should be as thorough as the surveillance conducted in the absence of adjuvant therapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with stage I testicular seminoma and planned adjuvant therapy with fertility concerns
selected_rules.execute: Offer sperm banking; if radiotherapy is used, offer scrotal shielding
selected_rules.source_statement: If adjuvant therapy is planned, sperm banking (and scrotal shielding with RT) should be offered if future fertility is of concern to the patient.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old man underwent right radical orchidectomy for a 2.1 cm pure seminoma confined to the testis, with negative margins and no metastatic disease on staging scans. He asks whether the size of the tumour and the absence of rete testis invasion could justify a less intensive follow-up plan. He is otherwise healthy and is considering options for post-orchidectomy management. After discussion, he is told that surveillance is the preferred approach after orchidectomy, and that management should not be based on a risk-adapted strategy using tumour size or rete testis invasion. He agrees to proceed with surveillance and understands the need for regular follow-up visits and imaging.
recommended_actions: Use surveillance as the preferred post-orchidectomy management strategy.
Do not use a risk-adapted approach as the basis for management.
source_case.rationale: This case matches adult stage I testicular seminoma after orchidectomy, triggering the recommendation for surveillance. The vignette also explicitly raises tumour size and rete testis invasion as the basis for tailoring follow-up, which triggers the rule against using a risk-adapted approach.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old man underwent right radical orchidectomy for a 2.1 cm pure seminoma. Final pathology shows the tumor is confined to the testis with negative margins, but staging CT reveals a 1.4 cm enlarged aortocaval lymph node that is considered suspicious but not yet biopsy-proven. He asks whether the absence of rete testis invasion could justify a less intensive follow-up plan. He is otherwise healthy and is considering options for post-orchidectomy management. After discussion, he is told that surveillance is the preferred approach after orchidectomy, and that management should not be based on a risk-adapted strategy using tumour size or rete testis invasion. He agrees to proceed with surveillance and understands the need for regular follow-up visits and imaging.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case preserves the same age, histology, tumor size, orchiectomy context, and discussion of rete testis invasion, but adds a subtly abnormal staging node that moves the patient away from true stage I disease.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start stage I surveillance with routine follow-up after orchidectomy
Avoid adjuvant treatment because the primary tumor is small and rete testis invasion is absent
Use tumor size and rete testis invasion to decide on a reduced-intensity follow-up schedule
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for stage I seminoma because the suspicious retroperitoneal node makes the disease no longer confined to the testis on standard staging. The stage I surveillance recommendation and the statement against risk-adapted management based on tumour size or rete testis invasion do not apply in the same way here. The safer interpretation is that this requires reassessment of staging and management for possible metastatic seminoma rather than routine stage I surveillance.
challenging_examples.rationale: The source recommendation applies to adult stage I seminoma after orchidectomy. Here, the added suspicious aortocaval node invalidates stage I status, so the original surveillance recommendation and the anti-risk-adapted statement are no longer directly applicable.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old man underwent right radical orchidectomy for a 2.1 cm pure seminoma confined to the testis, with negative margins and no metastatic disease on staging scans. He asks whether the size of the tumour and the absence of rete testis invasion could justify a less intensive follow-up plan. He is otherwise healthy and is considering options for post-orchidectomy management. Because he is worried about frequent clinic visits, he says he would prefer adjuvant treatment instead of surveillance. After discussion, he is told that surveillance is the preferred approach after orchidectomy, and that management should not be based on a risk-adapted strategy using tumour size or rete testis invasion. He agrees to proceed with surveillance and understands the need for regular follow-up visits and imaging.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The presentation remains classic stage I seminoma after orchiectomy with the same tumor features and follow-up discussion, but the patient’s preference is shifted toward immediate treatment rather than surveillance.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Recommend adjuvant radiotherapy because he wants treatment now
Choose a reduced-intensity follow-up plan based on tumor size and rete testis invasion
Frame surveillance as optional rather than the preferred post-orchidectomy strategy
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This vignette is not concordant with the surveillance-preferred recommendation because the patient no longer prefers primary surveillance; he is explicitly requesting immediate treatment. In that situation, the relevant alternative recommendation is adjuvant radiotherapy rather than surveillance, and the case should not be managed as a routine surveillance-only scenario. The risk-adapted approach based on tumour size or rete testis invasion still should not guide management.
challenging_examples.rationale: The original recommendation for surveillance applies after orchidectomy in stage I seminoma, but the adjuvant RT recommendation is triggered when a patient prefers immediate treatment or is unsuitable for surveillance. That preference change makes the source recommendation discordant.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering routine allogeneic transplantation for multiple myeloma
selected_rules.execute: Do not recommend allogeneic transplantation as routine therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: There is insufficient evidence to recommend allogeneic transplantation as routine therapy for multiple myeloma.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient may be eligible for transplantation and stem cells have not yet been collected
selected_rules.execute: Avoid extensive exposure to alkylating agents such as melphalan before stem cell collection
selected_rules.source_statement: Patients who are potentially eligible for transplantation should be referred for transplant assessment early after diagnosis and should not be given extensive exposure to alkylating agents such as melphalan prior to the collection of stem cells.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering tandem transplantation outside a clinical trial
selected_rules.execute: Do not use two transplants performed in succession outside a clinical trial
selected_rules.source_statement: There is insufficient evidence to recommend a treatment plan that includes two transplants performed in succession (tandem transplantation) outside of a clinical trial.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Newly diagnosed patient is being considered for thalidomide
selected_rules.execute: Use thalidomide only in the context of a clinical trial
selected_rules.source_statement: Thalidomide should only be used in newly diagnosed patients in the context of a clinical trial.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering planned double (tandem) autologous stem cell transplantation
selected_rules.execute: Do not recommend planned tandem ASCT on current evidence
selected_rules.source_statement: Planned double (tandem) ASCT cannot be recommended on the current evidence.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering purging or selection of harvested stem cells
selected_rules.execute: Do not use currently available purging methods routinely
selected_rules.source_statement: Currently available methods of purging have not demonstrated clinical benefit and are not, therefore, recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with advanced-stage multiple myeloma and good performance status
selected_rules.execute: Recommend autologous transplantation
selected_rules.source_statement: Autologous transplantation is recommended for patients with advanced-stage myeloma and good performance status.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient is under 65 years old, has no significant renal dysfunction, and has had hydration and remission-induction chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Proceed with autologous transplantation; evidence is strongest in this group
selected_rules.source_statement: The evidence is strongest for patients under 65 years of age without significant renal dysfunction following hydration and remission-induction chemotherapy.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man is newly diagnosed with IgG multiple myeloma after presenting with fatigue, anemia, lytic bone lesions, and mild hypercalcemia. He has ECOG performance status 1, normal creatinine, and no major comorbidities. After hydration and initial remission-induction chemotherapy, his disease burden has improved. He asks whether he should undergo stem cell transplant and whether he should receive a planned second transplant because a friend told him that “more is better.” His oncologist is also considering adding thalidomide to the upfront regimen and wonders if stem cells should be purged before collection. Stem cells have not yet been collected.
recommended_actions: Proceed with autologous transplantation; evidence is strongest in patients under 65 years old without significant renal dysfunction after hydration and remission-induction chemotherapy.
Refer for transplant assessment early and avoid extensive exposure to alkylating agents such as melphalan before stem cell collection.
Do not recommend planned tandem transplantation outside a clinical trial.
Use thalidomide only in the context of a clinical trial.
Do not use currently available stem cell purging methods routinely.
source_case.rationale: This patient matches the strongest evidence group for autologous transplantation: age under 65, preserved renal function, and completion of hydration plus induction therapy. Because stem cells have not yet been collected and he may be transplant-eligible, exposure to alkylating agents should be limited before collection. The vignette also raises two practices that are not routinely recommended on current evidence: planned tandem transplantation outside a trial and routine purging of harvested stem cells. Thalidomide should only be used in newly diagnosed patients within a clinical trial.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 66-year-old man is newly diagnosed with IgG multiple myeloma after presenting with fatigue, anemia, and multiple lytic bone lesions.
