He has ECOG performance status 1 and normal creatinine, and he has just completed hydration plus initial remission-induction chemotherapy with a good partial response. His oncologist is discussing whether to move forward with autologous stem cell transplantation now, and the patient asks whether a planned second transplant would improve his odds. Stem cells have not yet been collected. Because he is otherwise fit, the team is also considering adding thalidomide to the upfront regimen and wonders whether harvested stem cells should be purged before storage.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The workflow, disease stage, performance status, renal function, and transplant discussion closely mirror a typical transplant-eligible myeloma case, but he is just over the age group with the strongest evidence for autologous transplantation.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed directly with autologous transplantation as the preferred approach
Plan a tandem autologous transplant because he is fit and responding well
Add thalidomide to the initial regimen
Use a stem cell purging strategy before cryopreservation
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the strongest evidence-based transplant group because the patient is 66, which places him outside the under-65 subgroup where evidence is strongest. He remains potentially transplant-eligible, but the decision should be individualized rather than presented as a straightforward guideline-supported recommendation for immediate autologous transplantation. The other questioned strategies remain unsupported on current evidence.
challenging_examples.rationale: The near-miss is age: everything else aligns with the source case, but he is no longer under 65. That makes the specific strongest-evidence recommendation for autologous transplantation inapplicable as written, even though transplant evaluation may still be reasonable.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman is newly diagnosed with IgG multiple myeloma after presenting with fatigue, anemia, lytic bone lesions, and mild hypercalcemia. She has ECOG performance status 1 and normal renal function after hydration, and she has already completed one cycle of remission-induction chemotherapy. Her disease is improving, and the team is discussing whether she should proceed to autologous stem cell transplantation. She asks whether a planned second transplant would be worthwhile, and the oncologist is considering thalidomide as part of the upfront regimen. Stem cells have not yet been collected, but collection is being delayed because the patient had a recent mobilization failure after a prior course of melphalan-containing therapy at an outside hospital.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The overall presentation, age, performance status, and transplant planning are very close to the source vignette, but she has already had substantial alkylator exposure before stem cell collection.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with autologous transplantation without concern for prior therapy
Reinforce a planned tandem transplant because the disease is newly diagnosed
Use thalidomide in the induction regimen
Move ahead with routine stem cell purging
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the recommendation to limit alkylating agent exposure before stem cell collection. Because she has already received melphalan and stem cells have not yet been collected, the safer interpretation is that she may now have compromised stem cell mobilization and needs reassessment of collection strategy rather than routine continuation of alkylator-heavy therapy. The case still raises nonrecommended tandem transplantation, thalidomide use, and purging, but the key mismatch is prior alkylator exposure before collection.
challenging_examples.rationale: The case is intentionally close to a transplant-eligible myeloma vignette, but the prior melphalan exposure invalidates the recommendation to avoid extensive alkylator treatment before stem cell harvest. That subtle change makes the original transplant-planning advice no longer directly applicable.

selected_rules.activation_condition: accessory labeled single use or disposable
selected_rules.execute: do not reprocess the accessory
selected_rules.source_statement: Accessories labeled as either single use or disposable should not be reprocessed.

selected_rules.activation_condition: withdrawal time as a quality metric
selected_rules.execute: do not require a specific withdrawal time target or audit withdrawal time for quality assurance
selected_rules.source_statement: It is not necessary to achieve a specific withdrawal time target or to audit this indicator for quality assurance purposes.

selected_rules.activation_condition: gastroenterologist seeking credentialing
selected_rules.execute: complete a formal two-year subspecialty training program, with an option for a third year, before entering full-time practice
selected_rules.source_statement: To be considered for credentialing, gastroenterologists must complete a formal two-year subspecialty training program, with the option of a third year of subspecialty training, before entering full-time practice.

selected_rules.activation_condition: other physician including surgical resident seeking qualification for colonoscopy
selected_rules.execute: acquire the necessary specific knowledge and technical training in colonoscopy over at least six months
selected_rules.source_statement: Prior to being qualified, other physicians, including surgical residents, must acquire the necessary specific knowledge and technical training in colonoscopy over a period of at least six months.

selected_rules.activation_condition: trainee being assessed for competence
selected_rules.execute: ensure average independent cecal intubation rate is at least 85% for all colonoscopies
selected_rules.source_statement: To be considered competent colonoscopists, trainees should achieve an average independent cecal intubation rate (CIR) of at least 85% for all colonoscopies and are expected to have performed at least 300 colonoscopies during training.

selected_rules.activation_condition: trainee completing colonoscopy training
selected_rules.execute: ensure at least 300 colonoscopies are performed during training
selected_rules.source_statement: To be considered competent colonoscopists, trainees should achieve an average independent cecal intubation rate (CIR) of at least 85% for all colonoscopies and are expected to have performed at least 300 colonoscopies during training.

selected_rules.activation_condition: trainee completing 300 colonoscopies during training
selected_rules.execute: ensure at least 50 polypectomies are anticipated during training
selected_rules.source_statement: If 300 colonoscopies are performed during training, it is anticipated that at least 50 polypectomies would have been performed.

selected_rules.activation_condition: trainee completing training
selected_rules.execute: assess cognitive competence including contraindications, indications, screening and surveillance intervals, histologic classification of polyps, and management of findings
selected_rules.source_statement: In addition to proficiency in the technical aspects of colonoscopy, proficiency in cognitive aspects of the procedure is essential, including knowledge of appropriate contraindications and indications for colonoscopy, application of appropriate screening and surveillance intervals (4), histologic classification of polyps and their significance, and knowledge of how to deal with findings encountered at the time of colonoscopy.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old surgical resident is rotating through gastroenterology and asks to be signed off for independent colonoscopy practice before starting a community job next month. He has completed a short endoscopy elective and has assisted on several procedures, but his supervising physician notes that he has not yet completed a structured colonoscopy training period. During review, the resident says he is comfortable with basic scope handling but has not independently performed enough procedures to demonstrate consistent performance, and he is still learning indications, contraindications, and surveillance intervals.
recommended_actions: Acquire the necessary specific knowledge and technical training in colonoscopy over a period of at least six months before being qualified.

Assess cognitive competence, including contraindications, indications, screening and surveillance intervals, histologic classification of polyps, and management of findings encountered at colonoscopy.
source_case.rationale: The vignette describes an other physician/surgical resident seeking qualification for colonoscopy, which triggers the recommendation for at least six months of specific knowledge and technical training. It also describes a trainee completing training with incomplete cognitive knowledge, triggering assessment of the cognitive competencies required for colonoscopy.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old surgical resident is rotating through gastroenterology and asks to be signed off for independent colonoscopy practice before starting a community job next month. He has completed a short endoscopy elective and has assisted on several procedures, and his supervising physician notes that he is just finishing a structured colonoscopy training period. During review, the resident says he is comfortable with basic scope handling and has independently performed a substantial number of colonoscopies, but his case log shows a cecal intubation rate below the expected benchmark. He also admits that he is still unsure about a few screening and surveillance intervals and the management of some findings encountered at colonoscopy.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This remains a colonoscopy trainee near the end of training with apparently substantial hands-on experience and ongoing assessment of competence.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Sign him off for independent colonoscopy practice based on his procedural volume and apparent progress.
Focus only on the fact that he has completed many colonoscopies and overlook the remaining competency gaps.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant because competence requires both technical and cognitive proficiency. His independent cecal intubation rate is below the required threshold, and he still lacks full cognitive mastery of screening, surveillance, and management questions. The safer interpretation is that he needs further supervised training and reassessment before independent practice.
challenging_examples.rationale: The case deliberately resembles a qualifying trainee, but the benchmark cecal intubation rate is not met and cognitive competence remains incomplete, so the competence criteria are not satisfied.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old surgical resident is rotating through gastroenterology and asks to be signed off for independent colonoscopy practice before starting a community job next month. He has completed a short endoscopy elective and has assisted on several procedures, and his supervising physician notes that he has now completed a structured colonoscopy training period. During review, the resident says he is comfortable with basic scope handling and has reached the expected number of procedures during training, but the logbook shows only a handful of polypectomies. He can discuss indications and contraindications well, yet he has limited experience managing polyps and other lesions encountered during colonoscopy.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This looks like a trainee who has completed formal colonoscopy training with adequate case volume and otherwise solid knowledge.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Treat him as fully qualified for independent colonoscopy practice.
Approve credentialing without further attention to his therapeutic endoscopy experience.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant because completing 300 colonoscopies during training is expected to be accompanied by at least 50 polypectomies. Although he appears technically and cognitively prepared in other respects, his therapeutic experience is insufficient. The safer interpretation is that he still needs additional supervised exposure to polypectomy before being considered fully competent.
challenging_examples.rationale: The case preserves the overall training scenario but undermines the polypectomy requirement, so the expected training profile is incomplete.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has limited-stage SCLC, WHO performance status 0 to 2, and disease has not progressed on first-line treatment
selected_rules.execute: Offer prophylactic cranial irradiation at 25 Gy in 10 fractions
selected_rules.source_statement: Offer prophylactic cranial irradiation at a dose of 25 Gy in 10 fractions to people with limited-stage disease SCLC and World Health Organization (WHO) performance status 0 to 2, if their disease has not progressed on first-line treatment.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has extensive-stage SCLC and has had a partial or complete response to chemotherapy within the thorax and at distant sites
selected_rules.execute: Consider thoracic radiotherapy with prophylactic cranial irradiation
selected_rules.source_statement: Consider thoracic radiotherapy with prophylactic cranial irradiation for people with extensive-stage disease SCLC who have had a partial or complete response to chemotherapy within the thorax and at distant sites.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has extensive-stage SCLC, WHO performance status 0 to 2, and disease has responded to first-line treatment
selected_rules.execute: Consider prophylactic cranial irradiation at 25 Gy in 10 fractions
selected_rules.source_statement: Consider prophylactic cranial irradiation at a dose of 25 Gy in 10 fractions for people with extensive-stage disease SCLC and WHO performance status 0 to 2, if their disease has responded to first-line treatment.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has extensive-stage SCLC and has responded to treatment
selected_rules.execute: Consider PCI because the recommendation strength is not strong
selected_rules.source_statement: The committee agreed that "consider" is the appropriate strength for the recommendation on PCI.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has extensive-stage SCLC and has responded to treatment
selected_rules.execute: Consider PCI when benefits are judged to probably outweigh harms, acknowledging uncertainty
selected_rules.source_statement: The Working Group members agreed that the benefits are equal to or probably outweigh the harms, but they had some uncertainty about this. Therefore, they agreed with the NICE committee that the recommendation should not be strong and using the word "consider" was appropriate.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has SCLC without brain metastases and the question is whether to use prophylactic cranial irradiation instead of routine MRI follow-up
selected_rules.execute: Support further research comparing prophylactic cranial irradiation with routine magnetic resonance imaging follow-up
selected_rules.source_statement: What is the effectiveness and cost effectiveness of prophylactic cranial irradiation compared with routine magnetic resonance imaging follow-up in people with SCLC without brain metastases?

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering prophylactic cranial irradiation for SCLC
selected_rules.execute: Do not add extra recommendations beyond the endorsed NICE recommendations
selected_rules.source_statement: The Working Group members decided to endorse all of the recommendations from NICE without any modifications.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering prophylactic cranial irradiation for SCLC
selected_rules.execute: Use the endorsed recommendations as written because the Working Group endorsed them without modifications
selected_rules.source_statement: The Working Group members decided to endorse all of the recommendations from NICE without any modifications.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 63-year-old man with limited-stage small cell lung cancer completed four cycles of platinum-etoposide chemotherapy followed by concurrent thoracic radiotherapy. He has WHO performance status 1, no neurologic symptoms, and post-treatment CT shows no progression of disease. Brain MRI shows no metastases. He is recovering well, with mild fatigue but otherwise stable weight and appetite. At follow-up, the oncology team discusses next steps to reduce the risk of future brain metastases. The patient asks whether any additional treatment is recommended now that his cancer has not progressed on first-line therapy.
recommended_actions: Offer prophylactic cranial irradiation at 25 Gy in 10 fractions.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches limited-stage SCLC, WHO performance status 0 to 2, and no progression after first-line treatment, which directly triggers the recommendation for prophylactic cranial irradiation at 25 Gy in 10 fractions.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 63-year-old man with limited-stage small cell lung cancer completed four cycles of platinum-etoposide chemotherapy followed by concurrent thoracic radiotherapy. He has WHO performance status 1, no neurologic symptoms, and post-treatment CT shows no progression of disease. A brain MRI done for new intermittent headaches now shows a single 6 mm enhancing cerebellar lesion suspicious for metastasis, with no other intracranial disease. He is recovering well, with mild fatigue but otherwise stable weight and appetite. At follow-up, the oncology team discusses next steps to reduce the risk of future brain metastases. The patient asks whether any additional treatment is recommended now that his cancer has not progressed on first-line therapy.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like limited-stage SCLC after first-line chemoradiotherapy with good performance status and a follow-up brain MRI, but the MRI is no longer negative.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer prophylactic cranial irradiation at 25 Gy in 10 fractions.
Proceed directly to cranial irradiation for prevention without further intracranial workup.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for prophylactic cranial irradiation because the patient already has a brain metastasis on MRI. PCI is for patients without brain metastases after first-line treatment. The safer interpretation is that he needs management of established intracranial metastatic disease, not prophylactic treatment.
challenging_examples.rationale: Rule R001 requires limited-stage SCLC, WHO performance status 0 to 2, and no progression after first-line treatment, with no brain metastases. The new MRI finding breaks the no-brain-metastases condition.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old woman with extensive-stage small cell lung cancer completed four cycles of platinum-etoposide chemotherapy and had a good partial response in the thorax and liver. She has WHO performance status 1 and is feeling better, with improved cough and appetite. Brain MRI shows no metastases. At follow-up, the team reviews whether any additional treatment should be considered to reduce the risk of brain relapse, given that she responded to first-line therapy. Her disease remains controlled on imaging, but a chest CT obtained the same day shows a new small pleural-based nodule in the right lower lobe that was not present before treatment, while the original thoracic and liver lesions remain smaller.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The overall setting still resembles responding extensive-stage SCLC with good performance status and a negative brain MRI, but there is a new lesion suggesting the disease has not fully responded.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Consider prophylactic cranial irradiation at 25 Gy in 10 fractions.
Recommend PCI now because the patient has responded to first-line therapy and has no brain metastases.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for PCI under the response-based extensive-stage recommendation because the new pleural nodule means the disease is not clearly responding uniformly to first-line treatment. The safer interpretation is to reassess systemic disease status and confirm whether this represents progression before considering any brain-directed prophylactic treatment.
challenging_examples.rationale: Rule R003 applies to extensive-stage SCLC with WHO performance status 0 to 2 only if the disease has responded to first-line treatment. A new lesion on chest CT undermines the response requirement.

selected_rules.activation_condition: men with high-risk localized prostate cancer
selected_rules.execute: do not use bisphosphonates to reduce the risk of first bone metastasis
selected_rules.source_statement: In men with high-risk localized prostate cancer, bisphosphonates are not recommended to reduce the risk of first bone metastasis.

selected_rules.activation_condition: men with nonmetastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer
selected_rules.execute: do not use denosumab at the bone metastasis-indicated dosage to reduce the risk of first bone metastasis
selected_rules.source_statement: In men with nonmetastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (CRPC), denosumab at the bone metastasis-indicated dosage is not recommended to reduce the risk of first bone metastasis.

selected_rules.activation_condition: creatinine clearance is below 30 mL/min or serum creatinine is above 265 µmol/L in a man considered for zoledronic acid
selected_rules.execute: do not give zoledronic acid
selected_rules.source_statement: ZA is not recommended below a CrCl of 30 mL/min (or SCr >265 µmol/L).

selected_rules.activation_condition: a patient is receiving denosumab and zoledronic acid together
selected_rules.execute: do not give denosumab and zoledronic acid in combination
selected_rules.source_statement: Denosumab and ZA should not be given in combination.

selected_rules.activation_condition: men with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer
selected_rules.execute: use zoledronic acid at the bone metastasis-indicated dosage for preventing or delaying skeletal-related events
selected_rules.source_statement: In men with metastatic CRPC (mCRPC), either ZA (minimally symptomatic or asymptomatic disease) or denosumab (disease independent of symptoms) (both at bone metastasis-indicated dosages) is recommended for preventing or delaying SREs.

selected_rules.activation_condition: men with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer
selected_rules.execute: use denosumab at the bone metastasis-indicated dosage for preventing or delaying skeletal-related events
selected_rules.source_statement: In men with metastatic CRPC (mCRPC), either ZA (minimally symptomatic or asymptomatic disease) or denosumab (disease independent of symptoms) (both at bone metastasis-indicated dosages) is recommended for preventing or delaying SREs.

selected_rules.activation_condition: men with symptomatic metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer
selected_rules.execute: use radium-223 to extend overall survival
selected_rules.source_statement: In men with symptomatic mCRPC, Ra-223 is recommended to extend overall survival.

selected_rules.activation_condition: men with symptomatic metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer and predominantly bony metastases with no visceral metastases or large nodal metastases
selected_rules.execute: use radium-223 to extend overall survival
selected_rules.source_statement: This recommendation applies to men with predominantly bony metastases and no evidence of visceral metastases or large nodal metastases.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with prostate adenocarcinoma is seen after staging workup for a rising PSA and abnormal MRI. Biopsy shows Gleason 9 disease with extracapsular extension, but CT and bone scan show no distant metastases. He has no bone pain, maintains a good performance status, and has not started androgen deprivation therapy yet. His creatinine clearance is 78 mL/min. He asks whether he should start a bone-strengthening medication now to prevent cancer spread to the bones. He is otherwise healthy and takes only tamsulosin for urinary symptoms.
recommended_actions: Do not use bisphosphonates to reduce the risk of first bone metastasis in this patient with high-risk localized prostate cancer.
source_case.rationale: This vignette describes a man with high-risk localized prostate cancer without metastatic disease, which matches the activation condition for R001. The rule specifically states bisphosphonates are not recommended for reducing the risk of first bone metastasis in this setting.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with prostate adenocarcinoma returns after staging for a rising PSA and abnormal MRI. Biopsy shows Gleason 9 disease with extracapsular extension. CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis shows multiple sclerotic lesions in the pelvis and spine, and bone scan confirms osseous metastases; there is no visceral disease. He has new back pain but remains ambulatory, and his performance status is good. He has not started androgen deprivation therapy yet. His creatinine clearance is 78 mL/min. He asks whether he should start a bone-strengthening medication now to prevent cancer-related skeletal complications. He is otherwise healthy and takes only tamsulosin for urinary symptoms.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The patient still has prostate cancer, excellent renal function, and is asking about a bone-strengthening drug, but he now has established metastatic bone disease rather than high-risk localized cancer.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start a bisphosphonate now to reduce the risk of the first bone metastasis
Begin zoledronic acid as preventive therapy because the disease is high risk
Recommend a bone-protective agent to keep the cancer from spreading to bone
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not high-risk localized prostate cancer; he already has metastatic bone involvement. The question is no longer about preventing first bone metastasis, so the original non-recommendation for localized disease does not apply. Management should instead follow metastatic castration-resistant or metastatic hormone-sensitive pathways depending on androgen status and symptoms.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 only addresses men with high-risk localized prostate cancer and advises against bisphosphonates to prevent first bone metastasis. The presence of definite bone metastases invalidates that scenario.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 71-year-old man with prostate adenocarcinoma is reviewed after progression on androgen deprivation therapy and enzalutamide. He has worsening diffuse bone pain requiring opioids, with imaging showing extensive sclerotic skeletal metastases. CT shows a 1.5 cm liver lesion suspicious for metastasis, but no bulky nodal disease. His disease is castration-resistant, and he has not previously received radium-223. He asks whether an injection could help with bone pain and survival. He is otherwise alert, with creatinine clearance of 64 mL/min and no history of pathologic fracture.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still features symptomatic castration-resistant prostate cancer with predominantly bone metastases and no large nodal disease, but there is a subtle visceral metastasis on CT.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer radium-223 for symptom control and survival benefit
Proceed with radium-223 because the metastases are mainly osseous
Choose bone-targeted radiopharmaceutical therapy to extend overall survival
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: Radium-223 is not guideline-concordant here because the patient has suspected visceral metastatic disease in the liver. The recommendation for radium-223 applies to symptomatic mCRPC with predominantly bony metastases and no visceral metastases or large nodal metastases. A treatment plan should be based on the broader metastatic CRPC context rather than this specific radium-223 indication.
challenging_examples.rationale: R008 requires symptomatic mCRPC with predominantly bony metastases and no visceral metastases or large nodal metastases. The suspicious liver lesion breaks that eligibility condition.

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman is in risk category 5 and is age 25
selected_rules.execute: do not start screening from age 25
selected_rules.source_statement: This (Ontario) Working Group, however, did not find justification for screening from age 25 in this group (or any of the other high risk groups).

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman is in target population risk category 1, 2, 3, or 5
selected_rules.execute: perform MRI in addition to mammography
selected_rules.source_statement: MRI in addition to mammography is recommended for women in target population risk categories 1, 2, 3, and 5 above.

selected_rules.activation_condition: women are being screened for high breast cancer risk
selected_rules.execute: screen annually
selected_rules.source_statement: While there is insufficient evidence at this time to make a definitive recommendation regarding the appropriate screening frequency, it is the opinion of the Working Group that women should be screened annually, as this was the frequency typical of the identified studies on which the recommendation for screening is based.

selected_rules.activation_condition: women are being screened for high breast cancer risk
selected_rules.execute: screen from age 30 to 69 years
selected_rules.source_statement: While there is insufficient evidence at this time to make a definitive recommendation regarding the ages of patients who should be screened, it is the opinion of the Working Group that women should be screened annually from 30 to 69 years of age.

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman is in risk category 5 and received at least 20 Gy radiation before age 30
selected_rules.execute: use MRI in addition to mammography because benefits outweigh harms
selected_rules.source_statement: It is the opinion of the Working Group that the benefits of MRI in terms of increased sensitivity outweigh the potential harms of higher recall rates and biopsy rates for all women in risk category 5 who received ≥20 Gy radiation before the age of 30.

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman is in risk category 4 with high risk benign biopsy or previous breast cancer plus breast density at least 50% and family history of breast cancer but not enough to be category 3
selected_rules.execute: consider adding MRI to mammography
selected_rules.source_statement: With respect to risk category 4, there are preliminary data that a subgroup of patients in risk category 4 might benefit from the addition of MRI to mammography (e.g., women who in addition to a high risk benign biopsy or previous breast cancer, also have breast density ≥50% and a family history of breast cancer though insufficient to put them in category 3).

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is in risk category 4
selected_rules.execute: do not make a recommendation for MRI screening based on current evidence
selected_rules.source_statement: The evidence is insufficient to recommend MRI screening for patients in risk category 4.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 25-year-old woman presents to discuss breast cancer screening. She was treated for Hodgkin lymphoma at age 18 with mantle radiation totaling 30 Gy, and her current risk assessment places her in risk category 5. She has no breast symptoms and is anxious because a relative was recently diagnosed with breast cancer. She asks whether she should begin screening now, since she has heard that high-risk women need early imaging. Her clinician reviews her history and confirms that she falls into a high-risk group, but also notes that she is only 25 years old. The discussion focuses on what screening, if any, should be started at this age and whether MRI should be added to mammography later on.
recommended_actions: Do not start screening from age 25.
If/when screening is undertaken in risk category 5 patients who received at least 20 Gy radiation before age 30, use MRI in addition to mammography because the benefits outweigh the potential harms.
source_case.rationale: R001 is triggered because the patient is a woman in risk category 5 and is age 25, and the rule explicitly says not to start screening from age 25. R005 is also triggered because she is in risk category 5 and received at least 20 Gy radiation before age 30; for this group, the Working Group considers MRI in addition to mammography to have benefits that outweigh harms.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 25-year-old woman returns to discuss breast cancer screening. She was treated for Hodgkin lymphoma at age 18 with mantle radiation totaling 18 Gy, and her current risk assessment still places her in a high-risk category because of the prior cancer treatment. She has no breast symptoms and is concerned after a cousin was recently diagnosed with breast cancer. She asks whether she should begin imaging now because she was told some high-risk patients start early. Her clinician reviews the history, confirms she is only 25 years old, and notes that her radiation exposure was below the threshold usually used to justify certain intensified screening strategies. The discussion centers on whether any breast screening should be started at this age and whether MRI should be paired with mammography later on.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case keeps the same age, Hodgkin lymphoma history, and high-risk screening question, but the prior mantle radiation dose is slightly below the level that triggers the intensified MRI-plus-mammography recommendation.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start high-risk breast screening immediately at age 25
Order annual breast MRI in addition to mammography because of her prior chest radiation
Set up a high-risk screening protocol now and treat her as meeting the radiation-based MRI criteria
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the radiation-based MRI recommendation because she received 18 Gy, not at least 20 Gy, before age 30. Her age still does not support starting screening from 25 under the cited guidance. A safer interpretation is that she remains high risk, but the specific MRI-plus-mammography recommendation tied to ≥20 Gy is not met.
challenging_examples.rationale: The vignette is intentionally close to the source case, but the radiation dose falls below the cutoff required for the MRI recommendation. The age-related no-screening-from-25 point remains relevant, but the stronger mismatch is that the exposure threshold for R005 is not satisfied.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 25-year-old woman presents for a breast cancer screening discussion. She was treated for Hodgkin lymphoma at age 18 with mantle radiation totaling 30 Gy, and her risk assessment is otherwise high. She has no breast complaints and asks if she should begin screening now because a family member was recently diagnosed with breast cancer. Her clinician confirms her elevated lifetime risk, but also notes that she is currently 25 and that her treatment history included chest radiation. The patient assumes this means she should start the usual high-risk imaging pathway immediately, including MRI along with mammography, since she has heard that prior radiation can justify earlier surveillance.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The clinical context, age, Hodgkin lymphoma history, and 30 Gy mantle radiation are preserved, but the vignette is framed so that the only decision point is starting screening now at age 25.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Begin breast screening right away because she is high risk
Order MRI plus mammography now due to her prior mantle radiation
Enroll her in the standard early high-risk imaging surveillance pathway
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This case is not guideline-concordant for starting screening at age 25. Even though she meets the risk category and radiation-dose features associated with later intensified screening, the rule explicitly does not justify screening from age 25. The safer interpretation is to defer initiation until the recommended age window rather than start immediately.
challenging_examples.rationale: The subtle mismatch is timing: the patient otherwise fits the high-risk radiation history, but she is still too young for the screening start described in the rule set. The apparent eligibility for MRI does not override the age-based restriction.

selected_rules.activation_condition: A lung cancer survivor develops symptoms suggestive of recurrence
selected_rules.execute: Evaluate according to the symptoms
selected_rules.source_statement: Survivors who develop symptoms suggestive of recurrence, should be evaluated according to those symptoms.

selected_rules.activation_condition: After curative-intent treatment for NSCLC
selected_rules.execute: Schedule follow-up visits with medical history, physical examination, and chest imaging every 3 months in years 1 and 2, every 6 months in year 3, then annually thereafter
selected_rules.source_statement: Following curative-intent treatment for NSCLC, survivors should receive scheduled follow-up visits that include a medical history, physical examination and chest imaging. Clinical evaluations should be conducted every three months in years 1 and 2, every six months in year 3 and annually thereafter.

selected_rules.activation_condition: After curative-intent treatment for SCLC
selected_rules.execute: Schedule follow-up visits with medical history, physical examination, and chest imaging every 3 months in years 1 and 2, every 6 months in year 3, then annually thereafter
selected_rules.source_statement: Following curative-intent treatment for SCLC, survivors should receive scheduled follow-up visits that include a medical history, physical examination and chest imaging. Clinical evaluations should be conducted every three months in years 1 and 2, every six months in year 3 and annually thereafter.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Lung cancer survivor has late side effects of curative-intent therapy
selected_rules.execute: Provide support to help manage symptoms and improve quality of life
selected_rules.source_statement: Health-related quality of life (QoL) is very important for long-term survivors suffering from late side effects of their curative-intent therapy (including surgery, chemotherapy and radiation therapy). The following is a summary of issues reported by survivors. Health care professionals need to aid lung cancer survivors in handling these symptoms to improve QoL.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Lung cancer survivor has completed curative-intent therapy
selected_rules.execute: Provide surveillance follow-up care
selected_rules.source_statement: For lung cancer survivors who have completed curative-intent therapy, surveillance is required and may be provided by specialists, family physicians or hospital-based nurses.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Lung cancer survivor has completed curative-intent therapy for NSCLC or SCLC
selected_rules.execute: Provide smoking cessation counselling
selected_rules.source_statement: Smoking cessation counselling is recommended for patients who have completed curativeintent therapy for NSCLC and SCLC.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Providing smoking cessation advice to a lung cancer survivor
selected_rules.execute: Use behavioural and pharmacotherapy support in addition to verbal advice when possible
selected_rules.source_statement: Although verbal cessation advice from a health care professional is of benefit, interventions that involve behavioural and pharmacotherapy support in addition to verbal advice is recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: A lung cancer survivor has completed curative-intent therapy and ongoing surveillance is being planned
selected_rules.execute: Ensure surveillance includes medical history, physical examination, and chest imaging
selected_rules.source_statement: Following curative-intent treatment for NSCLC, survivors should receive scheduled follow-up visits that include a medical history, physical examination and chest imaging.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man is 18 months after lobectomy and adjuvant chemotherapy for stage II non-small cell lung cancer. He completed curative-intent therapy and has been attending routine surveillance visits. Over the past 3 weeks, he has developed a persistent dry cough, new left-sided chest discomfort, and unintentional weight loss of 4 kg. He also reports increasing fatigue but no fever or infectious symptoms. He continues to smoke about 5 cigarettes per day despite prior quit attempts. On exam, he is thin but in no acute distress; oxygen saturation is 96% on room air, and lung exam is unchanged from baseline. Given the new symptoms in a lung cancer survivor, recurrence must be considered, while he also remains due for ongoing surveillance after curative-intent treatment.
recommended_actions: Evaluate the new cough, chest discomfort, weight loss, and fatigue according to symptoms suggestive of recurrence.
Provide surveillance follow-up care after completion of curative-intent therapy.
Ensure surveillance includes medical history, physical examination, and chest imaging.
Provide smoking cessation counselling.
source_case.rationale: R001 is triggered because this lung cancer survivor has new symptoms suggestive of recurrence. R005 applies because he has completed curative-intent therapy, so surveillance follow-up care is required. R008 applies because ongoing surveillance should include medical history, physical examination, and chest imaging. R006 applies because smoking cessation counselling is recommended after curative-intent therapy for NSCLC.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man is 18 months after lobectomy and adjuvant chemotherapy for stage II non-small cell lung cancer. He completed curative-intent therapy and has been attending routine surveillance visits. Over the past 3 weeks, he has noticed a mild increase in chronic exertional dyspnea and intermittent left chest tightness, but he denies cough, hemoptysis, fever, or weight loss. He reports stable energy and appetite. He continues to smoke about 5 cigarettes per day despite prior quit attempts. On exam, he is thin but in no acute distress; oxygen saturation is 96% on room air, and lung exam is unchanged from baseline. A chest CT obtained 2 weeks ago for routine surveillance showed only expected postoperative changes with no new pulmonary nodules, pleural effusion, or suspicious adenopathy. He remains in survivorship follow-up after curative-intent treatment.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still involves a lung cancer survivor with ongoing surveillance, persistent smoking, and some respiratory complaints, but the current chest imaging is unrevealing and the new symptoms are nonspecific rather than clearly suggestive of recurrence.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Urgently work up the symptoms as probable recurrent lung cancer
Order a full recurrence evaluation despite the recent negative surveillance CT
Escalate to oncologic restaging based on the mild dyspnea and chest tightness
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for symptom-triggered recurrence evaluation because the presented symptoms are mild and nonspecific, and a recent surveillance CT showed no suspicious findings. The safer interpretation is to continue routine post-treatment surveillance and reassess if symptoms progress or new objective abnormalities appear.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 requires symptoms suggestive of recurrence. Here, the complaints are nonspecific and there is no corroborating imaging abnormality, making the recurrence-triggered pathway less clearly applicable.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man is 18 months after lobectomy and adjuvant chemotherapy for stage II non-small cell lung cancer. He completed curative-intent therapy and has been attending routine surveillance visits. Over the past 3 weeks, he has developed a persistent dry cough and increasing fatigue, but his weight has been stable and he denies chest pain, fever, or hemoptysis. He continues to smoke about 5 cigarettes per day despite prior quit attempts. On exam, he is thin but in no acute distress; oxygen saturation is 96% on room air, and lung exam is unchanged from baseline. Surveillance imaging from his last visit revealed a new 6 mm right upper-lobe pulmonary nodule without mediastinal adenopathy, and the radiologist recommended short-interval follow-up rather than immediate restaging. He remains in survivorship follow-up after curative-intent treatment.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The patient still has a prior NSCLC history, ongoing follow-up, smoking, and new respiratory symptoms, but the only imaging abnormality is a tiny indeterminate nodule that was not considered suspicious enough to represent recurrence.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Treat this as definite recurrent disease and initiate a recurrence workup
Assume the cough and fatigue indicate cancer relapse
Proceed directly to oncologic restaging and salvage treatment planning
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This vignette does not clearly meet the recurrence-symptom rule because the symptoms are limited and the imaging finding is an indeterminate small nodule without other evidence of relapse. A safer interpretation is close surveillance with interval reassessment rather than presuming recurrence.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 is intended for survivors with symptoms suggestive of recurrence. The lack of a suspicious imaging pattern and the presence of a small indeterminate nodule make the recurrence assumption premature.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient on active surveillance reaches age 80.
selected_rules.execute: Do not continue serial biopsy past age 80.
selected_rules.source_statement: Serial biopsy should not continue past the age of 80.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has low-risk localized prostate cancer (Gleason score ≤6).
selected_rules.execute: Use active surveillance as the preferred disease management strategy.
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with low-risk (Gleason score ≤6) localized prostate cancer, AS is the preferred disease management strategy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient is on active surveillance for localized prostate cancer.
selected_rules.execute: Include PSA testing every 3 to 6 months.
selected_rules.source_statement: The AS protocol should include the following tests: -PSA test every 3 to 6 months.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient is on active surveillance for localized prostate cancer.
selected_rules.execute: Include digital rectal examination every year.
selected_rules.source_statement: The AS protocol should include the following tests: -DRE every year.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient is on active surveillance for localized prostate cancer.
selected_rules.execute: Perform a 12- to 14-core confirmatory TRUS biopsy including anterior directed cores within 6 to 12 months.
selected_rules.source_statement: The AS protocol should include the following tests: -12-to 14-core confirmatory transrectal ultrasound (TRUS) biopsy (including anterior directed cores) within 6 to 12 months, then serial biopsy a minimum of every 3 to 5 years thereafter.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient is on active surveillance after confirmatory biopsy.
selected_rules.execute: Repeat serial biopsy at least every 3 to 5 years.
selected_rules.source_statement: The AS protocol should include the following tests: -12-to 14-core confirmatory transrectal ultrasound (TRUS) biopsy (including anterior directed cores) within 6 to 12 months, then serial biopsy a minimum of every 3 to 5 years thereafter.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient on active surveillance has rapidly rising PSA, PSA density over 0.2, higher PSA than expected for prostate size, DRE abnormality, or very low PSA free/total ratio.
selected_rules.execute: Investigate further with mpMRI or earlier repeat biopsy.
selected_rules.source_statement: Discordant findings between a patient's clinical course and pathologic findings can include rapidly rising PSA, PSA density over 0.2, higher PSA than expected for prostate size, DRE abnormality, and very low PSA free/total ratio. Presence of these findings requires further investigation with mpMRI or earlier repeat biopsy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient on active surveillance has discordant clinical findings and pathologic findings.
selected_rules.execute: Use mpMRI at entry or during follow-up when indicated.
selected_rules.source_statement: However, being cognizant of both the high cost of mpMRI and its promise, it is recommended that when a patient's clinical findings are discordant with the pathologic findings, a mpMRI is indicated. When indicated, it may be considered at entry or during follow-up.

source_case.patient_vignette: An 80-year-old man with localized prostate cancer diagnosed 4 years ago (Gleason 3+3) has been managed with active surveillance. His PSA has been stable around 5.1 ng/mL, his digital rectal exams have remained normal, and his confirmatory TRUS biopsy 3 years ago showed no upgrading. He feels well and asks whether he still needs scheduled biopsies now that he is older. He has no new urinary symptoms and no concerning changes in PSA kinetics or exam findings.
recommended_actions: Do not continue serial biopsy past age 80.
source_case.rationale: This vignette triggers the rule that serial biopsy should not continue once a patient on active surveillance reaches age 80. No additional discordant findings are present to trigger imaging or earlier repeat biopsy recommendations.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: An 80-year-old man with localized prostate cancer diagnosed 4 years ago (Gleason 3+3) has been on active surveillance. His PSA has been stable around 5.0 ng/mL, and his digital rectal exams have remained normal. A confirmatory TRUS biopsy 3 years ago showed no upgrading. At today’s visit, he reports occasional weak stream but no new bone pain or hematuria. However, his PSA density is now 0.24 based on a prostate volume that has fallen over the past year. He asks whether he can simply continue the same surveillance schedule without further testing because he is already 80.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Same age, same low-risk biopsy history, and same active surveillance follow-up, but the PSA density is now above the discordance threshold.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Stop all further biopsy surveillance because he has reached age 80.
Reassure him and continue routine PSA/DRE follow-up without additional workup.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for stopping serial biopsy solely because he is 80, because the rising PSA density (>0.2) is a discordant finding that warrants further evaluation. The safer interpretation is that he needs mpMRI or earlier repeat biopsy rather than automatic discontinuation of surveillance.
challenging_examples.rationale: The age-based discontinuation rule is overridden by discordant clinical-pathologic findings; PSA density over 0.2 is specifically listed as a trigger for further investigation.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: An 80-year-old man with localized prostate cancer (Gleason 3+3) has been followed on active surveillance for 4 years. His PSA has hovered between 4.8 and 5.4 ng/mL, and his rectal exams have been normal. He had a confirmatory TRUS biopsy 3 years ago that again showed Gleason 3+3 disease. At a routine follow-up he feels well, but the most recent exam documents a new left-sided firmness on DRE. He wonders if, given his age, he can avoid any more biopsies and just keep checking PSA every few months.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like routine surveillance in an 80-year-old with stable low-risk prostate cancer, but the DRE has become abnormal.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Discontinue serial biopsy because he is 80 and continue PSA monitoring only.
Treat this as routine surveillance without additional imaging or biopsy.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for stopping serial biopsy at age 80 because the abnormal DRE is a discordant finding. The safer interpretation is that he needs further evaluation with mpMRI or earlier repeat biopsy rather than no additional assessment.
challenging_examples.rationale: Although age 80 would usually argue against ongoing serial biopsy, the new DRE abnormality meets the rule for discordant findings and requires further investigation.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Document contains only publication/contact and copyright information, with no clinical recommendations.
selected_rules.execute: No clinical action can be extracted.
selected_rules.source_statement: For information about this document, please contact Dr. Eric Winquist, the lead author, through the PEBC via: Phone: 905-527-4322 ext. 42822 Fax: 905 526-6775 E-mail: ccopgi@mcmaster.ca

selected_rules.activation_condition: Document states that the evidence review has been published as a supplement.
selected_rules.execute: No clinical action can be extracted.
selected_rules.source_statement: The evidence review has been published as a Supplement

selected_rules.activation_condition: Document states copyright restrictions on reproduction of the report and illustrations.
selected_rules.execute: No clinical action can be extracted.
selected_rules.source_statement: This report is copyrighted by Cancer Care Ontario; the report and the illustrations herein may not be reproduced without the express written permission of Cancer Care Ontario.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man is referred to oncology after an incidental lung nodule is found on CT performed for persistent cough. At the end of the note, the document includes only publication details and contact information for the lead author, Dr. Eric Winquist, through the PEBC, along with a statement that the evidence review has been published as a supplement. It also notes that the report and illustrations are copyrighted by Cancer Care Ontario and may not be reproduced without written permission. No diagnostic algorithm, treatment recommendation, or follow-up plan is provided in the document excerpt.
recommended_actions: No clinical action can be extracted from this document excerpt.
source_case.rationale: The excerpt contains only publication/contact information, a statement that the evidence review was published as a supplement, and copyright restrictions, with no clinical recommendations. Therefore the triggered rules all map to no actionable clinical guidance.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man is referred to oncology after an incidental lung nodule is found on CT performed for persistent cough. The note attached to the imaging report includes the contact information for Dr. Eric Winquist through the PEBC and mentions that the evidence review was published as a supplement. It also states that the report and illustrations are copyrighted by Cancer Care Ontario and may not be reproduced without written permission. However, this excerpt is embedded within a larger consult note that separately documents a 1.2 cm spiculated right upper lobe nodule and recommends PET-CT and tissue diagnosis. The publication details appear at the end of the page, but no full guideline text is visible in the excerpt.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The vignette preserves the same lung nodule context and still includes PEBC contact details, supplement language, and copyright wording, but adds a clinically actionable consult note outside the excerpt.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Treat this as a no-recommendation document and stop workup.
Assume no diagnostic plan can be extracted from the note.
Ignore the nodule-specific consult recommendations because the excerpt mainly shows publication details.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the no-action rule because the document excerpt is not limited to publication/contact/copyright information; it also contains explicit clinical recommendations elsewhere in the note. The safer interpretation is that the excerpt is incomplete and the actionable imaging/workup plan should be followed from the full note, not dismissed as a nonclinical document.
challenging_examples.rationale: The source rule only applies when the document contains no clinical recommendations. Here, the same publication details are present, but the surrounding note includes an actual management plan, so the rule is invalidated by the added actionable content.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man is referred to oncology after an incidental lung nodule is found on CT performed for persistent cough. The report footer contains the contact information for Dr. Eric Winquist through the PEBC and notes that the evidence review was published as a supplement. It also includes the standard Cancer Care Ontario copyright notice restricting reproduction of the report and illustrations. Just above that footer, however, the radiologist impression states that the nodule is a benign calcified granuloma and explicitly says no further oncologic follow-up is required. The publication details are present, but they are attached to a routine radiology report rather than a guideline excerpt.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case keeps the same incidental lung nodule scenario and the same publication/contact/copyright language, but the clinical finding is a benign calcified granuloma with no oncology follow-up needed.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Refer the patient into an oncology pathway for the lung nodule.
Order PET-CT or biopsy because the document mentions a supplement and PEBC contact information.
Treat the footer text as the main content and ignore the benign radiology impression.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not a no-action guideline excerpt; it is a standard imaging report with a definitive benign interpretation. The safer interpretation is that the nodule does not warrant oncologic workup based on the stated calcified granuloma impression, and the publication/copyright footer is incidental.
challenging_examples.rationale: The rule requires a document containing only publication/contact and copyright information with no clinical guidance. Here, the report includes a clear clinical conclusion that changes management, so the no-recommendation condition is not met.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering granulocyte-colony stimulating factor as routine primary therapy with chemotherapy in this population
selected_rules.execute: Do not use G-CSF routinely as primary therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: There is insufficient evidence to support the routine use of granulocyte-colony stimulating factor as primary therapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering a fractionated weekly CHOP schedule instead of standard CHOP
selected_rules.execute: Use standard CHOP rather than fractionated weekly CHOP
selected_rules.source_statement: No benefits from fractionating the treatment schedule have been detected; the potential for outcomes to be inferior is suggested.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering CHOP plus etoposide in a compressed 14-day schedule
selected_rules.execute: Avoid CHOEP-14 because of excessive toxicity
selected_rules.source_statement: patients receiving CHOEP every 14 days experienced excessive toxicity

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering CHOP plus etoposide versus CHOP alone in older patients
selected_rules.execute: Do not add etoposide routinely to CHOP
selected_rules.source_statement: No differences were detected in response rate, TTF, or OS, and the authors commented that the addition of etoposide was associated with significant toxicity.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering more intensive regimens than CHOP
selected_rules.execute: Avoid more intensive regimens when they add toxicity without survival benefit
selected_rules.source_statement: Two studies testing chemotherapy regimens that are more intensive than CHOP both detected an increase in toxicity with no survival benefit detected.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering second- or third-generation regimens instead of CHOP
selected_rules.execute: Prefer CHOP because it is at least as effective or less toxic
selected_rules.source_statement: Subset analyses comparing CHOP with "second and third generation" regimens show that CHOP is at least as effective or less toxic than these other regimens.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is older than 60 with newly diagnosed advanced-stage aggressive histology non-Hodgkin lymphoma, ECOG less than 4, and no significant comorbid illness
selected_rules.execute: Use CHOP combination chemotherapy with the same dose and schedule as in younger patients
selected_rules.source_statement: Combination chemotherapy with cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine and prednisone (CHOP) is recommended for patients with no apparent cardiac disease or significant comorbidity. Dose and schedule should be the same as that used in younger patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has diffuse large B-cell lymphoma and meets the guideline target population
selected_rules.execute: Add rituximab to CHOP
selected_rules.source_statement: The addition of rituximab to CHOP is recommended for patients with diffuse large B-cell lymphoma.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man presents with newly diagnosed stage IV diffuse large B-cell lymphoma with bulky abdominal nodes, night sweats, and weight loss. His ECOG performance status is 1. He has well-controlled hypertension but no known cardiac disease, heart failure, or other major comorbid illness. Baseline labs show mild anemia and elevated LDH; renal and liver function are acceptable. The oncology team is planning first-line treatment and discusses CHOP-based chemotherapy. Because of his age, they consider whether to intensify treatment, add etoposide, or use a compressed 14-day schedule with growth factor support. The patient asks whether a more aggressive regimen would improve his chances of cure.
recommended_actions: Use CHOP combination chemotherapy with the same dose and schedule as in younger patients.
Add rituximab to CHOP.
source_case.rationale: This patient fits the guideline target population: older than 60, newly diagnosed advanced-stage aggressive histology non-Hodgkin lymphoma, ECOG less than 4, and no significant comorbidity. For this group, CHOP is recommended at the same dose and schedule as in younger patients. Because he has diffuse large B-cell lymphoma, rituximab should be added to CHOP.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 69-year-old man is newly diagnosed with stage IV diffuse large B-cell lymphoma presenting with bulky abdominal adenopathy, drenching night sweats, and weight loss. His ECOG performance status is 1, and he has well-controlled hypertension but no known coronary disease or heart failure. Baseline labs show mild anemia and elevated LDH, with acceptable renal and hepatic function. The oncology team is planning first-line treatment and reviews CHOP-based chemotherapy. Because of his age, they discuss whether to intensify treatment, add etoposide, or use a compressed 14-day schedule with growth factor support. The patient asks whether a more aggressive regimen would improve his chances of cure. On closer review, a pre-treatment echocardiogram done for anthracycline clearance shows a left ventricular ejection fraction of 38%, although he has no overt heart failure symptoms.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Very similar to the source case with the same age, stage IV DLBCL, bulky disease, ECOG 1, and treatment-planning workflow, but the newly found reduced ejection fraction changes anthracycline suitability.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start standard-dose CHOP with rituximab
Proceed with CHOP at the same dose and schedule used in younger patients
Escalate to a more intensive CHOP-based regimen because he is fit
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is no longer guideline-concordant for standard CHOP because the patient has occult cardiac dysfunction with an ejection fraction of 38%, which makes doxorubicin-based therapy unsafe or at least requires reassessment of the regimen. The safer interpretation is to avoid simply applying the older-adult CHOP recommendation and to select a cardiac-sparing approach or further cardiology input before treatment.
challenging_examples.rationale: The source recommendation applies to patients with no apparent cardiac disease or significant comorbidity. A low baseline LVEF invalidates that condition, so the original CHOP recommendation cannot be applied unchanged, even though the lymphoma stage, histology, and performance status remain otherwise similar.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man presents with newly diagnosed stage IV diffuse large B-cell lymphoma with bulky retroperitoneal nodes, night sweats, and weight loss. His ECOG performance status is 1. He has hypertension controlled on medication and no known heart failure or other major comorbid illness. Baseline labs show mild anemia and elevated LDH; renal and liver function are acceptable. The oncology team is planning first-line therapy and discusses CHOP-based chemotherapy. Because of his age, they consider whether to intensify treatment, add etoposide, or use a compressed 14-day schedule with growth factor support. The patient asks whether a more aggressive regimen would improve his chances of cure. A biopsy from the original mass is reported as CD20-negative, and the pathologist notes that the immunophenotype is more consistent with a rare plasmablastic variant than with classic diffuse large B-cell lymphoma.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case preserves the same older patient, advanced-stage aggressive lymphoma, and CHOP-planning context, but the tumor is not CD20-positive DLBCL, which undermines the rituximab recommendation.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Add rituximab to CHOP
Treat this as diffuse large B-cell lymphoma and include rituximab routinely
Use standard CHOP plus rituximab because the clinical presentation is aggressive
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for adding rituximab because the diagnosis is no longer classic diffuse large B-cell lymphoma with CD20 expression. The safer interpretation is that pathology should be reviewed and the regimen tailored to the actual lymphoma subtype rather than automatically adding rituximab.
challenging_examples.rationale: The rituximab recommendation is tied to diffuse large B-cell lymphoma. A CD20-negative plasmablastic-appearing lymphoma falls outside that target population, so the original recommendation does not apply.

selected_rules.activation_condition: HIV-related lymphoma and CD4 count below 50/mm3
selected_rules.execute: Do not add rituximab to chemotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: The addition of rituximab to chemotherapy in patients with CD4 <50/mm 3 is not recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Previously untreated aggressive histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphoma and candidate for curative-intent combination chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Give combination chemotherapy with rituximab
selected_rules.source_statement: Previously untreated patients with aggressive histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphomas who are candidates for treatment with curative intent and will receive combination chemotherapy with curative intent ... should receive this therapy in combination with rituximab.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Previously untreated HIV-related lymphoma, CD4 count at least 50/mm3, and candidate for curative-intent combination chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Give combination chemotherapy with rituximab
selected_rules.source_statement: Previously untreated patients with HIV-related lymphoma with a CD4 count ≥50/mm 3 who are candidates for treatment with curative intent and will receive combination chemotherapy with curative intent ... should receive this therapy in combination with rituximab.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Previously untreated indolent histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphoma excluding SLL and candidate for chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Give chemotherapy in combination with rituximab
selected_rules.source_statement: Previously untreated patients with indolent histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphomas, excluding small lymphocytic lymphoma (SLL), who are appropriate candidates for chemotherapy, should receive their chemotherapy in combination with rituximab.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Previously treated indolent histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphoma excluding SLL, no prior rituximab, and candidate for chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Give chemotherapy in combination with rituximab or rituximab monotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Patients who have not previously received rituximab and who are appropriate candidates for chemotherapy should receive this chemotherapy in combination with rituximab or as rituximab monotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Previously treated indolent histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphoma excluding SLL, prior rituximab exposure, response lasted at least one year, and candidate for therapy
selected_rules.execute: Give therapy in combination with rituximab or rituximab monotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Patients who have previously received rituximab ... and who have achieved a response of at least one year's duration from the last rituximab administration and who are appropriate candidates for therapy should receive this therapy in combination with rituximab or as rituximab monotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Indolent histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphoma excluding SLL and response to combination chemotherapy and/or rituximab
selected_rules.execute: Follow treatment with maintenance rituximab
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with indolent histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphomas, excluding SLL, who respond to treatment with combination chemotherapy and/or rituximab, this treatment should be followed by the use of maintenance rituximab.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Any patient planned for rituximab treatment
selected_rules.execute: Screen for hepatitis B surface antigen and hepatitis B core antibody before rituximab
selected_rules.source_statement: The Hematology Disease Site Group recommends that all patients be screened for surface antigen for hepatitis B (HBsAg) and for hepatitis B core antibody (HBcAb) prior to treatment with rituximab.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man with newly diagnosed HIV-associated diffuse large B-cell lymphoma presents for treatment planning. He has no prior lymphoma therapy. His CD4 count is 34/mm3, HIV viral load is detectable, and staging shows advanced nodal disease but no CNS involvement. He is otherwise fit, with ECOG performance status 1, and the oncology team considers him a candidate for curative-intent combination chemotherapy. Baseline labs show normal renal and hepatic function. He asks whether rituximab should be included with his chemotherapy regimen.
recommended_actions: Do not add rituximab to chemotherapy.
source_case.rationale: This vignette triggers R001 because the patient has HIV-related lymphoma and a CD4 count below 50/mm3. Under this rule, rituximab should not be added to chemotherapy.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man with newly diagnosed HIV-associated diffuse large B-cell lymphoma comes for treatment planning. He has no prior lymphoma therapy. His CD4 count is 62/mm3, HIV viral load remains detectable, and staging shows advanced nodal disease without CNS involvement. He is otherwise fit, with ECOG performance status 1, and the oncology team considers him a candidate for curative-intent combination chemotherapy. Baseline labs show normal renal and hepatic function. He asks whether rituximab should be included with his chemotherapy regimen.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case remains HIV-associated aggressive lymphoma with curative-intent chemotherapy, but the CD4 count is just above the threshold that changes the rituximab recommendation.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Omit rituximab from the chemotherapy regimen because of the HIV diagnosis
Treat with chemotherapy alone and avoid anti-CD20 therapy
Defer rituximab until immune recovery
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not discordant with the HIV-specific rituximab exclusion rule because the CD4 count is 62/mm3, not below 50/mm3. The safer interpretation is that he may receive rituximab with curative-intent combination chemotherapy under the HIV-related lymphoma recommendation for CD4 counts at or above 50/mm3.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 is triggered only when HIV-related lymphoma is present and CD4 count is below 50/mm3. Here the CD4 count is above that cutoff, so the original 'do not add rituximab' recommendation no longer applies.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man with newly diagnosed HIV-associated diffuse large B-cell lymphoma presents for initial treatment planning. He has no prior lymphoma therapy. His CD4 count is 41/mm3, HIV viral load is detectable, and staging shows advanced nodal disease without CNS involvement. He is otherwise fit, with ECOG performance status 1, and the oncology team is considering curative-intent combination chemotherapy. Baseline renal and hepatic function are normal. A prior hepatitis B screen done last month showed negative HBsAg and negative HBcAb. The patient asks whether rituximab should be included with chemotherapy.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The presentation closely mirrors the source case, but a recent hepatitis B screening result may distract from the more important CD4-based exclusion.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Add rituximab because hepatitis B screening has already been negative
Proceed with standard chemoimmunotherapy without further concern
Use rituximab since the lymphoma is CD20-positive and treatment is curative-intent
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This case is not guideline-concordant for rituximab because the patient has HIV-related lymphoma with CD4 count below 50/mm3, which is the condition that invalidates the add-rituximab recommendation. The prior hepatitis B results address a different safety step and do not change the CD4-based restriction.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 is activated by HIV-related lymphoma with CD4 below 50/mm3. Despite the reassuring hepatitis B screen, the CD4 count of 41/mm3 means rituximab should not be added to chemotherapy under this rule.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with relapsed SCLC
selected_rules.execute: Consider second-line chemotherapy only after weighing treatment-free interval, response to first-line therapy, residual toxicity, and performance status.
selected_rules.source_statement: The selection of patients for treatment with second-line therapy should be dependent on the treatment-free interval, the extent of response to first-line therapy, residual toxicity from first-line therapy, and the performance status of the patient.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient relapses three or more months after completion of first-line chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Consider retreatment with the same regimen that produced the initial response, generally etoposide-cisplatin.
selected_rules.source_statement: patients who relapse three or more months following the completion of first-line chemotherapy may benefit from retreatment with the same regimen that induced their initial response. This would generally mean retreatment with etoposide-cisplatin.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient relapses three or more months after completion of first-line chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Consider alternative regimens such as CAV or carboplatin plus etoposide.
selected_rules.source_statement: Alternative regimens may include cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, and vincristine (CAV) or carboplatin and etoposide.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient initially responded to chemotherapy and response duration was 45 days or longer
selected_rules.execute: Consider oral topotecan as an alternative second-line option.
selected_rules.source_statement: Oral topotecan is a possible alternative for patients who initially responded to chemotherapy and had a response duration of 45 days or longer.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is not suitable for intravenous therapy and topotecan is being considered
selected_rules.execute: Consider oral topotecan because it is more convenient and may be an option when IV therapy is unsuitable.
selected_rules.source_statement: Oral administration is more convenient and may be a treatment option for patients not suitable for intravenous therapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: topotecan is given orally
selected_rules.execute: Monitor for grade 3/4 diarrhea.
selected_rules.source_statement: Oral administration is associated with a higher incidence of grade 3/4 diarrhea, whereas intravenous administration may result in a higher frequency of grade 3/4 neutropenia.

selected_rules.activation_condition: topotecan is given intravenously
selected_rules.execute: Monitor for grade 3/4 neutropenia.
selected_rules.source_statement: Oral administration is associated with a higher incidence of grade 3/4 diarrhea, whereas intravenous administration may result in a higher frequency of grade 3/4 neutropenia.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient fails to respond to first-line therapy or relapses shortly after first-line therapy
selected_rules.execute: Consider enrollment in a clinical trial to help determine the optimal treatment regimen.
selected_rules.source_statement: Clinical trials are needed to determine the optimal treatment regimen.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 64-year-old man with extensive-stage small cell lung cancer completed first-line etoposide-cisplatin 5 months ago and had a partial response with marked symptom improvement. He now returns with worsening cough, weight loss, and new right hilar enlargement on CT, consistent with relapsed disease. His treatment-free interval is just over 5 months. He has mild residual peripheral neuropathy from prior cisplatin but remains ambulatory, independent in activities of daily living, and has an ECOG performance status of 1. He is interested in further treatment and asks about options. Because he previously responded and the relapse occurred more than 3 months after completion of chemotherapy, retreatment with the same regimen is being considered, along with other second-line options. Oral topotecan is also discussed as an alternative, since he responded initially and his response lasted longer than 45 days.
