recommended_actions: Consider second-line chemotherapy after weighing the treatment-free interval, the extent of response to first-line therapy, residual toxicity from first-line therapy, and performance status.
Because relapse occurred more than 3 months after completion of first-line chemotherapy, consider retreatment with the same regimen that produced the initial response, generally etoposide-cisplatin.
Because he initially responded and the response duration was longer than 45 days, consider oral topotecan as an alternative second-line option.
source_case.rationale: This vignette meets the activation conditions for relapsed SCLC, relapse more than 3 months after first-line chemotherapy, and prior response lasting at least 45 days. The recommendations are limited to those specified by the triggered rules and reflect the need to balance treatment-free interval, prior response, residual toxicity, and performance status.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 64-year-old man with extensive-stage small cell lung cancer completed first-line etoposide-cisplatin about 2 months ago and had a clear partial response with major improvement in cough and appetite. He now returns with recurrent cough, a 6-pound weight loss, and CT showing progressive right hilar and mediastinal disease. His treatment-free interval is therefore just under 3 months. He remains ambulatory, independent in activities of daily living, and has an ECOG performance status of 1, with only mild residual cisplatin-related neuropathy. He asks whether he can receive the same chemotherapy again because he tolerated it reasonably well and felt much better on treatment. The oncology team is considering second-line options and discussing whether the prior regimen should be reused, but the timing of relapse is earlier than expected.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Relapsed extensive-stage SCLC with prior response and good performance status, but the relapse occurred before the 3-month threshold needed for reusing the original regimen and before the 45-day response-duration condition for oral topotecan.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Retreat with etoposide-cisplatin because he responded the first time
Start oral topotecan as a standard second-line option based on the prior response
Proceed with the same regimen without considering the short treatment-free interval
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is relapsed SCLC, but the treatment-free interval is just under 3 months, so the case does not meet the usual criterion for retreatment with the same first-line regimen. The prior response is also not enough by itself to justify oral topotecan under the stated rule set. A safer interpretation is to consider non-rechallenge second-line options, supportive care, or trial enrollment based on the short interval and prior toxicity.
challenging_examples.rationale: Rule R002 requires relapse three or more months after completion of first-line chemotherapy; this relapse is earlier. Rule R004 requires an initial response with duration of 45 days or longer, which is not established here.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 64-year-old man with extensive-stage small cell lung cancer completed first-line etoposide-cisplatin 5 months ago and had a partial response with marked symptom improvement. He now returns with worsening cough, weight loss, and new right hilar enlargement on CT, consistent with relapsed disease. His treatment-free interval is just over 5 months. He remains ambulatory and independent with an ECOG performance status of 1, but he reports that his prior response lasted only about 6 weeks before clear radiographic progression was documented. He is interested in another line of therapy and asks whether the same chemotherapy can be repeated or whether oral topotecan is a reasonable alternative. The team is weighing second-line options in the setting of preserved function and a prior response, but the durability of that response was limited.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The overall scenario closely matches relapsed extensive-stage SCLC after prior platinum/etoposide, but the documented response duration was only about 6 weeks, which undermines the topotecan criterion and makes the decision to repeat the same regimen less straightforward.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Repeat etoposide-cisplatin because the relapse is later than 3 months
Offer oral topotecan based on the initial partial response
List retreatment with the same regimen as the preferred second-line approach
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: Although relapse occurred more than 3 months after first-line therapy, the prior response lasted only about 6 weeks and is not clearly a durable response in the sense needed for the topotecan recommendation. This makes the case less supportive of the oral topotecan option and calls for a more individualized second-line discussion, including alternative regimens or trial enrollment.
challenging_examples.rationale: R002 is not safely supported because the vignette does not preserve a clearly favorable retreatment context despite the long interval. R004 is invalidated because the response duration is only about 6 weeks and does not cleanly satisfy the intended durable-response criterion.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with inoperable, advanced stage IV colorectal cancer being considered for first-line systemic therapy
selected_rules.execute: Discuss intermittent systemic treatment strategies as a reasonable alternative to continuous administration as part of informed treatment decision-making
selected_rules.source_statement: Intermittent systemic treatment strategies should be part of an informed discussion of treatment options for this group of patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer wants a break from treatment
selected_rules.execute: Reassure the patient that intermittent first-line therapy is a reasonable alternative to continuous therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Patients who want a break from treatment can be reassured that intermittent strategies of administering first-line therapy are a reasonable alternative to continuous administration.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer is considering intermittent versus continuous first-line systemic therapy
selected_rules.execute: Use intermittent therapy as an option because it does not show a statistically significant reduction in overall survival compared with continuous therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Meta-analysis demonstrates no clinically significant survival difference between the continuous and intermittent chemotherapy strategies (HR, 1.02; 95%CI, 0.95-1.10, p=0.62).

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer is considering intermittent versus continuous first-line systemic therapy
selected_rules.execute: Consider intermittent therapy because it may improve or maintain quality of life compared with continuous therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Intermittent strategies of administering first-line systemic therapies to patients with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer (mCRC) do not result in a statistically significant reduction in overall survival and either improve or maintain quality of life compared to continuous administration of therapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer is receiving intermittent systemic therapy or a chemotherapy-free interval
selected_rules.execute: Continue best supportive care
selected_rules.source_statement: During maintenance therapy or a chemotherapy-free interval, best supportive care should be continued for patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Intermittent strategy is being used for unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Use 12 to 18 weeks of induction treatment as a reasonable guideline to consider
selected_rules.source_statement: All but one of the intermittent strategies offered 12 to 18 weeks of induction treatment and were monitored with imaging at least every 8 to 12 weeks during the intermittent phase of treatment, with reintroduction of the induction chemotherapy at disease progression. These represent reasonable guidelines to consider when using an intermittent strategy, but adaptation of a strategy to individual circumstances should always be considered.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Intermittent strategy is being used for unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Monitor with imaging at least every 8 to 12 weeks during the intermittent phase
selected_rules.source_statement: All but one of the intermittent strategies offered 12 to 18 weeks of induction treatment and were monitored with imaging at least every 8 to 12 weeks during the intermittent phase of treatment, with reintroduction of the induction chemotherapy at disease progression. These represent reasonable guidelines to consider when using an intermittent strategy, but adaptation of a strategy to individual circumstances should always be considered.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Disease progresses during the intermittent phase
selected_rules.execute: Reintroduce the induction chemotherapy at disease progression
selected_rules.source_statement: All but one of the intermittent strategies offered 12 to 18 weeks of induction treatment and were monitored with imaging at least every 8 to 12 weeks during the intermittent phase of treatment, with reintroduction of the induction chemotherapy at disease progression.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man with inoperable stage IV colorectal adenocarcinoma presents to discuss first-line systemic therapy. He has liver and lung metastases, good performance status, and no prior chemotherapy. After reading about treatment side effects, he says he would prefer a plan that includes treatment breaks if possible, because he wants to spend more time at home and maintain his energy for family activities. His oncologist reviews standard continuous therapy and explains that intermittent first-line systemic treatment is also a reasonable option to consider. The discussion includes the possibility that intermittent therapy may preserve quality of life without a statistically significant survival disadvantage compared with continuous therapy. The patient agrees that an intermittent approach fits his goals if disease monitoring is built in.
recommended_actions: Discuss intermittent systemic treatment strategies as a reasonable alternative to continuous administration as part of informed treatment decision-making.
Reassure the patient that intermittent first-line therapy is a reasonable alternative to continuous therapy.
Use intermittent therapy as an option because it does not show a statistically significant reduction in overall survival compared with continuous therapy.
Consider intermittent therapy because it may improve or maintain quality of life compared with continuous therapy.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches an adult with inoperable advanced stage IV colorectal cancer being considered for first-line systemic therapy, and he also explicitly wants a break from treatment. The selected rules support discussing and reassuring about intermittent therapy, noting no statistically significant overall survival reduction and potential quality-of-life benefit versus continuous therapy.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man with metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma presents to discuss first-line systemic therapy. He has liver and lung metastases, good performance status, and no prior chemotherapy. He says he would prefer a plan that includes treatment breaks if possible, because he wants to spend more time at home and maintain his energy for family activities. His oncologist reviews standard continuous therapy and explains that intermittent first-line systemic treatment is also a reasonable option to consider. The discussion includes the possibility that intermittent therapy may preserve quality of life without a statistically significant survival disadvantage compared with continuous therapy. The patient agrees that an intermittent approach fits his goals if disease monitoring is built in. However, staging review also notes a solitary brain metastasis that has not yet been treated, and the team is planning urgent local therapy before any systemic regimen.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still centers on first-line treatment discussion for metastatic colorectal cancer with a strong preference for treatment breaks, but the disease is not limited to unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer because there is an untreated brain metastasis requiring separate urgent management.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Discuss intermittent systemic treatment as a reasonable first-line option
Reassure the patient that intermittent therapy is appropriate because he wants breaks from treatment
Choose intermittent therapy on the basis of preserved quality of life and no survival disadvantage
Plan an intermittent chemotherapy schedule now with routine monitoring
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the intermittent-therapy recommendation because the vignette adds an untreated brain metastasis and immediate local management is being planned, so the simple unresectable mCRC first-line systemic therapy framework does not cleanly apply. The safer interpretation is to prioritize site-specific urgent management and reassess systemic strategy once the overall treatment plan is clarified.
challenging_examples.rationale: The recommendation applies to adults with inoperable unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer being considered for first-line systemic therapy. The added untreated brain metastasis creates a more complex scenario outside the clean guideline target group, so the intermittent-first-line recommendation should not be applied directly.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man with inoperable stage IV colorectal adenocarcinoma presents to discuss first-line systemic therapy. He has liver and lung metastases, good performance status, and no prior chemotherapy. After reading about treatment side effects, he says he would prefer a plan that includes treatment breaks if possible, because he wants to spend more time at home and maintain his energy for family activities. His oncologist reviews standard continuous therapy and explains that intermittent first-line systemic treatment is also a reasonable option to consider. The discussion includes the possibility that intermittent therapy may preserve quality of life without a statistically significant survival disadvantage compared with continuous therapy. The patient agrees that an intermittent approach fits his goals if disease monitoring is built in. But his tumor is now documented as MSI-H with a clearly actionable first-line immunotherapy indication, and the team is planning pembrolizumab rather than a chemotherapy-based intermittent strategy.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Most of the vignette is unchanged and still appears to be a classic metastatic colorectal cancer discussion about intermittent versus continuous first-line treatment, but the biomarker status shifts the preferred first-line approach away from the chemotherapy strategy addressed by the rule.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer intermittent chemotherapy as a reasonable alternative to continuous treatment
Reassure the patient that treatment breaks are appropriate in first-line therapy
Counsel that intermittent therapy can be chosen without sacrificing survival
Build an intermittent induction-and-holiday plan with routine restaging
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the intermittent-chemotherapy recommendation because MSI-H disease with a first-line immunotherapy indication changes the preferred systemic treatment approach. The safer interpretation is to follow biomarker-directed therapy rather than applying the intermittent-versus-continuous chemotherapy recommendation.
challenging_examples.rationale: The source recommendation concerns first-line systemic therapy strategies in unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer, especially chemotherapy scheduling. An MSI-H tumor with a clear immunotherapy indication invalidates direct application of that rule set, making the intermittent chemotherapy recommendation inappropriate here.

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman has never been sexually active
selected_rules.execute: Do not perform cervical screening.
selected_rules.source_statement: Women who have never been sexually active do not require cervical screening.

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman is younger than 21 years old
selected_rules.execute: Do not screen routinely.
selected_rules.source_statement: the authors of this guideline have concluded that the harms of screening women under 21 years of age significantly outweigh the benefits.

selected_rules.activation_condition: organized screening program with adequate database infrastructure
selected_rules.execute: Use HPV DNA testing of cervical cells for primary cervical screening.
selected_rules.source_statement: HPV DNA testing of cells collected from the cervix is recommended for primary cervical screening.

selected_rules.activation_condition: positive HPV DNA test result
selected_rules.execute: Perform cytology screening as triage rather than using cytology as the primary screen.
selected_rules.source_statement: Cytology screening, which was recommended for primary screening in the previous version of this guideline, is now recommended only in the event of a positive HPV DNA test result.

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman is 30 years of age or older
selected_rules.execute: Use HPV-based primary screening.
selected_rules.source_statement: the primary recommendations presented in Part 1 of this guideline are for HPVbased testing for women 30 years of age and over.

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman is 30 to 65 years old and initial HPV test is negative
selected_rules.execute: Repeat HPV DNA testing at five-year intervals.
selected_rules.source_statement: For women aged 30-65, HPV DNA testing is to occur at five-year intervals after an initial negative result.

selected_rules.activation_condition: HPV test is positive
selected_rules.execute: Assess the result with cytology testing and do not refer directly to colposcopy.
selected_rules.source_statement: HPV-positive tests should be assessed with cytology testing and not referred directly to colposcopy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: HPV positive and cytology negative
selected_rules.execute: Repeat HPV testing after one year.
selected_rules.source_statement: Repeat HPV testing for results of HPV positive/cytology negative should be conducted after one year.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 27-year-old woman presents for a routine primary care visit and asks whether she should have a Pap test. She reports that she has never been sexually active and has no history of genital symptoms, abnormal bleeding, or prior cervical screening. She is otherwise healthy and has no immunocompromising conditions. After discussing her preventive care questions, she specifically asks whether cervical cancer screening is needed now.
recommended_actions: Do not perform cervical screening.
source_case.rationale: This vignette triggers the rule that women who have never been sexually active do not require cervical screening. The recommended action is therefore to avoid cervical screening in this patient.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 27-year-old woman presents for a routine primary care visit and asks whether she should have cervical cancer screening. She reports that she has never been sexually active and has no history of genital symptoms, abnormal bleeding, or prior cervical screening. She is otherwise healthy and has no immunocompromising conditions. She mentions that she is planning to start a new relationship later this year but has not yet become sexually active. After discussing her preventive care questions, she specifically asks whether screening is needed now.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still centers on a healthy 27-year-old asking about cervical screening, but the patient is not currently sexually active rather than definitively never sexually active.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Recommend routine cervical screening at this visit because she is nearing sexual debut.
Order a Pap test now since she is asking about prevention and may soon become sexually active.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the never-sexually-active exemption because the history is not consistent with lifelong sexual inactivity. The safer interpretation is to follow the usual age-appropriate screening pathway rather than using the no-screening exception based on current information alone.
challenging_examples.rationale: The rule applies only to women who have never been sexually active. A future plan to become sexually active does not satisfy that condition, but the case remains tempting because it otherwise resembles the source vignette closely.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 27-year-old woman presents for a routine primary care visit and asks whether she should have a Pap test. She reports that she has never been sexually active vaginally and has no history of genital symptoms, abnormal bleeding, or prior cervical screening. She is otherwise healthy and has no immunocompromising conditions. In reviewing her history, she notes that she has had prior nonconsensual genital contact but no intercourse. She specifically asks whether cervical cancer screening is needed now.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The encounter still sounds like the classic low-risk preventive visit, but the sexual history is no longer a straightforward 'never sexually active' history.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Reassure her that no cervical screening is needed because she has never had intercourse.
Defer screening solely on the basis that she has no prior Pap tests and no current symptoms.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This case is not concordant with the exemption for women who have never been sexually active, because the history includes prior genital sexual contact and is not the clean low-risk scenario described in the rule. A more careful individualized screening discussion is warranted rather than automatically omitting screening.
challenging_examples.rationale: The recommendation is narrowly tied to never having been sexually active. Introducing prior genital contact invalidates the premise while keeping the vignette superficially similar and easy to misread.

selected_rules.activation_condition: inflammatory breast cancer
selected_rules.execute: Do not advise breast-conserving surgery.
selected_rules.source_statement: BCS is not advised in inflammatory breast cancer because the extent of tumour involvement cannot be reliably ascertained.

selected_rules.activation_condition: sentinel lymph node biopsy is being considered both before and after neoadjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Do not perform SLNB both before and after neoadjuvant chemotherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: This suggests that SLNB should not be performed both before and after NACT.

selected_rules.activation_condition: cases being discussed for treatment planning
selected_rules.execute: Discuss cases in a multidisciplinary case conference.
selected_rules.source_statement: Communication between oncologists, surgeons, radiologists, and pathologists is essential. A multidisciplinary case conference is the recommended forum for discussion of cases.

selected_rules.activation_condition: specimen submitted after neoadjuvant therapy
selected_rules.execute: Indicate any prior neoadjuvant therapy on the pathology specimen submission.
selected_rules.source_statement: Any prior use of neoadjuvant therapy should be indicated when specimens are submitted for pathologic examination.

selected_rules.activation_condition: before starting neoadjuvant therapy
selected_rules.execute: Insert surgical clips marking the original pretreatment tumour location.
selected_rules.source_statement: It is recommended that surgical clips marking the original (pretreatment) tumour location be inserted before administration of neoadjuvant therapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: female patient with locally advanced breast cancer and surgery is being planned after response to neoadjuvant therapy
selected_rules.execute: Consider mastectomy as the standard of care for most patients.
selected_rules.source_statement: For most patients with LABC, mastectomy should be considered to be the standard of care.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient strongly desires breast-conserving surgery
selected_rules.execute: Perform wide excision of the remaining tumour in the original tumour bed plus radiotherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: Wide excision of the remaining tumour in the region of the original pre-neoadjuvant treatment tumour bed plus RT is recommended for patients with LABC who strongly desire BCS.

selected_rules.activation_condition: female patient with locally advanced breast cancer after mastectomy
selected_rules.execute: Give postoperative radiotherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: Radiotherapy following mastectomy is recommended for patients with LABC.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 49-year-old woman presents with a 7 cm left breast mass, peau d’orange, and erythema involving more than one-third of the breast, consistent with inflammatory breast cancer. Core biopsy confirms invasive ductal carcinoma. She is being evaluated at diagnosis for local treatment options, and she asks whether she can have lumpectomy because she hopes to avoid mastectomy. The team is also planning neoadjuvant therapy, with surgery to be reconsidered afterward depending on response. No surgery has been performed yet.
recommended_actions: Do not advise breast-conserving surgery.
Consider mastectomy as the standard of care for most patients with locally advanced breast cancer after response to neoadjuvant therapy.
source_case.rationale: The vignette describes inflammatory breast cancer, which triggers the recommendation against breast-conserving surgery because tumour extent cannot be reliably ascertained. It also includes locally advanced breast cancer being managed with neoadjuvant therapy and later surgery planning, which supports considering mastectomy as standard of care for most such patients.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 49-year-old woman presents with a 7 cm left breast mass, peau d’orange, and erythema involving most of the breast. Core biopsy confirms invasive ductal carcinoma. She is being evaluated at diagnosis for local treatment options and asks whether she can have lumpectomy because she hopes to avoid mastectomy. The team is planning neoadjuvant therapy, with surgery to be reconsidered afterward depending on response. On exam, the skin changes are limited to a small inferolateral quadrant rather than more than one-third of the breast, and the breast is otherwise mobile without diffuse inflammatory involvement. No surgery has been performed yet.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still presents as a large newly diagnosed breast cancer with neoadjuvant treatment planning and a request to avoid mastectomy, but the skin findings do not meet inflammatory breast cancer criteria.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Recommend against breast-conserving surgery because of presumed inflammatory breast cancer
Proceed directly to mastectomy as the standard local option
Document the case as locally advanced inflammatory disease without further clarification
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not inflammatory breast cancer, because the erythema and peau d’orange are limited rather than involving more than one-third of the breast. The safer interpretation is locally advanced non-inflammatory breast cancer, where surgical planning should depend on response to neoadjuvant therapy and candidacy for breast conservation rather than automatically excluding lumpectomy on an inflammatory basis.
challenging_examples.rationale: Rule R001 requires inflammatory breast cancer, which is not present here. Rule R006 applies to locally advanced breast cancer when surgery is being planned after response to neoadjuvant therapy, but the altered skin distribution makes the inflammatory diagnosis unsupported and the local treatment decision less categorical.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 56-year-old woman with a cT3N1 invasive ductal carcinoma of the right breast is discussed in clinic before starting neoadjuvant chemotherapy. The team plans to place clips to mark the pretreatment tumor bed and review her case at a multidisciplinary conference. She is interested in breast conservation after treatment if feasible. The biopsy specimen was obtained from the breast mass last week, but the pathology requisition does not yet note any prior systemic therapy because neoadjuvant treatment has not started. Imaging shows no distant metastases and no skin involvement.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The workflow still centers on a newly diagnosed locally advanced breast cancer with planned neoadjuvant therapy and possible later breast-conserving surgery, but the timing of therapy makes the pathology and clip-related recommendations inapplicable.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Indicate prior neoadjuvant therapy on the pathology specimen submission
Assume surgical clips have already been or should already have been placed despite treatment not starting
Treat the biopsy requisition as needing neoadjuvant therapy documentation now
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This case is before neoadjuvant therapy begins, so there is no prior neoadjuvant treatment to document on the specimen submission, and the clip recommendation only applies before administration of neoadjuvant therapy. The appropriate approach is to ensure clip placement occurs at the correct pre-treatment time and to reserve pathology annotation for specimens actually obtained after neoadjuvant treatment.
challenging_examples.rationale: Rule R004 applies only to specimens submitted after neoadjuvant therapy, which is not the situation here. Rule R005 requires clip placement before starting neoadjuvant therapy; the vignette says treatment has not started yet, so the timing is not a guideline violation unless clips are omitted going forward.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult ALL patient in CR2 or greater, including refractory or relapsed disease
selected_rules.execute: Perform allo-SCT
selected_rules.source_statement: Allo-SCT is recommended in CR2 or greater (refractory or relapsed).

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult ALL patient in first complete remission (CR1) and eligible for allo-SCT
selected_rules.execute: Consider allogeneic stem cell transplantation (allo-SCT) as a treatment option
selected_rules.source_statement: Allogeneic stem cell transplantation (allo-SCT) is an option for adult patients with acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) in first complete remission (CR1).

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult ALL patient in remission who is deemed unsuitable for standard myeloablative conditioning (MAC)
selected_rules.execute: Use reduced-intensity conditioning (RIC) for SCT
selected_rules.source_statement: Reduced-intensity conditioning (RIC) is an option for patients with acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) in remission when they are deemed unsuitable for the standard myeloablative conditioning (MAC) regimen.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult ALL patient in remission who is ineligible for MAC allo-SCT
selected_rules.execute: Consider RIC as an effective therapeutic option
selected_rules.source_statement: Based on the evidence, the members of the Working Group have determined that RIC could be an effective therapeutic option for patients with ALL who are ineligible for MAC allo-SCT.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult ALL patient with high risk of treatment-related mortality from MAC
selected_rules.execute: Consider RIC instead of MAC
selected_rules.source_statement: The systematic review (4) stated that RIC may be a potential therapeutic option in patients with high risk of treatment-related mortality (TRM) associated with MAC regimens, as there was a lack of overall survival benefit of MAC over RIC regimens.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient with Philadelphia chromosome-positive ALL (Ph+ ALL) after allo-SCT
selected_rules.execute: Use a BCR-ABL tyrosine-kinase inhibitor (TKI) post-transplant
selected_rules.source_statement: Post-transplant use of a BCR-ABL tyrosine-kinase inhibitor (TKI) in patients with Philadelphia chromosome-positive ALL (Ph+ ALL) is a reasonable option.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient with Ph+ ALL undergoing allo-SCT
selected_rules.execute: Consider TKI therapy pre- and/or post-transplant
selected_rules.source_statement: The consensus is that TKI therapy is useful pre and/or posttransplant.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult ALL patient in CR1 or later who lacks a suitable related or unrelated donor
selected_rules.execute: Consider haploidentical hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (haplo-SCT)
selected_rules.source_statement: Haploidentical hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplantation (haplo-SCT) for patients with ALL in CR1 or later who lack a suitable related or unrelated donor is a reasonable option.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man with B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia initially achieved remission after induction and consolidation but relapsed 14 months later. He received salvage chemotherapy and now has a documented second complete remission (CR2) with no detectable blasts on marrow exam. He is otherwise fit, without major cardiac, renal, or pulmonary comorbidities, and he has an HLA-matched sibling donor available. The transplant team is evaluating next steps for durable disease control. Because he is an adult patient with ALL in CR2 after relapse, the case meets criteria for transplant in the recurrence setting.
recommended_actions: Perform allogeneic stem cell transplantation (allo-SCT).
source_case.rationale: The vignette matches adult ALL in CR2 after relapse, which directly triggers the recommendation for allo-SCT. No additional rules are activated because the patient is not described as Ph+ ALL, unsuitable for myeloablative conditioning, or lacking a suitable donor.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man with B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia initially achieved remission after induction and consolidation but relapsed 14 months later. He received salvage chemotherapy and now has a documented second complete remission (CR2) with no detectable blasts on marrow exam. He is otherwise fit, without major cardiac, renal, or pulmonary comorbidities, and he has an HLA-matched sibling donor available. The transplant team is evaluating next steps for durable disease control. Cytogenetic testing from the relapse specimen also shows a BCR-ABL1 fusion consistent with Philadelphia chromosome-positive disease, and he has already started a tyrosine kinase inhibitor while awaiting transplant planning. Because he is an adult patient with ALL in CR2 after relapse, the case appears to meet criteria for transplant in the recurrence setting.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Adult relapsed ALL in CR2 with an available matched sibling donor and transplant consultation, but the relapse is Philadelphia chromosome-positive and already being managed with TKI therapy.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed directly with allogeneic stem cell transplantation for CR2 relapsed ALL
Schedule transplant conditioning and donor collection without further nuance
Treat this as a straightforward recurrence case and move to allo-SCT
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not discordant with the recurrence trigger alone, but the vignette adds Ph+ disease and active TKI use, which shifts the clinical frame and introduces additional transplant-specific management considerations. The safer interpretation is that transplant remains a major consideration, but the case is no longer a clean match to the isolated CR2 relapse rule used here.
challenging_examples.rationale: The source rule is meant for adult ALL in CR2 or greater after relapse/refractory disease. Adding Ph+ biology and TKI therapy complicates the guideline mapping and makes the vignette no longer a pure match to the simple recurrence recommendation.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man with B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia initially achieved remission after induction and consolidation but relapsed 14 months later. He received salvage chemotherapy and now has a marrow exam showing no overt leukemia, but cytometry detects a very small abnormal B-cell population that is interpreted as measurable residual disease rather than a strict second complete remission. He is otherwise fit, without major cardiac, renal, or pulmonary comorbidities, and he has an HLA-matched sibling donor available. The transplant team is evaluating next steps for durable disease control. Because he is an adult patient with ALL after relapse, the team is considering transplant planning based on the overall course.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Relapsed adult ALL with a transplant-ready matched donor, but marrow evaluation shows residual disease and does not establish a true CR2.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with allo-SCT now that marrow is nearly clear
List the patient for transplant as CR2 relapsed ALL
Document the case as meeting the standard recurrence indication for allo-SCT
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the CR2 recurrence recommendation because the patient does not have a documented second complete remission; measurable residual disease remains detectable. The safer interpretation is that additional disease-directed therapy or response clarification is needed before applying the CR2 transplant recommendation.
challenging_examples.rationale: The trigger requires adult ALL in CR2 or greater. Detectable residual disease means the vignette no longer satisfies the remission condition, so the original recommendation cannot be applied directly.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient intends postoperative radiation and has lymph node positive early breast cancer, inflammatory breast cancer, or locally advanced breast cancer
selected_rules.execute: Do not use skin-sparing, nipple-sparing, or areola-sparing mastectomy
selected_rules.source_statement: SSM, NSM, and ASM are not recommended for women intending to receive postoperative radiation with: early breast cancer who are lymph node positive, inflammatory breast cancer or locally advanced breast cancer who will require postoperative RT.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Invasive tumor involves the skin or nipple-areolar complex clinically
selected_rules.execute: Do not use skin-sparing, nipple-sparing, or areola-sparing mastectomy
selected_rules.source_statement: SSM, NSM, and ASM are not recommended for women intending to receive postoperative radiation with: any clinical skin or nipple-areolar complex (NAC) involvement by invasive tumour

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has Paget disease of the breast or a retroareolar tumor
selected_rules.execute: Do not perform nipple-sparing or areola-sparing mastectomy
selected_rules.source_statement: NSM and ASM are not recommended for women with Paget disease of the breast or women with a retroareolar tumour.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Postmastectomy breast reconstruction has been performed
selected_rules.execute: Do not use surveillance mammography routinely in the reconstructed breast
selected_rules.source_statement: There is insufficient evidence to support the use of postmastectomy surveillance mammography in the reconstructed breast.

selected_rules.activation_condition: At the time mastectomy is offered for therapeutic mastectomy
selected_rules.execute: Initiate discussion of immediate or delayed breast reconstruction
selected_rules.source_statement: The discussion of immediate or delayed breast reconstruction should be initiated at the time that mastectomy is offered by the general surgeon.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Immediate breast reconstruction is being sought for DCIS
selected_rules.execute: Perform preoperative evaluation with a general surgeon and a plastic surgeon
selected_rules.source_statement: For women seeking immediate breast reconstruction for ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS), preoperative evaluation with a general surgeon and a plastic surgeon should be performed.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Immediate breast reconstruction is being sought for early stage breast cancer and adjuvant chemotherapy or radiotherapy may be needed
selected_rules.execute: Include a medical oncologist and/or radiation oncologist in preoperative evaluation, either by formal consultation or multidisciplinary cancer conference
selected_rules.source_statement: For women seeking immediate breast reconstruction for early stage breast cancer who will potentially require adjuvant chemotherapy or radiotherapy (RT), a medical oncologist and/or radiation oncologist should be included in preoperative evaluation, either through a formal consultation or by a multidisciplinary cancer conference.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Immediate reconstruction is being planned
selected_rules.execute: Obtain adequate preoperative breast imaging according to existing guideline recommendations
selected_rules.source_statement: For women seeking immediate reconstruction, there should be adequate preoperative imaging of the breasts, aligning with existing guideline recommendations.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 52-year-old woman with a newly diagnosed left breast invasive ductal carcinoma presents to discuss surgery. Imaging shows a 4.2 cm tumor with biopsy-proven axillary lymph node involvement. The breast surgeon recommends mastectomy, and the patient is interested in immediate reconstruction if feasible. Because she is node-positive and is expected to receive postoperative radiation, the surgical team reviews reconstruction options carefully. On exam, there is no clinical skin or nipple-areolar complex involvement, and she has no history of Paget disease. She has already had diagnostic mammography and breast MRI for staging. A multidisciplinary visit is arranged to coordinate surgical, reconstructive, and radiation planning before finalizing the operative approach.
recommended_actions: Do not use skin-sparing, nipple-sparing, or areola-sparing mastectomy because she intends to receive postoperative radiation and has lymph node-positive early breast cancer.
Initiate discussion of immediate or delayed breast reconstruction at the time mastectomy is offered.
Include a medical oncologist and/or radiation oncologist in the preoperative evaluation through formal consultation or a multidisciplinary cancer conference.
Obtain adequate preoperative breast imaging according to existing guideline recommendations.
source_case.rationale: R001 is triggered because the patient has lymph node-positive early breast cancer and will receive postoperative radiation, making skin-sparing, nipple-sparing, and areola-sparing mastectomy not recommended. R005 applies because mastectomy is being offered, so reconstruction should be discussed. R007 applies because immediate reconstruction is being considered and adjuvant radiotherapy is likely, so oncology input should be included preoperatively. R008 applies because immediate reconstruction is being planned and adequate preoperative breast imaging is required.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 52-year-old woman with a newly diagnosed left breast invasive ductal carcinoma returns to discuss surgery. Imaging shows a 4.2 cm tumor with biopsy-proven axillary lymph node involvement, and she is considering mastectomy with immediate reconstruction if feasible. The surgeon explains that because she is node-positive, postoperative radiation will likely be part of treatment planning, so the reconstruction team is asked to weigh options carefully. On exam, there is no clinical skin or nipple-areolar complex involvement, and she has no history of Paget disease. She has already had diagnostic mammography and breast MRI for staging. A multidisciplinary visit is arranged to coordinate surgical and reconstructive planning, but at this point her tumor board has not yet finalized whether radiation will actually be recommended after surgery.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Nearly identical to the source case, but postoperative radiation is still only a possibility rather than an expected component of care.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with a skin-sparing or nipple-sparing mastectomy given the absence of skin or nipple involvement.
Move directly to immediate reconstruction planning without further discussion of radiation implications.
Treat her as a standard node-positive reconstruction candidate and finalize the operative approach before multidisciplinary review.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the mastectomy-sparing restriction because the rule requires node-positive early breast cancer with intended postoperative radiation. Here, radiation has not been established as part of the plan, so the specific contraindication is not met. The safer interpretation is to continue multidisciplinary planning and confirm adjuvant treatment intent before deciding on the mastectomy approach.
challenging_examples.rationale: The case preserves node-positive early breast cancer and reconstruction planning, but removes the key condition that postoperative radiation is intended. Without that, the recommendation against skin-sparing, nipple-sparing, or areola-sparing mastectomy is not triggered.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 52-year-old woman with a newly diagnosed left breast invasive ductal carcinoma presents to discuss surgery. Imaging shows a 4.2 cm tumor with biopsy-proven axillary lymph node involvement, and the breast surgeon recommends mastectomy. The patient is interested in immediate reconstruction, and the team arranges a preoperative multidisciplinary visit. On exam, there is no clinical skin involvement, but there is subtle inversion and ulceration of the nipple-areolar complex overlying the tumor. She has no history of Paget disease. Diagnostic mammography and breast MRI for staging have already been completed. Because the disease appears otherwise operable, the surgeon is considering the same reconstruction pathway that would be used for a typical node-positive case.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The overall breast cancer and reconstruction workflow is unchanged, but there is now clinical nipple-areolar complex involvement.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer a nipple-sparing or areola-sparing mastectomy with immediate reconstruction.
Proceed with immediate reconstruction planning without adjusting the operative technique.
Assume the nipple can be preserved because the skin is otherwise uninvolved.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for nipple-sparing or areola-sparing mastectomy because clinical nipple-areolar complex involvement is present. The safer interpretation is that nipple-preserving approaches should be avoided, while reconstruction discussions can still occur using a non–nipple-sparing operative plan.
challenging_examples.rationale: The source case lacked skin or nipple involvement, but this version adds nipple-areolar complex involvement, which invalidates the recommendation for nipple-sparing or areola-sparing mastectomy even though the rest of the scenario remains similar.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When considering limited ACP implementation
selected_rules.execute: Do not rely on only one or a few ACP elements to achieve meaningful patient care impact.
selected_rules.source_statement: Addressing only one, or a few, of these elements creates the risks of leaving critical gaps that could prevent positive impacts on quality patient care.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When using education alone to improve ACP care
selected_rules.execute: Do not expect education alone to improve care or patient experience without supporting system changes.
selected_rules.source_statement: However, high-quality education, although essential, on its own is unlikely to improve care or the patient experience in the absence of supporting systemic changes.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When considering whether pediatric ACP causes distress
selected_rules.execute: Do not assume ACP discussions increase distress in children or parents.
selected_rules.source_statement: Concerns that ACP discussions will cause distress in children and parents are not supported by the evidence.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When considering ACP or GoCD tools for practice
selected_rules.execute: Use a system-wide approach to integrate ACP into routine practice and maximize interprofessional team skills.
selected_rules.source_statement: Outcome evidence for ACP suggests the greatest impact is likely to be made by a system-wide approach to integrating ACP into practice, one that maximizes clinical skills of all interprofessional team members.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When developing ACP tools or processes
selected_rules.execute: Focus tool development on enabling values-based discussions rather than treatment-focused discussions.
selected_rules.source_statement: Considering these emerging themes from the evidence suggests that tool development should focus on enabling healthcare providers to facilitate values-based discussions.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When evaluating ACP tools
selected_rules.execute: Use values-based outcome measures to assess impact on patient care.
selected_rules.source_statement: Future studies with corresponding valuesbased outcome measures are needed to evaluate the impact on patient care.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When implementing ACP programs
selected_rules.execute: Include community engagement, professional education, standard practices, quality improvement monitoring, supporting policies, and technology infrastructure.
selected_rules.source_statement: A comprehensive approach to ACP includes community engagement, professional education, development of standard practices, monitoring of these practices through Quality Improvement initiatives, supporting policies and practices, and a technology infrastructures.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When planning ACP implementation across care settings
selected_rules.execute: Apply ACP across public health and healthcare sectors with system-level coordination and planning.
selected_rules.source_statement: This approach must be applied across public health and healthcare sectors to support the exchange of clinical information and will involve system-level coordination and planning.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with metastatic lung cancer is admitted for progressive dyspnea and fatigue. His oncology team notes that advance care planning has been discussed inconsistently across visits: one clinician focused mainly on code status, while another gave a pamphlet about end-of-life decisions. The patient says he wants care that fits his priorities—staying alert enough to talk with family and avoiding prolonged ICU treatment if recovery is unlikely—but he has not had a structured conversation about these values. His daughter asks whether these discussions might upset him, since he becomes anxious when illness is mentioned. The team is considering a new outpatient ACP workflow and realizes that a few isolated steps have not changed care quality in the past.
recommended_actions: Do not rely on only one or a few ACP elements to achieve meaningful patient care impact.
Do not assume ACP discussions increase distress in children or parents.
Use a system-wide approach to integrate ACP into routine practice and maximize interprofessional team skills.
Focus tool development on enabling values-based discussions rather than treatment-focused discussions.
source_case.rationale: The vignette describes limited, fragmented ACP implementation, which triggers the warning against relying on only a few ACP elements. It also includes concern that ACP discussions may distress the patient and family, matching the rule that evidence does not support assuming increased distress. The team is planning broader ACP workflow changes, which supports a system-wide approach, and the patient’s stated priorities point to values-based rather than treatment-only discussions.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with metastatic lung cancer is admitted for progressive dyspnea and fatigue. His oncology team notes that advance care planning has been discussed inconsistently across visits: one clinician focused mainly on code status, while another gave a pamphlet about end-of-life decisions. The patient says he wants care that fits his priorities—staying alert enough to talk with family and avoiding prolonged ICU treatment if recovery is unlikely—but he has not had a structured conversation about these values. His daughter asks whether these discussions might upset him, since he becomes anxious when illness is mentioned. The team is considering a new outpatient ACP workflow, but the proposed plan is to give staff a short online module without changing clinic scheduling, documentation, or follow-up processes.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still centers on metastatic cancer, inconsistent ACP delivery, family concern about distress, and a proposed ACP workflow, but the intervention is limited to education alone rather than a broader system change.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Implement the online ACP training and expect it to improve completion of advance care planning visits.
Roll out the pamphlet-based counseling approach more broadly to standardize discussions.
Treat the education module as the main quality improvement intervention for the clinic.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for an education-only strategy. High-quality education may be necessary, but by itself it is unlikely to improve care or patient experience without supporting system changes. A safer interpretation is that the clinic would need workflow, documentation, and team-based practice changes in addition to staff education.
challenging_examples.rationale: The source recommendation warns against relying on education alone. In this vignette, the proposed intervention is explicitly a standalone online module with no structural changes, which makes the scenario discordant with the guideline.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with metastatic lung cancer is admitted for progressive dyspnea and fatigue. His oncology team notes that advance care planning has been discussed inconsistently across visits: one clinician focused mainly on code status, while another gave a pamphlet about end-of-life decisions. The patient says he wants care that fits his priorities—staying alert enough to talk with family and avoiding prolonged ICU treatment if recovery is unlikely—but he has not had a structured conversation about these values. His daughter asks whether these discussions might upset him, since he becomes anxious when illness is mentioned. The team is considering a new outpatient ACP workflow, and the current draft adds a single templated note for oncologists to complete after visits, but does not involve nursing, social work, or palliative care in the process.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case preserves the same metastatic cancer context, fragmented ACP history, and concern about distress, but the workflow is narrowed to one clinician-facing template rather than a system-wide interprofessional approach.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Adopt the oncologist-only template as the new ACP workflow.
Add the note template to clinic visits and expect it to meaningfully improve advance care planning.
Have the oncologist handle all values-based discussions without additional team support.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant because the proposed workflow is not system-wide and does not leverage interprofessional team skills. The safer interpretation is that meaningful ACP implementation should involve coordinated changes across roles and care processes, not a single documentation tool used by one clinician group.
challenging_examples.rationale: The recommendation supports a system-wide approach to integrating ACP into routine practice. Here, the proposed intervention is limited to a templated note for oncologists only, which misses the broader team-based implementation needed for the rule to apply.

selected_rules.activation_condition: platinum-resistant recurrent ovarian cancer and not eligible for the AURELIA trial criteria
selected_rules.execute: Do not apply the bevacizumab plus chemotherapy option described for AURELIA-eligible patients.
selected_rules.source_statement: Ineligible patients include those who have received >2 prior anticancer regimens or who had refractory disease, patients with a history of bowel obstruction (including subocclusive disease) related to underlying disease, a history of abdominal fistula, gastrointestinal perforation, or intra-abdominal abscess, or evidence of rectosigmoid involvement by pelvic examination, bowel involvement on computed tomography, or clinical symptoms of bowel obstruction.

selected_rules.activation_condition: all patients with recurrent epithelial ovarian cancer
selected_rules.execute: Assess each patient individually for recurrence status, platinum sensitivity, toxicity, ease of administration, and patient preference; recognize systemic therapy is not curative.
selected_rules.source_statement: Systemic therapy for recurrent ovarian cancer is not curative. As such, it is recognized that, to determine the optimal therapy, each patient needs to be assessed individually in terms of recurrence, sensitivity to platinum, toxicity, ease of administration, and patient preference.

selected_rules.activation_condition: all patients with recurrent epithelial ovarian cancer
selected_rules.execute: Offer participation in clinical trials if appropriate.
selected_rules.source_statement: All patients should be offered the opportunity to participate in clinical trials, if appropriate.

selected_rules.activation_condition: platinum-sensitive recurrent ovarian cancer and a single platinum agent is not being considered because of toxicity or allergy
selected_rules.execute: Use monotherapy with paclitaxel, topotecan, or pegylated liposomal doxorubicin.
selected_rules.source_statement: If a single platinum agent is not being considered (e.g., because of toxicity or allergy), then monotherapy with paclitaxel, topotecan, or pegylated liposomal doxorubicin is a reasonable treatment option.

selected_rules.activation_condition: platinum-sensitive recurrent ovarian cancer with known BRCA1 or BRCA2 mutation, somatic or germline, after platinum-based chemotherapy for recurrent disease
selected_rules.execute: Use PARP inhibitor maintenance treatment post platinum-based chemotherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: PARP inhibitors are recommended for patients with known BRCA 1 or 2 mutation (somatic and germline) as maintenance treatment post platinum-based chemotherapy for recurrent disease.

selected_rules.activation_condition: platinum-refractory or platinum-resistant recurrent ovarian cancer
selected_rules.execute: Set treatment goals to improving quality of life by extending symptom-free interval, reducing symptom intensity, increasing progression-free interval, or prolonging life if possible.
selected_rules.source_statement: Lower levels of response to treatment are expected for this group; therefore, the goals of treatment should be to improve patient's quality of life by extending the symptom-free interval, reducing symptom intensity, increasing progression-free interval, or if possible, prolonging life.

selected_rules.activation_condition: platinum-refractory or platinum-resistant recurrent ovarian cancer
selected_rules.execute: Use single-agent paclitaxel, topotecan, pegylated liposomal doxorubicin, or gemcitabine as reasonable treatment options.
selected_rules.source_statement: Single-agent paclitaxel, topotecan, pegylated liposomal doxorubicin, and gemcitabine have demonstrated activity in this patient population and are reasonable treatment options.

selected_rules.activation_condition: platinum-sensitive recurrent ovarian cancer
selected_rules.execute: Offer chemotherapy with biologics after discussing the safety profile.
selected_rules.source_statement: Women with platinum-sensitive recurrent ovarian cancer should be offered chemotherapy with biologics after a discussion concerning the safety profile

source_case.patient_vignette: A 64-year-old woman with high-grade serous epithelial ovarian cancer presents with symptomatic recurrence 5 months after completing her last platinum-based regimen. She has received three prior anticancer regimens and now has increasing abdominal discomfort, early satiety, and a CT scan showing peritoneal progression without bowel obstruction. Her disease is therefore platinum-resistant and she is not eligible for the AURELIA trial criteria because she has had more than two prior anticancer regimens. She asks about the next treatment steps and whether the goal is cure.
recommended_actions: Do not apply the bevacizumab plus chemotherapy option described for AURELIA-eligible patients.
Set treatment goals around improving quality of life, symptom control, and prolonging progression-free interval or life if possible.
Consider single-agent paclitaxel, topotecan, pegylated liposomal doxorubicin, or gemcitabine as reasonable treatment options.
Assess the patient individually for recurrence status, platinum sensitivity, toxicity, ease of administration, and patient preference, recognizing that systemic therapy is not curative.
Offer participation in a clinical trial if appropriate.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches platinum-resistant recurrent ovarian cancer and specifically excludes AURELIA eligibility because the patient has had more than two prior anticancer regimens. That triggers the recommendation not to use the bevacizumab-plus-chemotherapy option for AURELIA-eligible patients. Platinum-resistant disease also supports setting palliative goals and using one of the listed single-agent chemotherapy options. Because she has recurrent epithelial ovarian cancer, individualized treatment assessment and trial consideration are also recommended.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 64-year-old woman with high-grade serous epithelial ovarian cancer presents with symptomatic recurrence 5 months after completing her last platinum-based regimen. She has received three prior anticancer regimens and now reports increasing abdominal discomfort and early satiety. CT imaging shows peritoneal progression, but also new mild rectosigmoid wall thickening that appears to abut the pelvic tumor, without frank bowel obstruction. Her oncologist is considering the next treatment step and asks whether bevacizumab-containing therapy is appropriate. She is asking whether the goal of treatment is cure or symptom relief.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like platinum-resistant recurrent ovarian cancer with multiple prior regimens, but the added rectosigmoid involvement makes her ineligible for the AURELIA-based bevacizumab option.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start bevacizumab plus chemotherapy because the disease is platinum-resistant and recurrent.
Proceed with the AURELIA-style regimen without additional concern for the bowel findings.
Frame the treatment discussion primarily around adding bevacizumab to improve response rates.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for bevacizumab-plus-chemotherapy under the AURELIA eligibility framework because imaging suggests bowel/rectosigmoid involvement. The safer interpretation is to avoid that regimen and instead focus on palliative systemic options appropriate for platinum-resistant recurrence.
challenging_examples.rationale: The patient resembles the source case in having platinum-resistant recurrent ovarian cancer, but the new rectosigmoid involvement invalidates eligibility for the bevacizumab option described for AURELIA-eligible patients.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 64-year-old woman with high-grade serous epithelial ovarian cancer presents with symptomatic recurrence 5 months after completing her last platinum-based regimen. She has had only two prior anticancer regimens, and now has increasing abdominal discomfort, early satiety, and CT evidence of peritoneal progression without bowel obstruction. She asks about treatment options after hearing that a bevacizumab-containing regimen might be available. Her current team is also discussing whether the goal is to cure the cancer or focus on symptom control.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The recurrence timing, symptoms, and imaging are nearly identical to the source case, but the patient has not exceeded the prior-treatment limit that would exclude AURELIA eligibility.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Avoid bevacizumab plus chemotherapy on the assumption that she is already beyond the allowed number of prior regimens.
Treat this as clearly ineligible for the AURELIA-based option because it resembles heavily pretreated disease.
Default to single-agent palliative chemotherapy solely because the recurrence is platinum-resistant.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This vignette does not satisfy the exclusion criterion of having more than two prior anticancer regimens. The safer interpretation is that she may remain eligible for the bevacizumab-plus-chemotherapy approach if no other exclusion criteria are present, while goals of care should still emphasize palliation because the disease is platinum-resistant.
challenging_examples.rationale: The case is intentionally close to the source, but the prior-treatment count has been changed from three regimens to two, removing the specific ineligibility condition for the AURELIA-based bevacizumab option.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with advanced stage or recurrent endometrial cancer excluding sarcomas and squamous cell carcinomas
selected_rules.execute: use combination chemotherapy rather than single-agent chemotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Combination chemotherapy is favoured over single agent chemotherapy because of higher response rates.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer
selected_rules.execute: consider paclitaxel plus cisplatin/doxorubicin, recognizing improved response rate and median survival but increased toxicity
selected_rules.source_statement: Paclitaxel in combination with cisplatin/doxorubicin chemotherapy improves both response rate and median survival; however, the use of this three-drug combination is associated with increased toxicity.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer and minimal symptoms or non-life threatening disease
selected_rules.execute: consider hormonal therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Hormonal therapy may be a therapeutic option for those patients with minimal symptoms or non-life threatening advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer when choosing between cisplatin/doxorubicin/paclitaxel and doxorubicin/cisplatin
selected_rules.execute: weigh greater toxicity against about a three-month median survival increase before using the three-drug regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: The decision to use the three-drug combination, consisting of cisplatin/doxorubicin/paclitaxel, should be made with consideration of both the greater toxicity and the three-month increase in median survival time in comparison with the two-drug doxorubicin/cisplatin regimen.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer
selected_rules.execute: note that recent data suggest no recurrence-free survival benefit for the three-drug combination and increased toxicity
selected_rules.source_statement: However, recent data suggest no benefit to the three-drug combination in terms of recurrence-free survival and was associated with increased toxicity.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer
selected_rules.execute: consider doxorubicin/cisplatin as a comparator regimen with reported response rates of 28-45%
selected_rules.source_statement: For double-agent chemotherapy, randomized trials of doxorubicin/cisplatin reported response rates ranging from 28-45%.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer
selected_rules.execute: consider doxorubicin/paclitaxel as a double-agent option with reported response rate of 43%
selected_rules.source_statement: For double-agent chemotherapy, randomized trials of doxorubicin/cisplatin reported response rates ranging from 28-45%, other agents in combination with doxorubicin reported response rates of 30% (cyclophosphamide) and 43% (paclitaxel).

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer
selected_rules.execute: consider single-agent doxorubicin or platinum agents only with awareness of lower reported response rates
selected_rules.source_statement: Single-agent chemotherapy has reported response rates as follows: doxorubicin 17-27% and platinum agents 21%.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old woman with a history of stage IV endometrioid endometrial adenocarcinoma presents with progressive vaginal bleeding, pelvic discomfort, and new pulmonary nodules on surveillance CT, consistent with recurrent advanced disease. She remains ambulatory and has good renal function and performance status, but she is seeking treatment that offers the best chance of tumor shrinkage. Her oncology team discusses systemic therapy options, including single-agent and combination chemotherapy. Because her disease is advanced/recurrent and she is fit for treatment, the team considers multi-agent regimens rather than single-agent therapy. They also review the potential benefits and increased toxicity of a paclitaxel-containing three-drug regimen versus a two-drug comparator.
recommended_actions: Use combination chemotherapy rather than single-agent chemotherapy.
Consider paclitaxel plus cisplatin/doxorubicin, recognizing improved response rate and median survival but increased toxicity.
Weigh the greater toxicity against about a three-month median survival increase before using the three-drug cisplatin/doxorubicin/paclitaxel regimen.
Note that recent data suggest no recurrence-free survival benefit for the three-drug combination and increased toxicity.
Consider doxorubicin/cisplatin as a comparator regimen with reported response rates of 28-45%.
Consider doxorubicin/paclitaxel as a double-agent option with reported response rate of 43%.
Consider single-agent doxorubicin or platinum agents only with awareness of lower reported response rates.
source_case.rationale: This vignette fits advanced/recurrent endometrial cancer in an adult and triggers the chemotherapy selection rules. The patient is symptomatic and fit for systemic treatment, so combination chemotherapy is favored over single-agent therapy. The vignette also supports discussion of paclitaxel-containing combinations, the toxicity-survival tradeoff of the three-drug regimen, and comparator response-rate data for alternative regimens.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old woman with a history of stage IV endometrioid endometrial adenocarcinoma presents with several weeks of intermittent vaginal spotting and mild pelvic pressure. Surveillance imaging shows a small increase in a few pulmonary nodules, but no new organ involvement or rapid radiographic progression. She remains fully ambulatory, has preserved renal function, and is considering treatment primarily to control symptoms while minimizing adverse effects. Her oncologist reviews systemic options and notes that, because her disease burden is relatively limited and her symptoms are not severe, a less intensive approach may be reasonable. The discussion includes hormonal therapy and other palliative options, with chemotherapy reserved if her disease becomes more symptomatic or clearly progressive.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Same age, same stage IV recurrent endometrioid endometrial cancer, similar pulmonary metastases, and the same oncology workflow, but the symptom burden is minimal and the intent is palliation rather than maximal tumor shrinkage.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start combination chemotherapy immediately because advanced recurrent endometrial cancer is usually treated with multi-agent therapy.
Recommend paclitaxel plus cisplatin/doxorubicin for the best chance of response.
Use the three-drug cisplatin/doxorubicin/paclitaxel regimen after discussing toxicity and survival benefit.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not the typical guideline-concordant setting for aggressive combination chemotherapy because the disease is minimally symptomatic and not life-threatening. A safer interpretation is that hormonal therapy or other lower-intensity palliation can be considered first, with chemotherapy deferred until symptoms or progression justify it.
challenging_examples.rationale: The key mismatch is the clinical state: the source case supports fit, symptomatic advanced disease needing shrinkage, while this case has minimal symptoms and a palliative goal. That weakens the trigger for combination chemotherapy and makes hormonal or supportive approaches more appropriate.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old woman with recurrent stage IV endometrioid endometrial adenocarcinoma presents with worsening vaginal bleeding and pelvic discomfort. CT shows new pulmonary nodules and enlarging retroperitoneal nodes, and she is eager for the treatment with the greatest chance of tumor shrinkage. She remains ambulatory with good renal function and ECOG performance status 1. Her oncology team discusses chemotherapy options and specifically considers the paclitaxel-containing three-drug regimen versus doxorubicin/cisplatin. However, her record also shows a prior course of carboplatin/paclitaxel given for recurrence eight months ago, which produced an initial response but was stopped after neuropathy and later progression. The team is weighing next-line therapy in the setting of prior taxane exposure.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The presentation closely mirrors advanced recurrent disease with bleeding, pelvic symptoms, lung metastases, good performance status, and a desire for tumor shrinkage, but the patient has already received a taxane-containing regimen for recurrence.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Choose paclitaxel plus cisplatin/doxorubicin as the preferred first systemic option.
Escalate to the three-drug cisplatin/doxorubicin/paclitaxel regimen because she is fit and wants maximal response.
Treat this like a standard chemotherapy-naive recurrent endometrial cancer case.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The case is not guideline-concordant for simply applying the usual first-line combination options because she has already received prior taxane-based chemotherapy for recurrent disease. The safer interpretation is to review prior regimen exposure and toxicity before selecting next-line systemic therapy, rather than defaulting to the standard combination choices.
challenging_examples.rationale: The subtle but important change is prior treatment history. The source vignette implies treatment-naive recurrent advanced disease; here, prior carboplatin/paclitaxel alters the context and makes automatic use of the cited combination regimens less appropriate without individualized reassessment.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Metastatic gastric or GEJ carcinoma that does not overexpress HER2 and first-line chemotherapy is being given
selected_rules.execute: Do not add a biological agent to the first-line chemotherapy regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients with metastatic gastric cancer or GEJ carcinoma not overexpressing human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2), medical oncologists should not prescribe a biological agent in addition to a first-line chemotherapy regimen

selected_rules.activation_condition: Gastric or GEJ carcinoma is recurrent or metastatic and HER2 is not overexpressed
selected_rules.execute: Do not prescribe a biological agent with first-line chemotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients with metastatic gastric cancer or GEJ carcinoma not overexpressing human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2), medical oncologists should not prescribe a biological agent in addition to a first-line chemotherapy regimen

selected_rules.activation_condition: Advanced gastric or GEJ carcinoma and later-line therapy is being considered
selected_rules.execute: Do not prescribe an immune checkpoint inhibitor in addition to standard of care
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients with gastric or GEJ carcinoma undergoing later lines of therapy, medical oncologists should not prescribe ICI in addition to standard of care.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult with advanced, non-resectable gastric or GEJ carcinoma starting first-line treatment
selected_rules.execute: Prescribe a fluoropyrimidine-oxaliplatin doublet or a fluoropyrimidine-irinotecan doublet regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: Medical oncologists should prescribe either a fluoropyrimidine-oxaliplatin doublet or a fluoropyrimidine-irinotecan doublet regimen in the first-line treatment of patients with locally advanced, recurrent, or metastatic gastric and GEJ carcinoma.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Recurrent or metastatic gastric or GEJ carcinoma starting first-line treatment
selected_rules.execute: Add an immune checkpoint inhibitor to a fluoropyrimidine doublet chemotherapy regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients with recurrent or metastatic gastric and GEJ carcinoma, medical oncologists should prescribe an immune checkpoint inhibitor (ICI) in addition to a fluoropyrimidine doublet chemotherapy regimen in the first-line setting.

selected_rules.activation_condition: HER2 overexpressing gastric or GEJ carcinoma starting first-line treatment
selected_rules.execute: Add trastuzumab to a fluoropyrimidine doublet chemotherapy regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients with HER2 overexpressing gastric or GEJ carcinoma, medical oncologists should prescribe the addition of trastuzumab to a fluoropyrimidine doublet chemotherapy regimen in the first-line setting.
