selected_rules.activation_condition: Gastric or GEJ carcinoma is recurrent or metastatic and first-line treatment is being selected
selected_rules.execute: Use a fluoropyrimidine doublet chemotherapy regimen with an immune checkpoint inhibitor
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients with recurrent or metastatic gastric and GEJ carcinoma, medical oncologists should prescribe an immune checkpoint inhibitor (ICI) in addition to a fluoropyrimidine doublet chemotherapy regimen in the first-line setting.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Gastric or GEJ carcinoma is recurrent or metastatic and first-line treatment is being selected
selected_rules.execute: Use a fluoropyrimidine-oxaliplatin doublet or fluoropyrimidine-irinotecan doublet regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: Medical oncologists should prescribe either a fluoropyrimidine-oxaliplatin doublet or a fluoropyrimidine-irinotecan doublet regimen in the first-line treatment of patients with locally advanced, recurrent, or metastatic gastric and GEJ carcinoma.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man with progressive dysphagia, early satiety, and a 9-kg weight loss is diagnosed with metastatic gastroesophageal junction adenocarcinoma after CT shows liver and peritoneal metastases. Biopsy confirms adenocarcinoma, and testing shows HER2 is not overexpressed. He has not received prior systemic therapy and is being evaluated for first-line treatment. His performance status is fair, and he is interested in starting treatment promptly. The oncology team considers adding a targeted biologic agent to chemotherapy because of the metastatic disease, but the tumor is HER2-negative and this is his initial systemic regimen.
recommended_actions: Prescribe a fluoropyrimidine-oxaliplatin doublet or a fluoropyrimidine-irinotecan doublet regimen for first-line treatment.
Do not add a biological agent to the first-line chemotherapy regimen because the tumor does not overexpress HER2.
source_case.rationale: The vignette describes metastatic gastric/GEJ carcinoma starting first-line therapy with HER2 not overexpressed, which triggers the recommendation against adding a biological agent to first-line chemotherapy. It also meets the general first-line advanced gastric/GEJ treatment rule recommending a fluoropyrimidine-based doublet regimen.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man with progressive dysphagia, early satiety, and a 9-kg weight loss is diagnosed with metastatic gastroesophageal junction adenocarcinoma after CT shows liver and peritoneal metastases. Biopsy confirms adenocarcinoma, and HER2 testing is reported as negative on the primary tumor specimen. He has not received prior systemic therapy and is being evaluated for first-line treatment. His performance status is fair, and he is interested in starting treatment promptly. The oncology team is considering a fluoropyrimidine-based doublet and wondering whether to add a targeted biologic agent because of the metastatic setting. However, the pathology report notes that HER2 testing was done on a small biopsy with insufficient tumor cellularity, and repeat testing on a metastatic-site biopsy has not yet been performed.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Looks like newly diagnosed metastatic GEJ adenocarcinoma starting first-line therapy with HER2 negative results, but the HER2 result is not reliable enough to guide biologic selection.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start a fluoropyrimidine-oxaliplatin doublet and add trastuzumab immediately.
Treat as HER2-negative metastatic disease and proceed with a biologic added to first-line chemotherapy.
Select trastuzumab-based therapy without waiting for repeat biomarker confirmation.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the HER2-directed recommendation because the apparent HER2-negative result is based on an inadequate specimen with insufficient tumor cellularity, so HER2 status is not definitively established. The safer interpretation is to repeat biomarker testing on an adequate sample before deciding against or for HER2-targeted therapy. First-line chemotherapy remains appropriate, but the biologic decision should not be finalized from this questionable test result.
challenging_examples.rationale: The case preserves metastatic GEJ adenocarcinoma and first-line treatment planning, but the biomarker detail is undermined by inadequate sampling. That makes the HER2-directed recommendation inapplicable until confirmation.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man with progressive dysphagia, early satiety, and a 9-kg weight loss is diagnosed with gastroesophageal junction adenocarcinoma after CT shows liver and peritoneal metastases. Biopsy confirms adenocarcinoma, and HER2 testing is negative. He has not received prior systemic therapy and is being evaluated for treatment. His performance status is fair, and he is interested in starting promptly. The team is discussing first-line systemic therapy, including a fluoropyrimidine-oxaliplatin doublet. The chart also notes that he received adjuvant fluoropyrimidine-based chemotherapy for a resected GEJ cancer 8 months ago and now has biopsy-proven metastatic recurrence.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The presentation still sounds like metastatic GEJ adenocarcinoma at first-line decision-making, but this is recurrent disease after recent prior systemic therapy rather than a true treatment-naive first-line setting.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Choose the standard first-line fluoropyrimidine-oxaliplatin doublet without considering the recent prior regimen.
Add an immune checkpoint inhibitor to first-line chemotherapy for metastatic disease.
Treat it exactly like de novo metastatic disease and follow the usual initial regimen pathway.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for first-line de novo metastatic selection because he already received fluoropyrimidine-based systemic therapy in the recent adjuvant setting and now has recurrent metastatic disease. The safer interpretation is that regimen choice should account for prior exposure and treatment-free interval rather than automatically applying the untreated first-line metastatic pathway. The case no longer cleanly fits the recommended initial regimen rules.
challenging_examples.rationale: The disease context remains metastatic GEJ adenocarcinoma, but prior systemic therapy changes the clinical setting from treatment-naive first-line disease to recurrence after recent chemotherapy, invalidating the straightforward application of the initial first-line recommendations.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Using a 37-mm plastic cassette for acrylamide sampling
selected_rules.execute: Do not use plastic cassettes because recoveries are poor
selected_rules.source_statement: Plastic cassettes (37-mm) yielded poor recoveries of acrylamide and are therefore unsuitable

selected_rules.activation_condition: Monitoring airborne acrylamide exposure
selected_rules.execute: Use a personal sampling train with a glass-fiber filter in a 13-mm Swinnex cassette followed by a silica gel tube
selected_rules.source_statement: A worker's exposure to airborne acrylamide should be determined by using a personal sampling train consisting of a glass-fiber filter in a Swinnex cassette (13-mm) followed by a silica gel tube

selected_rules.activation_condition: Collecting airborne acrylamide samples
selected_rules.execute: Collect samples at a maximum flowrate of 1 liter/min for at least 2 hours and no more than 120 liters of air
selected_rules.source_statement: Samples should be collected at a maximum flowrate of 1 liter/min for a minimum of 2 hr; the maximum air volume should be 120 liters

selected_rules.activation_condition: After collecting airborne acrylamide samples
selected_rules.execute: Extract acrylamide from the silica gel tube with methanol
selected_rules.source_statement: The silica gel tube should then be treated with methanol to extract the acrylamide

selected_rules.activation_condition: After sampling airborne acrylamide on glass-fiber filters
selected_rules.execute: Transfer filters immediately to glass vials containing 1 ml methanol to avoid evaporation losses
selected_rules.source_statement: An important step in this method is the transfer of the glass-fiber filters to glass vials containing 1 ml of methanol immediately after sampling to avoid losses of acrylamide from the filter by evaporation

selected_rules.activation_condition: Analyzing airborne acrylamide samples
selected_rules.execute: Use gas chromatography with a nitrogen/phosphorus detector
selected_rules.source_statement: Analysis should be conducted by gas chromatography using a nitrogen/phosphorus detector

selected_rules.activation_condition: Using animal evidence to guide acrylamide exposure limits
selected_rules.execute: Base occupational exposure limits on animal studies showing exposure-response relationships
selected_rules.source_statement: However, many studies demonstrate a relationship between exposure and adverse health effects in animals. These studies are the bases for occupational exposure limits recommended by many organizations and government agencies

selected_rules.activation_condition: Evaluating acrylamide carcinogenicity in animals
selected_rules.execute: Treat acrylamide as an animal carcinogen based on rat and mouse studies
selected_rules.source_statement: Studies in rats and mice indicate that acrylamide is an animal carcinogen

source_case.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old quality-control technician in a cable-coating plant comes to occupational health after a routine air-monitoring review. The workplace uses acrylamide-based materials, and the hygienist is planning personal exposure sampling for the technician during a 3-hour shift near the mixing area. The technician has no symptoms, but the team wants a method that will accurately capture airborne acrylamide. They initially considered a 37-mm plastic cassette because it is commonly used for other dusts, but the sampling plan is being revised after a review of the acrylamide method. The hygienist also wants to ensure the sample can be analyzed reliably after collection and that losses during transport are minimized.
recommended_actions: Do not use a 37-mm plastic cassette for acrylamide sampling because recoveries are poor.
Use a personal sampling train with a glass-fiber filter in a 13-mm Swinnex cassette followed by a silica gel tube.
Collect samples at a maximum flowrate of 1 liter/min for at least 2 hours and no more than 120 liters of air.
After sampling, transfer the glass-fiber filters immediately to glass vials containing 1 ml methanol to avoid evaporation losses.
Extract acrylamide from the silica gel tube with methanol.
Analyze the samples by gas chromatography using a nitrogen/phosphorus detector.
source_case.rationale: The vignette describes workplace monitoring for airborne acrylamide and explicitly includes use of a 37-mm plastic cassette, which triggers the recommendation against that device due to poor recoveries. The scenario also supports the standard acrylamide monitoring method: a personal sampling train with a glass-fiber filter in a 13-mm Swinnex cassette followed by a silica gel tube, sampled at no more than 1 L/min for at least 2 hours and with a maximum air volume of 120 L. Post-collection handling and analysis steps are also triggered: immediate transfer of filters to methanol-containing glass vials, methanol extraction of the silica gel tube, and gas chromatography with a nitrogen/phosphorus detector.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old quality-control technician in a cable-coating plant is seen by occupational health after a routine air-monitoring review. The workplace uses acrylamide-based materials, and the hygienist is planning personal exposure sampling for the technician during a 3.5-hour shift near the mixing area. The technician has no symptoms, but the team wants a method that will accurately capture airborne acrylamide. They had initially planned a 37-mm plastic cassette, but that is being replaced after the method review. The current plan is a personal sampling train with a glass-fiber filter in a 13-mm Swinnex cassette followed by a silica gel tube, collected at 1 liter/min. However, the supervisor wants the filter cassettes left in the field box for several hours before transfer to the methanol vials, and the lab prefers to store the silica gel tube dry until shipment. The sample will then be analyzed on gas chromatography with a nitrogen/phosphorus detector.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Same acrylamide workplace monitoring setup, same personal sampling train, same flow rate, and same intended GC/NPD analysis; only the post-collection handling is subtly changed.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with the standard acrylamide sampling train and send the filter and silica gel components for routine analysis.
Leave the glass-fiber filters in the cassette until returning to the office before transferring them to methanol vials.
Store the silica gel tube dry during transport and extract it later in the lab.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant because the filter is not transferred immediately to glass vials containing 1 mL methanol, and the silica gel tube is not extracted with methanol promptly after sampling. Those handling steps are important to prevent acrylamide loss and preserve recoveries. The safer interpretation is to keep the approved sampling train but ensure immediate methanol-based preservation of both components after collection.
challenging_examples.rationale: The case preserves the core acrylamide monitoring scenario but changes two key handling steps. Delayed transfer of the glass-fiber filter and dry storage of the silica gel tube undermine the recommended method and risk analyte loss.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old quality-control technician in a cable-coating plant comes to occupational health after a routine air-monitoring review. The workplace uses acrylamide-based materials, and the hygienist is planning personal exposure sampling for the technician during a 3-hour shift near the mixing area. The technician has no symptoms, but the team wants a method that will accurately capture airborne acrylamide. The sampling plan has already been updated to use a personal sampling train with a glass-fiber filter in a 13-mm Swinnex cassette followed by a silica gel tube, rather than a 37-mm plastic cassette. The hygienist intends to collect the sample at 1.5 liters/min for the full shift to increase volume, then analyze it by gas chromatography with a nitrogen/phosphorus detector after methanol extraction of the silica gel tube.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The occupational setting, sampling train, and analytical method are unchanged, but the flow rate is only slightly increased, which makes the plan look more efficient while violating the collection limit.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Use the standard personal sampling train and keep the sample running for the full shift at the higher flow rate.
Collect the personal acrylamide sample at 1.5 L/min to maximize captured air volume.
Send the collected sample for GC with nitrogen/phosphorus detection after methanol extraction.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant because acrylamide samples should be collected at a maximum flow rate of 1 L/min, with at least 2 hours of sampling and no more than 120 liters of air. A higher flow rate risks invalidating the method. The safer interpretation is to reduce the flow rate to the specified limit before sampling.
challenging_examples.rationale: The vignette closely mirrors the source case but changes only the flow rate. That single detail is enough to violate the recommended sampling conditions even though the rest of the workflow is correct.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering any antiretroviral medication other than TDF/FTC or TDF for PrEP
selected_rules.execute: Do not use other antiretroviral medications for PrEP
selected_rules.source_statement: The use of other antiretroviral medications for PrEP, either in place of or in addition to TDF/FTC (or TDF) is not recommended. (IIIA)

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering noncontinuous or coitally timed oral PrEP
selected_rules.execute: Do not prescribe oral PrEP for intermittent, episodic, or other noncontinuous daily use
selected_rules.source_statement: The prescription of oral PrEP for coitally-timed or other noncontinuous daily use is not recommended. (IIIA)

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult MSM at substantial risk of HIV acquisition
selected_rules.execute: Offer daily oral PrEP with TDF/FTC as one prevention option
selected_rules.source_statement: PrEP is recommended as one prevention option for sexually-active adult MSM (men who have sex with men) at substantial risk of HIV acquisition (IA)

selected_rules.activation_condition: heterosexually active adult men or women at substantial risk of HIV acquisition
selected_rules.execute: Offer daily oral PrEP with TDF/FTC as one prevention option
selected_rules.source_statement: PrEP is recommended as one prevention option for adult heterosexually active men and women who are at substantial risk of HIV acquisition. (IA)

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult injection drug users at substantial risk of HIV acquisition
selected_rules.execute: Offer daily oral PrEP with TDF/FTC as one prevention option
selected_rules.source_statement: PrEP is recommended as one prevention option for adult injection drug users (IDU) at substantial risk of HIV acquisition. (IA)

selected_rules.activation_condition: before prescribing PrEP
selected_rules.execute: Exclude acute and chronic HIV infection by symptom history and HIV testing immediately before prescribing
selected_rules.source_statement: Acute and chronic HIV infection must be excluded by symptom history and HIV testing immediately before PrEP is prescribed. (IA)

selected_rules.activation_condition: prescribing PrEP
selected_rules.execute: Use daily TDF 300 mg co-formulated with FTC 200 mg (Truvada) as the approved regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: The only medication regimen approved by the Food and Drug Administration and recommended for PrEP with all the populations specified in this guideline is daily TDF 300 mg co-formulated with FTC 200 mg (Truvada) (IA)

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is taking PrEP
selected_rules.execute: Assess HIV infection at least every 3 months
selected_rules.source_statement: HIV infection should be assessed at least every 3 months while patients are taking PrEP so that those with incident infection do not continue taking it. The 2-drug regimen of TDF/FTC is inadequate therapy for established HIV infection, and its use may engender resistance to either or both drugs. (IA)

source_case.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old man who has sex with men presents for HIV prevention counseling. He reports multiple male partners over the past 6 months, inconsistent condom use, and one recent bacterial STI. He has no fever, rash, sore throat, or other symptoms suggestive of acute HIV infection, and he had a negative HIV test 2 weeks ago. He asks about starting PrEP and wonders whether he can take it only on days he expects sex, or whether he should add another antiretroviral medication for extra protection. He is otherwise healthy and has normal renal function. After discussing options, he is interested in daily PrEP and agrees to follow-up testing.
recommended_actions: Offer daily oral PrEP with TDF/FTC as one prevention option.
Exclude acute and chronic HIV infection by symptom history and HIV testing immediately before prescribing PrEP.
Use daily TDF 300 mg co-formulated with FTC 200 mg (Truvada) as the approved regimen.
Assess HIV infection at least every 3 months while the patient is taking PrEP.
Do not use other antiretroviral medications for PrEP.
Do not prescribe oral PrEP for intermittent, episodic, or other noncontinuous daily use.
source_case.rationale: This vignette triggers the recommendation for daily oral PrEP in an adult MSM at substantial risk of HIV acquisition, along with the requirement to exclude HIV infection before prescribing and to use the approved TDF/FTC regimen. It also triggers follow-up HIV assessment every 3 months while on PrEP. The patient’s questions about adding another antiretroviral or using PrEP only on sex days activate the not-recommended rules against other antiretrovirals and noncontinuous oral PrEP.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 31-year-old man who has sex with men presents for HIV prevention counseling. He reports multiple male partners over the past 6 months, inconsistent condom use, and a rectal gonorrhea infection 3 months ago. He has no fever, rash, sore throat, myalgias, or other symptoms suggestive of acute HIV infection. He had a negative HIV test 3 weeks ago and asks to start PrEP today. He is otherwise healthy with normal renal function. He says he would prefer to use the medication only around weekends when he expects sexual activity, rather than taking it every day, because his schedule is unpredictable. He is interested in the standard oral option and plans to return for follow-up testing.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: High-risk MSM with recent STI, negative recent HIV test, and normal renal function still resembles a typical PrEP candidate, but his intended intermittent use does not match the recommended dosing approach.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start oral PrEP and advise taking it only on days he expects sex
Prescribe the usual Truvada regimen but support event-driven use for convenience
Proceed with PrEP initiation without emphasizing continuous daily dosing
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for oral PrEP because the requested regimen is intermittent or coitally timed use, which is not recommended. The safer interpretation is that he remains a PrEP candidate, but if oral PrEP is chosen it should be prescribed as daily TDF/FTC rather than on-demand dosing.
challenging_examples.rationale: The patient otherwise meets the risk profile for PrEP, but the key mismatch is his plan for noncontinuous use. The guideline specifically recommends against intermittent or episodic oral PrEP.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 28-year-old man who has sex with men comes to an HIV prevention visit after learning that a partner recently tested positive for HIV. He reports multiple male partners and inconsistent condom use over the past several months. He had a negative HIV test 10 days ago at an urgent care clinic and has no fever, rash, sore throat, lymphadenopathy, or other symptoms suggestive of acute HIV infection. He is eager to start PrEP and asks whether a stronger antiretroviral combination would give him extra protection while he is trying to stay negative. His creatinine is normal and he has no chronic medical problems. The visit is otherwise routine, and he is expected to return in 3 months for HIV testing.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still centers on a high-risk MSM requesting PrEP with recent negative HIV testing and normal renal function, but the subtle issue is his request for an additional antiretroviral drug beyond the approved PrEP regimen.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Add another antiretroviral medication on top of standard PrEP for extra protection
Use a two-drug or intensified antiretroviral combination for prevention
Start PrEP with TDF/FTC plus an additional agent because of the recent exposure concern
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant because other antiretroviral medications should not be used in place of or in addition to TDF/FTC for PrEP. The safer interpretation is to use the approved daily TDF/FTC regimen if PrEP is indicated, while continuing the required HIV testing and counseling.
challenging_examples.rationale: The patient remains a typical PrEP candidate, but the proposed add-on antiretroviral is specifically not recommended. PrEP should not be intensified with extra ARVs for prevention.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Bird has signs of avian chlamydiosis
selected_rules.execute: Do not buy or sell the bird.
selected_rules.source_statement: Avoid purchasing or selling birds that have signs of AC.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Birds are being treated for avian chlamydiosis
selected_rules.execute: Do not expose treated birds to untreated birds or other potential sources of infection.
selected_rules.source_statement: Because treated birds can be reinfected, they should not be exposed to untreated birds or other potential sources of infection.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Humans with psittacosis
selected_rules.execute: Use tetracyclines as the drugs of choice.
selected_rules.source_statement: Tetracyclines are the drugs of choice (3 ).

selected_rules.activation_condition: Most patients with psittacosis
selected_rules.execute: Treat with oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily or tetracycline hydrochloride 500 mg four times daily.
selected_rules.source_statement: Most patients respond to oral therapy (100 mg of doxycycline administered twice a day or 500 mg of tetracycline hydrochloride administered four times a day).

selected_rules.activation_condition: Severely ill patient with psittacosis needing initial treatment
selected_rules.execute: Give intravenous doxycycline hyclate 4.4 mg/kg/day divided into two infusions, up to 100 mg per dose.
selected_rules.source_statement: For initial treatment of severely ill patients, doxycycline hyclate can be administered intravenously at a dosage of 4.4 mg/kg (2 mg/lb) body weight per day divided into two infusions per day (up to 100 mg per dose).

selected_rules.activation_condition: Psittacosis treatment
selected_rules.execute: Continue treatment for at least 10-14 days after fever abates.
selected_rules.source_statement: However, relapse can occur, and treatment must continue for at least 10-14 days after fever abates.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient with psittacosis
selected_rules.execute: Use standard infection-control precautions; do not use special isolation procedures such as private room, negative pressure airflow, or masks.
selected_rules.source_statement: Standard infection-control precautions are sufficient for patients with psittacosis, and specific isolation procedures (e.g., private room, negative pressure air flow, and masks) are not indicated.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Person in contact with infected birds
selected_rules.execute: Inform them about the disease and have them wear protective clothing, gloves, a disposable surgical cap, and an N95 or higher-efficiency respirator when cleaning cages or handling infected birds.
selected_rules.source_statement: Inform all persons in contact with infected birds about the nature of the disease. Instruct them to wear protective clothing, gloves, a disposable surgical cap, and a respirator with an N95 rating or a higher-efficiency respirator when cleaning cages or handling infected birds.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 54-year-old pet shop employee presents with 6 days of fever, headache, dry cough, and worsening fatigue. He reports cleaning several parakeet cages at work last week and says two birds had been “sneezing” and looked ill, but he still helped move them for sale. Today he is febrile to 39.1°C and mildly short of breath, but he is alert and hemodynamically stable. Chest exam shows scattered crackles. He asks whether he should keep working around the birds while he recovers.
recommended_actions: Do not buy or sell the birds that have signs of avian chlamydiosis.
Treat the patient with oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily or tetracycline hydrochloride 500 mg four times daily.
Use standard infection-control precautions; special isolation procedures such as a private room, negative pressure airflow, or masks are not indicated.
source_case.rationale: The patient has psittacosis consistent with exposure to ill birds, triggering the recommendation for tetracycline-class treatment and standard infection-control precautions. The vignette also includes birds with signs of avian chlamydiosis, which triggers the recommendation not to buy or sell those birds.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 54-year-old pet shop employee presents with 6 days of fever, headache, dry cough, and worsening fatigue. He reports cleaning several parakeet cages at work last week and says two birds had been “sneezing” and looked ill, but he still helped move them for sale. Today he is febrile to 39.1°C and mildly short of breath, but he is alert and hemodynamically stable. Chest exam shows scattered crackles. The shop owner mentions the birds were isolated from the rest of the flock and are now receiving treatment. The patient asks whether he should keep working around the birds while he recovers.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This preserves the same bird exposure, psittacosis-like syndrome, and outpatient stability, but the visibly ill birds are no longer being bought or sold and the patient is not clearly a typical oral-treatment candidate from the vignette alone.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for presumed psittacosis without further assessment.
Tell the shop to avoid selling the ill birds while the patient is treated.
Reassure that no special isolation is needed and continue routine cage work with standard precautions.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The case no longer supports the recommendation to not buy or sell the birds with signs of avian chlamydiosis because the birds are described as isolated and under treatment rather than actively being sold. The patient still has a compatible exposure history, but the vignette does not establish a guideline-matching action for the bird-sale recommendation; focus should be on clinical evaluation and appropriate human treatment decisions based on severity.
challenging_examples.rationale: A superficial read suggests psittacosis with sick birds, but the birds are no longer in the buy/sell scenario that triggers R001. The human management detail is also made less definitive, so the original treatment recommendation is not cleanly supported from the modified vignette.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 54-year-old pet shop employee presents with 6 days of fever, headache, dry cough, and worsening fatigue. He reports cleaning several parakeet cages at work last week and says two birds had been “sneezing” and looked ill, but he still helped move them for sale. Today he is febrile to 39.1°C and mildly short of breath, but he is alert and hemodynamically stable. Chest exam shows scattered crackles. In the emergency department he is placed in a negative-pressure room because staff worry about contagion. He asks whether he should keep working around the birds while he recovers.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The presentation still strongly suggests psittacosis from bird exposure, but the setting has been altered to include special airborne isolation despite the guideline not recommending it.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Keep the patient in negative-pressure isolation because of suspected psittacosis.
Use mask-based airborne precautions for routine inpatient management.
Order a private room as the default infection-control step.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for special isolation procedures. Psittacosis is managed with standard infection-control precautions; private room placement, negative-pressure airflow, and routine mask-based isolation are not indicated solely for this diagnosis. The safer interpretation is to use standard precautions while addressing the compatible zoonotic pneumonia clinically.
challenging_examples.rationale: The vignette looks nearly identical to the source, but the incorrect trigger is the implied need for airborne isolation. The rule specifically says special isolation procedures are not indicated for psittacosis, so that action would be discordant.

selected_rules.activation_condition: DEHP is used primarily as a softener in plastics, especially PVC; occupational exposure can occur during production, addition to plastics, or processing of softened plastics
selected_rules.execute: No clinical action specified
selected_rules.source_statement: Occupational exposure occurs during the production of DEHP, during the addition of DEHP to plastics, and in the processing of softened plastics.

selected_rules.activation_condition: DEHP exposure is being assessed in an occupational setting
selected_rules.execute: Consider that reported air concentrations in workplaces have ranged from very low to high levels depending on industry
selected_rules.source_statement: Air concentrations between 0.01 and 2.0 mg/m3 were recorded... Concentrations between 1.7 and 66 mg/m3 were recorded... DEHP air concentrations between 0.09 and 0.16 mg/m3 were reported.

selected_rules.activation_condition: DEHP is administered orally in rats
selected_rules.execute: Expect substantial gut hydrolysis to MEHP before absorption
selected_rules.source_statement: DEHP is hydrolyzed in the gut.

selected_rules.activation_condition: DEHP is given at high oral doses in rats
selected_rules.execute: Expect unmetabolized DEHP to appear in liver only after high doses
selected_rules.source_statement: In the liver unhydrolyzed DEHP can only be found after administration of high doses.

selected_rules.activation_condition: MEHP is administered orally
selected_rules.execute: Expect MEHP to be well absorbed
selected_rules.source_statement: Experiments with p.o. administration of MEHP indicated that this metabolite was well absorbed.

selected_rules.activation_condition: DEHP is administered intravenously in rats
selected_rules.execute: Expect rapid elimination from blood with short half-lives
selected_rules.source_statement: Intravenously administered DEHP is rapidly eliminated from blood.

selected_rules.activation_condition: DEHP exposure is being evaluated for distribution in the body
selected_rules.execute: Expect rapid distribution to liver, carcass, lung, and spleen after intravenous dosing
selected_rules.source_statement: Radioactivity from DEHP was rapidly distributed to liver, carcass, lung and spleen when administered i.v.

selected_rules.activation_condition: DEHP is administered orally
selected_rules.execute: Expect distribution mainly as MEHP
selected_rules.source_statement: Perorally administered DEHP is mainly distributed as MEHP.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 41-year-old man works in a PVC flooring plant where softened plastics are cut and heat-sealed on the production line. He reports that the job involves handling plasticizers during manufacturing and processing, and he asks about a chemical used in the materials. No acute symptoms are reported, but he is concerned after learning the compound is common in flexible plastics. The occupational health nurse documents that his exposure is related to production and processing tasks rather than a one-time spill. The workplace also has variable ventilation, and measured air levels in similar facilities are known to range widely depending on industry and task.
recommended_actions: Recognize that the patient’s occupational exposure is plausibly related to DEHP use in PVC/plastic production and processing.
Consider that workplace air concentrations can vary widely, from very low to high levels depending on the industry and task.
source_case.rationale: The vignette matches DEHP exposure in an occupational setting during production and processing of softened plastics, triggering R001 and R002. No clinical intervention is specified by these rules beyond recognizing the exposure context and noting that workplace air concentrations may vary widely.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 41-year-old man works in a PVC flooring plant where softened plastics are cut and heat-sealed on the production line. He reports that the job involves handling plasticizers during manufacturing and processing, and he asks about a chemical used in the materials. No acute symptoms are reported, but he is concerned after learning the compound is common in flexible plastics. The occupational health nurse documents that his exposure is related to production and processing tasks rather than a one-time spill. The workplace also has variable ventilation, but the worker says he only started on this line last week and has not yet been assigned to any tasks involving the plasticizer blend itself; he is currently doing packaging and inventory in a separate area of the plant.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The setting remains a PVC flooring plant with concern about a common plasticizer, but the described duties are now limited to packaging and inventory away from production and processing of softened plastics.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Attribute the exposure to DEHP based on the PVC plant setting alone
Document an occupational DEHP exposure from plastic production and processing
Comment that workplace air concentrations for this worker likely vary widely by task
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for DEHP exposure related to production/addition/processing because the worker is not actually performing those exposure-generating tasks. The safer interpretation is that he works in the same plant but, based on the current assignment, does not yet have a clearly documented occupational DEHP exposure.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 requires exposure during production, addition to plastics, or processing of softened plastics. R002 applies when DEHP exposure is being assessed occupationally. Here the contact is indirect and the current role is packaging/inventory away from the relevant tasks.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 41-year-old man works in a PVC flooring plant where softened plastics are cut and heat-sealed on the production line. He reports that the job involves handling plasticizers during manufacturing and processing, and he asks about a chemical used in the materials. No acute symptoms are reported, but he is concerned after learning the compound is common in flexible plastics. The occupational health nurse documents that his exposure is related to production and processing tasks rather than a one-time spill. The workplace also has variable ventilation, and measured air levels in similar facilities are known to range widely depending on industry and task. However, on review of the material safety data sheet, the plasticizer used in this specific batch is a non-phthalate substitute rather than DEHP, although the plant still handles PVC products.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The plant, workflow, and concern about flexible plastics are unchanged, but the actual plasticizer in this batch is a substitute rather than DEHP.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Recognize the exposure as DEHP because the plant makes PVC flooring
Reassure that the occupational air levels for DEHP in this facility are likely variable
Document DEHP exposure during production and processing of softened plastics
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The case no longer supports DEHP exposure because the relevant material is a non-phthalate plasticizer substitute. The safer interpretation is that this is a similar occupational setting, but not a confirmed DEHP exposure scenario.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 hinges on DEHP use as a softener in plastics, especially PVC. If the plant uses a non-DEHP substitute, the rule is not satisfied. R002 also depends on DEHP exposure being assessed.

selected_rules.activation_condition: child aged 2 through 4 years with asthma, recurrent wheezing, or wheezing/asthma in the past 12 months
selected_rules.execute: Do not give FluMist.
selected_rules.source_statement: Children whose parents or caregivers answer 'yes' to this question and children who have asthma or who had a wheezing episode noted in the medical record within the past 12 months should not receive FluMist.

selected_rules.activation_condition: person caring for severely immunosuppressed persons who require a protective environment
selected_rules.execute: Do not give FluMist / LAIV.
selected_rules.source_statement: Persons who care for severely immunosuppressed persons who require a protective environment should not receive FluMist given the theoretical risk for transmission of the live-attenuated vaccine virus.

selected_rules.activation_condition: child aged under 9 years
selected_rules.execute: Do not use Afluria.
selected_rules.source_statement: ACIP does not recommend the U.S.-licensed CSL Biotherapies' TIV, Afluria, for children aged <9 years.

selected_rules.activation_condition: person with a previous severe allergic reaction to influenza vaccine
selected_rules.execute: Do not give future influenza vaccine.
selected_rules.source_statement: A previous severe allergic reaction to influenza vaccine, regardless of the component suspected to be responsible for the reaction, is a contraindication to future receipt of the vaccine.

selected_rules.activation_condition: all persons aged 6 months or older
selected_rules.execute: Offer annual influenza vaccination every year.
selected_rules.source_statement: Routine annual influenza vaccination is recommended for all persons aged ≥6 months.

selected_rules.activation_condition: vaccination is being planned and influenza activity has not yet started in the community
selected_rules.execute: Vaccinate as soon as vaccine is available, before influenza activity begins if possible.
selected_rules.source_statement: Vaccination optimally should occur before onset of influenza activity in the community. Therefore, vaccination providers should offer vaccination as soon as vaccine is available.

selected_rules.activation_condition: influenza viruses are circulating in the community
selected_rules.execute: Continue offering vaccination throughout the influenza season.
selected_rules.source_statement: Vaccination should be offered throughout the influenza season (i.e., as long as influenza viruses are circulating in the community).

selected_rules.activation_condition: child aged 6 months through 8 years receiving influenza vaccine for the first season
selected_rules.execute: Give 2 doses of influenza vaccine at least 4 weeks apart.
selected_rules.source_statement: Children aged 6 months through 8 years require 2 doses of influenza vaccine (administered a minimum of 4 weeks apart) during their first season of vaccination to optimize immune response.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 3-year-old boy is brought to clinic in early October for a routine visit. He has a history of asthma with intermittent wheezing over the past year, including an urgent care visit for wheeze 4 months ago, but he is currently well and afebrile. His mother asks about the flu vaccine because the family wants him protected before flu season starts. He has never received an influenza vaccine before. The community has not yet reported influenza activity this season.
recommended_actions: Do not give FluMist.
Offer annual influenza vaccination every year.
Vaccinate as soon as vaccine is available, before influenza activity begins if possible.
Give 2 doses of influenza vaccine at least 4 weeks apart for his first season of vaccination.
source_case.rationale: R001 applies because he is age 2 through 4 years with asthma and wheezing in the past 12 months, so FluMist should not be used. R005 applies because routine annual influenza vaccination is recommended for all persons aged 6 months or older. R006 applies because vaccination is being planned before influenza activity has started in the community. R008 applies because he is 6 months through 8 years old and is receiving influenza vaccine for the first season, so he needs 2 doses at least 4 weeks apart.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 3-year-old boy is brought to clinic in early October for a routine visit. He has a history of asthma with intermittent wheezing over the past year, including an urgent care visit for wheeze 4 months ago, but he is currently well and afebrile. His mother asks about the flu vaccine because the family wants him protected before flu season starts. He has received one influenza vaccine last winter at an out-of-state urgent care, but the family cannot provide documentation and the state registry has no record. The community has not yet reported influenza activity this season.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The visit timing, age, asthma history, and pre-season vaccine request all mirror the standard pediatric influenza scenario, but prior influenza vaccination may not truly be absent.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Give FluMist since he is healthy today and the family wants nasal vaccine.
Administer 2 influenza vaccine doses 4 weeks apart for his first season.
Proceed with routine annual influenza vaccination without clarifying prior records.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for a first-season 2-dose schedule because he may already have received a prior influenza vaccine, and the missing documentation should be clarified before assuming he is influenza-vaccine naive. FluMist remains inappropriate because of asthma and wheeze within the past 12 months, so an age-appropriate inactivated vaccine should be used if vaccination is due.
challenging_examples.rationale: The vignette preserves the original asthma and preseason context, but the subtle change is a possible prior influenza vaccination that is undocumented. That invalidates automatic application of the first-season 2-dose recommendation. The asthma history still excludes FluMist.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 3-year-old boy is brought to clinic in early October for a routine visit. He has a history of asthma with intermittent wheezing over the past year, including an urgent care visit for wheeze 4 months ago, but he is currently well and afebrile. His mother asks about the flu vaccine because the family wants him protected before flu season starts. He has never received an influenza vaccine before. The community health department reported several confirmed influenza cases in nearby schools yesterday, although widespread community transmission has not yet been declared.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The child, age, asthma history, and first-time influenza vaccination request are unchanged, but the local timing of influenza activity is no longer clearly pre-circulation.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Vaccinate now because influenza season is starting and the vaccine is available.
Prioritize giving influenza vaccine before any further delays.
Offer the first dose today and schedule the second dose at least 4 weeks later.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The case no longer fits the pre-influenza-activity recommendation because influenza activity has already been detected locally, so the timing is not purely before onset of community circulation. Vaccination is still appropriate, but the safer interpretation is to follow routine seasonal vaccination guidance rather than relying on the pre-season timing rule.
challenging_examples.rationale: The change from no influenza activity to confirmed nearby cases makes the scenario less clearly within the before-circulation window. That breaks the specific recommendation about vaccinating before influenza activity begins, even though vaccination can still be offered.

selected_rules.activation_condition: workplace exposure is to inorganic lead
selected_rules.execute: Control employee exposure by adhering to the standard sections in this document.
selected_rules.source_statement: recommends that employee exposure to inorganic lead in the workplace be controlled by adherence to the following sections.

selected_rules.activation_condition: workplace exposure is to inorganic lead
selected_rules.execute: Apply the standard as a lifetime 8-hour day, 40-hour week protection target.
selected_rules.source_statement: The standard is designed to protect the health and safety of workers for an 8-hour day, 40-hour week over a working lifetime; compliance with the standard should therefore prevent adverse effects of lead on the health and safety of workers.

selected_rules.activation_condition: workplace exposure is to inorganic lead
selected_rules.execute: Use valid, reproducible, and available measurement techniques for compliance determinations.
selected_rules.source_statement: The standard is measurable by techniques that are valid, reproducible, and available to industry and government agencies.

selected_rules.activation_condition: workplace exposure is to inorganic lead
selected_rules.execute: Recognize that sufficient technology exists to comply with the recommended standard.
selected_rules.source_statement: Sufficient technology exists to permit compliance with the recommended standard.

selected_rules.activation_condition: workplace air exposure to inorganic lead
selected_rules.execute: Keep the 8-hour time-weighted average airborne lead concentration at or below 0.15 mg Pb/m^3.
selected_rules.source_statement: workers shall not be exposed to inorganic lead at a concentration greater that 0.15 mg Pb/m^ determined as a time-weighted average (TWA) exposure for an 8-hour workday.

selected_rules.activation_condition: environmental samples are collected for lead monitoring
selected_rules.execute: Collect environmental samples using Appendix I or an equivalent method, and analyze them using Appendix II or an equivalent method with comparable precision and accuracy.
selected_rules.source_statement: Procedures for collection of environmental samples shall be as provided in Appendix I, or by an equivalent method. Analysis of samples shall be as provided in Appendix II, or by any method shown to be equivalent in precision and accuracy to the method specified in Appendix II.

selected_rules.activation_condition: worker is subject to exposure to inorganic lead
selected_rules.execute: Make biologic monitoring available to the worker.
selected_rules.source_statement: Biologic monitoring shall be made available to all workers subject to "exposure to inorganic lead."

selected_rules.activation_condition: worker is subject to exposure to inorganic lead
selected_rules.execute: Use whole blood lead testing, or urine lead testing as an alternative, to assess lead absorption.
selected_rules.source_statement: It consists of sampling and analysis of whole blood, or alternatively, of urine for lead content.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man works in a battery recycling plant and presents for a routine occupational health visit. He reports no acute symptoms, but says his job involves handling lead-containing dust during dismantling and cleanup of lead-acid batteries. The company recently began air sampling in the work area, and the occupational nurse is reviewing exposure controls. His supervisor asks whether the current monitoring program is appropriate and whether workers like him should have medical surveillance. The patient has been on the job for 6 years, works full time, and spends most shifts near the smelting and sorting stations where inorganic lead exposure is possible.
recommended_actions: Control employee exposure to inorganic lead by adhering to the standard sections in this document.
Use valid, reproducible, and available measurement techniques for compliance determinations.
Make biologic monitoring available to the worker.
Use whole blood lead testing, or urine lead testing as an alternative, to assess lead absorption.
source_case.rationale: The vignette describes a worker with occupational exposure to inorganic lead, which triggers the general control and monitoring recommendations. Because he is subject to exposure, biologic monitoring should be made available, and lead absorption should be assessed with whole blood lead testing or urine lead testing as an alternative. The workplace is also performing air sampling, so compliance determinations should use valid, reproducible, and available measurement techniques.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man works in a battery recycling plant and presents for a routine occupational health visit. He reports no acute symptoms, but says his job involves handling lead-containing dust during dismantling and cleanup of lead-acid batteries. The company recently began air sampling in the work area, and the occupational nurse is reviewing exposure controls. His supervisor asks whether the current monitoring program is appropriate and whether workers like him should have medical surveillance. The patient has been on the job for 6 years, works full time, and spends most shifts near the smelting and sorting stations where inorganic lead exposure is possible. However, for the past month he has been assigned exclusively to a separate administrative office and has not entered the production area or handled lead-containing material during that period.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The workplace and job history strongly suggest inorganic lead exposure, but the current assignment has removed him from exposure for the relevant monitoring period.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Continue lead-specific exposure controls and surveillance without rechecking whether he is still subject to exposure
Order routine biologic monitoring with whole blood lead testing at this visit
Treat the workplace air sampling program as the basis for current lead compliance monitoring
Assume his prior battery plant work alone makes lead testing immediately indicated now
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the lead surveillance recommendation because he is no longer currently subject to inorganic lead exposure after being moved to a separate office role. The safer interpretation is to verify current exposure status before applying lead-specific monitoring or compliance actions.
challenging_examples.rationale: The recommendation applies to workers subject to exposure to inorganic lead. A temporary administrative reassignment breaks that condition, so the vignette is a near-miss rather than a clear exposure case.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man works in a battery recycling plant and presents for a routine occupational health visit. He reports no acute symptoms, but says his job involves handling lead-containing dust during dismantling and cleanup of lead-acid batteries. The company recently began air sampling in the work area, and the occupational nurse is reviewing exposure controls. His supervisor asks whether the current monitoring program is appropriate and whether workers like him should have medical surveillance. The patient has been on the job for 6 years, works full time, and spends most shifts near the smelting and sorting stations where inorganic lead exposure is possible. The industrial hygienist notes, however, that the current measurements are from wipe samples on surfaces and historical blood lead trends from prior employees; no valid, reproducible personal air sampling method has yet been implemented for compliance determinations.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The setting still appears to be a classic lead-exposed workplace with active monitoring, but the described testing methods are not the type required for compliance determinations or biologic assessment.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Use the current surface wipe results to make compliance decisions for lead exposure
Proceed with the existing monitoring program as sufficient for lead surveillance
Order whole blood lead testing based on the plant’s historical lead data alone
Document the air sampling effort as adequate without confirming method validity and reproducibility
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant because the monitoring described does not use valid, reproducible, and available measurement techniques for compliance determinations, and the environmental sampling details are not equivalent to the required methods. The safer interpretation is to first establish proper compliant sampling and analysis before treating the program as meeting the lead standard.
challenging_examples.rationale: The recommendation requires valid, reproducible, and available measurement techniques, and environmental sampling should follow specified or equivalent methods. Wipe samples and historical trends alone do not satisfy that condition for compliance or surveillance decisions.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When the public may be worried or outraged
selected_rules.execute: Do not dismiss outrage or tell people they should not be afraid.
selected_rules.source_statement: Don't Dismiss Outrage. ... When people are afraid and worried, the worst thing to do is pretend that they're not. The second worst thing is to tell them they shouldn't be.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When giving reassurance during an early crisis
selected_rules.execute: Do not over-reassure or minimize the risk.
selected_rules.source_statement: Don't over-reassure. ... Reassurance can backfire.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When the public or media ask about cause, blame, or liability early in the event
selected_rules.execute: Do not discuss liability, blame, or cause before the situation is contained.
selected_rules.source_statement: Avoid discussing liability: Questions of cause, blame, responsibility, and liability often follow a crisis. It is not appropriate to discuss them at the early stages.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When the event involves a possible bioterrorism claim or rumor
selected_rules.execute: Do not speculate about bioterrorism; refer questions to law enforcement and coordinate statements.
selected_rules.source_statement: CDC will not comment to the media about the possibility of an outbreak being a bioterrorist event; nor will it comment on the FBI's participation in an investigation unless first confirmed by the FBI to the media.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When communicating during a crisis or emergency
selected_rules.execute: Use crisis and emergency risk communication principles to help people make the best possible decisions for their well-being.
selected_rules.source_statement: CERC is the attempt by public health professionals to provide information that allows individuals, stakeholders, and entire communities to make the best possible decisions for their well-being during a crisis or emergency.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When communicating during a crisis or emergency
selected_rules.execute: Be first, be right, be credible, express empathy, promote action, and show respect.
selected_rules.source_statement: Throughout this book, six principles of effective crisis and risk communication are emphasized: 1. Be First... 2. Be Right... 3. Be Credible... 4. Express Empathy... 5. Promote Action... 6. Show Respect.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When the public is affected by a crisis
selected_rules.execute: Provide simple, credible, accurate, consistent information on time.
selected_rules.source_statement: When communicating in the initial phase of an emergency, it is important to present information that is simple, credible, accurate, consistent, and delivered on time.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When people need to know what to do during a crisis
selected_rules.execute: Give people meaningful actions they can take and offer a range of responses.
selected_rules.source_statement: Giving people meaningful things to do calms anxiety, helps restore order, and promotes a restored sense of control.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 34-year-old elementary school teacher comes to urgent care after hearing that several students in her district developed sudden vomiting and diarrhea within the same day. She is visibly anxious and says parents are “panicking” on social media, asking whether the school cafeteria food was contaminated and whether this could be a deliberate poisoning. She has no fever or abdominal tenderness and is mainly worried about what to tell her family and coworkers. In the waiting room, she repeatedly asks for confirmation that the outbreak is “probably nothing serious” and wants to know the exact cause immediately. The clinic has not yet received confirmed public health information, and local officials are still investigating the source and scope of the illness.
recommended_actions: Use crisis and emergency risk communication principles to guide the response.
Be first, be right, be credible, express empathy, promote action, and show respect.
Provide simple, credible, accurate, consistent information on time.
Give people meaningful actions they can take and offer a range of responses.
Do not dismiss the anxiety or tell people they should not be afraid.
Do not discuss blame, responsibility, liability, or cause before the situation is contained; avoid speculating about a deliberate cause.
source_case.rationale: The vignette involves an unfolding community illness event with public anxiety, early uncertainty, and questions about cause and blame. That triggers the crisis communication rules: communicate with CERC principles, provide timely accurate information, offer actionable steps, and respond with empathy and respect. Because the patient and community are worried, the response should not minimize fear or dismiss outrage. Because the source is not yet confirmed, discussions of blame or a deliberate cause should be avoided.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 34-year-old elementary school teacher comes to urgent care after hearing that several students in her district developed sudden vomiting and diarrhea within the same day. She is visibly anxious and says parents are “panicking” on social media, asking whether the school cafeteria food was contaminated and whether this could be a deliberate poisoning. She has no fever or abdominal tenderness and is mainly worried about what to tell her family and coworkers. In the waiting room, she repeatedly asks for confirmation that the outbreak is “probably nothing serious” and wants to know the exact cause immediately. The clinic has not yet received confirmed public health information, and local officials are still investigating the source and scope of the illness. However, the school nurse notes that the affected students are all from the same kindergarten class and one child has already been found to have a confirmed norovirus infection from a household contact, with no evidence of a wider cluster yet.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The setting still involves worried community members and an apparent outbreak, but the source is already partly identified as a routine norovirus exposure rather than an unresolved event with cause speculation still off-limits.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Launch into discussion of whether the school cafeteria or another party is responsible for the illness
Frame the event as a possible deliberate poisoning and advise contacting law enforcement immediately
Provide broad crisis messaging about an ongoing unknown outbreak without acknowledging the confirmed household-source norovirus case
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the no-cause/blame recommendation because a confirmed, mundane infectious source has already been identified for the affected children. The safer interpretation is to respond with accurate, targeted counseling about the known norovirus exposure and routine infection-control advice rather than treating it as an unresolved public health event requiring speculation avoidance.
challenging_examples.rationale: R003 applies to early events when cause, blame, or liability are still unknown. Here, the illness source has already been linked to a confirmed norovirus household contact, so the specific recommendation against discussing cause is no longer the limiting issue.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 34-year-old elementary school teacher comes to urgent care after hearing that several students in her district developed sudden vomiting and diarrhea within the same day. She is visibly anxious and says parents are “panicking” on social media, asking whether the school cafeteria food was contaminated and whether this could be a deliberate poisoning. She has no fever or abdominal tenderness and is mainly worried about what to tell her family and coworkers. In the waiting room, she repeatedly asks for confirmation that the outbreak is “probably nothing serious” and wants to know the exact cause immediately. The clinic has not yet received confirmed public health information, and local officials are still investigating the source and scope of the illness. But before she arrived, the county health department had already issued a detailed advisory stating that the outbreak was traced to a one-time contaminated catered event, all exposed attendees have been identified, and there are no additional cases under investigation.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The vignette still feels like an anxious early outbreak encounter, but the public health investigation is actually contained and no longer an open-ended crisis with unresolved community fear.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Reassure her that there is nothing to worry about and that the outbreak is already over
Use broad crisis communication language aimed at an evolving community emergency
Tell her to stop overreacting because the source has been identified and there is no reason for concern
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the recommendation about responding to ongoing public worry or outrage because the situation has been contained and formally characterized by public health. The safer interpretation is to provide routine, factual counseling about the completed exposure and advice for monitoring symptoms, rather than crisis messaging aimed at active community alarm.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 is triggered when the public may be worried or outraged in an active crisis. In this case, the health department has already contained and explained the outbreak, so the emotional and informational context no longer matches the rule’s intended setting.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has latent tuberculosis infection
selected_rules.execute: do not generally offer rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: ATS and CDC now recommend that this regimen should generally not be offered to persons with LTBI.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has latent tuberculosis infection and is HIV-negative
selected_rules.execute: do not generally offer rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: ATS and CDC now recommend that this regimen should generally not be offered to persons with LTBI for either HIV-negative or HIV-infected persons.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has latent tuberculosis infection and is HIV-infected
selected_rules.execute: do not generally offer rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: ATS and CDC now recommend that this regimen should generally not be offered to persons with LTBI for either HIV-negative or HIV-infected persons.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with latent tuberculosis infection is taking other liver-injury medications
selected_rules.execute: do not offer rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: this regimen should never be offered to patients who 1) are concurrently taking other medications associated with liver injury

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with latent tuberculosis infection drinks excessive alcohol
selected_rules.execute: do not offer rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: this regimen should never be offered to patients who 2) drink excessive amounts of alcohol, even if alcohol use is discontinued during treatment

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with latent tuberculosis infection has underlying liver disease
selected_rules.execute: do not offer rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: this regimen should never be offered to patients who 3) have underlying liver disease

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with latent tuberculosis infection has a history of isoniazid-associated liver injury
selected_rules.execute: do not offer rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: this regimen should never be offered to patients who 4) have a history of INH-associated liver injury.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient taking rifampin plus pyrazinamide has AST greater than five times upper limit of normal without symptoms
selected_rules.execute: discontinue rifampin plus pyrazinamide immediately and do not resume
selected_rules.source_statement: Use of RZ should be discontinued immediately and not resumed for any of the following findings: 1) AT greater than five times the upper limit of normal range in an asymptomatic person

source_case.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man is evaluated after a positive interferon-gamma release assay during work screening. He has no cough, fever, weight loss, or night sweats, and chest radiograph is normal, consistent with latent tuberculosis infection. He reports drinking 4–6 beers nightly for years, though he recently cut down to 2 beers on weekends. He asks whether he can take the short-course rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen because a coworker completed it. He has no known HIV infection, but he does take atorvastatin and has mildly elevated baseline liver enzymes from suspected fatty liver disease.
recommended_actions: Do not generally offer the rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen for latent tuberculosis infection.
Do not offer rifampin plus pyrazinamide because the patient drinks excessive amounts of alcohol, even if alcohol use is reduced during treatment.
Do not offer rifampin plus pyrazinamide because he has underlying liver disease.
source_case.rationale: This vignette triggers latent tuberculosis infection with HIV-negative status, excessive alcohol use, and underlying liver disease. Under the supplied rules, rifampin plus pyrazinamide should generally not be offered for LTBI, and it should never be offered in patients with excessive alcohol use or underlying liver disease.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man is evaluated after a positive interferon-gamma release assay during work screening. He has no cough, fever, weight loss, or night sweats, and chest radiograph is normal, consistent with latent tuberculosis infection. He reports that he stopped drinking alcohol after a DUI last year and now has no current alcohol use. He asks whether he can take the short-course rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen because a coworker completed it. He has no known HIV infection, and his baseline liver tests are normal; he takes atorvastatin but no other potentially hepatotoxic medications.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The overall LTBI workup, the request for rifampin plus pyrazinamide, and the concern about liver safety are unchanged, but the alcohol exposure is now remote rather than current excessive use and there is no underlying liver disease.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start the rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen for latent tuberculosis infection
Reassure him that prior alcohol use alone should not matter and proceed with the short-course regimen
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is still latent tuberculosis infection, so rifampin plus pyrazinamide is generally not recommended, but the specific contraindications of ongoing excessive alcohol use and underlying liver disease are not present. The safer interpretation is to avoid this regimen on general guideline grounds and consider an alternative LTBI regimen instead.
challenging_examples.rationale: The vignette preserves LTBI and the request for RZ, but removes the alcohol and liver-disease contraindications that made the source case clearly discordant for those specific rules.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man is evaluated after a positive interferon-gamma release assay during work screening. He denies cough, fever, weight loss, or night sweats, and chest radiograph is normal. He is being followed for latent tuberculosis infection and asks about the short-course rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen because his brother tolerated it well. He has never had hepatitis, drinks only one beer on special occasions, and his liver tests are normal. He is HIV-negative. He also mentions that he previously took isoniazid for several weeks years ago without any liver-related adverse effects, but he stopped early because he moved.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case remains a routine LTBI consultation with the same medication question and a similar risk profile, but the prior isoniazid history is incomplete and does not establish isoniazid-associated liver injury.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer rifampin plus pyrazinamide because his prior isoniazid course was tolerated
Select the short-course rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen since he has latent TB and normal baseline labs
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: He has latent tuberculosis infection, so rifampin plus pyrazinamide is not generally recommended. The extra detail about prior isoniazid use does not indicate a history of isoniazid-associated liver injury, so that specific contraindication is not met. A safer reading is to avoid RZ and choose another LTBI regimen if treatment is indicated.
challenging_examples.rationale: The source case’s strong hepatotoxicity risk factors are removed, and the only prior isoniazid exposure is explicitly described as non-hepatotoxic, invalidating the liver-injury-based exclusion.

selected_rules.activation_condition: heat exposure is prolonged or severe enough to cause body temperature above 38°C
selected_rules.execute: do not continue further exposures during the same day
selected_rules.source_statement: There is abundant evidence that the physiological strain of an exposure which raises body temperature above 38°C is such as to contraindicate further exposures during the same day; it may take hours for complete recovery.

selected_rules.activation_condition: employee exposure to heat in the workplace
selected_rules.execute: control heat exposure by requiring compliance with the work practice standard
selected_rules.source_statement: NIOSH recommends that employee exposure to heat in the workplace be controlled by requiring compliance with the work practice standard set forth in the following sections.

selected_rules.activation_condition: hot environmental conditions exceed WBGT 79°F
selected_rules.execute: apply the standard to all places of employment indoors and outdoors and to all employees except those required to wear impermeable protective clothing
selected_rules.source_statement: The provisions of this standard are applicable to all places of employment, indoors and outdoors, and to all employees except those who are required to wear impermeable protective clothing.

selected_rules.activation_condition: continuous exposure for one hour or intermittent exposure for two hours and WBGT exceeds 79°F for men or 76°F for women
selected_rules.execute: initiate one or a combination of the listed protective practices to keep body core temperature from exceeding 100.4°F
selected_rules.source_statement: When exposure of an employee is continuous for one hour or intermittent for a period of two hours and the time-weighted average WBGT exceeds 79°F for men or 76°F for women, then any one or combination of the following practices shall be initiated to insure that the employee's body care temperature does not exceed 100.4°F:

selected_rules.activation_condition: employee is unacclimatized
selected_rules.execute: acclimatize over 6 days starting at 50 percent of anticipated total work load and time exposure on day 1, then increase by 10 percent daily to 100 percent on day 6
selected_rules.source_statement: Unacclimatized employees shall be acclimatized over a period of 6 days. The acclimatization schedule shall begin with 50 percent of the anticipated total work load and time exposure on the first day, followed by daily 10 percent increments building up 100 percent total exposure on the sixth day.

selected_rules.activation_condition: regular acclimatized employee returns after nine or more consecutive calendar days of leave
selected_rules.execute: use a 4-day reacclimatization schedule starting at 50 percent on day 1 and increasing by 20 percent daily to 100 percent on day 4
selected_rules.source_statement: Regular acclimatized employees who return from nine or more consecutive calendar days of leave, shall undergo a four day acclimatization period. The acclimatization schedule shall begin with 50 percent of the anticipated total exposure on the first day, followed by daily 20 percent increments building up to 100 percent total exposure on the fourth day.

selected_rules.activation_condition: regular acclimatized employee returns after four consecutive days of illness
selected_rules.execute: obtain medical permission to return to work and use a 4-day reacclimatization schedule as above
selected_rules.source_statement: Regular acclimatized employees who return from four consecutive days of illness should have medical permission to return to the job, and should undergo a four day re-acclimatization period as defined in (2) above.

selected_rules.activation_condition: workers are exposed to hot jobs
selected_rules.execute: implement a work and rest regimen to reduce peaks of physiological strain and improve recovery during rest periods
selected_rules.source_statement: A work and rest regimen shall be implemented to reduce the peaks of physiological strain and to improve recovery during rest periods.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old warehouse worker presents to occupational health after a late-afternoon shift in a non-air-conditioned loading dock. He had been stacking boxes and moving pallets for about 90 minutes with only brief water breaks. On the site nurse’s check, his oral temperature is 38.4°C, and he reports marked fatigue, heavy sweating, and a throbbing headache. He says he feels better after resting indoors for 20 minutes, but the supervisor wants him to return to the dock to finish the shift. He is otherwise healthy and has no history of heat illness. The workplace had been unusually hot all day, and several coworkers were also complaining of the heat.
recommended_actions: Do not continue further heat exposures during the same day.
Control employee heat exposure by requiring compliance with the work practice standard.
Implement a work and rest regimen to reduce peaks of physiological strain and improve recovery during rest periods.
source_case.rationale: The worker had prolonged workplace heat exposure with a measured body temperature above 38°C, which triggers the recommendation to stop further exposures for the rest of the day. Because this is employee heat exposure in the workplace, heat exposure should be controlled through compliance with the work practice standard. Since workers are exposed to hot jobs, a work-and-rest regimen is recommended to reduce physiological strain and support recovery.
