challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old warehouse worker presents to occupational health after a late-afternoon shift in a non-air-conditioned loading dock. He had been stacking boxes and moving pallets for about 90 minutes with only brief water breaks. On the site nurse’s check, his oral temperature is 37.8°C, and he reports marked fatigue, heavy sweating, and a throbbing headache. He says he feels somewhat better after resting indoors for 20 minutes, but the supervisor wants him to return to the dock to finish the shift. He is otherwise healthy and has no history of heat illness. The workplace had been unusually hot all day, and several coworkers were also complaining of the heat.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Same hot workplace exposure and heat symptoms as the source case, but the measured temperature stays below the 38°C threshold.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Stop all work for the rest of the day solely because of suspected heat strain
Treat this as a clear contraindication to any further heat exposure without reassessment
Send the worker home under the assumption that the temperature criterion has been met
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The case remains concerning for heat stress, but it does not meet the cited cutoff of body temperature above 38°C that would make further exposure contraindicated for the same day. A safer interpretation is to reassess symptoms, cool the worker, and make a conservative return-to-work decision based on ongoing clinical status and workplace controls rather than applying the same-day exclusion rule automatically.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 requires body temperature above 38°C. Here the temperature is 37.8°C, so the specific prohibition on further same-day exposure is not triggered.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old warehouse worker presents to occupational health after a late-afternoon shift in a non-air-conditioned loading dock. He had been stacking boxes and moving pallets for about 90 minutes with only brief water breaks. On the site nurse’s check, his oral temperature is 38.4°C, and he reports marked fatigue, heavy sweating, and a throbbing headache. He says he feels better after resting indoors for 20 minutes, but the supervisor wants him to return to the dock to finish the shift. He is otherwise healthy and has no history of heat illness. The workplace had been unusually hot all day, but this was a temporary outage in the dock’s ventilation system, and he is normally assigned to climate-controlled indoor clerical duties.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The heat illness presentation is unchanged, but the employee is not actually being exposed as a heat employee in the workplace on a heat-exposed job; his usual assignment is clerical and the dock work was temporary.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Apply the workplace heat work-practice standard to his job assignment without qualification
Implement occupational heat-exposure controls for his regular position immediately
Manage him under the assumption that he is a routine employee heat-exposure case requiring standard workplace heat regulation
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is still an acute heat illness scenario, but the specific recommendation to control employee exposure by requiring compliance with the work practice standard applies to employee heat exposure in the workplace. Because his usual role is not a hot job and the heat exposure described was temporary and situational, the case does not cleanly match the intended workplace program trigger; immediate clinical cooling and individualized occupational assessment are more appropriate.
challenging_examples.rationale: R002 is aimed at employee exposure to heat in the workplace. The vignette shifts the context to a temporary non-routine task rather than an ongoing hot-job exposure pattern, making the rule less directly applicable.

selected_rules.activation_condition: occupational exposure to HIV with negligible risk
selected_rules.execute: do not give postexposure prophylaxis
selected_rules.source_statement: For exposures with negligible risk, PEP is not justified (Table 1).

selected_rules.activation_condition: exposure is not occupational exposure to HIV
selected_rules.execute: do not offer PEP
selected_rules.source_statement: Not offer-PEP should not be offered because these are not occupational exposures to HIV (1 ).

selected_rules.activation_condition: skin exposure to HIV without increased risk
selected_rules.execute: do not give PEP because drug toxicity outweighs benefit
selected_rules.source_statement: For skin exposures without increased risk, the risk for drug toxicity outweighs the benefit of PEP.

selected_rules.activation_condition: suspected heroin overdose with coma or severe respiratory distress
selected_rules.execute: give naloxone
selected_rules.source_statement: Naloxone remains the treatment of choice for coma and severe respiratory distress associated with possible drug overdose.

selected_rules.activation_condition: new case of scopolamine-containing heroin overdose
selected_rules.execute: report promptly to local poison-control center and health department
selected_rules.source_statement: new cases should be reported promptly to the local poison-control center and health department.

selected_rules.activation_condition: occupational exposure to HIV with highest risk for transmission
selected_rules.execute: recommend chemoprophylaxis
selected_rules.source_statement: Chemoprophylaxis should be recommended to exposed workers after occupational exposures associated with the highest risk for HIV transmission.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering any HIV postexposure prophylaxis regimen
selected_rules.execute: include zidovudine (ZDV)
selected_rules.source_statement: At present, ZDV should be considered for all PEP regimens because ZDV is the only agent for which data support the efficacy of PEP in the clinical setting.

selected_rules.activation_condition: HIV postexposure prophylaxis regimen is being selected
selected_rules.execute: usually add lamivudine (3TC) to ZDV
selected_rules.source_statement: 3TC should usually be added to ZDV for increased antiretroviral activity and activity against many ZDV-resistant strains.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old emergency department nurse sustains a needlestick injury while drawing blood from a patient with known untreated HIV infection and a high viral load. The needle was visibly blood-filled and penetrated deeply into her finger before she was able to wash the area. She presents within 45 minutes of the exposure. She is otherwise healthy, not pregnant, and has no contraindications to antiretroviral medications. This is an occupational exposure with a substantial risk of HIV transmission, rather than a negligible-risk contact or a nonoccupational exposure.
recommended_actions: Recommend HIV chemoprophylaxis after this high-risk occupational exposure.
Include zidovudine (ZDV) in the postexposure prophylaxis regimen.
Usually add lamivudine (3TC) to ZDV when selecting the regimen.
source_case.rationale: The vignette describes a high-risk occupational exposure to HIV, which triggers the recommendation for chemoprophylaxis. The selected rules also support including zidovudine in all PEP regimens and usually adding lamivudine to zidovudine to increase antiretroviral activity.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old emergency department nurse sustains a needlestick injury while drawing blood from a patient with untreated HIV infection and a high viral load. The needle was visibly blood-filled and punctured the skin of her finger, but it barely grazed the surface and did not bleed. She cleans the area immediately and presents to occupational health 50 minutes later. She is otherwise healthy, not pregnant, and has no contraindications to antiretroviral medications. The exposure is clearly occupational, but the puncture is superficial and the source patient’s HIV status is known from a recent negative rapid test performed after admission, despite the older chart label of HIV infection.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Same setting, same job-related needlestick, same rapid presentation, and the source is still described as HIV-related; the key difference is that the exposure is superficial and the current source testing does not support a true high-risk HIV exposure.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start HIV postexposure prophylaxis immediately
Use a zidovudine-based regimen with lamivudine
Treat this as a high-risk occupational exposure requiring chemoprophylaxis
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for HIV PEP because the current source evaluation does not confirm an ongoing high-risk HIV exposure, and the injury is superficial rather than a substantial inoculation. The safer interpretation is to reassess source status and exposure severity before initiating antiretroviral prophylaxis.
challenging_examples.rationale: The original recommendation applies to occupational exposures associated with the highest risk for HIV transmission. Here, the superficial injury and updated source testing make the exposure much less clearly actionable for PEP, so automatic chemoprophylaxis is not justified.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old emergency department nurse sustains a needlestick injury while drawing blood from a patient with known untreated HIV infection and a high viral load. The needle was visibly blood-filled and penetrated deeply into her finger before she was able to wash the area. She presents 6 days after the exposure because she initially assumed the wound was minor. She is otherwise healthy, not pregnant, and has no contraindications to antiretroviral medications. The exposure is occupational and biologically concerning, but the delay in presentation is substantial.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The source patient, exposure mechanism, and risk profile all look nearly identical to the original case; the main change is the late presentation well beyond the usual window for effective postexposure prophylaxis.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start HIV chemoprophylaxis now
Choose a zidovudine-containing regimen with lamivudine
Manage this as a routine high-risk occupational exposure despite the delay
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for immediate HIV PEP because the exposure occurred nearly a week earlier, outside the time frame in which postexposure prophylaxis is typically considered beneficial. The safer interpretation is urgent expert consultation, baseline testing, and follow-up rather than starting PEP late without a clear rationale.
challenging_examples.rationale: Although the exposure itself resembles a high-risk occupational needlestick, the delayed presentation undermines the recommendation for prompt chemoprophylaxis. ZDV and 3TC selection also depends on actually initiating PEP, which is no longer straightforward here.

selected_rules.activation_condition: routine prevention of hepatitis A in the United States
selected_rules.execute: Implement routine hepatitis A vaccination for children nationwide.
selected_rules.source_statement: All children should receive hepatitis A vaccine at age 1 year (i.e., 12-23 months).

selected_rules.activation_condition: state, county, or community already has a hepatitis A vaccination program for children aged 2-18 years
selected_rules.execute: Maintain the existing childhood hepatitis A vaccination program.
selected_rules.source_statement: States, counties, and communities with existing hepatitis A vaccination programs for children aged 2-18 years are encouraged to maintain these programs.

selected_rules.activation_condition: person is traveling to or working in a country with high or intermediate hepatitis A endemicity and is susceptible
selected_rules.execute: Vaccinate before departure or give immune globulin before departure; vaccine is preferred.
selected_rules.source_statement: All susceptible persons traveling to or working in countries that have high or intermediate hepatitis A endemicity (Figure 4) should be vaccinated or receive IG before departure (Tables 1-4). Hepatitis A vaccination at the age-appropriate dose is preferred (Tables 2-4).

selected_rules.activation_condition: travel is planned and first vaccine dose can be given now
selected_rules.execute: Give the first hepatitis A vaccine dose as soon as travel is considered.
selected_rules.source_statement: The first dose of hepatitis A vaccine should be administered as soon as travel is considered.

selected_rules.activation_condition: traveler departs in less than 4 weeks and cannot or will not receive immune globulin
selected_rules.execute: Still give hepatitis A vaccine and inform the traveler protection may be incomplete for the next 2-4 weeks.
selected_rules.source_statement: Travelers departing in <4 weeks who do not or cannot receive IG should nonetheless receive hepatitis A vaccine and be informed that they might not be optimally protected from acquiring hepatitis A in the immediate future (i.e., subsequent 2-4 weeks).

selected_rules.activation_condition: traveler is allergic to a vaccine component or declines vaccine
selected_rules.execute: Give a single dose of immune globulin 0.02 mL/kg; if travel lasts more than 2 months, use 0.06 mL/kg and repeat if travel lasts more than 5 months.
selected_rules.source_statement: Travelers who are allergic to a vaccine component or who elect not to receive vaccine should receive a single dose of IG (0.02 mL/kg), which provides effective protection against hepatitis A for up to 3 months (Table 1). Travelers whose travel period is >2 months should be administered IG at 0.06 mL/kg; administration must be repeated if the travel period is >5 months (Table 1).

selected_rules.activation_condition: person is an adolescent or adult man who has sex with men
selected_rules.execute: Vaccinate against hepatitis A.
selected_rules.source_statement: MSM (both adolescents and adults) should be vaccinated.

selected_rules.activation_condition: person uses injection or noninjection illicit drugs
selected_rules.execute: Vaccinate against hepatitis A.
selected_rules.source_statement: Vaccination is recommended for users of injection and noninjection illicit drugs.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old man comes to a primary care visit 3 weeks before a planned 10-day trip to rural Peru. He has never received hepatitis A vaccine and does not recall prior infection. He says he is healthy, takes no regular medications, and has no vaccine allergies. He asks whether he can still do anything to reduce his risk before leaving. Because departure is soon, he would like protection as quickly as possible and is willing to be vaccinated today.
recommended_actions: Vaccinate before departure because he is a susceptible traveler going to a country with high or intermediate hepatitis A endemicity.
Give the first hepatitis A vaccine dose as soon as travel is considered.
Because departure is in less than 4 weeks, administer hepatitis A vaccine now and inform him that protection may be incomplete for the next 2-4 weeks.
source_case.rationale: This traveler is susceptible and is going to a country with high or intermediate hepatitis A endemicity, so pre-departure hepatitis A vaccination is recommended. Since travel is already planned, the first dose should be given as soon as travel is considered. Because he is leaving in less than 4 weeks, he should still receive vaccine now and be told that immediate protection may be incomplete for the next 2-4 weeks.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old man comes to a primary care visit 3 weeks before a planned 10-day trip to Lima, Peru. He has never received hepatitis A vaccine and does not recall prior infection. He says he is healthy, takes no regular medications, and has no vaccine allergies. He asks whether he can still do anything to reduce his risk before leaving. He has already decided to get the vaccine today if it is indicated.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The travel timing, susceptibility, and request for rapid pre-travel protection closely resemble a routine pre-exposure hepatitis A vaccination scenario.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Administer hepatitis A vaccine today for imminent travel to Peru
Tell him vaccination should be given now because departure is less than 4 weeks away
Recommend hepatitis A vaccine as the preferred pre-travel protection
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This vignette does not support the travel-related recommendation because Lima is not the same as travel to a country with high or intermediate hepatitis A endemicity based on the supplied rule trigger. The safer interpretation is that the case no longer clearly meets the endemic-travel indication, so the pre-departure hepatitis A recommendation cannot be assumed from the vignette alone.
challenging_examples.rationale: The original recommendation requires travel to a country with high or intermediate hepatitis A endemicity. Changing the destination to Lima, Peru, makes the case less clearly aligned with that rule trigger while keeping the rest of the scenario similar.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old man comes to a primary care visit 3 weeks before a planned 10-day trip to rural Peru. He received a full hepatitis A vaccine series in childhood but does not remember the dates. He says he is healthy, takes no regular medications, and has no vaccine allergies. He asks whether he can still do anything to reduce his risk before leaving. Because departure is soon, he would like protection as quickly as possible and is willing to be vaccinated today if needed.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The travel context, short departure window, and request for immediate protection remain almost unchanged, making it easy to overlook the prior immunity history.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Give hepatitis A vaccine now because he is leaving in less than 4 weeks
Recommend pre-departure hepatitis A vaccination for travel to Peru
Tell him to receive hepatitis A vaccine today and expect partial protection within the next few weeks
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This case is not guideline-concordant for first-time pre-travel hepatitis A vaccination because he is not susceptible; he reports a completed childhood hepatitis A vaccine series. The safer interpretation is that the travel recommendation applies to susceptible persons, so this vignette should prompt verification of immunization history rather than automatic revaccination.
challenging_examples.rationale: The rule applies to susceptible travelers. A documented or credible prior hepatitis A vaccine series removes the susceptibility assumption, so the original travel-based recommendation no longer fits.

selected_rules.activation_condition: older adults aged 65 years or older
selected_rules.execute: implement fall prevention efforts to reduce injury, disability, and death
selected_rules.source_statement: Falls are the leading cause of injury deaths and disabilities among persons aged ≥65 years.

selected_rules.activation_condition: older adults at risk of falling
selected_rules.execute: use multifaceted fall prevention programs that combine exercise, environmental modifications, education, medication review, and risk factor reduction
selected_rules.source_statement: Research has established that effective fall intervention programs employ a multifaceted approach and incorporate both behavioral and environmental elements: exercises to improve strength and balance, environmental modifications, education about fall prevention, medication review and assessment to minimize side effects, and risk factor reduction.

selected_rules.activation_condition: community-dwelling older adults with fall risk factors
selected_rules.execute: provide behavioral instructions, targeted training, exercise to increase strength, and medication adjustments
selected_rules.source_statement: A total of 153 persons participated in an intervention that consisted of behavioral instructions and training to reduce specific risk factors ... exercise programs to increase strength, and medication adjustments.

selected_rules.activation_condition: older adults living in the community
selected_rules.execute: increase physical activity that improves strength, balance, and coordination
selected_rules.source_statement: Activities that improve strength, balance, and coordination can reduce the risk for falls and fall-related injuries among healthy and frail persons.

selected_rules.activation_condition: older adults living at home
selected_rules.execute: address home hazards and use home-visiting programs to identify and correct fall risks
selected_rules.source_statement: Because approximately 50%-60% of all falls among older adults occur at home, fall prevention programs should address home hazards that can contribute to falls.

selected_rules.activation_condition: older adults with chronic diseases or hip fracture risk factors
selected_rules.execute: maximize control of concomitant chronic diseases and counsel against inappropriate weight loss
selected_rules.source_statement: Medical approaches might include reducing fall risk factors (e.g., maximizing control of concomitant chronic diseases) and reducing hip fracture risk factors (e.g., counseling women aged ≥65 years against inappropriate weight loss).

selected_rules.activation_condition: nursing-home residents at high risk of falls
selected_rules.execute: implement intensive multifaceted interventions including environmental modifications, medication review, and attention to individual needs
selected_rules.source_statement: A randomized trial ... evaluated an intensive, multifaceted intervention of extensive environmental modifications ... medication review, and increased attention to individual resident needs.

selected_rules.activation_condition: women starting an exercise program
selected_rules.execute: start slowly at a level matched to current physical fitness and set realistic goals
selected_rules.source_statement: Women starting exercise programs should be realistic about their goals and start slowly at frequency, duration, and intensity levels commensurate with their current physical fitness condition.

source_case.patient_vignette: Mrs. L is a 78-year-old woman who lives independently in her own home. Over the past 6 months, she has had two near-falls while carrying laundry down the stairs and one minor fall in the kitchen, though she was not seriously injured. She has osteoarthritis and hypertension, takes a diuretic and a sedating sleep aid, and says she feels unsteady when getting up at night. Her daughter notes that the hallway is dimly lit and that loose rugs are present near the bedroom and bathroom. Mrs. L wants to stay active but has become less confident walking outside and has stopped attending her weekly neighborhood walks.
recommended_actions: Implement fall prevention efforts to reduce injury, disability, and death.
Use a multifaceted fall prevention program that combines exercise, environmental modifications, education, medication review, and risk factor reduction.
Address home hazards and consider a home-visiting program to identify and correct fall risks.
Maximize control of concomitant chronic diseases and counsel against inappropriate weight loss if relevant.
source_case.rationale: She is an older adult aged 65 years or older, which triggers general fall prevention. She is also at risk of falling due to prior falls, unsteadiness, home hazards, and medications, supporting a multifaceted approach. Because she lives at home, home hazard assessment and correction are indicated. Her chronic disease burden supports attention to optimizing chronic disease control.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: Mrs. L is a 78-year-old woman who recently moved into an assisted-living apartment and no longer lives in her own home. Over the past 6 months, she has had two near-falls while carrying laundry down the stairs and one minor fall in the kitchen, though she was not seriously injured. She has osteoarthritis and hypertension, takes a diuretic and a sedating sleep aid, and says she feels unsteady when getting up at night. Her daughter notes that the hallway is dimly lit and that loose rugs are present near the bedroom and bathroom in the previous house, where she now visits only occasionally. Mrs. L wants to stay active but has become less confident walking outside and has stopped attending her weekly neighborhood walks.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case preserves the same age, fall history, medications, and home-hazard language, but she is no longer living at home, which changes the setting for home hazard assessment.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Arrange a home-visiting program to inspect and correct hazards in the residence
Focus the fall-prevention plan on removing loose rugs and improving hallway lighting at home
Document that home safety modifications are indicated because she is an older adult with recent falls
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the home-hazard/home-visiting recommendation because she is now living in assisted living rather than at home. Fall prevention is still appropriate, but the safer interpretation is to address risks in her current living environment and medication contributors rather than ordering a home-based hazard visit for a place she no longer resides in.
challenging_examples.rationale: Rule R005 requires older adults living at home. The key mismatch is that she has moved to assisted living, so a home-visiting program for her former house is not the appropriate application of that recommendation.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: Mrs. L is a 78-year-old woman who lives independently in her own home. Over the past 6 months, she has had two near-falls while carrying laundry down the stairs and one minor fall in the kitchen, though she was not seriously injured. She has osteoarthritis and hypertension, takes a diuretic and a sedating sleep aid, and says she feels unsteady when getting up at night. Her daughter notes that the hallway is dimly lit and that loose rugs are present near the bedroom and bathroom. Mrs. L wants to stay active but has become less confident walking outside and has stopped attending her weekly neighborhood walks. She also mentions she intentionally lost 15 pounds over the last 4 months after starting a strict diet because she thought it would improve her energy.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The vignette keeps the same older community-dwelling woman with falls, home hazards, and chronic disease burden, but the weight change is deliberate and recent, making the counseling point different from the source case.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Counsel her against weight loss and emphasize hip fracture prevention
Focus on maximizing chronic disease control and advising her to stop the diet
Treat the recent weight reduction as an indication for the usual fall-risk counseling on inappropriate weight loss
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the inappropriate-weight-loss recommendation because the weight loss was intentional and she is not described as having unintentional or inappropriate weight loss. Fall prevention and chronic disease review remain appropriate, but the specific counseling against inappropriate weight loss does not cleanly apply here.
challenging_examples.rationale: Rule R006 applies when there are chronic diseases or hip fracture risk factors and the patient is being counseled against inappropriate weight loss. Here, the weight loss is intentional, so that portion of the recommendation is not triggered in the same way.

selected_rules.activation_condition: planning school food environment
selected_rules.execute: Discourage the sale of foods high in fat, sodium, and added sugars on school grounds and as part of fundraising activities.
selected_rules.source_statement: In addition, schools should discourage the sale of foods high in fat, sodium, and added sugars (e.g., candy, fried chips, and soda) on school grounds and as part of fundraising activities.

selected_rules.activation_condition: teachers use food in classroom management
selected_rules.execute: Discourage teachers from using food to reward or discipline students.
selected_rules.source_statement: Schools should discourage teachers from using food for disciplining or rewarding students.

selected_rules.activation_condition: school-based nutrition education is being planned or implemented
selected_rules.execute: Use a comprehensive school health program to promote healthy eating.
selected_rules.source_statement: School-based programs can play an important role in promoting lifelong healthy eating.

selected_rules.activation_condition: developing school nutrition policy
selected_rules.execute: Adopt a coordinated school nutrition policy that promotes healthy eating through classroom lessons and a supportive school environment.
selected_rules.source_statement: Policy: Adopt a coordinated school nutrition policy that promotes healthy eating through classroom lessons and a supportive school environment.

selected_rules.activation_condition: developing school nutrition policy
selected_rules.execute: Include input from students, teachers, coaches, staff, administrators, food service personnel, nurses, counselors, public health professionals, and parents.
selected_rules.source_statement: A school nutrition policy should be a brief document that incorporates input from all relevant constituents of the school community: students, teachers, coaches, staff, administrators, food service personnel, nurses, counselors, public health professionals, and parents.

selected_rules.activation_condition: developing school nutrition policy
selected_rules.execute: Adapt the policy to local needs and the health concerns, food preferences, and dietary practices of different ethnic and socioeconomic groups.
selected_rules.source_statement: The policy should meet local needs and be adapted to the health concerns, food preferences, and dietary practices of different ethnic and socioeconomic groups.

selected_rules.activation_condition: developing school nutrition policy
selected_rules.execute: Include follow-up input and a process for revising the policy as needed.
selected_rules.source_statement: The policy plan should include means of obtaining follow-up input from all parties and means of revising the plan as needed.

selected_rules.activation_condition: developing school nutrition policy
selected_rules.execute: Use a nutrition advisory committee or nutrition subcommittee with student members to develop and promulgate the policy.
selected_rules.source_statement: A nutrition advisory committee or a nutrition subcommittee of the school health advisory council having student members can develop and promulgate a coordinated school nutrition policy.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 15-year-old student is brought to a school health planning meeting after the district notices inconsistent food practices across campuses. The middle school is drafting a new nutrition policy because vending machines, classroom celebrations, and fundraisers currently vary by teacher and club. Parents have asked for healthier options, while students want foods that fit cultural preferences and after-school sports schedules. The school nurse reports that some teachers use candy as rewards, and the PTA is considering a fundraiser built around soda and chips. Administrators want a policy that can be rolled out this semester but also updated after feedback from families, staff, and students. The team is looking for a practical plan that supports healthy eating without ignoring local food traditions or the needs of different student groups.
recommended_actions: Adopt a coordinated school nutrition policy that promotes healthy eating through classroom lessons and a supportive school environment.
Include input from students, teachers, coaches, staff, administrators, food service personnel, nurses, counselors, public health professionals, and parents.
Adapt the policy to local needs and the health concerns, food preferences, and dietary practices of different ethnic and socioeconomic groups.
Include follow-up input and a process for revising the policy as needed.
Use a nutrition advisory committee or nutrition subcommittee with student members to develop and promulgate the policy.
Discourage the sale of foods high in fat, sodium, and added sugars on school grounds and as part of fundraising activities.
Discourage teachers from using food to reward or discipline students.
source_case.rationale: The vignette centers on developing a school nutrition policy, which triggers the policy-related recommendations. It also includes planning a school food environment and teacher use of food for classroom management, triggering the corresponding discouragement recommendations. No unsupported recommendations were added.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 15-year-old student is brought to a district planning meeting after several campuses report inconsistent food practices. The middle school is drafting a nutrition policy because vending machines, classroom celebrations, and fundraisers currently vary by teacher and club. Parents have asked for healthier options, while students want foods that fit cultural preferences and after-school sports schedules. The school nurse notes that some teachers use candy as rewards, and the PTA is considering a fundraiser built around soda and chips. Administrators want a policy that can be rolled out this semester, but the committee is focused on keeping the draft simple enough to avoid changing it later. The team is looking for a practical plan that supports healthy eating without ignoring local food traditions or the needs of different student groups.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The setting still involves a school nutrition policy, broad stakeholder concerns, and the same classroom and fundraising issues, but the draft intentionally omits any mechanism for follow-up input or later revision.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Adopt the coordinated school nutrition policy now and finalize it without a formal process for later feedback or revisions.
Bring together students, teachers, coaches, staff, administrators, food service personnel, nurses, counselors, public health professionals, and parents to approve the policy this semester.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the revision recommendation because the plan lacks a follow-up input pathway and a method to revise the policy after implementation. A safer interpretation is that the school may still develop the policy now, but it should also build in structured review and revision.
challenging_examples.rationale: The vignette matches school nutrition policy development but removes the required follow-up and revision process, invalidating the revision-related recommendation while preserving the rest of the context.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 15-year-old student is brought to a school health planning meeting after the district notices inconsistent food practices across campuses. The middle school is drafting a new nutrition policy because vending machines, classroom celebrations, and fundraisers currently vary by teacher and club. Parents have asked for healthier options, while students want foods that fit cultural preferences and after-school sports schedules. The school nurse reports that some teachers use candy as rewards, and the PTA is considering a fundraiser built around soda and chips. Administrators want a policy that can be rolled out this semester and are forming a small committee to write the draft. Because the district wants a quick turnaround, the committee includes administrators, food service personnel, and parents, but no students. The team is still trying to create a practical plan that supports healthy eating without ignoring local food traditions or the needs of different student groups.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The vignette preserves the same school nutrition policy workflow, stakeholders, and food-environment concerns, but the advisory group is missing student members.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Use the nutrition advisory committee to develop and promulgate the policy with the existing adult members.
Move ahead with the policy draft using administrators, food service personnel, and parents only so it can be launched this semester.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the advisory committee recommendation because the committee does not include student members. The safer interpretation is that the policy can still be developed, but the committee should be expanded to include students before promulgation.
challenging_examples.rationale: The case looks highly similar to the source but subtly violates the student-members requirement for the nutrition advisory committee, making the original recommendation inapplicable.
